plant_note: Start indoors early; seeds benefit from cold stratification.
directions: Alpine Regina is an everbearing woodland strawberry that produces small, intensely aromatic berries all season on tidy, runnerless clumps. Surface-sow the tiny seeds indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost, pressing them onto moist mix without covering, since light aids germination; a few weeks of cold stratification improves the sprouting rate. Transplant seedlings 8-12 inches apart in rich, well-drained soil with morning sun and some afternoon shade. Plants make an excellent edible border or container crop and often fruit the first year.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; seeds are very small and should be pressed onto the soil surface without covering.
directions: Alpine strawberries are compact, clumping, runner-free plants that produce small but intensely fragrant and flavored berries continuously from early summer through fall frost. Seeds are tiny and require light for germination — surface sow in trays and do not cover; keep moist at 65–75°F. Unlike garden strawberries, alpines form clumps rather than runners and are best propagated by division every 3–4 years or from seed. They are more shade-tolerant than hybrid strawberries, making them excellent for edging partially shaded paths. Harvest individual berries when fully red and aromatic.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; gooseberries may be illegal to plant in some US states where they are regulated as alternate hosts of white pine blister rust — check local regulations before planting.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun — American gooseberry is the native gooseberry of the northeastern US and Canada, one of the most productive edible native shrubs for cool-climate food gardens. The tart-sweet berries (ripening from green to reddish-purple in midsummer) are excellent for pies, jams, preserves, and fresh eating when fully ripe. The shrub tolerates partial shade better than most fruiting shrubs and produces reliably in conditions too shaded for currants and jostaberries. The stems bear short, sharp spines at the nodes; wear gloves when harvesting. American gooseberry is more resistant to powdery mildew than European gooseberry (R. uva-crispa), making it easier to grow in humid climates. Best in zones 3–7; does not perform well in hot, humid summers of zone 8 and south. Check state regulations on Ribes planting before ordering.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; at least two plants improve cross-pollination and fruit set.
directions: American highbush cranberry is a native Viburnum — not a true cranberry — bearing clusters of bright red, tart berries in fall that persist on the shrub into winter, providing critical food for wildlife during cold months. The berries have a distinct musky aroma when raw that largely disappears with cooking; they are high in pectin and excellent for jellies, sauces, and syrups as a cranberry substitute. Do not confuse with the European cranberrybush (V. opulus), which produces lower-quality, more bitter fruit. Prune out the oldest canes at the base every 3–4 years to maintain vigor and an open, multi-stemmed form.

plant_note: Set canes early spring in a sheltered, sunny spot.
directions: 'Anne' produces exceptionally large, pale-yellow fall-bearing berries with a sweet, apricot-like flavor. Grow in rich, moisture-retentive soil and support the tall canes with a two-wire trellis to keep fruit off the ground. As a primocane variety it can be mown down each winter for a single autumn crop, simplifying pruning. The soft golden fruit does not ship well, making it an ideal home-garden and fresh-eating selection.

plant_note: Plant in early spring for an erect, thornless hedge.
directions: 'Arapaho' is an early-ripening erect thornless blackberry with medium-large berries containing small, unobtrusive seeds. Its self-supporting canes need minimal trellising, making it well suited to compact plantings and home gardens. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil and provide even moisture during the fruiting period for best size and flavor. Remove floricanes after harvest and thin new canes to five or six vigorous shoots per plant.

plant_note: Plant one male per 4–6 female vines after last frost; protect new shoots from cats, which are strongly attracted to kolomikta vines.
directions: Actinidia kolomikta is the hardiest kiwi species, tolerating temperatures to -40°F in zone 3, and is grown for its small (1 in), sweet, smooth-skinned fruits that ripen in late summer. The vine is ornamentally striking due to pink-and-white variegated foliage on male plants. It is dioecious and requires both male and female plants — one male per 4–6 females. Cats are intensely attracted to the plants and will damage young vines, so protect new plantings with wire cages. Prune each dormant season to maintain a framework of lateral arms on a sturdy arbor or fence.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; extremely adaptable to wet or dry soils.
directions: Aronia is exceptionally hardy and tolerates wet, poorly drained soils, making it suitable for rain gardens. It is self-fruitful and rarely needs a pollinator, though yields improve with a second plant nearby. Prune by removing the oldest, thickest canes at the base every 3–4 years to encourage vigorous new growth and better light penetration. Harvest clusters in late summer when berries are very dark purple-black; the astringent flavor mellows after a light frost or processing.

plant_note: Plant in spring in any well-drained soil; autumn olive is highly invasive in the eastern U.S. — confirm it is legal in your state before planting.
directions: Autumn olive is a nitrogen-fixing Asian shrub with extremely high yields of small red-speckled berries that are rich in lycopene and have a sweet-tart flavor; it is among the most productive fruiting shrubs per acre in temperate climates. However, it is considered an invasive species across much of the eastern U.S. and is prohibited or discouraged in many states — verify legal status in your jurisdiction before planting. Named fruit-producing selections such as 'Garnet' or 'Red Wing' are more compact and productive than seedlings. Harvest by hand-stripping clusters in fall when berries are fully red; fruit quality improves after light frost.

plant_note: Plant two or more named cultivars for cross-pollination in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil.
directions: Beach plum is a native Atlantic coastal shrub that thrives in sandy, salt-spray-exposed sites where few other fruits survive, making it invaluable for coastal and xeric gardens. The small (0.5–1 in), round fruits range from red to purple to blue at maturity in late summer and are traditionally used for preserves and jams on Cape Cod and Long Island. Plant at least two named cultivars such as 'Autumn' and 'Premier' for adequate cross-pollination, as seedlings are variable and often infertile. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain shape; heavy pruning reduces fruiting the following year.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in very well-drained, sandy or rocky, acidic soil; bearberry strongly resents wet, fertile conditions.
directions: Bearberry is a prostrate, evergreen groundcover native to circumpolar regions, widely used in northern and mountain landscapes for erosion control on dry, acidic, sandy slopes. The small red berries ripen in fall and persist through winter, providing important wildlife food for bears, grouse, and other species; they are mealy and bland fresh but edible when cooked or dried. Bearberry is best propagated from rooted stem cuttings as seed germination is notoriously slow and difficult. Once established, the plant is extremely drought-tolerant and essentially maintenance-free; avoid fertilizing or overwatering, which quickly kills it.

plant_note: Plant bare-root bushes in autumn or early spring while dormant.
directions: 'Ben Lomond' is a heavy-cropping Scottish black currant with large, aromatic berries high in vitamin C, ideal for cordials and preserves. It flowers slightly late, giving some protection from spring frosts, and tolerates a range of soils if kept moist. Plant deeply, burying the crown a couple of inches, to encourage strong shoots from the base. Prune in late winter by removing about a third of the oldest wood, since fruit is borne best on one- and two-year-old stems.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in early spring in strongly acidic, humus-rich, moist soil, ideally under light tree canopy.
directions: Bilberry is a low-growing European relative of blueberry that differs in producing berries singly rather than in clusters; the deep purple-red flesh (unlike blueberry's white flesh) stains heavily and has an intense, slightly tart flavor. It demands strongly acidic soil (pH 4.0–5.0) with high organic matter and cool, moist conditions, making it challenging to grow in most American gardens but worth attempting in zones 3–7 under high shade or at elevation. Minimal pruning is needed; shear plants lightly every 3–4 years to rejuvenate. Harvest in mid- to late summer when berries are fully dark blue-black and tender.

plant_note: Plant container-grown or bare-root shrubs in spring or fall; aronia is highly adaptable and establishes quickly. A single plant is self-fertile but two plants will increase fruit production.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; black chokeberry is an exceptionally hardy native shrub valued both for its extraordinary fall foliage (brilliant orange-red) and for its clusters of small, astringent black berries that ripen in August–September. The berries have among the highest antioxidant content of any commercially available fruit — higher than blueberries and acai — and are increasingly marketed as a health food. Fresh berries are intensely astringent and rarely eaten raw; they excel as juice, wine, jam, and syrup once sweetened. Pollinators are attracted to the flat-topped white flower clusters in spring. Extremely tolerant of wet soil, clay, and urban conditions; one of the most versatile and underused native fruiting shrubs.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or dormant bare-root stock in early spring, burying the cutting deeper than other Ribes to encourage multiple basal shoots.
directions: Black currants fruit most heavily on one-year-old wood, so annual renewal pruning is critical: remove one-third of the oldest canes each late winter to stimulate vigorous new shoots. The berries have a strong, tannic flavor prized for jams and juices; harvest when the entire strig has turned glossy black. Choose blister-rust-resistant cultivars such as 'Consort' or 'Titania' where regulations permit. Verify local restrictions before planting.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in strongly acidic, sandy or rocky, well-drained soil; grows naturally in dry woodland edges and barrens.
directions: Black huckleberry (Gaylussacia baccata) is a native eastern North American shrub distinct from Vaccinium huckleberries, bearing shiny blue-black berries in summer that differ from blueberries in having 10 large, hard seeds rather than many tiny seeds. Plants form slow-spreading colonies via rhizomes in dry, acidic soils (pH 4.0–5.0) and are extremely fire-adapted and long-lived. The berries have a rich, complex flavor prized for pies and preserves — often considered superior to blueberries by foragers. Difficult to cultivate commercially; best suited to naturalistic plantings in appropriate native habitat.

plant_note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; keep well away from other Rubus species to limit virus transmission.
directions: Black raspberries are erect, arching canes that propagate by tip layering rather than suckering; they have a richer, more intense flavor than red raspberries and are popular for jams and wines. Tip-prune primocanes at 24–30 in in midsummer to force lateral branching, then shorten those laterals to 8–12 in the following late winter for maximum fruit bud development. Remove all floricanes after harvest and any tips that have rooted outside the row. Do not plant near red raspberries or wild Rubus, as black raspberries are highly susceptible to aphid-spread viruses.

plant_note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; trailing and semi-erect types need a trellis installed at planting.
directions: Blackberries fruit on second-year floricanes; remove all fruited canes at the base after harvest and thin new primocanes to 4–6 per hill. Erect types can be tip-pruned at 3–4 ft in midsummer to encourage lateral branching and larger yields. Harvest when berries are fully black, slightly soft, and the white core is not visible — they do not ripen further off the plant. Trailing cultivars require a T-trellis; thornless varieties are easier to manage but may be less cold-hardy.

plant_note: Plant dormant bushes in fall or early spring.
directions: 'Blanka' is a heavy-yielding white currant producing long trusses of large, translucent, mild-sweet berries that are less tart than red types. It is a robust, healthy bush that crops later in the season and holds its fruit well on the plant. Grow in fertile, well-drained soil with partial shade in warm areas to avoid scorching the pale fruit. Prune to an open framework in winter and mulch annually to conserve moisture and feed the shallow roots.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs or hardwood cuttings in late winter or spring in any moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; blue elderberry is far more drought and alkaline-soil tolerant than American elderberry (S. canadensis) and thrives in western conditions.
directions: Plant in any moist to well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — blue elderberry is the western North American native elderberry, native from British Columbia to Baja California and east to the Rocky Mountains, producing large, flat-topped clusters of dark blue to blue-black berries with a distinctive waxy, blue-gray bloom (giving both plant and fruit their names). The berries are slightly sweeter than American elderberry (S. canadensis) and are used the same way — in jellies, juices, wines, elderflower cordial, and traditional medicine. Blue elderberry is significantly more drought-tolerant than American elderberry, adapts to alkaline soils, and grows in the dry, sunny conditions of western landscapes where the eastern species struggles. It is an important food and medicine plant in the traditions of dozens of western Native American nations. Plant where spreading by suckers is acceptable.

plant_note: Set out bare-root or potted bushes in early spring once soil is workable.
directions: Bluecrop is a dependable northern highbush blueberry that yields large, mild, juicy berries in mid-season. Plant in acidic soil with a pH between 4.5 and 5.5, amending with peat or pine bark if needed, and mulch heavily to keep the shallow roots cool and moist. Give it full sun and consistent water, especially during fruit development. Prune out old canes each late winter to keep the bush productive, and plant a second highbush variety nearby to improve pollination and yields.

plant_note: Plant in early spring after frost danger passes; boysenberries are trailing types requiring a stout trellis.
directions: Boysenberries are vigorous trailing hybrids (blackberry x raspberry x loganberry lineage) that must be trained to a two- or three-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft. Cut all fruited canes to the ground after the summer harvest and tie new canes along the wires for next year's crop. Harvest when berries turn deep maroon-purple and have a rich, winey aroma. In zone 5–6 winters, remove canes from wires and lay them on the ground under mulch for protection.

plant_note: Plant one male plant for every 5–7 female plants in early spring; buffaloberry is wind-pollinated and both sexes must be within 50 ft.
directions: Silver buffaloberry is an extremely cold-hardy nitrogen-fixing shrub native to the Great Plains, valued for its bright red, tart berries that have historically been used by Indigenous peoples of North America for pemmican and sauces. It is dioecious and wind-pollinated, requiring male and female plants; purchase sexed plants, as seedlings cannot be sexed until flowering at 3–5 years. The thorny, silver-leaved plants thrive in alkaline, dry, or infertile soils where other fruits fail. Harvest by spreading a tarp and gently shaking or beating branches after a light frost, which softens the bitterness; berries are very tart raw but excellent cooked.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring in highly acidic, humus-rich soil; requires consistent moisture and does not tolerate heat or dry conditions — best suited to northern gardens and high elevations.
directions: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), consistently moist, conifer-needle-rich soil in full to partial shade; this diminutive groundcover grows naturally in boreal and mixed forests under spruce and fir where acid humus accumulates thickly. The four-petaled white flowers with green centers (like tiny dogwood blossoms) appear in spring, followed by clusters of bright red berries in summer that are edible, mildly sweet, and popular with thrushes, waxwings, and grouse. Amend soil heavily with aged pine bark, peat moss, or decomposed conifer needles to achieve the required acidity before planting. Heat and alkaline soil are the two primary causes of failure; this is not a plant for the southern United States or urban heat islands.

plant_note: Plant dormant bushes in fall or early spring.
directions: 'Captivator' is a nearly thornless American-type gooseberry producing sweet, teardrop-shaped reddish-purple berries that are pleasant for fresh eating. Its low thorn count and good mildew resistance make it one of the most user-friendly varieties for home gardeners. Grow in full sun to light shade in cool, moist, well-drained soil with a thick organic mulch. Prune lightly in winter to remove old and crowded wood, keeping the bush open and productive.

plant_note: Plant dormant canes in early spring as soon as ground can be worked.
directions: 'Caroline' is a vigorous fall-bearing red raspberry prized for large, intensely flavored fruit with high antioxidant content. Plant in full sun in fertile, slightly acidic soil and irrigate consistently during fruit development. Prune as a primocane type by cutting canes to the ground in late winter for one large fall harvest. Its heat tolerance makes it a strong choice for warmer parts of its range, though it benefits from afternoon shade in hot climates.

plant_note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after frost in a sheltered site; appreciates afternoon shade in hot climates.
directions: Chilean guava (Ugni molinae) is a compact, evergreen myrtle-family shrub native to Chile, grown for its small, crimson, aromatic berries with a flavor often described as strawberry-vanilla-bubblegum. The Queen of England's personal favorite berry, it is widely grown in the UK and mild coastal climates. It tolerates some frost but is best suited to USDA zones 8–10 in the ground, or grown in containers and overwintered frost-free. Prune lightly after harvest to maintain shape; no heavy pruning is needed. Harvest in late fall and winter when berries are deep red-purple and fully aromatic.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; Chinese wolfberry spreads by roots and can become invasive in suitable soils — plant in a contained area or where spreading is acceptable.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade — Chinese wolfberry is similar to goji berry (L. barbarum) but more cold-hardy and shade-tolerant, producing red-orange elongated berries with the same sweet-astringent, tart-sweet flavor. In traditional Chinese medicine, the leaves (often cooked as a vegetable), young shoots, and berries are all used. The dried berries (gou qi zi) are a fundamental ingredient in Chinese tonic soups and herbal preparations, used for eye health and as a general tonic for centuries. Fresh berries are also edible and pleasant. The arching, thornless-to-spiny stems produce attractive light purple flowers in summer before the berries. Remarkably adaptable to difficult soils including alkaline, clay, and dry soils, and more shade-tolerant than most full-sun fruiting shrubs. Note that it spreads by roots and seeds; manage accordingly.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in acidic, peaty, boggy or moist woodland soil; requires both male and female plants for fruit set.
directions: Cloudberry is a low-growing, rhizomatous Arctic-boreal species that requires cold winters, strongly acidic peat or sphagnum-rich soil (pH 3.5–5.0), and consistent moisture — it is exceptionally difficult to cultivate outside its native subarctic habitat. It is dioecious, so both male and female plants must be planted in proximity for fruit to set. The amber-golden berries are among the most highly prized wild fruits in Scandinavia and are used in traditional preserves; ripe berries are extremely soft and must be processed immediately. Not suited to continental climates south of USDA zone 5.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; highly adaptable to dry, alkaline soils unlike most Ribes.
directions: Clove currant (Ribes odoratum, syn. R. aureum) is a tough, drought-tolerant native prairie shrub with intensely clove-spice-scented yellow spring flowers and glossy black berries in mid-summer. It tolerates alkaline, clay, or droughty soils far better than European currants, making it the best Ribes choice for the Great Plains and intermountain West. The berries range from mild to somewhat astringent depending on selection; look for named fruit-producing cultivars such as 'Crandall'. Prune by removing the oldest canes at the base every few years; the plant suckers moderately and can form a thicket if not managed. Verify state restrictions before planting.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in acidic, peaty, moisture-retentive soil with access to irrigation.
directions: Cranberries require strongly acidic soil (pH 4.0–5.0), consistent moisture, and sandy-peat substrate — conditions that closely mimic their native bog habitat. A 3–4 in layer of coarse sand mulch helps suppress weeds and maintains the proper surface environment. The vines are slow to establish and typically take 3–4 years to produce a meaningful crop. Harvest in fall when berries turn deep red and bounce when dropped; home growers pick by hand rather than flooding.

plant_note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic soil; excellent groundcover for dry, shaded slopes in western gardens.
directions: Creeping Oregon grape is a low-growing, mat-forming relative of upright Oregon grape, native to dry mountain forests and plains of western North America and exceptionally hardy once established. It spreads slowly by underground stems to form weed-suppressing colonies bearing clusters of blue-purple berries in summer that are sour but edible and rich in vitamin C. Like its taller relative, the berries are best used in jellies and syrups with added sweetener rather than eaten fresh. The plant requires no fertilizer and very little irrigation once established in appropriate rocky or sandy soil; excess moisture and fertility promote root rot.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in acidic, peaty, or sandy well-drained soil; thrives in cool, northern or high-altitude climates.
directions: Crowberry is a low, mat-forming evergreen shrub of arctic and subarctic bogs, heathlands, and tundra, requiring strongly acidic soil (pH 4.0–5.0) and cool summer temperatures to thrive. The glossy black berries ripen in late summer and persist into winter under snow, remaining palatable after freezing — they are a critical subsistence food for northern Indigenous peoples and a major food source for migratory birds. Propagate by semi-hardwood cuttings taken in mid-summer; the plant rarely sets fruit in warm, humid climates. Minimal maintenance is needed once established as a groundcover in appropriate cold, northern gardens.

plant_note: Plant container stock in spring in acidic, well-drained, sandy or rocky soil; best suited to woodland edge plantings in its native Southeast.
directions: Deerberry is a native eastern North American Vaccinium species bearing clusters of small, blue-green to purple berries that ripen in midsummer with a flavor ranging from tart to mildly sweet depending on provenance and growing conditions. Unlike most blueberries, the flowers have exserted stamens (protruding well beyond the petals), giving them a distinctive open, star-shaped appearance that attracts native bees. The plant is very drought-tolerant once established and naturalizes well in dry, acidic woodland margins. Berries are edible fresh or cooked but have variable palatability; select superior-tasting wild ecotypes or named cultivars when available.

plant_note: Plant in spring in acidic soil; add sulfur or peat if needed.
directions: 'Duke' is a highly reliable early-season northern highbush blueberry with firm, mild-flavored berries that ripen over a concentrated window. It blooms late but ripens early, helping it dodge spring frosts while still producing an early harvest. Plant in full sun in acidic soil with a pH of 4.5 to 5.5, amending heavily with peat moss and mulching with pine bark. Provide consistent moisture, avoid lime, and prune out weak twiggy growth each late winter to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; at least two cultivars within 60 ft improve cross-pollination and yields.
directions: Elderberries are fast-growing shrubs that fruit on current-season wood arising from two-year-old stems; cut one-third of the oldest canes to the ground each late winter to keep the planting vigorous. Plant two or more cultivars for cross-pollination — solo plantings produce far fewer berries. Harvest entire umbels (flat-topped clusters) in late summer when at least 95% of berries in a cluster are dark purple-black. Raw berries and green plant parts contain cyanogenic glycosides; always cook berries before eating.

plant_note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; primocane-fruiting types can yield a fall crop in their first year.
directions: Primocane-fruiting (everbearing) raspberries bear on the top portion of first-year canes in fall, then on lower portions the following summer if left. For the simplest management, mow all canes to the ground each late winter and enjoy a single fall crop on new primocanes — this eliminates disease buildup and minimizes pruning. Harvest from late summer through first frost when berries release easily from the plug. Consistent moisture during fruit development prevents crumbling.

plant_note: Plant in spring in full sun with well-drained soil.
directions: This everbearing mulberry yields sweet, plump berries over a long season on a compact, shrubby tree that is easy to manage. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil; it tolerates a range of conditions and is quite drought resistant once established. Prune in winter to keep it at a pickable height and to open the canopy. The fruit ripens over many weeks, so harvest regularly and expect birds to compete for the crop. Note that ripe berries stain, so site it away from patios and walkways.

plant_note: Set out crowns in early spring with the crown level at the soil surface.
directions: This everbearing 'Delizz' strawberry produces flushes of fruit from summer through the first frost rather than a single early crop. Plant crowns so the growing point sits right at the soil line, since burying it invites rot and planting too high dries out the roots. Grow in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist while berries are forming. It performs well in containers, hanging baskets, and beds. Pinch off early flowers on new plants to build strong roots before letting them fruit.

plant_note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil; tolerates deep shade and is also grown as an ornamental landscape shrub.
directions: Evergreen huckleberry is a slow-growing Pacific Coast native prized both as an ornamental evergreen shrub and as a source of small, sweet-tart black berries that ripen from October through January — one of the few edible fruits to ripen in the Pacific Northwest winter. Maintain soil pH between 4.5 and 5.5 with pine bark or sawdust mulch. The plant is extremely slow to establish from container stock and may take 5–7 years for significant fruit production. Florists prize the glossy foliage as a cut green; pruning for floral use also serves to shape the shrub.

plant_note: Plant dormant canes early spring before growth begins.
directions: 'Fall Gold' is an extra-hardy everbearing yellow raspberry that produces very sweet amber berries on both first- and second-year canes. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil amended with organic matter and keep evenly moist. It bears a summer crop on overwintered canes and a fall crop on new primocanes; leave floricanes standing for two harvests or mow for one large fall picking. Suckers freely, so contain the planting with regular removal of wandering shoots.

plant_note: Plant softwood cuttings or container stock in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich acidic soil; provide a sturdy trellis or arbor — the twining vine reaches 20–30 ft.
directions: Schisandra chinensis is a vigorous deciduous twining vine native to northeastern China and Russia, bearing pendant clusters of small, brilliant red berries in late summer whose juice is famously described as simultaneously sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and pungent — hence the common name wu wei zi ('five flavor fruit'). The plant is predominantly dioecious, so plant at least one male and one female vine for fruit production, though some cultivars bear perfect flowers. Train on a trellis, pergola, or fence with afternoon shade; the vine prefers cool, moist, slightly acidic woodland conditions. Prune to control size after fruiting; berries are used medicinally and in herbal preparations worldwide.

plant_note: Plant after last frost in spring; requires a long frost-free season of 200–240 days for fruit to mature.
directions: Fuzzy kiwi (the standard grocery-store kiwifruit) requires a long, warm growing season and is best suited to zones 7–9 with at least 200 frost-free days. Like hardy kiwi, it is dioecious and requires male and female plants — one male pollinates up to 8 females. Train vines on a T-bar or pergola trellis and prune hard each dormant season to manage the vigorous growth. Harvest in late fall before hard frost when the seeds have turned black and the flesh is tender; fruit ripens off the vine.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed, spacing plants 24–30 in apart.
directions: Garden huckleberry (Solanum scabrum) is an African-origin annual grown for its jet-black, blueberry-sized fruit, which must be cooked before eating as raw berries contain solanine. Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 75–80°F and transplant outdoors after frost danger has passed. The bushy plants are prolific producers and generally need no staking. Harvest when all berries in a cluster have turned uniformly shiny black; cook with sugar and lemon juice to reduce bitterness and break down solanine before use in jams or pies.

plant_note: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring after frost danger; goji tolerates alkaline soils and drought once established.
directions: Goji berries are highly adaptable, tolerating poor, alkaline soils where other fruits fail, but perform best in well-drained loam with a pH of 6.5–8.0. The arching, slightly thorny canes can be left as a shrub or trained to a central leader with a stake. Prune lightly after fruiting to maintain shape and encourage new fruiting laterals. Harvest ripe berries by gently rolling the stem between your fingers — ripe fruits are bright red-orange, soft, and drop easily (avoid touching them, as the sap stains).

plant_note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; most golden raspberries are primocane-fruiting and will yield a fall crop in their first year.
directions: Golden raspberries are yellow-fruited mutations of red raspberry cultivars, most commonly primocane-fruiting (everbearing) types such as 'Anne' or 'Fall Gold', with exceptionally sweet, mild flavor and low acidity. Manage as you would a primocane-fruiting red raspberry: for the simplest system, mow all canes to the ground each late winter and allow new primocanes to grow and fruit in fall. For a two-crop system, retain canes over winter to produce a summer crop on floricanes. Harvest when berries turn fully golden-yellow and release easily; they are more fragile than red types and should be processed quickly.

plant_note: Plant dormant cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; fall planting works well in milder zones.
directions: Gooseberries tolerate partial shade better than most fruits and prefer cool, well-drained soil with a pH of 6.0–6.5. Prune to an open-vase structure each late winter, removing canes older than 3 years and any crossing branches to reduce disease pressure. Pick berries early for cooking (when green and firm) or allow them to ripen to red, yellow, or white for fresh eating. Note: check local regulations, as Ribes species are restricted in some U.S. states due to white pine blister rust.

plant_note: Plant in early spring; goumi is partially self-fertile but yields improve with cross-pollination from a second plant.
directions: Goumi is a nitrogen-fixing shrub from East Asia that produces abundant small, cherry-red, speckled fruits with a sweet-tart flavor reminiscent of cherry. It is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, making it a valued plant in permaculture systems. Prune lightly after fruiting to shape the plant and encourage denser branching; heavy pruning stimulates excessive vegetative growth at the expense of fruit. Harvest when berries are fully red and slightly soft — ripe fruits have little to no astringency and taste excellent fresh or dried.

plant_note: Transplant starts after all frost danger has passed; start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost.
directions: Ground cherries are grown as warm-season annuals in most climates; start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after danger of frost has passed. The bushy plants produce prolific small fruits enclosed in papery husks and often sprawl without support — a tomato cage or light staking helps. Harvest when the papery husk has dried to tan-brown and the golden berry inside detaches easily. Fallen fruits left in their husks on the ground remain fresh for several weeks.

plant_note: Plant two or more compatible cultivars in early spring in very acidic soil; half-high types excel in zones 3–5 where standard highbush varieties suffer winter dieback.
directions: Half-high blueberries are crosses between highbush and lowbush species, combining the large-berry quality of highbush with the extreme cold hardiness of lowbush — cultivars such as 'Northblue', 'Northcountry', and 'Polaris' survive to -35°F when snow-covered. Maintain soil pH between 4.5 and 5.0 and mulch heavily with pine bark or sawdust. Cross-pollination between two compatible cultivars improves berry size and total yield. Pruning is minimal for the first five years; thereafter remove the thickest, oldest canes each late winter to encourage fresh productive wood.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger; provide a very sturdy trellis or pergola capable of holding hundreds of pounds of vine.
directions: Hardy kiwi vines are dioecious — plant one male vine for every 6–8 female vines or choose a self-fertile cultivar such as 'Issai'. The vines can take 4–7 years to bear fruit but become very productive once established, requiring a pergola or heavy-gauge trellis rated to hold at least 200 lbs. Prune to a permanent framework of main arms and renewal spurs each late winter, cutting lateral shoots back to 2–4 buds. Harvest when smooth, grape-sized fruits yield slightly to pressure and taste sweet.

plant_note: Set dormant canes early spring while soil is workable, before bud break.
directions: Plant 'Heritage' canes in well-drained loam enriched with compost, spacing them 2-3 feet apart in rows. This reliable everbearing (primocane) variety fruits on first-year canes in late summer and again the following early summer. For a single heavy fall crop, mow all canes to the ground each late winter. Provide a trellis or T-post support and keep beds mulched to conserve moisture and suppress weeds.

plant_note: Plant dormant bare-root or container stock in early spring once soil is workable; fall planting is possible in zones 6–8.
directions: Blueberries require acidic soil with a pH of 4.5–5.5; amend with elemental sulfur and use acidic mulch such as pine bark or wood chips 3–4 in deep. Plant at least two cultivars for cross-pollination and better fruit set. Prune out old canes older than 6 years at the base each late winter to stimulate productive new wood. Harvest when berries turn deep blue and detach with minimal resistance, typically 7–10 days after color change.

plant_note: Plant dormant bushes in fall or early spring.
directions: 'Hinnonmaki Red' is a hardy Finnish gooseberry with dark-red, aromatic berries that are tart when green for cooking and sweet when fully ripe for dessert. It shows good mildew resistance and thrives in cool, moist conditions. Grow in full sun to partial shade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. Prune to an open-centered bush in winter, removing crossing and low branches, and wear gloves when harvesting the thorny stems.

plant_note: Plant two or more compatible cultivars in early spring for cross-pollination; haskap blooms very early and needs a matching early-blooming partner.
directions: Honeyberries are among the earliest fruits to ripen — often 2–3 weeks before strawberries — and flowers can survive temperatures as low as -4°F, making them ideal for short-season climates. Two compatible cultivars from the same breeding group must be planted for cross-pollination. Minimal pruning is needed for the first 5 years; thereafter, remove a few of the oldest, thickest canes annually to renew. Harvest when berries are entirely blue-purple inside (cut one open to check), as color alone can be misleading.

plant_note: Plant dormant bushes fall through early spring.
directions: 'Invicta' is a heavy-cropping green culinary gooseberry with outstanding resistance to American gooseberry mildew. The vigorous, spreading, and notably spiny bush yields large pale-green berries excellent for pies, jams, and preserves. Plant in full sun to partial shade in fertile, well-drained soil and space generously for airflow. Prune to an open goblet each winter to ease harvest among the sharp thorns and shorten leading shoots to encourage fruiting spurs.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container canes in spring on a trellis in well-drained soil; note that Japanese wineberry is invasive in parts of the eastern U.S. — check local regulations before planting.
directions: Japanese wineberry is an Asian Rubus species with distinctive red glandular-hairy canes that are ornamentally attractive year-round. It fruits on floricanes in mid-to-late summer, bearing translucent orange-red to deep ruby berries enclosed in a sticky calyx that opens at ripeness — a feature that helps distinguish ripe from unripe fruit and reduces bird predation. Cut all fruited floricanes to the ground after harvest and thin primocanes to 4–6 per hill for next year's crop. The species naturalizes aggressively in disturbed areas in the eastern U.S. and is considered invasive in several states; confirm legality before planting.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; jostaberry is self-fertile and a single plant will produce fruit.
directions: Jostaberry is a thornless hybrid of black currant and gooseberry, combining the vigor and disease resistance of both parents with a flavor profile between the two. It is significantly larger and more vigorous than either parent, and is notably resistant to gooseberry mildew and currant big bud mite. Prune as you would a black currant — remove one-third of the oldest canes each late winter to stimulate new productive growth. Harvest in mid-summer when berries have turned from red to very dark purple-black and developed a sweet, slightly musky flavor; verify local Ribes restrictions before planting.

plant_note: Plant after last frost with a very sturdy pergola or trellis; self-fertile cultivars such as 'Issai' are suitable for single-plant planting.
directions: Kiwiberry is a compact-fruited selection of hardy kiwi (Actinidia arguta) grown specifically for smooth-skinned, grape-sized fruits that are eaten whole without peeling — they have the same flavor as standard kiwifruit but ripen 3–4 weeks earlier. Self-fertile cultivars such as 'Issai' and 'Ken's Red' allow single-plant production, but yields are higher when a male pollenizer is present. The extremely vigorous vines require a heavy-gauge trellis or pergola capable of bearing hundreds of pounds of vine and fruit; prune hard each dormant season to renewal spurs. Harvest when fruits yield to gentle pressure and taste sweet with no starchiness.

plant_note: Plant rooted plugs or divisions in early spring in acidic, well-drained soil; also useful as a groundcover under acid-loving shrubs.
directions: Lingonberries require acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) and perform well as an ornamental groundcover in zones 2–7. They produce two crops per year — a smaller crop in midsummer and a larger one in fall. Minimal pruning is needed; shear plants to 3–4 in every few years to rejuvenate. Harvest when berries are deep red and slightly tart; they store well refrigerated and are traditionally used in Scandinavian preserves and sauces.

plant_note: Plant rooted tip cuttings in early spring after last frost; provide a sturdy T-trellis at planting since canes reach 8–10 ft.
directions: Loganberry, a natural hybrid of blackberry and raspberry discovered in California in the 1880s, produces long, dark red, tart berries that are excellent for jams and wine. Train the vigorous trailing canes on a two- or three-wire trellis, weaving new primocanes along the wires during the growing season. After the summer harvest, cut all floricanes that bore fruit to the ground and secure the new primocanes in their place. The thornless 'LY59' strain is preferred for home gardens; in borderline zones, lay canes down and mulch after leaf drop.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in early spring in very acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: Lowbush blueberries require strongly acidic soil (pH 4.0–5.0) and spread by underground rhizomes to form colonies. Manage in a two-year cycle: mow or burn half the planting to the ground each year so alternating sections produce a full crop annually. Harvest in midsummer when berries are deep blue-purple and sweet. Drought-tolerant once established, but irrigation during fruit fill improves berry size.

plant_note: Plant in early spring once soil is workable; elderberries fruit best with a second variety nearby for pollination.
directions: Marge is a prolific European elderberry prized for heavy yields of dark berries used in syrups, jams, and wine. Plant in full sun in moist, fertile soil and keep it well watered, as elderberries are shallow-rooted and thirsty. Fruit set improves dramatically with a second, different elderberry variety planted nearby for cross-pollination. Prune out the oldest canes each late winter to keep the plant vigorous and productive. Always cook the berries before eating, since raw fruit and other plant parts can cause stomach upset.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or tip-rooted canes in early spring as soon as soil is workable, with a T-trellis capable of handling 8-ft canes installed at the same time.
directions: Marionberry is a trailing blackberry hybrid (Chehalem x Olallie) developed by Oregon State University and is the most widely grown blackberry in the Pacific Northwest, prized for its deep, complex, slightly tart flavor. The thorny, vigorous canes must be trained along a two-wire trellis; weave new primocanes along the lower wire during the summer and tie them to the upper wire the following spring. After the mid-summer harvest, remove all fruited floricanes at the base and train the new primocanes into their place. Marionberries are sensitive to freezes below about 10°F; in colder areas, protect canes over winter.

plant_note: Plant grafted named cultivars in early spring in moist to wet, acidic, bottomland soil for best fruit production.
directions: Mayhaw is a small flowering hawthorn tree native to swampy bottomlands of the Gulf South, prized for its tart red fruits — ripening in May — that make exceptional jelly of deep pink color. It is well adapted to poorly drained, acidic, wet soil and is often planted along pond edges or low spots. Grafted cultivars such as 'Big Red', 'Super Berry', and 'Texas Star' bear much more reliably and earlier than seedlings. Fruit is harvested by spreading tarps under the tree and shaking, or by netting floating fruit from ponds in traditional southern production.

plant_note: Plant after last frost in full sun with a trellis or fence; roots are cold-hardy to zone 6 and re-sprout from the roots each spring.
directions: Maypop (Passiflora incarnata) is a North American native passionflower that is cold-hardy to zone 6, distinguishing it clearly from the tropical Passiflora edulis (passionfruit). The ornamental lavender flowers are stunning and the egg-sized yellow-green fruits have sweet, aromatic pulp similar to tropical passionfruit. Plants die back to the roots in zone 6–7 winters and re-sprout vigorously in late spring — do not remove the crown before new growth appears. Plant cross-pollinators nearby (more than one plant) for reliable fruit set. Harvest when fruits turn yellowish-green and fall or detach easily.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container vines in early spring; use self-fertile cultivars or plant one male ('bronze') vine per 4–6 female vines.
directions: Muscadine grapes are native to the southeastern U.S. and far more heat- and disease-resistant than European or labrusca types. Train to a single trunk with two permanent lateral cordons on a high-wire trellis at 5–6 ft; spur-prune cordons each dormant season to 2–3 bud spurs spaced 4–6 in apart. Many cultivars are not self-fertile, so check pollination requirements before purchasing. Harvest by shaking or hand-picking individual berries when they reach full color and slip easily from the skin.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted runners in spring in moist, acidic, peaty soil; requires cool summer temperatures and will not thrive where summers are hot.
directions: The nagoonberry (arctic raspberry) is a low-growing, rhizomatous Rubus native to Alaska, Scandinavia, and northern Russia, bearing small, deep rose-red berries in early summer that are considered among the finest-flavored wild berries in the boreal world — exceptionally fragrant, rich, and complex. Plants require cool, moist, acidic bog or tundra-edge conditions (pH 4.5–5.5) and cool summer temperatures; they will not survive heat and are rarely successful south of zone 5 except at high elevations. Propagate by dividing rhizomes in early spring. The Finnish-bred cultivar 'Mespi' (a nagoonberry x Arctic raspberry hybrid) shows improved yield and adaptability while retaining the outstanding flavor of the wild species.

plant_note: Plant two or more plants for cross-pollination in early spring; extremely adaptable to cold, dry, or alkaline sites.
directions: Nanking cherry is one of the hardiest fruiting shrubs available, reliably surviving temperatures below -40°F and tolerating poor, dry, or alkaline soils. The abundant small red cherries ripen in early summer and have a sweet, slightly tart flavor excellent for jams, juices, and fresh eating. Two or more plants are needed for cross-pollination; plants flower very early in spring and may need frost protection of blossoms in zones 3–4 during cold snaps. Prune after harvest to remove dead wood and open the canopy; the shrub is generally very healthy with minimal pest and disease problems.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger eases.
directions: 'Navaho' is an erect, thornless blackberry that produces firm, very sweet berries with excellent shelf life on self-supporting canes. Because it grows upright it needs little or no trellising, though a single wire helps in windy sites. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and keep suckers thinned to maintain a manageable hedgerow. Prune out fruited floricanes each fall and tip new primocanes to encourage branching.

plant_note: Plant in spring; heat-tolerant dewberry-raspberry hybrid.
directions: The nessberry is an old dewberry-raspberry cross developed for the hot South, producing dark, wine-red berries with a sprightly flavor. Its trailing canes benefit from a low trellis and adapt well to heat and heavier soils where standard raspberries struggle. Plant in full sun in well-drained ground and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Remove spent floricanes after the summer crop and train fresh primocanes for the following season.

plant_note: Plant canes in early spring while dormant.
directions: Nova is a hardy, disease-resistant red raspberry that stands up to harsh winters and yields firm, flavorful berries. Plant dormant canes in early spring in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, spacing them to allow airflow and easy picking. Provide a trellis or support to keep the fruiting canes upright. This summer-bearing type fruits on second-year canes, so remove spent floricanes after harvest while leaving new primocanes for next season. Mulch to conserve moisture and suppress weeds.

plant_note: Plant in spring in mild-winter regions; needs a long growing season.
directions: The olallieberry is a trailing blackberry hybrid (a cross involving loganberry and youngberry parentage) popular along the California coast for its rich, sweet-tart flavor. Its long, sprawling canes must be trained onto a trellis and are best separated by fruiting year for easier pruning. Grow in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil with regular irrigation through the dry season. Prune out fruited canes after the early-summer harvest and tie in the new canes for next year.

plant_note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic soil; thrives in dry shade where few other fruiting shrubs succeed.
directions: Oregon grape is a native Pacific Northwest evergreen shrub with holly-like spiny leaves that produces clusters of tart, powder-blue berries in midsummer — one of the most shade- and drought-tolerant edible-fruiting shrubs available. The berries are too tart to eat fresh in quantity but are excellent for jellies, syrups, and wine when sweetened; they are high in berberine, a compound with traditional medicinal use. Plant in groups of three or more for best cross-pollination and fruit set. Prune by removing the oldest, leggiest canes at the base every few years to encourage compact, multi-stemmed growth.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants in spring in humus-rich, strongly acidic, moist woodland soil in full shade.
directions: Partridgeberry is a native North American groundcover for shaded, acidic woodland gardens, forming a dense mat of evergreen, trailing stems barely 2 in tall. The bright red, twin-fused berries persist through winter and are edible but bland and seedy, making them more valuable as wildlife food and ornamental ground cover than for human harvest. Each berry is the fused product of two twin flowers — visible as two distinct calyxes on mature fruit. Grow in strongly acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) under deciduous trees; it is slow to establish but extremely long-lived with virtually no maintenance needed.

plant_note: Plant after all frost danger has passed in zones 9–11; in cooler zones grow in large containers and overwinter indoors.
directions: Passionfruit is a vigorous, tendril-climbing vine requiring a sturdy wire trellis or fence. Train the main stem vertically and allow lateral shoots to drape horizontally from the top — these laterals bear the flowers and fruit. Prune heavily after fruiting to rejuvenate the vine and maintain productive growth. Harvest when the fruit skin begins to wrinkle and the fruit falls or detaches easily; purple cultivars are generally more cold-tolerant than yellow types.

plant_note: Plant bare-root crowns in early spring with crowns level at the soil surface; must be grown near standard red strawberries for adequate pollination.
directions: Pineberry is a white-fruited strawberry cultivar (largely derived from the South American species Fragaria chiloensis crossed with F. x ananassa) that produces pale cream-white berries with red achenes and a distinctive pineapple-like aroma when fully ripe. Pineberries have low self-fertility and require a red-fruiting strawberry variety planted nearby as a pollinator for adequate fruit set. Grow exactly as you would a june-bearing or everbearing strawberry, removing blossoms the first season to build root strength. Harvest when berries are white to pale pink with red seeds and give off a sweet pineapple fragrance.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; grows well alongside red and white currants with identical cultural requirements.
directions: Pink currants are naturally occurring or selected mutations of red currant (Ribes rubrum) producing pale rose-pink to blush-colored berries that are milder and sweeter than the typical red forms — popular cultivars include 'Pink Champagne' and 'Gloire de Sablons'. Culture is identical to red currant: plant in cool, well-drained soil with a pH of 6.0–6.5 and prune each late winter to retain 8–12 mixed-age canes, removing any older than 5 years. The translucent pink strigs are ornamentally striking and ripen slightly earlier than most red currants. Check state Ribes restrictions before ordering.

plant_note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: 'Pink Lemonade' is an ornamental rabbiteye-type hybrid bearing translucent pink berries with a mild, sweet flavor that deepen in color as they ripen. It offers attractive white-pink spring blooms and fiery fall foliage in addition to novelty fruit. Grow in full sun in acidic soil (pH 4.5 to 5.5) kept evenly moist with a pine-bark or pine-needle mulch. Yields improve with a second blueberry variety nearby for cross-pollination; prune lightly after the third year to renew fruiting wood.

plant_note: Plant in spring; primocane-fruiting so it can crop the first year.
directions: 'Prime-Ark Freedom' is the first thornless primocane-fruiting blackberry, capable of bearing very large berries on first-year canes. It offers an early summer crop on floricanes and a late-summer-to-fall crop on new primocanes. Grow in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and steady moisture, and support the canes on a trellis in windy locations. In hot climates the fall crop yields the sweetest fruit when temperatures moderate; soft-tip the primocanes to boost lateral fruiting.

plant_note: Plant divisions or bare-root stock in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; tolerates more shade than most Rubus species — ideal for woodland edges.
directions: Purple-flowering raspberry is a thornless native North American Rubus producing large, showy rose-purple flowers attractive to pollinators followed by broad, flat, raspberry-like fruits in midsummer. The berries are edible but dry and seedy — more appealing to wildlife than humans — making this species primarily grown as an ornamental fruiting shrub for naturalizing woodland edges and native plantings. It spreads vigorously by root suckering and can colonize large areas; control spread by removing suckers as needed at the perimeter. No trellising is required; prune out older, dying canes to the ground in late winter to encourage fresh, vigorous shoots.

plant_note: Plant two or more compatible cultivars in early spring in acidic, well-drained soil; rabbiteye types require cross-pollination for good yields.
directions: Rabbiteye blueberries are native to the southeastern U.S. and are far more heat- and drought-tolerant than northern highbush types, thriving in zones 7–9 with minimal chilling hours. Soil pH must be maintained between 4.5 and 5.5; amend with elemental sulfur well in advance of planting and mulch thickly with pine bark or wood chips. Plant at least two different cultivars from the same ripening window (early, mid, or late) for adequate cross-pollination. Fruit typically ripens over a 4–6-week window in summer, turning fully blue-black and softening before peak sweetness — taste-test before harvesting large quantities.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; adapts to a wide range of soils including wet, poorly drained sites.
directions: Red chokeberry (Aronia arbutifolia) is a taller, more upright native shrub than its black-fruited relative, bearing bright red berries that ripen in fall and persist well into winter, providing exceptional wildlife food and four-season ornamental interest. The berries are intensely astringent fresh but soften after frost and are suitable for jellies, juices, and wines with added sweetener. The cultivar 'Brilliantissima' is widely grown for its especially vibrant fall foliage and heavy fruit set. Prune oldest canes to the ground every 3–4 years to control suckering and maintain vigor.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring as soon as soil is workable.
directions: Red currants fruit heavily on spurs of wood two years old and older; prune each late winter to keep 8–12 canes of mixed ages and remove any cane older than 5 years. They tolerate more shade than most fruits and appreciate afternoon shelter in hot climates. Harvest entire strigs (clusters) when berries are uniformly bright red and slightly translucent. Check state restrictions on Ribes planting before ordering.

plant_note: Plant in spring in very acidic, humus-rich soil or on decaying conifer wood; native to the Pacific Northwest understory and tolerates deep shade.
directions: Red huckleberry is a Pacific Northwest native that naturally colonizes rotting conifer stumps and logs, requiring extremely acidic conditions (pH 4.0–5.0) and excellent drainage with abundant organic matter. The bright scarlet berries ripen in summer and are tart — excellent for jams, syrups, and pies — and are an important wildlife food for birds and bears. Established plants spread slowly by rhizomes and require minimal pruning; remove only dead or crossing branches in late winter. Difficult to transplant as a wild specimen; use nursery-grown container stock for best results.

plant_note: Plant dormant bushes in fall or early spring.
directions: 'Rovada' is a late-season red currant that bears exceptionally long strigs of bright, uniform berries prized for jelly and fresh use. It flowers late, escaping frost, and crops reliably even in cooler summers. Unlike black currants, red currants fruit on spurs of older wood, so prune to an open goblet framework and shorten new growth in winter. Grow in partial shade in hot regions to prevent leaf scorch, and net the bushes to protect ripe fruit from birds.

plant_note: Plant early spring; keep away from wild brambles to limit disease.
directions: 'Royalty' is a hybrid of red and black raspberry yielding large, richly flavored purple berries excellent for jam and baking. The tall, sturdy canes do not sucker as aggressively as red types and are best tip-pruned in summer to encourage lateral branching. Grow in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and remove old floricanes after fruiting. Plant at least 300 feet from wild or black raspberries to reduce viral disease pressure.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; running serviceberry spreads by stolons to form low, attractive colonies and is excellent for erosion control on shaded slopes.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; running serviceberry is the low-growing, colony-forming native serviceberry of the northeastern US and Appalachians, spreading by stolons to form attractive low thickets 1–3 feet tall on rocky slopes, woodland edges, and open forests. Like all serviceberries, it produces masses of white flowers in early spring that are an important nectar and pollen source for early-emerging native bees, followed by sweet, blueberry-like June berries in early summer that are excellent fresh, dried, or cooked into pies and preserves. The low, suckering habit makes it more useful as a shade-tolerant groundcover or erosion control plant than as a specimen shrub. It colors brilliantly orange-red in fall. An ideal native edible shrub for shaded slopes, woodland edges, and low-maintenance natural landscapes in the Northeast and Appalachians.

plant_note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in moist, strongly acidic, well-drained soil under conifer canopy.
directions: Salal is an evergreen native shrub of the Pacific Northwest coast, found beneath conifers from central California to Alaska, where it forms dense thickets on acidic, well-drained forest soils. The dark blue-purple berries are similar in size to a blueberry but have mealy, mildly sweet flesh and were an important food source for coastal Indigenous peoples, eaten fresh or dried into cakes. Salal is propagated by division or from container-grown nursery stock; seed germination is slow and unreliable. The glossy foliage is commercially valuable in the floral trade, and home growers can cut leathery stems for arrangements without harming the plant.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich soil; salmonberry naturalizes readily in Pacific Northwest conditions.
directions: Salmonberry is a Pacific Northwest native shrub that thrives in moist to wet, shaded sites along streams and forest edges, making it one of the few fruiting canes suitable for boggy ground. Fruits ripen very early — often in May or June — in shades from yellow-orange to deep red-salmon, with a mild, slightly bland flavor best suited to fresh eating or jams. Thin old canes to the ground each year after fruiting to maintain vigor, as the plant spreads by rhizomes and can form dense thickets. Wildlife value is extremely high; berries attract birds and bears within days of ripening.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring in well-drained, sandy, or gravelly soil; sand cherry excels on poor, droughty sites where other stone fruits fail.
directions: Sand cherry is a small, sprawling native North American shrub bearing small dark purple to black cherries in midsummer with a flavor ranging from pleasantly tart to bitter depending on provenance and ripeness stage. The fruits are consumed fresh, dried, or processed into pemmican and preserves and were an important food source for Plains Indigenous peoples. The plant is extraordinarily cold-hardy and drought-tolerant once established, spreading by root suckers to form low thickets excellent for erosion control. Harvest when berries are fully dark and soft; cross-pollination between multiple plants improves fruit set significantly.

plant_note: Plant one male plant for every 5–7 female plants in early spring; sea buckthorn is wind-pollinated and needs close proximity between sexes.
directions: Sea buckthorn is a thorny, nitrogen-fixing shrub that thrives in poor, dry, sandy soils where most fruits struggle. It is dioecious and requires male plants for pollination — one male per 5–7 females; sex is not identifiable until first flowering, so purchase sexed plants. The tiny berries are borne directly on the branches in dense clusters and are difficult to harvest without damaging the plant; a common approach is to cut fruiting branches in fall and strip berries indoors or by freezing and shaking. The tart, orange berries are extremely high in vitamin C.

plant_note: Set crowns in early spring; day-neutral so it fruits the first season.
directions: 'Seascape' is a day-neutral strawberry that produces large, firm, sweet berries continuously from late spring through fall regardless of day length. Set crowns at soil level so the growing point is neither buried nor exposed, spacing plants about a foot apart in raised beds or containers. Grow in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist with drip irrigation and straw mulch. Pinch off early flowers for the first few weeks after planting to build strong plants, then allow continuous fruiting.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring while still dormant; fall planting works well in zones 5–8.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–7.0) in full to partial sun; serviceberry tolerates wet sites and woodland edges better than most fruiting shrubs. No cross-pollinator is required, though planting two or more selections increases yield. Fruits ripen in late spring to early June, turning from red to deep purple-blue when fully sweet — birds will compete for them, so netting is advisable. Prune crossing or crowded stems in late winter to maintain an open, multi-stemmed form and remove root suckers to control spread.

plant_note: Plant in early spring in very acidic, well-drained soil; low-chill cultivars such as 'Sunshine Blue' or 'O'Neal' are essential in mild-winter climates.
directions: Southern highbush blueberries are interspecific hybrids bred for low chill-hour requirements (150–500 hours below 45°F), making them the only highbush-type blueberry suitable for Gulf Coast, Florida, and mild-winter California climates. Maintain soil pH at 4.5–5.5 with pine bark mulch and acidifying fertilizers; do not use lime. Plant two or more cultivars for cross-pollination and improved fruit size. Prune lightly for the first three years, then begin removing the oldest, least productive canes each late winter to stimulate vigorous new wood.

plant_note: Set crowns in early spring; everbearing types can yield a partial crop the first fall if planted promptly.
directions: Plant in spring with crowns at soil level; remove only the spring flush of blossoms and allow the summer and fall crops to set. Everbearing types produce two distinct flushes — early summer and late summer into fall — with fewer runners than june-bearing types, making them well suited to hill culture or raised beds. Harvest when berries are fully red and fragrant. Replace plants every 3–4 years as productivity declines.

plant_note: Set bare-root crowns in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, keeping the crown level with the soil surface.
directions: Plant bare-root crowns in spring with the crown at soil level, setting runners into a matted-row system 12–18 in apart. Pinch all blossoms the first year to build root vigor; harvest begins the following June when berries are uniformly red and pull easily from the stem. Renovate beds immediately after harvest by mowing foliage to 3–4 in and thinning plants to 4–6 per square foot. Mulch crowns with straw in fall in zones 4 and colder.

plant_note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; tolerates drought, salt spray, and wind — excellent for coastal gardens in zones 7–10.
directions: The strawberry tree is an attractive Mediterranean evergreen grown as a large shrub or small tree prized for the remarkable phenomenon of simultaneously bearing flowers and ripe fruit in autumn and winter — fruit from the previous year's flowers ripens just as the new season's flowers open. The round, warty, red-orange fruits are mildly sweet and grainy when fully ripe, high in sugar and suitable for jams, liqueurs, and fermented beverages (the species name 'unedo' is said to derive from the Latin 'I eat only one'). It tolerates poor, shallow, well-drained soils and thrives in Mediterranean-climate gardens with minimal summer irrigation once established. Little pruning is required; remove dead wood and shape lightly to maintain structure.

plant_note: Plant dormant bare-root canes in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Summer raspberries fruit on floricanes — canes produced in year one that overwinter and bear fruit the following summer. After harvest, cut all fruited floricanes to the ground and thin new primocanes to 4–6 per linear foot for best airflow. Support canes on a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft. Harvest when berries pull cleanly from the plug with no resistance, typically over 3–4 weeks in early summer.

plant_note: Plant in spring; well suited to containers and mild-winter areas.
directions: 'Sunshine Blue' is a compact, semi-dwarf southern highbush blueberry that is self-fertile and needs only about 150 chill hours, making it ideal for warm regions and pots. It tolerates a slightly higher soil pH than most blueberries but still performs best in acidic, well-drained media. Grow in full sun, water consistently, and feed with an acid-formulated fertilizer during the growing season. Its tidy habit and showy pink-to-white flowers make it a productive ornamental for patios and small gardens.

plant_note: Plant dormant bare-root vines in early spring; install a sturdy trellis or arbor before planting.
directions: Table grapes require a robust trellis capable of supporting 15–30 lbs of fruit per vine; the two-wire Kniffin system or a pergola are common choices. Train a permanent trunk and 2–4 fruiting arms during the first 3 years; thereafter, annual dormant pruning should remove 90% of the previous year's growth, leaving spurs or canes with 2–4 buds each. Thin clusters after fruit set to improve berry size and airflow. Harvest when berries reach full color, the tendrils nearest the cluster have dried, and sugar is confirmed by taste.

plant_note: Plant rooted tip cuttings or bare-root canes in early spring and install a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft at the same time.
directions: Tayberry is a Scottish-bred hybrid (blackberry x raspberry) with long, conical, deep purple-red berries that ripen in mid-summer; the flavor is richer and sweeter than loganberry. Train the long trailing canes fan-style along wires, keeping current-year primocanes separate from fruiting floricanes to simplify post-harvest cleanup. After harvest, remove all fruited canes to the ground and tie in the new primocanes. In zone 5–6, protect canes over winter by detaching them from wires and covering with straw mulch or a frost-cloth tunnel.

plant_note: Plant root divisions or container-grown plants in early spring in partial shade with moist, well-drained soil.
directions: Thimbleberry is a large-leafed, thornless native Rubus that thrives along woodland edges and mountain slopes from Alaska to Mexico, tolerating more shade than almost any other fruiting cane. The soft, bright red berries resemble a wide, shallow thimble and have a delicate, sweet flavor but are extremely fragile — they must be eaten or processed within hours of harvest. Propagate by dividing root clumps in spring; seed germination is erratic and requires cold stratification. Allow the plant to naturalize in a semi-wild planting rather than managing it as a formal fruiting cane.

plant_note: Set out dormant plants in early spring once soil can be worked.
directions: Prime-Ark Freedom is a thornless, primocane-fruiting blackberry that bears large, sweet berries on first-year canes, giving a summer crop that can extend into fall. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and provide a trellis or support for the vigorous canes. Because it fruits on new wood, you can mow floricanes to the ground in late winter for a single heavy late-season crop, or leave them for two harvests. Keep soil evenly moist during fruit development and mulch to suppress weeds. Feed in early spring with a balanced fertilizer.

plant_note: Plant dormant bushes autumn to early spring.
directions: 'Titania' is a vigorous, disease-resistant black currant valued for strong resistance to white pine blister rust and mildew. It produces long strigs of large, glossy berries over an extended ripening window, making harvest easier. Grow in full sun to light shade in fertile, moisture-retentive soil enriched with compost. Feed with a high-potassium fertilizer in spring and prune out the oldest quarter of the wood each dormant season to keep the bush productive.

plant_note: Plant after last hard frost in spring; provide a strong trellis.
directions: 'Triple Crown' is a thornless semi-erect blackberry famed for high yields of large, sweet, glossy-black fruit in mid to late summer. Train the vigorous canes onto a sturdy T-trellis and tip the primocanes at about four feet to promote fruiting laterals. Grow in full sun in deep, well-drained soil with consistent summer moisture during ripening. Remove spent floricanes to the ground after harvest to make room for next year's fruiting wood.

plant_note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring on a trellis; tummelberry is more cold-hardy than tayberry and better suited to short-season northern gardens.
directions: The tummelberry is a Scottish-bred Rubus hybrid developed at the Scottish Crop Research Institute from a tayberry cross, selected specifically for greater cold hardiness and easier mechanical harvest than its parent. It produces large, dark red to maroon conical berries slightly smaller than tayberries in midsummer with a rich, aromatic flavor well suited to preserves, juices, and desserts. Train the long, semi-trailing canes on a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft, fruiting on second-year floricanes; cut all fruited canes to the ground after harvest and train new primocanes for the following year. It is substantially hardier than tayberry and performs reliably in zones 4–5 with minimal winter protection.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; white currants perform well trained as a cordon against a north- or east-facing wall.
directions: White currant is a yellow-fruited mutation of red currant sharing identical cultivation requirements: it fruits most heavily on spurs of two-year-old and older wood. The translucent, ivory-white berries are sweeter and milder than red currants and are prized for elegant fresh-eating and jellies. Prune each late winter to maintain 8–12 canes of mixed ages, removing canes older than 5 years. Harvest entire strigs when berries have turned fully translucent white-golden and taste sweet; check local Ribes restrictions before purchasing.

plant_note: Transplant rooted runners in spring or fall; pin runner tips to moist soil and cut free when rooted. Easiest to establish from rooted plugs or runners rather than seed.
directions: Plant rooted runners in moderately moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; this native species is one parent of the cultivated strawberry and produces intensely flavorful small red fruits that surpass commercial strawberries in taste, though not in size. The plant spreads vigorously by runners to form a dense groundcover that suppresses weeds and tolerates moderate foot traffic. Fruits are borne in late spring through early summer; birds, chipmunks, and turtles compete for the harvest so a loose barrier net may be needed. Divide or thin the planting every 2–3 years by removing older central crowns and retaining the vigorous outer runners.

plant_note: Plant grafted or own-rooted dormant vines in early spring on a well-drained slope with good air drainage.
directions: Wine grapes thrive in lean, well-drained soils with a pH of 5.5–6.5 and perform best on slopes with excellent air circulation to reduce disease pressure. Establish a vertical shoot positioning (VSP) trellis with catch wires at 4 and 5 ft above the cordon wire. Rigorous dormant pruning — leaving only 30–60 buds per vine depending on vigor — is essential for fruit quality. Harvest timing is cultivar- and use-specific; measure Brix (sugar) with a refractometer and monitor seed color and berry flavor.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants in early spring; note that wineberry is invasive in much of the eastern U.S., so check local regulations before planting.
directions: Wineberry, native to Japan and China, produces small, brilliant red, jewel-like drupes with a sweet, delicate flavor; it is grown for fruit production in Asia and has naturalized extensively in the eastern U.S. and should be grown only in contained settings or where legal. The canes are covered in red glandular hairs and tip-root readily, so remove spent canes promptly and clear any rooted tips. Harvest when berries are uniformly bright red and detach easily from the sticky calyx — they do not ripen after picking and must be used within 1–2 days. Pruning follows the same floricane/primocane cycle as standard raspberries.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring in well-drained, fertile soil; worcesterberry is very thorny — wear heavy gloves when handling.
directions: The worcesterberry is a North American species of gooseberry (Ribes divaricatum) sometimes grown as a productive alternative to European gooseberries because it shows good resistance to powdery mildew and American gooseberry mildew. The small, dark purple-black berries resemble currants more than typical gooseberries and ripen in midsummer with a tart, rich flavor excellent for jams and pies. Prune to an open-vase structure each late winter, keeping 8–10 canes of mixed ages. Verify state restrictions on Ribes planting before ordering, as some states still restrict the genus.

scientific_name: Rubus caesius x idaeus x ursinus hybrid
plant_note: Plant bare-root canes in spring on a sturdy two- or three-wire trellis; youngberry is a trailing type requiring support and grows best in warm, humid climates.
directions: The youngberry is a dewberry hybrid developed in Louisiana in the early 1900s by B.M. Young, producing very large, dark maroon-purple berries with a high juice content and sweet-tart flavor excellent for wine, juice, and pies. Culture is similar to boysenberry: train long, trailing canes on a T-trellis at 3 and 5 ft, fruiting on second-year floricanes; after harvest, cut all fruited canes to the ground and tie new primocanes in their place. The plants are less cold-hardy than standard blackberries and benefit from cane protection or mulching in zone 6 winters. Harvest when berries turn uniformly dark purple-black and release easily from the plug.

plant_note: Sow in early spring around the last frost date, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before first frost to allow fall establishment.
