Remove the occasional dead cane in winter but otherwise leave unpruned to preserve the natural, wiggly growth habit.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Carolina silverbell is an understory tree that requires acidic soil and performs best with afternoon shade in zone 7–8.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Carolina silverbell is one of the most beautiful and underused native understory trees of the southern Appalachians, producing pure white, bell-shaped flowers in clusters all along the branches in mid-spring when the tree is still essentially leafless — making the display exceptionally striking. The flowers are an important nectar source for early bumblebees and long-tongued native bees. The four-winged seed pods are ornamental through summer and fall. Fall color is clear yellow. It grows naturally as a subcanopy tree beneath taller hardwoods in moist, acidic ravines and slopes and is genuinely shade-tolerant, making it one of the best small-to-medium flowering trees for the shaded garden or woodland garden. An outstanding substitute for Bradford pear in the southern landscape, providing similar white spring bloom but with genuine wildlife value and a graceful natural form.

plant_note: Surface sow around the last frost; light aids germination.
directions: Sprinkle Carpet of Snow alyssum onto firmed soil around the last frost and press in without covering, as light aids germination. This low, spreading heirloom carpets beds and container edges with masses of tiny, honey-scented white flowers. It draws in bees and beneficial insects and blooms from spring into fall, pausing only in extreme heat. Shear lightly if it flags mid-season to spark a fresh flush of bloom.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in rich, moist, acidic soil.
directions: Cascade Spice bears large, ruffled Japanese iris blooms in rich purple and white for a bold late-spring to summer display. Site in full sun to part shade in acidic, humus-rich soil kept consistently moist, ideal at the edge of a pond or in a rain garden. Do not allow the soil to dry out during active growth, and avoid alkaline amendments. Deadhead spent stalks and divide crowded clumps in fall to sustain strong flowering.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface sow the tiny seed, which needs light.
directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface indoors several weeks before the last frost, as it requires light to germinate. This trailing blend spills over hanging baskets and container edges with blooms of blue, blue with a white eye, lilac, red, ruby, and white. Keep it consistently moist and provide afternoon shade where summers are hot. A light midseason shearing renews the display and keeps plants full.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost in a warm, sunny site with good drainage.
directions: This fast-growing crape myrtle covers itself in vivid purple flower panicles from summer into fall, followed by attractive fall foliage and exfoliating bark. Plant in full sun for the heaviest bloom in average, well-drained soil; too much shade reduces flowering. Water regularly while establishing, then it tolerates heat and moderate drought. Prune lightly in late winter to shape, removing crossing branches rather than topping the tree to preserve its natural form.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; plants establish quickly and bloom the first season.
directions: Plant in lean, well-drained soil — catmint thrives in conditions that would challenge many perennials and blooms heavily from spring into early summer. Shear the entire plant back by one-half after the first flowering flush to refresh the mounding habit and trigger a second wave of lavender-blue flowers in late summer. The silvery-green aromatic foliage is deer-resistant and provides a reliable edging throughout the season. Divide in spring every 3–4 years or propagate from cuttings taken in late spring.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; the plant self-sows reliably and will naturalize once established, filling a shaded bed with bright spring color.
directions: Direct sow in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in fall; celandine poppy produces stunning bright yellow four-petaled flowers in early spring, making it one of the most brilliant color plants for the shade garden before the tree canopy closes. The plants bloom for several weeks in spring, then go partially dormant in summer heat but re-emerge in fall for additional sporadic flowering in cool climates. The bright orange sap from cut stems has traditional medicinal uses but is toxic and a skin irritant. Once established, it self-sows abundantly to naturalize throughout a shaded bed, so site carefully if you don't want it to spread. Combine with Virginia bluebells and trilliums for a classic spring shade display.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after soil is warm, as cold temperatures cause stunted plants.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and lightly press in — seed needs light and warmth to germinate. Do not rush transplanting into cold soil, as chilling causes plants to stall and go prematurely to seed. Deadhead spent plumes on crested and feathered types to extend the display. Celosia makes an excellent dried flower; harvest spikes just as they reach full color.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: Center Court is an elegant creamy-white dahlia that blooms nonstop all season and produces tubers reliably, making it a great choice for beginners. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil once the ground has warmed. Pinch young plants to promote branching and cut flowers regularly to prolong blooming. In cold-winter regions, dig and store the tubers indoors after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Start indoors early since these carnations are slow to reach bloom.
directions: Start seed indoors well before the last frost, as this blend is slow to reach flowering. Chabaud carnations bear fragrant, clove-scented double blooms in scarlet, purple, pink, white, yellow, and bicolors atop blue-green foliage, one flower per stem for excellent cutting. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil and even moisture. Deadhead and pinch early to build sturdy, well-branched plants.

plant_note: Start indoors well before the last frost; slow to reach bloom.
directions: Start Chabaud carnations indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, covering seed lightly, then harden off and transplant after frost danger passes. This heirloom Dianthus bears fragrant, fully double blooms in a rich mix of colors prized for cut arrangements. Give it full sun, well-drained soil, and support the tall flower stems. Deadhead to prolong the display, and in mild zones it will return as a short-lived perennial.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; chalk maple performs better in zone 7–9 summer heat than sugar maple, Japanese maple, or most other ornamental maples.
directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; chalk maple is a small-to-medium native understory maple of the Southeast Appalachians and Piedmont, closely related to sugar maple but significantly more heat-and-drought-tolerant and better suited to the zone 7–9 garden than its more famous relative. The common name comes from the distinctive light-colored, chalky-white to pale gray bark. The 3–5 lobed leaves turn magnificent shades of yellow, orange, and scarlet in fall — one of the best native maples for fall color in the South. It grows naturally as an understory tree on rocky slopes and bluffs with well-drained, calcareous soils. An outstanding and underused native small tree for shaded gardens in the Southeast where sugar maple and Japanese maple both struggle with the heat and humidity.

plant_note: Direct sow where plants are to grow, as flax resents transplanting.
directions: Direct sow in early spring where plants will grow, since flax does not transplant well. This easy, low-maintenance blend produces showy flowers on delicate stems that dance in the breeze and draw pollinators. It tolerates hot, dry conditions once established, so water sparingly. Allow some plants to go to seed for reseeding and successive bloom.

plant_note: Often grown as a biennial; sow in spring or fall for bloom the next year.
directions: Sow in spring or fall in a sunny, sheltered spot, as this classic biennial typically flowers the second year. Chater's Double, developed in the 1880s, produces tall spires of ruffly double blooms in golden-yellow, rose, maroon, salmon-pink, scarlet, and white over a long bloom period. Stake the tall stalks in windy sites and water at the base to reduce rust. Let some flowers set seed, as hollyhocks reseed reliably.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall promptly; they dry out quickly.
directions: Plant checkered fritillary bulbs in fall as soon as possible, since the delicate bulbs dry out quickly, setting them about four inches deep. The nodding bell flowers display a distinctive checkerboard pattern in purple or white in mid-spring. It prefers moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade and naturalizes well in damp meadows and woodland edges. Handle the fragile bulbs gently at planting and let foliage die back to ensure return.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a site with morning sun and afternoon shade in hot climates.
directions: Cherokee Brave opens deep pink spring blooms with reddish new foliage, followed by red fall color and berries that attract birds. Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, siting it in part shade or where it gets afternoon protection in hot regions. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist, as dogwoods are shallow-rooted and dislike drought. This selection shows improved resistance to powdery mildew; prune only to remove dead or crossing branches after flowering.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered spot with dappled light in warm climates.
directions: Cherokee Chief bears rich rosy-red bracts in spring on graceful horizontal branches, with glossy foliage that turns red in autumn. Plant in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and give it part shade or afternoon protection where summers are hot. Maintain even soil moisture with mulch, since the shallow roots suffer in dry conditions. Prune sparingly right after bloom to shape the canopy and remove any damaged wood.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in dappled light with cool, moist, acidic soil.
directions: Cherokee Princess is prized for its heavy set of large, pure-white spring bracts and a well-branched, rounded form. Plant in moist, acidic, organically rich soil in part shade, protecting it from harsh afternoon sun and drying wind. Mulch to keep the shallow root system cool and consistently moist. Enjoy red fall foliage and berries that feed birds; prune only lightly after flowering to maintain the tidy structure.

plant_note: Surface sow, as the seed needs light; blooms the first year from an early start.
directions: Start indoors and surface sow, since the seed needs light to germinate and benefits from an early start to bloom the first year. Cherry Brandy bears three-to-four-inch maroon-red velvet petals around a dark chocolate center for a rich, luxurious look. Grow in full sun with average, well-drained soil and moderate water. Deadhead to extend the bloom and cut stems freely for long-lasting arrangements.

plant_note: Start indoors 8 weeks early; surface sow for light.
directions: Surface sow this unique deep-red rudbeckia indoors eight weeks before the last frost, as light aids germination. Cherry Brandy produces rich burgundy daisies with dark centers, prized as long-lasting cut flowers. Transplant to full sun and average well-drained soil after frost danger passes. Deadhead regularly to keep new blooms coming, and treat it as an annual though it may overwinter in mild zones.

plant_note: Sow in darkness, as phlox seed germinates best when covered.
directions: Start seed indoors and cover it, since annual phlox germinates best in darkness. Cherry Caramel offers unusual creamy-beige flowers with dark cherry centers that make dreamy, fragrant cut flowers and draw pollinators and hummingbirds. Grow in full sun to part shade with well-drained soil and even moisture. Bloom may pause in intense summer heat and renew as cooler weather returns, so keep plants deadheaded.

plant_note: Direct sow the large seeds after frost; they resent root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow the large, pebble-like seeds after frost about half an inch deep, an easy task for young gardeners. Cherry Rose Jewel bears bright, peppery, edible blossoms and rounded leaves that make elegant additions to salads and appetizer plates. Keep soil lean rather than rich to encourage flowers over foliage, and water moderately. Nasturtiums are known to deter some garden pests while attracting pollinators.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in morning sun with afternoon shade in hot climates.
directions: This bigleaf hydrangea produces color-shifting blooms that mature to rich cherry-red tones from spring into fall on a compact, rounded shrub. Plant in part shade with morning sun and afternoon protection in moist, well-drained, organically rich soil. Water deeply and regularly, especially in heat, and mulch to conserve moisture. It blooms on both old and new wood, so prune only to remove dead stems in early spring for the most reliable flowering.

plant_note: Start indoors in warmth; celosia needs steady heat to grow well.
directions: Start seed indoors in warm conditions a few weeks before the last frost and transplant only after the soil is warm. Red Flame forms compact, uniform cockscomb heads made of countless tiny flowers in waves of deep red that last a long time in the garden or vase. Give it full sun, warmth, and well-drained soil for the best color. Harvest stems for fresh or dried arrangements as the heads reach full size.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix at 65–70°F. Rotate planting locations each year, as China asters are prone to Fusarium wilt when grown in the same soil repeatedly. Avoid overhead watering and ensure good air circulation to reduce disease pressure. Harvest cut flowers when petals are just beginning to open fully for maximum vase life.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors; succession sow for prolonged bloom.
directions: An old-fashioned single-flowered China aster blend in cool reds, pinks, purples, and white for midsummer color. Sow seed directly after frost or start indoors, barely covering the seed. Plant toward the back of the bed where the two-to-three-foot stems provide height and long-lasting cut flowers. Deadhead spent blooms and rotate planting sites each year to reduce aster wilt.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into the garden 2–4 weeks before last frost, as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and can be transplanted while still small. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral-to-slightly-alkaline soil; heavy clay and overwet conditions lead to crown rot. Deadhead spent flowers continuously to prevent early seed set and extend the blooming season well into summer. Plants often decline in intense summer heat but may rebloom in fall if sheared back and kept watered.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also sow in fall in mild winter climates.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in early spring directly where they are to grow; cover seeds lightly as they germinate better in darkness. The clusters of tiny, intensely blue flowers resemble true forget-me-nots but bloom for a much longer period — from late spring well into summer. Shear plants back by one-third after the first main flush to encourage a second wave of bloom. Plants self-sow freely; allow some seeds to drop for naturalised succession planting in subsequent years.

plant_note: Plant in spring after the last frost, or in fall in zones 8–9; loropetalum establishes quickly in warm soil and rewards with rapid growth during the first growing season with adequate moisture.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; loropetalum is one of the most popular landscape shrubs for the Gulf Coast and Southeast, prized for its burgundy to purple-bronze foliage that holds color year-round and its showy fringe-like flowers in spring and sporadically through the year. The purple-leaved forms ('Plum Delight', 'Burgundy', 'Purple Diamond') are especially striking contrasted against green neighbors. It tolerates summer heat and humidity better than most ornamental shrubs. Prune immediately after the main spring flowering to shape; do not shear into tight balls — this destroys the natural graceful arching habit and removes flowering wood. Apply a slow-release, acid-forming fertilizer in early spring.

plant_note: Chilling seed before sowing improves germination; spreads by runners.
directions: Start seed indoors well before the last frost, ideally after a brief cold period to improve germination. Also called winter cherry, this hardy perennial forms papery orange husks prized for dried arrangements and fall decor. Grow in full sun to part shade with average soil, giving it room since it spreads by underground runners and can be aggressive. Harvest the lanterns once they turn bright orange and hang them to dry.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow Chocolate Cherry sunflowers an inch deep after the last frost, spacing them to allow for their branching habit. Each plant carries multiple five-to-eight-inch blooms in deep burgundy-to-black petals around dark chocolate centers on six-to-seven-foot stems. They make dramatic, pollen-rich cut flowers that keep coming through summer and fall. Cut regularly to encourage more side blooms and leave a few for the pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow in lean, well-drained soil; drought tolerant once established.
directions: Direct sow in spring in lean, well-drained soil and full sun, where the plants thrive with minimal fuss. Native to the dry southwestern U.S., this hardy heirloom, also called chocolate daisy, gives off a genuine chocolate fragrance strongest in the morning and late afternoon. Plant it along walkways or patios where visitors can catch the scent, and water sparingly once established. Deadhead to encourage repeat bloom through the season.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed before sowing; provide a trellis for the vines.
directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis, arbor, or fence. This Japanese morning glory carries large, four-to-five-inch, rosy-chocolate blooms above attractive green-and-white variegated foliage. Give it full sun and moderate water, and avoid overly rich soil that favors leaves over flowers. Provide sturdy support early since the vines climb quickly in warm weather.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in a sheltered, shaded spot; tolerates transplanting well.
directions: Set crowns just below soil level in well-drained but moisture-retentive soil amended with leaf mold or compost. This native fern stays evergreen through winter in zones 6 and warmer, making it valuable for year-round ground cover under deciduous trees. Once established it tolerates moderate drought better than most ferns, though it prefers steady moisture through summer. Old fronds flatten to the ground in winter but remain green; remove them in early spring to allow unobstructed emergence of the new season's fronds.

plant_note: Plant crown divisions in early spring, keeping the fibrous root ball intact and the crown at soil level; constant moisture is critical during establishment.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this stately native fern is one of the few that thrives in saturated conditions around pond margins, rain gardens, and boggy areas while also adapting to ordinary woodland garden soil. The erect, fertile fronds with their distinctive cinnamon-colored spore cases emerge in the center of the vase-shaped plant in spring and are the source of the common name. Divide the large, fibrous rootball in early spring every 5–7 years; each division should include multiple crowns and a generous portion of roots. The plant is deer resistant and largely pest-free.

plant_note: Set out nursery plants in spring; roses are grown from grafted or rooted stock, not seed.
directions: Plant this compact shrub rose in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed, spacing plants for good airflow. Cinnamon Hearts forms tidy mounds topped with clusters of fragrant cherry-red blooms from spring into fall. Water deeply and regularly during the first season to establish roots, then keep soil evenly moist. Prune lightly in late winter and remove spent blooms to encourage continued flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost while soil is still cool; succession sow every 3 weeks for extended bloom.
directions: Direct sow on the soil surface or barely cover; seeds germinate best at cool soil temperatures and seedlings dislike transplanting. Thin ruthlessly to 9–12 in because overcrowded plants bloom poorly and are prone to powdery mildew in humid conditions. Clarkia thrives on neglect in poor, well-drained soil — fertilizing too heavily causes lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants naturally set seed and die when summer heat arrives; in mild-winter climates, sow in fall for late-winter to spring bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow at or 2 weeks before last frost; cold stratification of seed for 7 days in the refrigerator improves germination.
directions: Press seed lightly onto the soil surface with barely 1/8 in of cover — light aids germination. Cleome self-sows prolifically and can become weedy; deadhead to limit reseeding if preferred. The tall, airy flower heads open from the base upward over several weeks, providing an extended season of bloom. Handle plants with care as stems bear small spines and the foliage has a musky scent.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall against a sturdy wall, fence, or large tree it can cling to.
directions: This vigorous woody vine climbs walls and trees by aerial rootlets, covering surfaces with heart-shaped leaves and fragrant, lacy white blooms in late spring. Plant in part shade to full shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; it also tolerates morning sun with steady moisture. Be patient, as it is slow to establish but grows rapidly once settled, eventually reaching great heights. Provide strong support and prune after flowering only to control size.

plant_note: Start indoors and surface sow, as light aids germination; transplant to well-drained soil.
directions: A hardy, drought-tolerant perennial yarrow blend with feathery foliage and flat flower clusters in red, rose, pink, yellow, and white. Surface sow seed indoors and press it in without covering, since light aids germination. Transplant to lean, well-drained soil in full sun, where it needs little water once established. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and cut flowers for fresh or dried arrangements.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall with plenty of room for the broad pyramidal form.
directions: This iconic conifer forms a stately pyramid of silvery-blue needles, serving as a striking specimen or windbreak. Plant in full sun in deep, moist, well-drained soil and give it space, as mature trees grow large. Water regularly while young to establish deep roots, then it tolerates cold and moderate drought well. Avoid crowding to reduce needle-cast and canker problems, and prune only to remove dead or damaged branches.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seeds indoors for 3–4 weeks before spring sowing.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly on the soil surface and provide 3–4 weeks of cold stratification (fall sowing outdoors achieves this naturally) to break dormancy. Columbines bloom most freely in their second and third years and are prolific self-seeders that will hybridize freely, creating a constantly shifting mix of colors. Leafminer damage creates white tunnels in the foliage but rarely threatens plant health; remove affected leaves and dispose of them. Plants are short-lived perennials (3–5 years) but the colony self-perpetuates readily through self-seeding.

plant_note: Surface sow, as the seed needs light; stock thrives in cool weather.
directions: Start seed indoors and surface sow, since stock germinates best with light, then transplant while the weather is still cool. This blend forms upright, single-stemmed columns of fully double, clove-scented blooms excellent for bouquets and drying. Grow in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and even moisture, and set plants out early because stock dislikes summer heat. It performs especially well in containers and cool spring gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in wet to consistently moist soil; this is one of the few large shrubs that thrives in standing water and wet clay where most plants fail.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, or even periodically flooded soil in full sun to partial shade; buttonbush is a native shrub of swamps, pond margins, and floodplains throughout eastern North America, producing creamy white spherical flower heads resembling pincushions in July and August that are among the most intensively visited flowers by butterflies, native bees, and hummingbirds. The round seed heads provide food for waterfowl and songbirds through winter. It is one of the premier plants for rain gardens, bioswales, and pond edges where periodic flooding occurs. Prune in late winter to shape and stimulate new flowering wood; it can be cut back hard if it becomes too large.

plant_note: Fall sow or early spring; biennial that blooms in its second year.
directions: Surface sow common evening primrose in fall or early spring, pressing the seed in lightly since it needs light to germinate. This hardy native biennial forms a low rosette its first year, then sends up tall stems of fragrant yellow flowers that open at dusk the second season. It thrives in poor, dry soil and tolerates neglect, making it ideal for tough spots and pollinator plantings. Let it self-sow to keep the two-year cycle going in the garden.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify 30 days before spring planting.
directions: Sow common milkweed in fall or cold-stratify the seed for a month before spring planting to break dormancy. This vigorous native perennial grows two to three feet tall with broad leaves and fragrant clusters of pink star-shaped flowers that are vital to Monarch butterflies. It spreads by rhizomes and can colonize, so site it where it has room to naturalize. Give it full sun and average soil, and it will host caterpillars and feed countless pollinators.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify seed for several weeks before spring sowing.
directions: Sow in fall or cold-stratify the seed for several weeks before spring sowing to break dormancy. This native heirloom is an essential host plant for monarch caterpillars and offers fragrant pink to purplish or white summer flowers followed by decorative seedpods. Grow in full sun in average soil, giving it room since it spreads by rhizomes. Leave some pods to mature for reseeding and to support the monarch lifecycle.

plant_note: Sow directly outdoors once soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.
directions: This native wild sunflower is a vigorous, widely branching annual that can top ten feet, so give it room and a sunny, open spot. Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm garden soil after the last frost, thinning seedlings to prevent crowding. The branching habit produces many golden-yellow blooms that feed bees and later provide seed for birds. Stake tall plants in windy sites and water steadily until they are well established.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall for natural stratification; seed requires 60-90 days cold-moist stratification before germination. Direct sow 1/4 inch deep where plants are to grow permanently — compass plant has a deep taproot and transplants very poorly.
directions: Sow seed directly in its permanent location — deeply and well-drained, full sun; compass plant is a dramatic prairie giant, eventually producing 6–10 foot flower stalks lined with sunflower-like yellow flowers from July through September in mature plants. The deeply cut, pinnate basal leaves are oriented north-south on the plant (hence the common name), a feature used by prairie travelers for navigation. The deep taproot (reaching 15+ feet in mature plants) makes it essentially indestructible to drought and allows it to colonize harsh, rocky, and dry sites. Plants are extremely slow to bloom from seed — typically 3–5 years — but then persist for decades without any care. A cornerstone statement plant for native meadow gardens and prairie restorations.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seeds for 60–90 days or direct sow in fall; requires 2–3 years from seed to first bloom. Deep taproot makes transplanting of established plants impossible.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in deeply prepared, well-drained soil in full sun and be patient — the compass plant spends its first 2–3 years building a massive taproot that may reach 14 feet deep before producing its first flower stalk. Once established it is virtually drought-proof and may live for decades. The deeply lobed basal leaves align their edges north-south to minimize midday sun exposure, giving the plant its common name and serving as a primitive compass for prairie travelers. The tall, yellow composite flower stalks in midsummer provide pollen for native bees; goldfinches harvest the seeds in fall. Never transplant established plants.

plant_note: Sow in darkness; violas are frost tolerant and thrive in cool weather.
directions: Start seed indoors and cover it, as violas germinate best in darkness, then set out the frost-tolerant plants early. This blend blooms reliably in cool weather and does best with afternoon shade to prolong flowering during warm spells. Keep soil evenly moist and deadhead to encourage continued bloom. Violas often reseed, popping up in unexpected corners the following season.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The starchy trunk contains cycasin toxins and was traditionally processed through extensive washing before use as food starch.
directions: Plant in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; coontie is the only cycad native to continental North America and the only host plant for the spectacular Atala butterfly (Eumaeus atala), a striking Caribbean species with iridescent blue-green wings that was once thought extinct in Florida before recovering after landscape plantings of coontie increased. This prehistoric-looking plant with stiff, dark green pinnate fronds emerges almost stemless from a large underground trunk and is extraordinarily drought, heat, and salt-spray tolerant once established in zone 8–11. The large red seeds are produced in dramatic cones and are toxic; plant away from areas accessible to children and pets. Plant coontie specifically to support Atala butterfly populations in South Florida.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in late summer to early fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; fall planting allows roots to establish before winter.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; copper iris is the only native American iris with brick-red to burnt-orange-copper flowers — a unique and striking color that no other wild iris can match. It grows naturally in swamps, bayou margins, and bottomland forests of the lower Mississippi Valley and Gulf Coast, where it tolerates periodic inundation. The plant is one of the parent species of the spectacular Louisiana iris hybrids and contributes their warm red-copper-orange color range to hybrid cultivars. Plant at pond margins, in consistently wet rain garden areas, or in soil that stays moist throughout the growing season. It spreads by rhizome into colonies and can be divided in late summer to expand plantings.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered site with protection from hot afternoon sun.
directions: Prized for its glowing coral-red winter bark, this Japanese maple also offers soft green spring foliage that shifts to golden-yellow in fall. Plant in part shade with rich, moist, well-drained soil, protecting it from drying wind and intense afternoon sun that can scorch leaves. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist. Prune minimally in late winter to reveal the colorful stems and maintain the graceful branching form.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 8 the stems freeze to the ground in hard winters but resprout vigorously from the deep, heat-tolerant root system each spring.
directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; coral bean is one of the most striking native shrubs of the Gulf Coast and Southeast, producing brilliant scarlet tubular flowers in long spikes in spring before the leaves emerge — they are among the most important hummingbird nectar plants in the Southeast. In fall the pods split open to reveal vivid coral-red seeds that are highly ornamental but extremely toxic if swallowed. The stems bear recurved spines that deter browsing; the plant dies to the ground in hard freezes in zone 7–8 but regrows rapidly from an established root crown. It tolerates partial shade, sandy soil, drought, and salt spray — making it an outstanding native plant for difficult spots in Gulf Coast gardens. The red seeds were traditionally used as ornamentation and have important Native American cultural uses.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface sow as seeds need light to germinate — do not cover.
directions: Press seeds onto the surface of a moist germination medium — do not cover, as light is required for germination; transplant to a humus-rich, well-drained site after last frost. Grown primarily for their spectacular mound-forming foliage in shades of burgundy, copper, lime, silver, and purple, heucheras also produce airy wands of tiny flowers beloved by hummingbirds. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to rejuvenate the woody crowns; reset divisions with the crown at soil level. Most modern hybrids tolerate more sun with adequate moisture but prefer afternoon shade in hot climates.

plant_note: Surface sow into warm soil, as the fine seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Surface sow into warm soil after frost, since the small seed needs light to germinate. Coral Fountain produces dramatic pendulous tassels in shades of coral that dry beautifully for everlasting arrangements, wreaths, and crafts. Grow in full sun with average soil and moderate water for the fullest tassels. Harvest stems as the flowers reach full color and hang them to dry.

plant_note: Plant container-grown vines in spring near a trellis, fence, or other support; stems twine and will climb 10–15 feet.
directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil at the base of a trellis or fence; unlike invasive Japanese honeysuckle this native species is well-behaved and will not escape into natural areas. The long, tubular scarlet-to-coral flowers bloom heavily in spring and continue sporadically through fall, providing the long-tube nectar that ruby-throated hummingbirds require. Prune in late winter or just after the main spring flush to shape the vine and remove dead wood. In the deep South it is semi-evergreen and may retain foliage through mild winters; in colder zones it loses leaves but regrows vigorously from the base in spring.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors a few weeks earlier for a head start. Coral Zinnia carries four-to-five-inch blooms in sun-kissed coral on sturdy stems that are ideal for cutting and beloved by butterflies. Space plants for good airflow and water at the base to limit powdery mildew. Cut or deadhead often to keep the plants blooming until frost.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs or rooted suckers in spring in any soil in partial to full shade; coralberry tolerates dry shade better than almost any other native shrub, making it useful under mature trees where most plants fail.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil — including dry, rocky, and infertile — in partial to full shade; coralberry is one of the most shade-tolerant and drought-tolerant of all native shrubs, growing naturally on dry limestone bluffs, rocky woodland slopes, and under dense tree canopy where little else survives. The small pinkish flowers in early summer attract native bees; the small coral-pink to deep red berry-like drupes that follow in fall persist into winter and provide food for bobwhite, wild turkey, mockingbirds, and other birds. The plant spreads by root suckers to form dense, erosion-controlling colonies — excellent for dry, difficult slopes under trees. The cultivar 'Hancock' is particularly dense and ground-hugging. All parts mildly toxic if ingested in quantity.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil and stake tall plants.
directions: Coralie blends the most perfect blush and pink shades, pairing beautifully with other soft-toned flowers in the cutting garden. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil after the ground warms. Because the plant grows tall, provide sturdy staking or support unless you are cutting frequently. Pinch early for branching, cut often to keep blooms coming, and lift tubers to store indoors over winter in cold zones.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date; seeds tolerate light frost and germinate best in cool soil.
directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and tamp lightly; good drainage is essential since coreopsis rots in wet soil over winter. Deadhead regularly to keep the bright yellow flowers coming all summer. Shear plants back by half after the first flush to encourage compact regrowth and a second bloom. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring to prevent the center from dying out.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: Cornel Bronze is a burnt-orange ball dahlia related to Cornel, sharing its prolific bloom, excellent tuber production, and tidy plant habit. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after the last frost. Pinch young plants for bushier growth and harvest the round, tightly petaled blooms frequently for the vase. Lift and store the abundant tubers indoors over winter in regions colder than Zone 8.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: Cornel is the classic burnt-red ball dahlia, a shorter, sturdy plant that often needs no staking and blooms prolifically. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil once the soil has warmed. Pinch the growing tips to encourage branching and cut the tightly formed blooms often for long-lasting arrangements. In cold-winter regions, dig and store the tubers, which this variety produces reliably, after the first frost.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; give the long-season vines a sturdy trellis.
directions: Direct sow after frost in rich soil and full sun, giving the vigorous vines a strong trellis to climb. Corsican produces round, hard-shelled gourds about six to ten inches across that cure to last indefinitely and can be carved, painted, or drilled for crafts. Provide a long, warm growing season and steady moisture while the fruit develops. Harvest before hard frost and cure the gourds in a dry, airy spot for several weeks.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; cosmos thrives in poor soil and needs no fertilizing.
directions: Press seed lightly onto the soil surface and cover with 1/4 in of fine soil. Cosmos germinates best in poor, well-drained soil — avoid fertilizing, which promotes foliage over flowers. Pinch young plants at 12 in to encourage branching. Deadhead regularly or allow seed to set for self-sowing the following year.

plant_note: Start indoors early; tolerant of coastal conditions and needs no cold period.
directions: Start seed indoors several weeks before the last frost and transplant after the soil warms. This blend produces pastel, papery blooms on stems up to eighteen inches long that are prized for fresh and dried arrangements. It grows well in coastal areas and needs no cool period to flower. Harvest stems when the blooms are fully open and hang them upside down to dry for lasting color.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: Direct sow after frost or start indoors a few weeks early for a jump on the season. Crackerjack is a superior tall blend with a mounding form and sturdy stalks bearing four-inch blooms in tangerine orange, rich gold, and lemony yellow. Grow in full sun with average, well-drained soil and moderate water. Deadhead spent blooms to keep the plants tidy and flowering into fall.

plant_note: Start indoors a few weeks before last frost or sow directly once soil warms.
directions: Crackerjack is a tall African marigold prized for large, showy double pompons in gold, orange, and yellow on sturdy stems. Start seeds indoors for a head start or sow directly after frost danger passes, barely covering them. Plant in full sun where the strong scent helps deter garden pests, making it a classic vegetable-garden companion. Deadhead spent blooms to keep flowers coming all summer and pinch young plants to encourage branching.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; cream violet is one of the most adaptable native violets for the shaded garden, tolerating a range of moisture conditions and significant shade.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — cream violet is a native woodland violet of eastern deciduous forests, distinguished by its cream to white flowers with distinctive purple veining (striping) on the lower three petals that gives it both the species name striata and the common name striped cream violet. Unlike the stemless violets (V. sororia, V. cucullata), cream violet is leafy-stemmed, growing from branched stems rather than a basal rosette, which gives it a somewhat different growth form in the woodland garden. It blooms in spring and often reblooms again in fall. Grows naturally in moist, rich woodland, stream banks, and moist forest clearings throughout the eastern US. Forms attractive groundcover colonies in shaded settings. Like all native violets, it supports native fritillary butterfly caterpillars as a larval host plant. An excellent white-flowered native violet alternative to the more common blue species.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; do not confuse with moss phlox (Phlox subulata), which requires full sun — this species is the shade-tolerant woodland species.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; creeping phlox is the shade-tolerant native phlox species of the Southern Appalachian woodland floor, quite different from the sun-loving moss phlox (Phlox subulata) that cascades over walls in full sun. It produces fragrant lavender, pink, or blue-violet flowers in early spring just as trilliums and spring ephemerals are blooming, spreading by stolons to form a low, attractive mat of semi-evergreen foliage that brightens shaded areas year-round. It performs best in the cool, moist, shaded conditions of a woodland garden and is not suited to zone 9 and warmer. Named cultivars like 'Blue Ridge' (blue-violet) and 'Sherwood Purple' (vivid purple) are widely available from native plant nurseries.

plant_note: Plant corms in spring around last frost; in zones 6–7 apply a thick mulch in fall to protect overwintering corms.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in groups of 6 or more for the best visual impact; crocosmia forms clumps through underground stolons. Arching sprays of scarlet, orange, or yellow tubular flowers appear from midsummer into fall and are excellent for cutting. Where hardy, divide congested clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to refresh bloom; in zone 5 and colder, lift and store over winter like gladiolus. 'Lucifer' is among the hardiest cultivars, tolerating zone 5 with reliable mulching.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall, 6–10 weeks before the ground freezes, for late-winter to early-spring bloom.
directions: Plant corms pointed side up at a depth of 3–4 in in well-drained soil; they naturalize quickly and look best planted in large, informal drifts. Squirrels frequently dig freshly planted corms, so covering the bed with wire mesh until the ground freezes can protect plantings. Foliage is narrow and grassy; allow it to die back completely before removing. Species crocus tend to be more reliably perennial than large-flowered Dutch types.

plant_note: Plant container vines near a sturdy support structure in spring; crossvine will attach to brick, wood, or bark by tendrils and does not damage surfaces the way some ivies do. Blooms most heavily in sun but performs well in shade.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to full shade; crossvine is a vigorous native semi-evergreen climbing vine of the Southeast with spectacular trumpet-shaped orange-red flowers with yellow throats in early spring that are among the first abundant nectar sources for hummingbirds returning from migration. The common name comes from the cross-shaped pith visible in cross-section of the stem. It climbs by branched tendrils and clings securely to brick, masonry, bark, and fences without causing damage. Unlike many vines, it blooms well even in moderate to deep shade and is one of the best native choices for covering a shaded fence or wall. The foliage is semi-evergreen to evergreen in zone 8–9 and takes on bronze-purple winter color. Prune after bloom to control size.

plant_note: Surface sow the tiny seed indoors early, as it needs light to germinate.
directions: Surface sow the fine seed indoors several weeks before the last frost, since it needs light to germinate. Crystal Palace forms compact mounds of rich, dark-blue flowers with bronze-tipped foliage that deepens in full sun, lovely in containers, borders, and baskets. Keep it evenly moist and provide afternoon shade where summers are hot. A light shearing midseason refreshes the plant and renews bloom.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; cucumber magnolia is the most cold-hardy of the deciduous magnolias and tolerates more cold than virtually any other Magnolia species.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — cucumber magnolia is the largest and most cold-hardy native magnolia of eastern North America and the only magnolia native to the Great Lakes region and far north, surviving to zone 3. The greenish-yellow flowers (2–4 inches) in spring are not the showiest, but the distinctive cucumber-shaped bright red-pink fruiting cones in summer that split to reveal brilliant orange-red seeds are highly ornamental. The tree develops an impressive pyramidal to broadly rounded form 50–80 feet tall — one of the largest deciduous trees in the native flora. Crucially, it is a parent in the development of numerous hybrid 'yellow magnolias' such as 'Elizabeth' (with Magnolia denudata), which have transformed the magnolia world. The tree produces a yellow pigment in the wood historically used by Native Americans. An excellent choice for large-scale landscape planting and ecological restoration in zone 3–8.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination rates.
directions: Sow seeds indoors after cold stratification or plant divisions in spring in moist to moderately moist, organically rich soil; culver's root is an imposing native prairie plant with whorled, lance-shaped leaves and tall candelabra-like spikes of white to pale lavender flowers in mid- to late summer. The architectural form makes it a striking back-of-border plant, and the flowers attract a wide variety of native bees and butterflies. Rarely needs staking when grown in adequate light; divide every 4–5 years in spring to maintain vigor. Excellent companion for joe-pye weed, prairie blazing star, and native grasses.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for 60 days before spring sowing, or direct sow in fall for natural stratification; plants bloom in the second year from seed.
directions: Sow stratified seed or transplant into deep, fertile, moist soil in full sun; the distinctive paired leaves wrap the square stem to form a cup that collects rainwater — providing a drinking and bathing site for birds. Tall yellow composite flowers bloom from midsummer through fall and are important sources of pollen and nectar for native bees, including specialist prairie bees. The large stature (6–10 feet) requires placement at the back of the border or in a wildflower meadow. Divide established clumps in spring every 4–5 years; the plant spreads by self-seeding in congenial conditions and can naturalize aggressively. Goldfinches harvest the seeds in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8 weeks early; sow seed on edge.
directions: Start cup and saucer vine indoors eight weeks before the last frost, sowing the flat seeds on edge to prevent rot. This vigorous climber uses tendrils to scale trellises and can reach twenty feet in a single season. The large bell-shaped flowers open greenish and mature to deep purple, resembling a cup on a leafy saucer. Transplant after frost into full sun and rich, moist soil, providing sturdy support.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost into a dry, sunny site.
directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to a sunny, very well-drained site — catananche is native to dry Mediterranean hillsides and rots in moist soil. The papery, cornflower-blue blooms on wiry stems are excellent cut or dried flowers that hold their color well. Shear plants by half after the first flush in early summer to encourage repeat flowering into fall. Best treated as a short-lived perennial; replace plants every 2–3 years or allow to self-sow for a continuing colony.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost for fast, easy germination.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors for an earlier display. As the name promises, cutting these old-fashioned two-to-two-and-a-half-inch blooms makes the plants flower more, producing continuously until fall frost. Space for airflow and water at the base to limit powdery mildew. Cut stems often to fill bouquets and keep the plants covered in blooms.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before the ground freezes, for spring bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times the bulb's height (typically 6–8 in) in well-drained soil with the pointed end up. Daffodils naturalize readily and multiply over time; deer and rodents avoid them because the bulbs are mildly toxic. Allow foliage to yellow and die back naturally — at least 6 weeks — so the bulb can store energy for next year's bloom. Divide congested clumps every 5–7 years when flowering diminishes.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 60°F.
directions: Plant tubers horizontally 4–6 in deep with the eye pointing up after the last frost when soil reaches 60°F; set a stake at planting time to avoid spearing the tuber later. Pinch the growing tip when plants are 12 in tall to encourage branching and more flowers. In zones below 8, dig tubers after the first killing frost, cure them for a week, then store in slightly damp peat at 40–50°F through winter. Disbudding side buds around the main bud produces exhibition-quality blooms.

common_name: Dainty Red and Yellow Lady's Purse
plant_note: Surface sow the fine seed, which needs light; keep cool and evenly moist.
directions: Surface sow the very fine seed indoors, since it needs light and cool conditions to germinate well. This compact, bushy plant produces one-to-two-inch inflated, slipper-shaped blooms in sunny yellow marked with striking red blotches. It performs beautifully cascading from containers, hanging baskets, and garden edges. Keep it consistently moist, give it part shade in warm climates, and protect it from intense heat.

plant_note: Surface sow the fine seed, which needs light; keep cool and evenly moist.
directions: Surface sow the tiny seed indoors, giving it light and cool temperatures for reliable germination. This charming, compact plant bears one-to-two-inch slipper-shaped blooms in sunny yellow dusted with delicate red spots for a hand-painted effect. It shines in containers, hanging baskets, and along garden edges. Keep the soil evenly moist and provide part shade and protection from heat in warm regions.

plant_note: Direct sow, as this ornamental carrot resents transplanting.
directions: Direct sow where plants are to grow, since this ornamental carrot dislikes root disturbance. Also called false Queen Anne's lace, Dara adds soft, airy, lacy umbels to the garden and to fresh or dried arrangements without the aggressiveness of the true species. It is fairly drought tolerant once established, so water moderately. Cut stems when the umbels are freshly open for the longest vase life.

plant_note: Plant a young tree in spring after the ground has thawed, in deep moist soil with room to grow large.
directions: Dawn redwood is a fast-growing deciduous conifer often called a living fossil, prized for feathery fern-like foliage that turns coppery orange before dropping in fall. Give it full sun and consistently moist, well-drained soil; it tolerates wet sites and even seasonal flooding better than most conifers. Choose a location with abundant vertical and horizontal space, as mature trees reach 70 to 100 feet with a broad conical form. Water deeply during establishment and through dry spells for the first few seasons. It needs no pruning to develop its naturally elegant shape.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Day Dreamer is a waterlily-form dahlia bearing soft blooms blending apricot, pink, and buttercream on productive plants. Plant tubers horizontally about 4 to 6 inches deep once soil warms, positioning the eye upward, and hold off watering until sprouts emerge to prevent rot. Pinch the growing tip when plants reach about a foot to encourage bushier growth and more blooms. Stake or corral tall stems and cut flowers regularly to keep the plant producing. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter.

plant_note: Plant bare-root divisions in spring or fall; set crowns just 1 in below soil level.
directions: Plant fans with the crown about 1 in deep, spreading roots outward over a small mound of soil in the hole. Water thoroughly after planting and keep consistently moist the first season. Divide overcrowded clumps every 4–5 years in early spring or late summer when bloom quality declines. Removing spent scapes after all buds have opened keeps the planting tidy.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for a few weeks to improve germination of this perennial.
directions: Cold-stratify the seed for a few weeks and start indoors, since this perennial germinates better after a chill. Dazzler Blend suits any garden and especially rarely watered spots, offering colorful, pollinator-friendly blooms and excellent drought tolerance once established. Grow it in full sun with lean, sharply drained soil, making it a fine choice for rock gardens. Deadhead to tidy plants and encourage additional bloom.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in mild climates (zones 7–10) or in early spring after danger of hard frost has passed in colder zones.
directions: Soak the small, irregular corms in water for 4–6 hours before planting; set them about 2 in deep with the pointed side down (or on their side if unsure). Anemones prefer cool, well-drained soil and perform best where winters are mild and springs are long and cool. Poppy-like blooms in vivid red, blue, purple, white, and bicolors appear over a 6–8 week period in late winter to spring. In cold climates treat as an annual, planting corms in spring for early summer color.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; not suitable for zone 9 and warmer where summer heat causes crown rot.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; dead nettle is one of the most effective and ornamental shade groundcovers for zones 3–8, valued for its silvery-marked, heart-shaped leaves that brighten dark corners throughout the growing season and its whorls of pink or white flowers in late spring and early summer. It is a rapid spreader that forms a dense, weed-suppressing carpet and is easy to propagate by stem cuttings or division. Unlike the weedy annual red dead nettle (L. purpureum), this perennial species is non-invasive and stays where it is planted. Named cultivars like 'White Nancy' (white flowers, heavily silver leaves), 'Beacon Silver', and 'Purple Dragon' are widely available. In the South, plant in a cool, shaded spot and provide afternoon shade from heat; it goes dormant and may die back in high summer in zone 8.

plant_note: Start indoors for earlier bloom; tubers can be lifted and stored over winter.
directions: Start seed indoors about a month before the last frost and transplant into rich, well-drained soil once weather settles. This very tall blend delivers eye-popping colors on long, sturdy stems, blooming about 90 days from sowing and returning year after year in mild zones. Stake the tall plants and plant them toward the back of the border. Dig and store the tubers over winter in colder climates to replant the following spring.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; fresh seed germinates best and benefits from 1–2 weeks of cold stratification.
directions: Use fresh seed — viability drops quickly in storage — and cold-stratify for 1–2 weeks before sowing at 60–70°F for best germination. Transplant after the last frost into rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil and stake early, as the tall spikes are highly susceptible to wind damage. Cut flower spikes back to basal foliage after the first bloom to encourage a second, smaller flush in late summer. All parts of the plant are toxic; all plant contact should be followed by hand washing.

plant_note: Fall sowing provides the cold stratification the seed needs; spring sowing also works after chilling.
directions: This native prairie perennial sends up tall, upright spikes of vivid purple bottlebrush flowers that bloom from the top down. Seeds germinate best after a cold period, so sow outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, where the plants are drought tolerant once established and popular with butterflies. The long-lasting spikes make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers.

plant_note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring in rich, moist soil.
directions: Deutschland astilbe is a shade-loving perennial that sends up feathery white plumes above glossy green foliage in early summer. Plant it in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil, as it dislikes drying out. Work compost into the planting hole and mulch to conserve moisture around its shallow roots. Divide clumps every three to four years in spring to maintain vigor. Deadheading is optional; the dried plumes add winter interest, and the flowers attract pollinators.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in any moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; devil's walking stick spreads by suckering roots and can colonize large areas over time — site it where spreading is welcome or in contained plantings.
directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; devil's walking stick is one of the most architecturally dramatic native plants in eastern North America — a large, coarse shrub or small tree with massive bipinnate-to-tripinnate compound leaves (sometimes 3–4 feet long) arranged in a palm-like whorl at the top of unbranched, viciously prickle-armed stems. The effect in the landscape is aggressively tropical and primitive — unlike anything else in the native plant palette. In late summer, enormous flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers (12–18 inches across) provide a spectacular nectar feast for dozens of native bee and butterfly species. The dark purple berries in fall are consumed by more than 100 bird species during fall migration. It suckers to form dense colonies in moist woodland edges — give it space or contain it.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into lean soil and full sun.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost in lean soil and full sun, avoiding rich ground that reduces flowering. Diablo carries vibrant, bold blossoms on tall stems from midsummer until first fall frost, feeding bees, hummingbirds, and butterflies. Once established it is drought tolerant and low maintenance, so water sparingly. Deadhead or cut for bouquets to keep the flowers coming, and let a few set seed to reseed.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost; lift and store where not hardy.
directions: Plant dinnerplate dahlia tubers after the last frost, laying them horizontally about six inches deep with the eye facing up. These giant blooms can reach ten to twelve inches across and require staking at planting to support the tall stems. Grow in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, watering deeply once shoots emerge and pinching to promote branching. In cold climates lift and store the tubers in a frost-free place over winter.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost danger passes and soil warms.
directions: Diva is a tall, prolific dahlia producing rich plum blooms with exceptional vase life for a large flower. Set tubers about 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun once soil is warm, and wait to water heavily until growth appears. Because plants grow very tall, install sturdy stakes or a support cage early in the season. Cut or deadhead spent flowers frequently to prolong blooming into fall. Lift and store tubers over winter where the ground freezes.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors, as the seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Surface sow the seed indoors several weeks before the last frost, since it needs light to sprout, and transplant after frost. Dondo White forms billowy clusters of fluffy, pure-white blooms on upright plants reaching twenty-four to thirty inches, adding vertical interest and texture to borders and containers. It flowers continuously from summer through frost. Grow in full sun with even moisture and deadhead to prolong the display.

plant_note: Surface sow into warm soil, as the fine seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Surface sow into warm soil after frost, since the fine seed needs light to germinate. These low-growing plants form a carpet of brilliantly colored, rose-like double flowers that open on sunny days through the heat of summer. Very drought tolerant, moss rose needs only occasional watering and thrives in dry banks and hot, reflected-heat spots. Give it full sun and sharply drained soil for the best flowering.

plant_note: Often grown as a biennial; sow in spring or summer for bloom the next year.
directions: Sow in spring or summer, since this short-lived perennial is usually grown as a biennial that blooms its second year. Double Blend forms large clusters of edible, double flowers in many colors that rank among the longest-lasting cut flowers. In the garden the sweet, spicy-scented blooms draw butterflies and bees, and the mild clove-flavored petals garnish salads. Grow in full sun to part shade with well-drained soil and even moisture.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into lean soil and full sun.
directions: Direct sow after frost in average-to-lean soil and full sun, since rich ground limits flowering. Double Click produces a profusion of mostly double, some semi-double, chrysanthemum-like blooms in pink, white, and carmine red. Once established it is easy to care for and drought tolerant, so water sparingly. Deadhead or cut for bouquets to prolong bloom, and let a few flowers set seed to reseed.

plant_note: Set out nursery plants in spring; grown from grafted or rooted stock, not seed.
directions: Plant this low-maintenance shrub rose in spring in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing plants for airflow. Double Knock Out blooms from spring to fall with two layers of fiery red petals and strong disease resistance. Water deeply during the first season to establish, then keep the soil evenly moist. It needs little pruning, though a hard cut in late winter keeps the shrub vigorous and shapely.

plant_note: Set out the shrub after danger of hard frost; it is a woody deciduous shrub, not typically grown from seed for this cultivar.
directions: Plant this double-flowered althea in full sun with well-drained soil, spacing shrubs 5-8 feet apart to allow their broad upright habit room to mature. It blooms late summer into fall on the current season's growth, so prune in late winter or early spring to shape and encourage flowering wood. Water regularly the first two seasons until the roots establish, after which it tolerates heat and short dry spells. The full, layered pink blooms are sterile, so this cultivar reseeds far less than single-flowered forms.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost, barely covering seed since light aids germination.
directions: Direct sow or start indoors, pressing the seed onto the surface since light improves germination. This carefree perennial produces semi-double and double golden-yellow blooms from summer until frost and thrives in full sun even in heat, humidity, and poor or dry soil. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong the display and prevent excessive self-seeding. Once established it is quite drought tolerant and needs only occasional watering.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface-sow since light is needed to germinate, and set out hardened plants around last frost.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost, pressing seed onto the mix surface as light is required for germination. This variety carries delicate 1.5-inch bicolor blooms in soft pink and white that resemble tiny apple blossoms on heavily branched, wind-resistant stems. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so transplant early once seedlings are hardened, and pinch young plants to encourage more flowering stems. Regular deadheading and cutting keeps the plants producing long, upright spikes for bouquets.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors early and set out hardened transplants near last frost for cool-season bloom.
directions: Sow indoors 8-10 weeks ahead of the last frost, leaving seed uncovered since light triggers germination. This F1 hybrid bears luminous 1.5-inch open-faced double blooms that shift from peach in cool light to buttery yellow in full sun, on strong branched stems reaching 20-24 inches. Pinch seedlings when a few inches tall to build bushy, floriferous plants and transplant while weather is still cool. Cut and deadhead frequently to keep the wind-resistant stems flowering through the season.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; downy yellow violet is the primary yellow-flowered violet of eastern woodland understories and naturalizes readily by self-seeding in appropriate conditions.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — downy yellow violet is the most common and widespread yellow-flowered native violet of eastern deciduous woodlands, producing bright yellow, purple-veined five-petaled flowers on hairy stems 6–14 inches tall in early to mid-spring, simultaneously with the emergence of the trout lilies and trilliums in the same woodland community. The broad, heart-shaped, softly hairy leaves (giving the common names 'downy' and 'hairy') are attractive throughout the growing season. It grows naturally in rich, moist, deciduous hardwood forest from Canada to the Deep South. Like all violets, downy yellow violet is a critical larval host plant for native fritillary butterfly caterpillars (Speyeria and Boloria species) — the presence of violets in a garden directly supports breeding populations of these spectacular native butterflies. Self-sows modestly to naturalize. Outstanding native for spring woodland wildflower gardens.

plant_note: Plant in spring with the crown a couple inches below the soil surface.
directions: Dr. Ruppel is a vigorous large-flowered clematis with pink, star-shaped blooms accented by a bold magenta bar down each petal. Give it a sunny position for the vines but keep the roots cool and shaded with mulch or low companion plantings. Provide a trellis, arbor, or fence for the climbing stems to grip, and tie in new growth as needed. This variety blooms on both old and new wood, so prune only lightly in late winter to shape and remove dead stems. Water regularly and feed in spring for the best flower show.

plant_note: Plant in fall or early spring in acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: Dream Weaver is a sasanqua camellia bearing abundant double blooms of deep pink to lavender edged in white, flowering from fall into winter. Plant it in partial shade in moist, acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter, and mulch to keep roots cool. Protect from harsh afternoon sun and drying winds, especially in warmer zones. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the evergreen form. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer in spring for lush foliage and reliable bloom.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; the plant naturalizes by suckering to form attractive colonies along stream banks and shaded slopes.
directions: Plant in consistently moist, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; drooping leucothoe is a graceful native evergreen shrub of mountain stream banks and ravines in the Southern Appalachians, with long arching stems clothed in lustrous lance-shaped leaves that turn bronzy-burgundy-purple in winter — one of the finest winter foliage colors in the shade garden. Tiny white bell-shaped flowers in May are fragrant and nectar-rich for early bees. The plant spreads by stolons to form a layered, naturalistic colony that suppresses weeds effectively. The cultivar 'Rainbow' has cream-pink-green variegated foliage; 'Girard's Rainbow' and 'Zeblid' (Scarletta) are also widely available. All parts are toxic. Excellent massed under rhododendrons, azaleas, or as an understory layer beneath oaks.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow after frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. This branching mix delivers 4-5 inch florist-quality blooms in deep burgundy, valentine red, and pumpkin-orange-red shades on strong stems ideal for cutting. Sow successively every couple weeks for continuous bouquets through the season. Water evenly while young and stake in windy sites, since heavy branching heads can lean.

plant_note: Seeds need darkness to germinate; cover completely.
directions: Cover the seed completely because Drummond phlox requires darkness to germinate. Direct sow in early spring in mild climates or start indoors six weeks before the last frost. The clustered flowers come in a wide range of colors and make excellent low bedding and cut arrangements. Deadhead regularly and shear back leggy plants in midsummer to encourage a fresh flush of bloom.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes and self-sows.
directions: Plant drumstick allium bulbs in fall about four inches deep in well-drained soil and full sun. The egg-shaped flower heads emerge green and mature to deep burgundy on wiry stems in early summer, weaving beautifully through borders. Bees and butterflies love the blooms, and the seed heads dry well for arrangements. Keep the site on the dry side in summer and the bulbs will naturalize and self-sow over time.

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after frost; grown as an annual in cold climates.
directions: Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant to full sun once weather settles. This florist favorite produces cheerful golden globe blooms on tall wiry stems that are prized in fresh and dried arrangements. The tough plants tolerate drought, heat, and heavy or poor soil, so avoid overwatering. Harvest stems when the orbs are fully colored and hang upside down to dry for long-lasting decor.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist soil with room for a large shade tree.
directions: Dura Heat is a river birch selection with creamy exfoliating bark and strong tolerance of summer heat and humidity. Plant it in full sun to part shade in moist, slightly acidic soil; it thrives in wet spots where other trees struggle. Water deeply during establishment and dry spells to prevent leaf drop. Its resistance to bronze birch borer makes it a durable landscape and shade tree. Prune only in summer or fall, since birches bleed heavily if cut in late winter or early spring.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost risk passes.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist germination mix and provide light at 65–75°F; germination is slow and uneven over 10–14 days. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; excellent salt and drought tolerance makes this ideal for coastal and hot dry sites. The silvery white, felted foliage is the ornamental feature — pinch off yellow flower buds when they appear to keep plants neat and maintain vigorous leaf growth. In zones 8–11 dusty miller overwinters as a short-lived perennial.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in drifts for best effect.
directions: Plant Dutch crocus corms in fall three to four inches deep in well-drained soil, setting them in generous drifts. The large goblet-shaped flowers in purple, white, and yellow open in early spring for bees emerging from winter. Grow in full sun to light shade and avoid summer irrigation while corms are dormant. Let the grassy foliage ripen fully, and the corms will multiply into showy colonies over time.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–6 in deep; in zones 5–6 apply a winter mulch for protection.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Dutch iris bloom in late spring to early summer and are superb cut flowers with a vase life of 7–10 days. The large falls and standards come in shades of blue, purple, yellow, white, and bicolor. After bloom, allow foliage to die back naturally; in warm, humid climates the bulbs may rot in summer — lift and store dry in a cool place if this occurs. Replant in early fall and incorporate grit into heavy clay soils to improve drainage.

plant_note: Plant small, grain-like tubers 1–2 inches deep in fall in groups of at least 12 for effect; they naturally self-sow after flowering to build colonies over several years.
directions: Plant tiny tubers shallowly in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in full to dappled shade under deciduous trees; this charming spring ephemeral blooms for only 2–3 weeks in early April with distinctive white pantaloon-shaped flowers dangling from arching stems above ferny blue-green foliage. The plant then goes completely dormant by late spring, disappearing entirely until the following year. Plant in large drifts of at least 25–50 tubers for a meaningful display, and interplant with hostas, ferns, or native gingers to fill the void after dormancy. The entire plant is toxic; do not confuse with edible plants. Plants self-sow slowly to build colonies over many years.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.
directions: Dwarf Alberta spruce is a slow-growing conifer forming a naturally dense, cone-shaped pyramid of soft green needles, ideal for small gardens and containers. Plant it in full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding hot, reflective spots that can scorch the fine foliage. Provide good air circulation to reduce spider mite pressure, and rinse the foliage periodically in dry weather. Water consistently, as its shallow roots dry out quickly in pots. It rarely needs pruning thanks to its tidy, formal habit.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions just at or slightly above the soil surface in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; do not plant too deeply or the rhizome will rot.
directions: Plant rhizomes just at the soil surface in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; dwarf crested iris is one of the most charming native shade wildflowers, carpeting the woodland floor with colonies of pale lavender-blue iris flowers marked with white and yellow crests in early spring before the tree canopy fully opens. It grows naturally on wooded slopes, creek banks, and woodland paths across the eastern US. The low-growing, grass-like foliage spreads quickly by shallow rhizome to form dense, weed-suppressing mats — an excellent living mulch under trees. Plant where you can enjoy the flowers up close. The plant goes semi-dormant in summer heat but greens up in fall. Divide crowded colonies in late summer or early fall. The white form 'Alba' is occasionally available.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.
directions: Dwarf Hinoki cypress is a slow-growing evergreen with rich green, fan-shaped sprays of foliage that form a graceful, sculptural pyramid. Plant it in full sun to light shade in moist but well-drained soil, avoiding soggy sites that cause root rot. It appreciates shelter from harsh drying winter winds, which can brown the foliage. Water regularly during establishment and in droughts, then it becomes fairly self-sufficient. Little pruning is needed, and its naturally elegant layered form makes it a favorite for rock gardens and containers.
