plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in very well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy, humus-rich, dry to moist soil in partial shade; dwarf iris requires excellent drainage and strongly acidic soil — it will not tolerate clay, wet, or neutral to alkaline conditions.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in very well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade; dwarf iris is one of the smallest and most charming native irises of the eastern US, producing miniature (2–3 inch) violet-blue flowers with yellow signals on stems only 3–5 inches tall in early spring — often the first iris to bloom, along with hepatica and spring beauty. It grows naturally in dry, acidic, sandy soils of open pine woodlands and dry, rocky woodland edges from Virginia to Georgia, in conditions very different from the moist requirements of most native irises. The leaves are narrow and grasslike. It spreads slowly by slender rhizomes to form a low colony. Excellent for naturalizing in acidic, dry-shaded rock gardens or under pines in the Southeast — one of the few dwarf irises suited to zone 8–9 conditions.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out after all frost danger passes.
directions: This compact dahlia mix grows to about eighteen inches, making it ideal for containers, borders, and small-space gardens. Start seeds indoors under bright light six weeks before the last frost, then move plants outdoors once nights stay warm. The cheerful single blooms come in reds, pinks, yellows, and whites and draw bees and butterflies through summer. Deadhead regularly for continuous color, and in cold zones lift and store the tubers over winter.

plant_note: Start indoors well ahead of last frost; seeds need light to germinate.
directions: This dwarf petunia blend stays compact and covers itself in trumpet-shaped flowers in red, purple, blue, and white. Surface-sow the tiny seeds on moist mix and press them in without covering, since they need light to sprout, and start them indoors a couple months before planting out. Grow in full sun in containers, borders, or hanging baskets and feed regularly for nonstop bloom. Pinch back leggy stems and deadhead to keep the plants tidy and flowering.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil in early spring; soaking seeds overnight aids germination.
directions: This naturally compact sweet pea mix keeps the classic fragrance without needing a tall trellis, so it suits containers and small beds. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil, since sweet peas love chilly starts and dislike summer heat. Provide steady moisture and a sunny spot, and pick blooms often to prolong the flowering season. A short bit of twiggy support or a low fence helps the bushy vines stay upright.

plant_note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; virtually all growth is underground for the first few years as the palm establishes an extensive root system before producing visible trunk.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; dwarf palmetto is the most shade-tolerant of the native southeastern palms, growing naturally in the understory of wet coastal plain forests, swamp margins, and bottomland forests from North Carolina to Florida and Texas. It is usually stemless or produces a very short trunk close to the ground, with bold fan-shaped leaves creating a dramatic tropical effect in the shaded garden. More cold-hardy than cabbage palmetto (surviving to -5°F), it is an excellent choice for providing a Gulf Coast tropical appearance in zone 7 and warmer. The palm grows extremely slowly but is very long-lived. The small black berries produced in tall panicles are eaten by birds. It tolerates periodic flooding and is one of the few palms that performs in significant shade.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; needs a dry summer baking.
directions: Plant dwarf reticulated iris bulbs in fall about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun. The jewel-toned blue and purple flowers with contrasting markings bloom in late winter before most other bulbs. It needs a warm, dry summer dormancy to bloom well in subsequent years, so avoid irrigated beds. Plant it at the front of borders or in rock gardens where its early flowers can be admired up close.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last frost; soak seed overnight to speed germination.
directions: Soak seed overnight, then direct sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last frost at the base of a trellis or support. This blend blooms up to two weeks earlier than typical early types, producing mildly fragrant flowers in nearly every color but yellow on long sturdy stems. Keep the roots cool and evenly moist, and cut flowers often to prolong blooming before summer heat. Note that parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost once soil warms.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. Earthwalker is a side-branching sunflower in warm earthy tones of orange, mahogany, coppery brown, and rusty red, excellent for cutting and for feeding bumblebees and seed-eating birds. Because it branches, a single plant supplies many stems over a long period. Water regularly while establishing and stake in exposed sites to keep tall stalks upright.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or early spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, cool, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; eastern hemlock is one of the few conifers that actually grows better in shade than in full sun, especially in zone 6–7 where afternoon shade is essential. Treat proactively for hemlock woolly adelgid in eastern US with imidacloprid soil drench.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, cool soil in partial to full shade — eastern hemlock is the only large native conifer that genuinely thrives in shade and is often the dominant tree in cool, shaded, north-facing ravines and steep, moist slopes where other conifers cannot compete. The delicate, feathery, dark green foliage and graceful, pendulous branch tips create an exceptionally beautiful, textured specimen in the shade garden. It is considered a foundational 'keystone' species supporting 96 species of Lepidoptera caterpillars, and its dense, multilayered canopy provides critical winter shelter for birds. However, the hemlock woolly adelgid, an introduced pest from Asia, has devastated populations throughout the eastern range — all planted trees in the eastern US should receive preventive treatment with systemic insecticide. In zone 3–7, an irreplaceable shade conifer for the woodland garden.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or cold-stratify seed 4 weeks before spring sowing. Surface sow only — seed needs light to germinate. Self-seeds freely once established.
directions: Sow on the soil surface in partial shade to full sun in moist to well-drained, average soil; wild columbine is a delicate native perennial of rocky woodlands, ledges, and open forest edges whose unique red-and-yellow nodding spurred flowers in April–June are the primary nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds returning from spring migration. The blue-green, lobed foliage is attractive all season. Plants self-seed prolifically and naturalize freely in appropriate conditions — allow seedlings to establish a colony rather than removing all volunteers. Short-lived (3–5 years) but self-perpetuating through seeding. Columbine leaf miners leave unsightly tunnels in foliage; tolerate minor damage or simply remove affected leaves.

plant_note: Surface-sow in fall or early spring; cold stratification improves germination for this native perennial.
directions: Surface-sow this North American native in fall or early spring, as a period of cold moist chilling greatly improves germination. Its nodding red and yellow lantern-like blooms attract hummingbirds and thrive in the dappled light of woodland edges, borders, and streamsides. Give it moist, well-drained soil and part shade, though it tolerates more sun where summers are cool. It self-sows freely and is short-lived, so let some flowers set seed to maintain the planting.

plant_note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; provide room for a small ornamental tree.
directions: Eastern redbud is a small deciduous tree famed for the rosy-pink blossoms that clothe its bare branches in early spring before the heart-shaped leaves emerge. Plant it in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil; it naturally grows as an understory tree and tolerates some shade. Water regularly during the first couple of years to establish its somewhat sensitive root system. Prune only to remove dead or crossing branches after flowering, since it blooms on old wood. Its early nectar is valuable to bees and other pollinators.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; this is a native alternative to the invasive Asian burning bush (E. alatus).
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; eastern wahoo is a native alternative to the invasive Asian burning bush (Euonymus alatus) and performs the same landscape function — stunning fall color — while supporting native wildlife instead of escaping into woodlands. In fall, the foliage turns brilliant scarlet-red while the seed pods split open to reveal dangling bright coral-pink and orange arillate seeds that attract migratory birds including thrushes, bluebirds, and waxwings. The purple-tinged flowers in late spring are modest but charming. The plant tolerates deep shade and is one of few shrubs that thrives under the dense canopy of mature oaks and maples. All parts are toxic to humans and livestock. This is the true 'burning bush' of the native landscape.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; lobelia has extremely tiny seeds and a slow early growth rate.
directions: Surface-sow the dust-like seeds on moist fine-textured mix; do not cover, as they need light to germinate, and use a spray mist to avoid washing them away. Grow seedlings on at cool temperatures (60–65°F) once germinated; pot up in small clusters of 3–5 seedlings rather than separating individuals. Lobelia blooms best in cool weather and partial shade in hot climates, often slowing or stopping in midsummer heat — shear back by half and water regularly for rebloom as fall approaches. The vivid blue, trailing types are classic basket and container plants.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil before the last frost; in mild climates sow in fall for winter and spring bloom.
directions: Soak seed overnight and sow in cool soil several weeks before the last frost, or in fall where winters are mild. This blend offers mildly scented, long-stemmed cut-flower cultivars that flower in the short days of winter and cool spring. Provide a trellis, keep roots cool and evenly moist, and harvest often to extend the bloom period. Parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil before the last frost for spring and early summer bloom.
directions: Soak the seed overnight and sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last frost beside a sturdy trellis. This striking variety bursts with large ruffled blooms in electric magenta-red and a powerful sweet perfume, carrying three to four flowers per long, sturdy stem. Keep roots cool and consistently moist, and cut frequently for vase-worthy bouquets that hold well. Parts of the plant are toxic if ingested, so site it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; elm-leaved goldenrod is one of the most shade-tolerant goldenrods in the eastern US, blooming reliably in the conditions of an open woodland understory.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — elm-leaved goldenrod is an eastern North American native recognized by its leaves, which closely resemble elm leaves with strongly toothed, slightly rough-textured blades on short petioles. It grows naturally in moist, open woodland and woodland edges, making it one of a small number of goldenrods genuinely adapted to shaded conditions. The arching, one-sided (secund) racemes of small golden flowers appear in late summer through fall in shaded locations where most goldenrods cannot bloom. Like blue-stemmed goldenrod, it fills an important ecological niche by providing late-season nectar and pollen in shaded settings where pollinators searching for fall resources would otherwise find nothing. Spreads by rhizome to form a colony. An excellent native goldenrod for adding late-season ecological value to the woodland garden.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; compact enough for containers.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost about an inch deep in full sun, spacing 8-12 inches apart. This blend of shorter, branching sunflowers includes classic single forms and multi-petaled pom-pom types that draw pollinators and make cheery bouquets. Their compact, drought-tolerant habit suits containers, borders, and small gardens. Water regularly while young and deadhead to keep new blooms coming.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil, spacing for a hedge if desired.
directions: Emerald Green arborvitae is a narrow, upright evergreen with dense sprays of bright green foliage, widely planted in rows for privacy screens and hedges. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil, spacing plants roughly 2 to 4 feet apart for a solid living wall. Water regularly during the first two seasons to establish deep roots and prevent winter browning. It holds its color and slender form well without shearing, though it can be trimmed lightly. Protect young plants from heavy snow load and browsing deer.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.
directions: Endless Summer is a reblooming bigleaf hydrangea that flowers on both old and new wood, producing large mophead blooms from spring through fall. Plant it in morning sun with afternoon shade in rich, consistently moist soil, and mulch to keep roots cool. Flower color shifts with soil chemistry: acidic soil yields blue blooms while alkaline soil produces pink, so amend accordingly. Because it blooms on new growth too, light pruning is forgiving, though it rebounds best with minimal cutting. Keep it well watered, as wilting in heat is common.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, or start indoors a few weeks early, spacing plants about a foot apart. This unusual heirloom bears 3-4 inch chartreuse-green edible blooms that are striking in the garden and on the plate. Heat-loving and productive, it flowers endlessly until frost when regularly cut and deadheaded. Water at the base and space for airflow to reduce powdery mildew.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in any well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; once established, epimedium is remarkably drought tolerant and can thrive in the dry root zone under large trees.
directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; epimedium is among the most valuable groundcovers for difficult dry shade — one of the few plants that genuinely thrives in the dry, root-filled zone under mature maples, beeches, and other large shade trees where almost nothing else survives. The heart-shaped, often evergreen or semi-evergreen leaves are attractive year-round, turn bronze in winter, and the small but intricate crimson and pink bicolored flowers in early spring are delightful at close range before most shade perennials emerge. It spreads slowly but persistently by rhizome to form a dense, tidy, weed-suppressing mat. Shear back old foliage in late winter before new growth and flowers emerge. The genus contains dozens of outstanding species and cultivars; E. × rubrum is one of the most widely available and adaptable.

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after frost; surface-sow as light aids germination.
directions: Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface since light aids germination, and transplant once frost danger passes. This blend produces upright architectural spikes of red, purple, white, and Fleuroselect gold-medal coral that bloom nonstop and draw butterflies and other pollinators. Give it full sun and evenly moist, well-drained soil. Deadhead faded spikes to keep the color coming through the season.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface-sow and provide light, then set out after frost as a foliage annual.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost, leaving it uncovered on the surface where light and cool nights encourage germination. Grown for its fragrant, frosty blue-green rounded foliage, Silver Drop makes long-lasting filler for fresh and dried arrangements. Transplant to full sun after frost and pinch to encourage bushy, well-branched stems. Harvest foliage once stems are firm and cure it upright or hang to dry.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; European wild ginger is slower to establish than native Asarum canadense but has glossier, more ornamental leaves and is more tolerant of hot, dry shade.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — European wild ginger is a low, slowly spreading shade groundcover with extraordinarily glossy, deep green, kidney-shaped leaves that remain evergreen through winter in zones 5–8, creating a lustrous, polished carpet under trees and shrubs. The leaves are significantly glossier and more ornamental than native wild ginger (A. canadense), which is deciduous. Small, brownish-purple, three-lobed flowers appear hidden at ground level in spring. It spreads slowly and politely by rhizome, creating a refined, weed-suppressing mat in appropriate conditions. It tolerates dry shade better than the native species once established. Unlike native Asarum, the European species does not host certain specialist insects, but its ornamental value is superior. An outstanding foliage plant for shaded woodland gardens in zones 4–8.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. This branching heirloom bears 4-6 inch sunset-hued blooms with dark centers in many rich tones on one plant, over a long bloom period that keeps vases full and pollinators fed. Because it branches, cut stems regularly to encourage more flowers. Water while establishing and stake tall stalks in windy locations.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil with afternoon shade.
directions: Ever After Ruby is a reblooming azalea that produces vivid ruby-red flowers in spring and again from summer into fall. Plant it in dappled or partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and mulch to protect the shallow roots. Keep the soil evenly moist but never waterlogged, since azaleas resent both drought and soggy feet. Shear lightly right after the spring flush to encourage a fuller shape and stronger repeat bloom. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer in spring.

plant_note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; excellent drainage is essential.
directions: Evolution Emerald Ice is a low, drought-tolerant sedum grown as a groundcover, offering fresh green succulent foliage and pink summer blooms that draw pollinators. Plant it in full sun in gritty, sharply drained soil, since wet or heavy ground causes rot. Once established it thrives on neglect and needs little supplemental water. Shear back lightly after flowering to keep the mat dense and tidy. Divide clumps in spring every few years to expand your planting or renew vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil before or around the last frost; it dislikes summer heat.
directions: Direct sow in cool soil around the last frost, barely covering the fine seed. Fairy Bouquet is a charming blend of dainty three-quarter-inch snapdragon-like blooms in bright colors above airy foliage, also known as toadflax. It makes a colorful mass planting, container filler, or border edging and attracts pollinators. It prefers cool weather, so sow early and provide even moisture before summer heat arrives.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or scarify and cold-stratify seeds before spring planting.
directions: Scarify seeds and cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing, or direct sow in fall to let natural winter conditions break dormancy. Baptisia is a slow-starting plant — expect minimal bloom for the first 2–3 years, but once established, the clump requires virtually no care and lives for decades. The indigo-blue lupine-like flower spikes appear in late spring and are followed by attractive, inflated seedpods that rattle in the wind. Do not attempt to transplant established plants, as the deep taproot does not tolerate disturbance.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in any moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; false indigo is extremely adaptable to difficult wet, flooded, or droughty conditions once established and can be used for streambank stabilization.
directions: Plant in any moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; false indigo is a large, airy native shrub with pinnately compound leaves and 3–6 inch spikes of unusual dark purple-violet flowers with bright orange anthers in early summer — a striking color combination. It blooms at a time when few other shrubs are flowering, providing important early nectar for native bees and the southern dogface butterfly, whose caterpillars feed exclusively on the plant. It is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for wet, poorly drained soils, riverbanks, and flood-prone areas, tolerating temporary inundation and spreading to stabilize streambanks. After the flowers, clusters of resin-dotted seed pods remain through winter. Prune back by one-third after flowering to maintain a compact form. Despite its common name it has no connection to true indigo (Indigofera).

common_name: False Solomon's Seal (Feathery False Lily of the Valley)
plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring, setting them horizontally 2–3 inches deep; each piece should have at least one visible growth bud.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; distinguished from true Solomon's seal (Polygonatum) by its feathery terminal cluster of tiny white flowers in spring rather than hanging bell-shaped flowers. The arching stems hold alternating oval leaves and terminate in airy white plumes in May, followed by green berries that turn red and speckled in fall. The berries are edible in small quantities but mildly laxative and should be consumed cautiously. The plant spreads slowly by rhizome and self-seeds minimally, making it a well-behaved companion for trilliums, ferns, and wild ginger in a woodland garden.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes 1–2 inches deep in spring; the rhizome is longer than Solomon's seal and should be laid horizontally in a trench rather than planted vertically.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; false Solomon's seal closely resembles true Solomon's seal in arching habit and foliage, but differs by bearing a plume of creamy white flowers at the stem tip in late spring rather than pendant bells along the stem. Ruby-red berries follow in late summer, providing important food for migratory thrushes and other birds before turning speckled red-purple at maturity. The architectural arching stems are ideal for naturalized woodland plantings combined with ferns, hostas, and trilliums. Plants spread slowly by thick horizontal rhizomes to form colonies; division every 4–5 years maintains vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in the garden or start indoors; unlike true perennial rudbeckia, heliopsis often blooms robustly in its first season from an early start. Plant in full sun in average, well-drained soil; plants tolerate heat and short dry periods better than helenium. Deadhead regularly throughout summer to keep the golden-yellow, semi-double or double flowers coming continuously from July through September. Divide clumps every 3 years in spring or fall to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

plant_note: Direct sow in place; tolerates poor dry soil well.
directions: Direct sow this western native in early spring, barely covering the small seed. Farewell to spring produces spikes of ruffled pink to lavender flowers on slender upright stems that make good cut flowers. It prefers lean, dry soil and full sun, blooming as spring transitions into summer. Avoid overwatering and fertilizing, which reduce flowering, and let plants self-sow for future displays.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow in warm soil.
directions: Start seed indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow in warm soil, spacing plants about a foot apart. Fata Morgana offers soft, ruffled pincushion blooms in shades of peach and apricot on slender stems, lovely in borders, cottage gardens, and bouquets. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep the long-lasting flowers coming and to draw pollinators. Give it full sun and well-drained soil.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors a few weeks early.
directions: Direct sow after frost or start indoors 4-6 weeks early, spacing compact plants 8-10 inches apart. These single-row-petaled French marigolds make pollen easy for bees to reach, and their tidy, full habit shines at the front of a bed or tucked among vegetables. They grow and flower quickly with continuous blooms from spring to frost. Deadhead for tidiness and prolonged flowering, and water at the base.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring; 'Karl Foerster' is sterile and must be propagated by division.
directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in average, moisture-retentive soil; 'Karl Foerster' feather reed grass is one of the most reliable and widely adapted ornamental grasses, tolerating clay soil, humidity, and cold winters that defeat many others. Strongly upright, narrow clumps emerge early in spring with feathery bronze-pink plumes appearing in early summer that fade to wheat-gold and persist through winter. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter or early spring before new growth begins. Divide every 4–5 years in spring when the center begins to die out.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade; fetterbush requires very acidic, waterlogged conditions and will not thrive in neutral or alkaline soil.
directions: Plant in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; fetterbush is a native evergreen shrub of pine flatwoods, pocosins, and bog margins across the coastal South, producing glossy, leathery, sharply ribbed leaves (the midrib runs parallel to the leaf margin — a key identification feature) and small pink to white urn-shaped flowers in early spring that are critically important for native mining bees and early bumblebee queens. The plant naturally grows in waterlogged, very acidic soil and is adapted to conditions that are difficult for most other plants. An outstanding native plant for bog gardens, wet acid borders, and naturally moist sandy soils in the Southeast. All parts are toxic to livestock and humans if ingested.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: Direct sow the large seed about half an inch deep after frost, since nasturtiums dislike root disturbance from transplanting. Fiesta Blend brings spicy edible flowers in orange, yellow, and orange-peach bicolors that brighten hanging baskets, containers, and garden walls. Both the peppery blossoms and leaves are edible and prized in salads. Grow in lean soil and go easy on fertilizer, as rich soil favors foliage over flowers.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in a sheltered, partly shaded spot; cold stratification for 30 days improves germination. Division of crowns in fall provides faster establishment.
directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky to loamy, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; fire pink naturally grows on rocky woodland slopes and bluffs, where its brilliant scarlet flowers from spring through early summer are among the most vivid colors in the eastern woodland garden. The tubular, five-petaled flowers are perfectly shaped for ruby-throated hummingbirds and are a critical food source for hummingbirds moving through the mid-Atlantic states in spring. This plant is short-lived (3–4 years) but self-sows reliably to maintain a colony. Unlike most Silene, it performs best with afternoon shade rather than full sun. Combine with wild columbine and native ferns for a stunning spring vignette.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, spacing tall plants about a foot apart for airflow. Fireball Blend delivers intense 4-5 inch blooms that are stunning at the back of a bed or planted in mass. The long-lasting flowers hold well both on the plant and in the vase, so cut and deadhead often to keep them coming until frost. Water at the base and space generously to limit powdery mildew.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; firebush is a Florida and Gulf Coast native that quickly reaches shrub size and blooms nonstop in summer heat.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full sun; firebush tolerates heat, drought, and poor soils once established and reblooms continuously from late spring through frost. The tubular orange-red flowers are magnets for hummingbirds and butterflies, while the small black fruits that follow attract songbirds — making it one of the most wildlife-productive native shrubs for Gulf Coast gardens. In zone 8–9 the top freezes back in winter, but the plant regrows vigorously from the base each spring; cut dead stems to the ground once new growth begins. Native firebush (var. patens) is superior to the commonly sold 'Compacta' cultivar for wildlife value and cold hardiness.

plant_note: Nick seed and direct sow after frost.
directions: Nick the seed coat and direct sow firecracker vine after the last frost in warm soil. This twining morning glory relative bears small scarlet trumpet flowers with yellow throats that draw hummingbirds. Provide a fence or trellis in full sun and average soil, and it will climb quickly through summer. Deadheading is unnecessary, but remove faded flowers if you wish to limit self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.
directions: Firefly is a compact bush honeysuckle prized for its glowing amber-to-gold foliage and small yellow flowers borne over a long season. Adaptable and tough, it grows in full sun to part shade and tolerates poor, dry, or rocky soils once established. Its dense habit and spreading roots make it useful for erosion control on slopes and banks. Prune in late winter or early spring to renew and control size, since it blooms on new wood. The nectar-rich flowers attract bees and other pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost, barely covering the seed, and thin plants to 12-18 inches apart. Fizzy White is a show-stopper with 2.5-4 inch semi-double pure white blooms of pleated, zigzag-edged petals around fluffy tufted centers. This heat-loving annual attracts butterflies and other pollinators and makes an airy cut flower. Grow in lean soil with modest water, since rich, wet conditions favor foliage over flowers; deadhead to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in dappled shade; does not tolerate transplanting well once established — site carefully.
directions: Plant in moist, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade from taller deciduous trees; flame azalea is the most spectacular of the native deciduous azaleas, producing brilliant orange, red, and yellow flowers (often in multiple hues on the same plant) in late spring that blaze against the emerging green foliage at the peak of the Appalachian wildflower season. The flower display can range from pale yellow to deep orange-red depending on the individual plant's genetics — purchase in bloom to get the color you want. Plant with the crown above soil level and mulch lightly with pine bark or oak leaves to maintain acidity. Prune only lightly after bloom to shape.

plant_note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil with room for a small tree.
directions: Flame Thrower is a redbud selection celebrated for multicolored foliage that emerges burgundy and matures through orange and yellow to green, plus rosy-red spring flowers on bare branches. Plant it in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil, giving the best color in a spot with some sun. Water regularly during the first couple of seasons to establish its roots. Prune lightly after flowering only to shape or remove dead wood. The early blooms are a valuable nectar source for pollinators.

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after frost, or direct sow in warm soil.
directions: Start indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow in warm soil, spacing plants about a foot apart. This wheat celosia adds a vertical element with rosy-pink, soft, textured flower spikes that invite a touch and shine in beds and large containers. It loves heat and full sun and makes an excellent fresh or dried cut flower. Water evenly and harvest spikes for drying when fully colored.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; excellent as a specimen, screen, or hedge under tree canopy.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Florida anise tree is one of the finest native evergreen shrubs for deep shade in the Deep South, producing lustrous dark green aromatic foliage (strongly anise-scented when crushed) that forms a dense, refined screen under mature trees. The unusual maroon-red star-shaped flowers in spring are striking at close range though small. It grows naturally in moist, shaded ravine heads and stream banks in the Florida Panhandle and adjacent states. An excellent substitute for the invasive Japanese anise tree. All parts of the plant are toxic; it is not related to culinary anise despite the scent. Performs best with consistent moisture and mulch to keep roots cool.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; keep the crown at or above soil level and mulch to maintain moisture and acidity.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Florida azalea is one of the earliest and most fragrant of the native deciduous azaleas, producing clusters of brilliant golden-yellow to orange-yellow tubular flowers with exserted stamens in early spring before or with the emerging leaves. The fragrance is intensely sweet and carries far in the still spring air. It is native to the wooded ravines and stream banks of the Florida Panhandle, south Georgia, and Alabama and is one of the best native azaleas for Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens, tolerating the heat and humidity better than northern species. Plant as a foundation shrub, in a native garden, or massed along a woodland edge. Prune only immediately after bloom if needed; never shear. The color is exceptionally vivid when purchased in bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; succession sow for continuous cutting.
directions: Direct sow after frost in full sun, spacing closer at 6 inches for single stems or wider for branching plants. This florist mix blends apricot, lemon, peach, and gold shades from named cultivars, offering both single-stemmed and branching types for varied bouquets. Sow successively every couple weeks for a continuous cutting supply. Cut when the first petals lift and water evenly while establishing.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface sow tiny seed on moist starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed is extremely small and needs light to germinate. Nicotiana tolerates partial shade better than most annuals and performs well in dappled light. Fragrant varieties release scent primarily in the evening, making them ideal planted near a patio or window. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a support.
directions: Nick the hard seed coat and soak overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or fence. Flying Saucer bears 5-inch white blooms streaked with blue pinwheel stripes and occasional flecks, each flower slightly different, on vigorous vines to 10 feet. On cloudy days the flowers stay open all day, and the heart-shaped leaves lend a tropical look. Note that parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost with a cold stratification period; or plant divisions in spring or fall.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; foamflower spreads by stolons to form a weed-suppressing ground cover with attractive, deeply lobed, often patterned foliage. Frothy spikes of tiny white to pink flowers appear in mid-spring above the foliage and last for 4–6 weeks. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain vigor. Many named cultivars with exceptional leaf markings are available and are best propagated by division to maintain cultivar characteristics.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; avoid planting too deeply — the crown should rest at soil level.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade, mimicking its native habitat on the forest floor; foamflower produces mounded clumps of deeply lobed, often burgundy-marked foliage and sends up airy white flower spikes in mid-spring that give the plant its common name. Running forms spread by stolons to colonize an area; clump-forming varieties stay tighter. Modern cultivars (many under the Heucherella group when crossed with Heuchera) offer extraordinary foliage variation. Pair with ferns, hostas, and wild ginger for a classic woodland floor combination. Plants dislike summer drought — mulch to retain moisture and site in a spot with consistent moisture.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to early fall so plants overwinter as rosettes and bloom in spring alongside tulips and daffodils.
directions: Scatter seeds on the soil surface or barely cover them; they germinate best in cool conditions and seedlings overwinter as low rosettes in mild climates. Forget-me-nots self-sow prolifically once established — let plants go to seed, then pull them after flowering to control spread. The sky-blue flowers are traditionally combined with spring bulbs in beds and create a charming filler under shrubs in partial shade. Remove spent plants when temperatures rise, as they become straggly and die back in summer heat.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; self-sows readily in the garden.
directions: Chinese forget-me-not produces soft clouds of tiny sky-blue flowers that suit borders and cottage plantings. Scatter seeds directly on prepared soil in early spring and press them in lightly, keeping the bed evenly moist until seedlings appear. This easy annual tolerates partial shade and often self-sows to return in following seasons. Cut a few stems for small bouquets, and shear lightly after the first flush to encourage more bloom.

plant_note: Sow after frost; soaking hard seeds overnight speeds sprouting.
directions: Four o'clocks earn their name by opening fragrant, trumpet-shaped blooms in the late afternoon and evening in a lively mix of colors. Soak the hard seeds overnight and sow after frost in a warm, sunny spot where the fast-growing bushy plants can fill out. The evening flowers attract moths and hummingbirds, and the plants form tuberous roots that can be lifted and stored in cold regions. In mild zones they return each year and often self-sow.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; a brief chill before sowing can improve germination.
directions: Direct sow after frost, barely covering seed; chilling the seed in the fridge for a week beforehand can boost germination. Fountain Blend forms tall plants topped with large clusters of fragrant spider flowers in white, pink, rose, and violet, with interesting seed pods and palmate leaves. It attracts butterflies and hummingbirds and needs little care once established. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and expect some self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; can also start indoors 4–6 weeks early.
directions: Sow seed 1/4–1/2 in deep in a warm, sunny location after the last frost. Four o'clocks form a tuberous root and tolerate drought well once established. Flowers open in late afternoon and remain open through the following morning, releasing a sweet fragrance that attracts sphinx moths. Plants self-sow prolifically and the fleshy roots can be dug and stored in frost-prone areas.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seeds need light and should not be covered.
directions: Press seeds onto the surface of moist potting mix under bright light — do not cover, as light aids germination. Foxgloves are biennial or short-lived perennials; most produce a rosette in year 1 and the spectacular tall spikes in year 2 before setting seed and dying. Cutting the main spike after bloom often stimulates smaller side spikes for additional flowers, and self-sowing keeps the planting going indefinitely. All parts of the plant are highly toxic; use caution and wash hands thoroughly after handling.

plant_note: Plant the spider-like fleshy roots in fall, spreading them horizontally 4 in deep in a well-drained, sunny site.
directions: Spread the octopus-like fleshy roots horizontally over a mound of gritty soil, crown just below the surface, with perfect drainage — wet winter soil rots the crowns quickly. The strap-like foliage emerges in early spring and can be killed by a late frost; a light cover of straw protects emerging growth in zones 5–6. Spectacular bottle-brush spikes up to 8 ft tall erupt in late spring to early summer in shades of white, yellow, orange, and pink. Leaves die back after bloom, so interplant with later-emerging perennials to fill the gap.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant sumac is one of the best native shrubs for dry, shaded slopes and is excellent for erosion control.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant sumac is one of the most versatile, adaptable, and underused native shrubs in North America — tolerating dry shade, rocky slopes, alkaline soil, and drought conditions that defeat most plants. The crushed leaves have a distinctly pleasant lemon-citrus fragrance. Small yellow flowers in early spring before the leaves emerge provide important early nectar for native bees emerging from overwintering. The fuzzy red fruit clusters feed dozens of bird species through fall and winter. The three-parted leaves turn brilliant orange-red and burgundy in fall — among the best native plants for fall color — and the low, spreading form makes it excellent for groundcover use on slopes. The cultivar 'Gro-Low' is a compact, 2-foot-tall groundcover form widely used in landscape planting.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shady soil.
directions: Francee is a classic shade hosta with dark green leaves crisply edged in white, forming a tidy mounded clump and sending up lavender flower spikes in summer. Plant it in partial to full shade in rich, moist, well-drained soil, as too much sun scorches the variegated foliage. Keep the soil evenly moist and mulch to retain moisture and suppress weeds. Divide clumps in spring every few years to propagate or reduce crowding. Watch for slugs, which can chew the leaves, especially in damp conditions.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in frost-free areas (zones 9–10) or start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost in cold climates.
directions: Plant corms 2 in deep and 2–3 in apart in gritty, well-drained soil; freesias resent wet conditions especially after bloom. The fragrant, funnel-shaped flowers on arching stems need staking or a grow-through support as stems are slender. In frost-free gardens the corms are perennial and naturalize freely; in colder climates lift after foliage dies, dry thoroughly, and store in a cool dry place. Corms multiply quickly — divide every 2–3 years to maintain bloom quality.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix. Pinch out the first flower bud to encourage bushier branching and heavier bloom set. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep plants producing through summer. Blooms appear approximately 6–8 weeks after transplant and continue until frost.

plant_note: Start indoors for early bloom or direct sow after frost.
directions: This French marigold mix stays compact at about a foot tall with bright, two- to three-inch double blooms in gold, orange, yellow, and red. Sow seeds shallowly after the last frost or start indoors for earlier color, and place in full sun. The tidy mounded plants edge beds neatly and pair well with vegetables to help repel pests. Deadhead often for continuous flowering right up to fall frost.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.
directions: Frill Ride is a bigleaf hydrangea bearing bold mophead blooms with distinctive frilled petal edges in shades that shift with soil pH. Plant it in morning sun and afternoon shade in rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil, mulching to keep roots cool. Acidic soil pushes blooms toward blue while alkaline soil yields pink, so amend to steer the color you want. Prune just after flowering, since it blooms mainly on old wood. Water generously in heat to prevent the large leaves and flowers from wilting.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; also sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification. Plants may also be divided in fall.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; fringed bleeding heart is the native eastern species with deeply cut, ferny blue-green foliage and pendant rosy-pink heart-shaped flowers that bloom from late spring through fall — an exceptionally long season for a shade perennial, unlike the common garden bleeding heart (Dicentra spectabilis) which dies back completely after spring bloom. It is native to rocky woodland slopes in the Appalachians from New York to Georgia. The foliage may die back partially in summer heat but rebounds with fall cooling. It spreads slowly by self-seeding to naturalize a shaded bed and is an outstanding companion for hostas, ferns, and native wildflowers. All parts are toxic if ingested.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; transplant while cool, or sow in late summer for fall and spring bloom.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before setting out, covering it since pansies germinate best in darkness, and transplant while weather is cool. Frizzle Sizzle Lemonberry features ruffled 2 to 2.5-inch tricolor blooms of lemon, deep burgundy, and berry-purple edges that intensify in cool spring temperatures. These low-growing edible flowers suit beds, borders, and containers. Deadhead to prolong blooming and provide even moisture, as they fade in summer heat.

plant_note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained soil in a sheltered spot.
directions: Frost Proof is a cold-tolerant gardenia bearing intensely fragrant white flowers against glossy evergreen leaves, and it withstands cooler temperatures better than typical gardenias. Plant it in morning sun with afternoon shade in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Keep watering consistent, as fluctuating moisture causes bud drop. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer during the growing season and prune lightly after the main bloom. Protect from harsh winter wind at the colder edge of its range.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis.
directions: Nick and soak the seed overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or screen. This easy vine climbs with delicate ferny foliage and produces small tubular blooms that stay open all day and attract hummingbirds. Give it full sun and a support to twine up, watering while young until established. Most parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; purchase only from nursery-propagated sources — galax has been over-collected from the wild for the floral trade and is declining in many areas.
directions: Plant in moist, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; galax is one of the premier evergreen groundcovers for deep shade in acidic woodland gardens. The round, glossy, leathery leaves form a dense, weed-suppressing carpet that turns brilliant bronze-wine-red through fall and winter before returning to rich green in spring — providing bold, year-round color in the shade garden. The small white flowers on tall wands in late spring are attractive to bees. It grows naturally on the rocky, acidic woodland slopes of the Southern Appalachians, where it is sometimes called 'coltsfoot.' Galax leaves are highly prized in the floral industry and are heavily wild-harvested — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. Spreads slowly but persistently by rhizome to form colonies.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil before the last frost, or in fall in mild climates; seed benefits from chilling.
directions: Direct sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last frost, or in fall where winters are mild; chilling the seed improves germination. This memorable cut flower blooms from late spring into early summer in carmine, lilac, salmon, blue, pink, and white, adding height at the back of the bed. It reseeds readily and prefers cool conditions. Note that all parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so site it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring, 2–6 weeks after the last frost, for fall bloom.
directions: Plant in spring into fertile, well-drained soil; pinch growing tips every 2–3 weeks from planting until midsummer (around July 4) to develop compact, well-branched plants with dozens of buds. Mums are short-day plants that begin flower bud formation as nights lengthen; stop pinching by midsummer to avoid disrupting this process. Mulch heavily after the first hard frost to improve winter hardiness in colder zones. Divide annually in spring to rejuvenate the planting and maintain plant vigor.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface-sow as light is needed, and set out after frost.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost, pressing the tiny seed onto the surface since light is required for germination. Garden Party Blend produces large 3-inch ruffled floribunda blooms in abundance all season on mounding, upright plants that draw butterflies. Set out after frost in full sun in beds or containers around the patio. Deadhead and feed lightly to keep the colorful display going into fall.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth emerges or in early fall for establishment before frost.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil with good air circulation to reduce powdery mildew, which is the plant's main problem. Thin stems to 5–7 per clump in spring to improve airflow and produce larger flower trusses on the remaining stems. Deadhead the central flower cluster as it fades to encourage lateral buds to open and extend the bloom season. Divide every 3 years in spring, replanting only the outermost, most vigorous sections.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in loose, well-drained germination mix; plants develop a deep taproot early so pot on before roots become crowded. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil — gaura thrives on neglect and declines in fertile, wet conditions. The wand-like stems bearing butterfly-shaped white or pink flowers sway gracefully all season without deadheading. Cut plants back to 6 in after the first heavy bloom flush to encourage a tidy second flush through autumn.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; they germinate readily at 65–75°F and grow quickly into compact transplants. Plant in sharply drained, even poor soil in the hottest, sunniest location available — gazania excels in sandy or gravelly beds where other plants fail. Flowers close at night and on overcast days, but modern hybrid varieties stay open longer than older types. Drought tolerance is excellent once established; overwatering and heavy clay soils are the most common causes of failure.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds vertically, pointed end down, barely covered, as they are sensitive to orientation; maintain 70–75°F for germination. Plant so the crown sits slightly above soil level to prevent crown rot, which is the most common cause of plant failure. Water at the base rather than overhead; gerberas prefer a dry-wet-dry cycle rather than constantly moist soil. Deadhead spent flowers by cutting the stem to the base to encourage continuous bloom from spring through fall in mild climates.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs in fall at a depth of 3–4 times the bulb diameter (typically 6–8 in) in well-drained soil.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall, pointed side up, at 3–4 times their diameter in depth; good drainage is essential as bulbs rot in waterlogged soil. The bold, softball-sized purple globes atop tall stems create dramatic vertical accents in late spring to early summer borders. Foliage, which smells of onion when crushed, often begins to yellow before flowers are fully open — conceal the base with later-rising perennials. Allow seedheads to remain for architectural interest; deadhead if you wish to prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Surface sow seed in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; seeds require cold stratification so fall sowing or winter sowing is preferred. Giant ironweed naturalizes readily in moist soils and can be aggressive — allow 4–6 feet of space.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; giant ironweed is one of the most dramatic native wildflowers for the back of a large border or naturalized area, growing 5–10 feet tall with flat-topped clusters of intense royal purple flowers in late summer and fall that are irresistible to monarchs, painted ladies, giant swallowtails, and dozens of native bee species. The vivid purple color is one of the most saturated of any native wildflower. It grows naturally along stream banks, moist woodland edges, and roadside ditches throughout the eastern US and is one of the most reliable and showy native plants for late-season pollinator value. The tall stems and architectural seed heads provide winter interest and bird food. It spreads by rhizome in moist soil and should be given space or divided annually to control spread in the border.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, spacing tall plants about a foot apart for good airflow. Giant Purple bears bright 4-6 inch blooms on long, sturdy stems that make excellent long-lasting cut flowers. It is heat tolerant and disease resistant, blooming endlessly until frost when cut and deadheaded. Water at the base and space generously to reduce powdery mildew.

plant_note: Plant corms after the last frost; make successive plantings every 2 weeks through early summer for extended bloom.
directions: Plant corms 4–6 in deep in well-drained soil with the pointed end up after the last frost; succession plant every 2 weeks through midsummer for continuous cut flowers. Tall varieties need staking; a single bamboo cane tied loosely to the stem works well. In zones below 7, dig corms after frost blackens the foliage, dry for 2–3 weeks, remove offsets (cormels), and store at 35–45°F in mesh bags through winter. Harvest cut spikes when the lowest 2–3 buds show color for longest vase life.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants typically bloom in their second year from seed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; cold stratification is not required but may speed germination. Established globe thistle is extremely drought-tolerant and thrives in poor, sandy, or chalky well-drained soil — fertile, moist soil causes lax growth and flopping. The spiky steel-blue globe flowers are excellent for fresh cutting and dry perfectly for everlasting arrangements if cut just before fully open. Divide clumps in spring every 4–5 years using a sharp spade; they are slow to re-establish so divisions should be generous.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs in fall for dramatic late-spring globes.
directions: Plant Globemaster allium bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Each stout stem produces a massive violet globe up to ten inches across that lasts for weeks and draws countless bees. Position the plants among perennials that hide the fading foliage, which yellows before the flowers finish. The dried seed heads make striking architectural accents in the garden or in arrangements.

plant_note: Plant tubers horizontally 4 in deep after last frost; in zones 8+ can be planted in early spring once soil is 65°F.
directions: Plant the fragile, finger-shaped tubers horizontally 4 in deep, handling carefully to avoid snapping them; provide a trellis or support as the vine climbs by leaf-tip tendrils. The striking reflexed flowers in flame-red and yellow with wavy-edged petals are produced freely from midsummer into fall. All parts of the plant are highly toxic if ingested — site away from children and pets. In zones below 8, dig tubers after foliage dies, dry them, and store in dry peat moss at 50–60°F over winter.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs 3 in deep in fall; naturalize in lawns, rock gardens, or under deciduous trees.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall, 3 in deep and 2–3 in apart, in any well-drained soil; glory-of-the-snow blooms very early, often pushing through snow in late winter to early spring. The vivid blue, star-shaped flowers with white centers naturalize freely by both offsets and self-seeding, forming large colonies over time. Plant in large drifts under deciduous trees where they receive full sun while leafless and will not be disturbed during summer dormancy. No special care is needed once established; bulbs rarely need dividing.

plant_note: Surface-sow since light aids germination; as a biennial it flowers the second year.
directions: Surface-sow spring through midsummer since light aids germination, thinning plants to 12-18 inches. This sturdy biennial produces foliage the first year and tall spikes of extra-large gloxinia-like blooms the second year, and it self-sows readily for naturalized areas. It performs best in part shade with rich, moist, well-drained soil. Note that all parts of foxglove are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Sow seeds on the surface (needs light to germinate) in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; goatsbeard is dioecious — plant multiple plants for the best display, as male plants produce slightly more dramatic flower plumes.
directions: Sow on the soil surface in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in early spring; goatsbeard is one of the most magnificent native shade perennials in North America, forming large, bold clumps of compound, ferny leaves topped in early to midsummer by massive, arching plumes of creamy-white flowers resembling a giant astilbe — earning it the common name. Male plants produce the showiest, most upright plumes; female plants produce somewhat more pendulous plumes followed by seed heads that dry attractively. It grows naturally in moist, rich woodland slopes and creek banks throughout the eastern US and Pacific Northwest, performing best in cool, moist, shaded conditions. An outstanding bold-textured plant for the large-scale woodland garden or shaded border, paired with hostas, ferns, and native wildflowers. Not suited to hot, dry climates; performs best in zone 3–7.

plant_note: Surface-sow as light aids germination; blooms the first year from an early start.
directions: Surface-sow, pressing seed onto the mix since light aids germination, and thin compact plants to about a foot apart. Goblin is a first-year-flowering, compact perennial with red and yellow bicolored blooms that keep coming from summer through frost and attract butterflies. Round, reddish-brown seed heads follow the petals and add interest. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil and tolerates heat and drought once established.

plant_note: Sow in place as soon as soil is workable; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow godetia into lean, well-drained soil in early spring, barely covering the fine seed. It resents rich soil and heavy feeding, which produce foliage at the expense of the satiny cup-shaped blooms. Thin seedlings to 8-12 inches and keep evenly moist until established, then water sparingly. Cool weather intensifies the pink and lavender flower colors, so it performs best in spring and early summer before extreme heat.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; seed requires 60–90 days of cold, moist conditions before spring germination. Division of established clumps in spring provides faster establishment.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall or early spring in moist, average to rich, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; this early-blooming native carrot-family plant produces flat-topped clusters of small yellow flowers in April–May, making it one of the earliest sources of pollen for native bees, beetles, and flies at a time when little else is blooming. It is also an important early host plant for black swallowtail butterflies, whose caterpillars feed on the ferny foliage. The plants are short-lived but self-sow reliably in suitable conditions. Excellent at the front of the native plant border or naturalized in a moist meadow.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in early spring in moist, shaded areas; spreads by stolons and self-sows to form dense colonies in favorable conditions.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full shade to partial sun; golden groundsel is one of the few native plants that blooms brilliantly in deep shade, producing bright yellow daisy-like flowers in early spring that provide critical early pollen for native mining bees. The semi-evergreen, kidney-shaped basal leaves form an attractive groundcover year-round. It spreads aggressively in moist shade by stolons and self-seeding — excellent for naturalizing a problem wet-shade area but may need management in a manicured garden. Plants in dry shade will grow more slowly and remain smaller. Combine with wild blue phlox and Virginia bluebells for a spring shade spectacle.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring where plants are to grow; poppies resent transplanting.
directions: This California poppy carries sunny yellow petals with warm orange centers on wispy, blue-green foliage. Sow seeds directly where they are to grow, since the taproot dislikes being moved, and simply press them into the soil surface in early spring. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained ground, where it thrives with little water once established. It self-sows freely and naturalizes beautifully in meadows and dry banks.

plant_note: Sow seed in early spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; the golden cultivar 'Aureum' (Bowles' Golden Grass) seeds true to color and naturalizes readily in appropriate shaded, moist conditions.
directions: Sow seed in early spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; golden wood millet — especially the cultivar 'Aureum' (Bowles' Golden Grass) — is one of the most luminous and valuable shade plants available, producing tufts of graceful, arching bright gold-yellow leaves that seem to glow with internal light in the darkest garden corners. The delicate, open-branched flower panicles appear in spring with golden seeds carried on wispy threads, adding airy texture. A cool-season grass that peaks in spring, goes semi-dormant in summer heat, then revives in fall. It self-sows generously (the golden form seeds true) to naturalize in moist, shaded woodland gardens. Cut back in late summer if it looks tired. Combines beautifully with blue hostas, spring wildflowers, and ferns where its golden light provides striking contrast in deep shade.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; clump-forming cultivars are better-behaved than the straight species.
directions: Plant in full sun in average to poor, well-drained soil; goldenrod thrives in lean conditions and becomes overly lax and floppy in rich, moist soil. The arching plumes of golden-yellow flowers from late summer through fall are essential late-season food for monarch butterflies and dozens of native bee species. Contrary to popular belief, goldenrod does not cause hay fever — its pollen is heavy and insect-carried, not windborne. Choose named compact cultivars like 'Fireworks' or 'Little Lemon' for well-behaved garden varieties; divide every 2–3 years to control spread.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. Goldy Honey Bear has thick, multi-branching stalks that stay upright without staking and open to reveal green centers that mature into fully double, solid-gold 5-7 inch blooms. Its nearly pollenless flowers are ideal for clean-cut bouquets. Water while young and cut regularly to encourage more of the branching blooms.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; soak seed overnight to speed germination. Gomphrena thrives in heat and humidity and is highly drought tolerant once established. The clover-like flowers hold their color and shape exceptionally well when dried — harvest stems just as the heads reach full color. Deadheading encourages continuous fresh bloom throughout the summer.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness and transplant while cool, or sow late summer for fall and spring color.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before setting out, covering it since pansies germinate best in darkness, and transplant while weather is cool. This custom mix brightens the garden with light and dark blue blooms and provides color for spring and fall even in cold climates. The edible flowers are lovely as a garnish on soft cheese or baked goods. Deadhead to prolong bloom and keep the soil evenly moist.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; needs a long season to mature and cure.
directions: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, giving the vines plenty of room or a sturdy trellis to sprawl. This playful blend yields petite festive gourds in warty, smooth, striped, and bumpy shapes in vibrant orange and green, perfect for fall decorating and crafts. Allow the gourds to fully mature on the vine, then cure the hard shells so they last for years. Water evenly during fruit set and keep foliage dry to limit mildew.

plant_note: Nick and soak seeds, then direct sow once soil is warm.
directions: This beloved heirloom climbs quickly to produce large, velvety purple trumpets marked with a striking star-shaped center. Nick the hard seed coats and soak overnight, then sow directly against a trellis or fence once the soil is warm. Blooms open in the morning and feed pollinators, with heart-shaped foliage covering the support all season. Provide sturdy vertical structure, as vines can reach ten feet or more.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a support.
directions: Nick the seed coat and soak overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a fence or trellis. This dependable heirloom is a vigorous climber with deep purple blooms and heart-shaped leaves that cover supports, trail on the ground, or spill from baskets. It may self-sow, so remove spent flowers if you want to limit volunteers. Note that parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 3–4 in deep; foliage emerges in fall and overwinters, so plant early enough for roots to establish.
directions: Plant small bulbs 3–4 in deep in any well-drained soil; foliage emerges in fall and overwinters as a low ground cover, with tight clusters of cobalt-blue, urn-shaped flowers appearing in early to mid-spring. Muscari naturalizes aggressively by offsets and self-seeding and can spread widely — site where this is desirable or divide clumps every few years in fall to control spread. Excellent planted beneath tulips and daffodils to create complementary color combinations. Deer and rodents generally leave them alone.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, poor to average soil in full sun; gray goldenrod is the most drought-tolerant of the common eastern goldenrods, thriving in dry, sandy, and rocky soils where other goldenrods cannot establish.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained, dry to average, poor to average soil in full sun — gray goldenrod is the most drought-tolerant of the common eastern North American goldenrods, taking its common name from the grayish, downy-hairy stems and leaves that help reduce water loss in its characteristic dry, open habitats: rocky outcrops, sandy barrens, dry roadsides, and dry open woodland edges throughout the eastern US and into the Great Plains. The one-sided arching racemes of golden yellow flowers curve gracefully toward the ground in late summer and fall, providing important late-season nectar for migrating monarch butterflies and a wide range of native bees. While not as showy as some taller goldenrods, gray goldenrod is invaluable for difficult dry, sandy, or rocky sites where creating a pollinator-supporting native planting is otherwise challenging. Historically used by Native American nations as a yellow dye plant.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; cold stratification for 30 days improves germination rate. Plants may bloom the first year from an early sowing.
directions: Sow in well-drained, average to poor, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; the gray-headed coneflower is a tall, drought-tolerant prairie native with drooping yellow petals reflexed away from a distinctive elongated gray-brown cone, giving it an unmistakable silhouette in the summer meadow. It blooms from midsummer through fall and spreads gently by self-seeding without becoming aggressive. The crushed foliage smells pleasantly of anise. An important pollen source for prairie bees and the seed is taken by goldfinches in fall. Mixes beautifully with other tall prairie plants like goldenrod and ironweed in naturalistic plantings.

plant_note: Surface-sow seed (do not cover — light required for germination) 4–6 weeks before last frost; alternatively divide rosettes in early spring from established plants.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface — do not cover, as light is essential for germination — in consistently moist, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun. This native provides brilliant blue flower spikes in late summer when most other plants are winding down, bridging the gap between midsummer and fall bloomers. It naturally grows in wet meadows, stream banks, and woodland edges and is one of the best plants for rain gardens, retention areas, and moist shade gardens. The plant is monocarpic (dies after flowering) but reliably self-sows in favorable conditions, and new basal rosettes can be separated and replanted in fall or early spring. Excellent companion for cardinal flower (L. cardinalis) in moist areas.

plant_note: Soak tubers overnight before planting in fall.
directions: Soak the small knobby tubers overnight, then plant Grecian windflower in fall about two to three inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil. The daisy-like flowers in blue, pink, or white carpet the ground in early spring beneath deciduous trees and shrubs. It naturalizes freely in sun to partial shade and goes dormant by early summer. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand year after year.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; green dragon is closely related to jack-in-the-pulpit but differs in having a single large compound leaf and a dramatically elongated spadix protruding 3–6 inches from the spathe.
directions: Plant corms in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — green dragon is the less-commonly-grown native relative of jack-in-the-pulpit, equally bizarre and fascinating in flower but with a different structure: a single large compound leaf (7–15 leaflets radiating from the tip of the stem) and a distinctive flower featuring a narrow, elongated spadix that projects 3–6 inches beyond the green spathe like the tongue of a dragon — giving both the common name and the specific epithet dracontium. The bright red berries clustered on the spadix in late summer are strikingly ornamental. It grows naturally in moist, rich, shaded bottomland forests and stream banks throughout the eastern US. The corms and all parts of the plant contain calcium oxalate crystals and are toxic if consumed raw; like jack-in-the-pulpit, historically used by Native American nations after careful preparation to neutralize the crystals. Outstanding bold-textured native for shaded, moist woodland gardens.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil around the last frost; it dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow in cool soil around the last frost since ammi resents transplanting, barely covering the seed. Green Mist produces large rounded umbels that start bold green and turn white as the tiny flowers open, a favorite filler for mixed bouquets. Also called bishop's flower or false Queen Anne's lace, it is nearly carefree and attracts pollinators. Provide full sun and steady moisture, and cut stems for the vase as blooms open.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring once the soil is workable; foliage emerges after the last frost.
directions: Green Patriot is a shade-loving perennial grown for its bold foliage of dark-green centers edged in lighter margins, forming a tidy mound. Plant in partial to full shade in rich, moisture-retentive soil and keep it evenly watered. Lavender summer flowers rise above the leaves and attract pollinators. Cut back spent foliage in fall and divide clumps every few years in early spring to maintain vigor; watch for slugs.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in partial shade; space at 12 inches for coverage within one full growing season as plants spread by both stolons and seed.
directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; green and gold is one of the finest native groundcovers for zone 8–9 southern gardens, producing bright yellow daisy flowers from early spring through late fall — an extraordinarily long bloom season for a shade plant. The semi-evergreen foliage forms a dense, weed-suppressing mat that spreads by stolons and self-sows without becoming invasive. It tolerates the root competition and dry summer shade under mature trees better than most groundcovers. The northern ecotype (var. virginianum) has a more upright habit, while the southern ecotype (var. australe) is more prostrate and heat tolerant — choose the southern ecotype for zone 8–9 gardens.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; tolerates light frost and cool weather.
directions: This rare heirloom calendula opens warm gold, peach, and orange blooms each accented by an unusual lime-green center. Sow seeds directly in early spring, covering lightly since they germinate best in darkness, and thin to give plants room. This cool-season annual tolerates light frost and blooms best before summer heat, so successive sowings extend the show. The edible petals brighten salads and the plant has a long history of medicinal use; deadhead often to prolong flowering.

plant_note: Set out the potted or bare-root shrub after hard frost; this named cultivar is grown from plants, not seed.
directions: Plant this shrub rose in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, spacing plants 2-3 feet apart for their rounded habit. Gumball Goody blooms from spring to fall in a candy mix of light pink, deep pink, and yellow on a compact, tidy plant well suited to borders and containers. Water deeply and regularly during establishment and the growing season, keeping foliage dry to limit disease. Prune in late winter to shape and remove dead wood, and deadhead to encourage repeat flushes.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is the most heat-and-humidity-tolerant native Heuchera species and outperforms H. americana and H. sanguinea cultivars in zone 7–9 summer heat.
