directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is the native Heuchera of the Southern Appalachians and Ozarks and is significantly more tolerant of heat and humidity than other native Heuchera species — making it the top choice for shade gardens in zone 7–9 where summer temperatures are brutal. The large, maple-shaped leaves with dense soft hairs (giving a velvety texture) are handsome throughout the season, and small white flowers on 2–3 foot stems appear in late summer when little else in the shade garden is blooming. This species is the parent of many popular hybrid 'coral bells' cultivars, including 'Autumn Bride' (exceptionally heat-tolerant with large white flowers in fall). The foliage is semi-evergreen in zone 7–8 and provides attractive winter interest. Divide every 3–4 years as the crown heaves upward from the soil.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; hairy skullcap grows naturally in dry to moist hardwood forest understories and tolerates a range of shade from partial to fairly deep — one of the more adaptable native skullcaps.
directions: Sow seed in fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade — hairy skullcap is a native Scutellaria of eastern deciduous woodland, producing terminal clusters of blue-violet, 3/4-inch, two-lipped flowers on hairy stems 6–16 inches tall from late spring through early summer. The genus name comes from the small, helmet-shaped (scutellum) protrusion on the upper side of the calyx visible in fruit. Native bumblebees are the primary pollinators. Hairy skullcap grows naturally in dry to moist hardwood forest understories from New York south to Florida and west to the Midwest — one of the more shade-tolerant and drought-tolerant native skullcaps, able to perform in the challenging dry-shade conditions under mature deciduous trees. The basal and stem leaves are opposite and elliptic, distinctly hairy. An attractive and ecologically valuable small native for the shaded wildflower garden and native plant border.

plant_note: Plant tubers shallowly in late summer under trees.
directions: Plant hardy cyclamen tubers shallowly in late summer or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees. The dainty pink or white reflexed flowers appear in autumn, followed by beautifully marbled ivy-shaped leaves that persist through winter. It thrives in dry shade where little else grows and slowly self-sows to form colonies. Keep the tubers on the dry side during their summer dormancy and mulch lightly with leaf mold.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; tolerates a wide range of soil conditions once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in a location with morning sun and afternoon shade; hardy geraniums are adaptable to poor soils and are generally low-maintenance once established. Shear plants back by one-half after the initial bloom to refresh the foliage and encourage a second flush of flowers. The attractive lobed leaves often turn red or orange in fall, extending the ornamental season. Divide every 3–5 years in spring to reinvigorate the planting.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after nicking the seed coat and soaking 24 hours; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Nick or file seed coats and soak in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination rates significantly. Plant in full sun in moist, fertile soil — hardy hibiscus is native to wetlands and performs best with consistent moisture but tolerates average garden beds with regular irrigation. This is a late riser in spring; the shoots emerge much later than other perennials, so mark plants to avoid accidental disturbance. The dinner-plate-sized flowers in white, pink, and crimson open each morning and close by evening, with new blooms produced daily from midsummer through frost.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; slow to establish from division but once settled forms a dense weed-suppressing mat. Purchase only from nursery-propagated sources.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; heartleaf wild ginger is the evergreen southeastern counterpart to the deciduous Canadian wild ginger (Asarum canadense), forming a dense, low mat of glossy, evergreen heart-shaped leaves that is one of the finest shade groundcovers available for southern gardens. The curious brown jug-shaped flowers (earning the folk name 'little brown jugs') bloom at ground level in early spring, hidden beneath the leaves, and are pollinated by small ground-crawling insects. The aromatic rhizomes smell and taste of ginger. It colonizes slowly from rhizomes to fill a shaded bed over several years. Plant under oaks, maples, or at the base of large shrubs in woodland gardens. All parts mildly toxic if consumed in quantity.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a support.
directions: Nick the seed coat and soak overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or fence. This showy climber opens sky-blue flowers in the cool of morning that close in afternoon heat but stay open all day when cloudy or in fall. Give it full sun and a support to twine up, watering while young until established. Note that parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. This heirloom mix blends the sunny yellows of Lemon Queen, warm tones of Autumn Beauty, and deep reds of Velvet Queen in 4-8 inch multi-branching blooms. Because the plants branch, cut stems regularly to encourage a long succession of flowers for bouquets and pollinators. Water while establishing and stake tall stalks in exposed sites.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant only after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; germination is slow (14–28 days) and benefits from bottom heat at 70–80°F consistently. Transplant into rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; heat brings out the intensely vanilla-scented, deep purple flower clusters. Pinch tips once or twice before transplanting to produce bushier plants with more flowering heads. In zones 10–11 heliotrope is a shrubby perennial; elsewhere take cuttings in late summer to overwinter prized fragrant selections indoors.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall in humus-rich, well-drained shade; transplanted divisions establish more reliably than seed.
directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in well-drained, humus-rich soil in dappled to full shade — morning sun is tolerated, but afternoon sun causes leaf scorch. Hellebores bloom in late winter to early spring, often through snow, and the nodding flowers face downward; tipping the pot or planting on a slope allows a better view of the flower interior. Remove old foliage in late winter before new growth emerges to improve air circulation and reduce leaf spot diseases. Hellebores self-sow freely, and seedlings from hybrid plants will vary; divide named selections in early spring to maintain specific colors.

plant_note: Plant divisions or nursery-grown plants in early spring in humus-rich, slightly alkaline soil; purchase only nursery-propagated plants as wild collection is unsustainable and transplants rarely survive.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil (unlike most woodland plants, hepatica prefers near-neutral to mildly basic soil from calcareous bedrock) in full to dappled shade. One of the earliest spring wildflowers in eastern North America, the cup-shaped flowers in shades of white, pink, and lavender open in late February–March before any leaves appear, emerging through snow on fuzzy, insulating stems. The leathery, three-lobed leaves that develop after flowering persist through winter and often have beautiful mottled markings. Extremely slow-growing from seed (3–5 years to bloom); purchase only from ethical native plant nurseries. May live for decades undisturbed.

plant_note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring; sweet peas thrive in cool weather.
directions: Nick or soak the hard seeds overnight to speed germination, then sow directly 1 inch deep in cool, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. This tall vining variety produces intensely fragrant cream flowers edged in lavender, so provide a trellis or netting 5 to 6 feet tall for support. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut blooms frequently to prolong flowering. Plants fade in summer heat, so start them early where summers are hot.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out in cool spring weather for best bloom.
directions: Hoary stock, also called gillyflower, produces tall stems packed with intensely fragrant blooms in soft shades. Start seeds indoors and set young plants out in cool spring weather, as stock performs best in mild temperatures and dislikes heat. Grow in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and even moisture for the fullest flower spikes. Harvest stems for long-lasting, sweetly scented bouquets.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; hollow Joe-Pye weed is the tallest of the common Joe-Pye weeds — regularly reaching 6–12 feet in moist, fertile soil — and requires adequate moisture to achieve its impressive stature.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — hollow Joe-Pye weed is the largest native Joe-Pye weed, regularly reaching 5–12 feet in moist, fertile conditions, making it one of the most imposing native perennials in the eastern flora. It is identifiable by its hollow, purple-spotted stems (the 'fistulosum' meaning tubular, and the purple nodes). Large, domed clusters of mauve-pink flowers in midsummer to late summer are massively attractive to migrating monarchs, eastern tiger swallowtails, fritillaries, and dozens of native bee species — few plants concentrate as many pollinators in a single location as a blooming Joe-Pye weed. It grows naturally along stream banks, moist meadows, and wet open woodland edges throughout the eastern US. Named varieties including 'Gateway' (more compact, 5–6 feet) are widely available. Outstanding back-of-border or screening native for moist, sunny to partly shaded gardens.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; holly fern is one of the most drought-tolerant of all ferns and one of the best for Gulf Coast shade gardens — tolerating clay soil, drought, and considerable heat.
directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — holly fern is the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of the widely grown shade ferns and one of the finest ferns for Gulf Coast gardens. The bold, lustrous, dark green fronds with glossy, sickle-shaped (falcate) pinnae have an unmistakably bold, coarse texture reminiscent of holly leaves — giving the fern its common name. It is essentially evergreen through zone 6–11, maintaining handsome fronds year-round even through light freezes. Unlike most ferns it tolerates dry spells between watering and grows in relatively dry soil conditions once established. Excellent for shade planting under trees where soil competition and drought would stress more delicate ferns. An outstanding and low-maintenance fern for southern shade gardens in zones 6–11.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring around the last frost date or in late summer for bloom the following year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; hollyhocks are biennial or short-lived perennials that flower the second year from seed and self-sow freely to maintain the planting. Rust fungus is the most common disease — look for orange pustules on leaf undersides and remove affected foliage promptly, avoiding overhead watering. Plant against walls or fences and provide loose staking for the tall, top-heavy flowering spikes. Allow some seed to mature and fall to keep the colony self-renewing.

plant_note: Sow in spring for bloom the following year, or in fall to establish over winter.
directions: This heirloom hollyhock sends up dramatic five- to six-foot spikes lined with large blooms in a mix of colors, perfect for the back of a cottage border. Sow seeds shallowly in spring or fall, keeping in mind that as a biennial it usually flowers the second season. Plant in full sun with support in windy sites, and space generously for good airflow to reduce rust. Allow some seed to drop and it will happily naturalize year after year.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or in summer for flowers the following spring; behaves as a biennial in most climates.
directions: Sow seeds in spring or early summer; plants develop as a rosette the first year and flower the following spring — a true biennial. Purple or white cross-shaped flowers appear in mid-spring, followed by the flat, papery-silver seed pods that give the plant its common names 'honesty' and 'money plant.' To harvest pods for dried arrangements, cut when the outer husks have dried and carefully peel away both sides to reveal the lustrous translucent membrane. Plants self-sow freely and once established will perpetuate themselves year after year.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall in zones 7+ for overwintering.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in cool soil; germination is rapid in the 55–65°F range and slows dramatically in warm conditions. The blue-green waxy foliage and pendant, tubular flowers in deep violet-purple with showy blue-purple bracts are a magnet for bumblebees. Cerinthe prefers cool weather and will bolt and die in summer heat; in mild climates it self-sows prolifically and may return year after year. Harvest stems when bracts are fully colored for a distinctive and long-lasting cut flower.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth emerges or in fall at least 6 weeks before frost.
directions: Set crowns at soil level in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; avoid planting too deep or crowns may rot. Hostas tolerate deep shade but produce their best foliage color with 2–4 hours of dappled morning light. Divide large clumps every 4–5 years in early spring by slicing through the crown with a sharp spade. Slugs are the primary pest; use iron phosphate bait around vulnerable new growth.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, barely covered, in warm soil after the last frost, or start indoors a few weeks early. Upright plumes of caramel-brown seed heads rise on sturdy stems and make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers, especially for autumn arrangements. Heat-tolerant and low-maintenance, this amaranth thrives in full sun and average soil. Cut plumes for drying just as they fully color, and expect self-sowing if seed heads are left in place.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before hard frost, for intensely fragrant spring bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs at a depth of 6–8 in (about 3 times the bulb height) in well-drained soil with the pointed end up; wear gloves as the bulb sap can irritate sensitive skin. Hyacinths produce their most spectacular, dense spikes in year 1; subsequent years yield looser, more relaxed flower clusters that many gardeners find charming. Allow foliage to yellow completely before removing it so the bulb can replenish its energy. In warm climates (zones 8+), lift and chill bulbs for 6 weeks before replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil warms; loves heat.
directions: Direct sow hyacinth bean after the soil has warmed, or start indoors a few weeks early in cool climates. This ornamental vine bears purple stems, spikes of pea-like lavender flowers, and glossy magenta seed pods. Provide a sturdy trellis in full sun and average soil, and it will climb rapidly in summer heat. The pods are ornamental but require thorough cooking, so it is grown chiefly for its striking display.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed into sharply drained soil.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, gritty mix without covering, as they need light to germinate; avoid overwatering at all stages. Plant in the hottest, driest, sunniest spot in the garden — sandy, rocky, or gravelly soil is ideal, and root rot is the main cause of failure in clay or poorly drained sites. The magenta to purple daisy-like flowers cover the succulent mats nearly all summer and reopen only in bright sunlight. Hardy ice plant spreads steadily to make a weed-suppressing groundcover on slopes and rock gardens.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost once the soil has warmed; lift and store over winter in cold zones.
directions: Ice Tea is a smaller coral-salmon dahlia that shifts through terracotta and vivid coral tones across the season for a chameleon-like display. The sturdy stems often stand without staking, and it is a reliable cut flower and good tuber producer. Plant tubers eye-up in full sun and well-drained soil after frost, and water once shoots appear. Deadhead regularly for continuous bloom, and in cold climates dig and store the tubers dry over winter.

plant_note: In zones 7–10 sow in fall 4–6 weeks before first frost for winter and spring bloom; in colder zones direct sow in very early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Surface-sow or barely cover seeds on well-prepared, fine-textured soil; seeds need light and cool temperatures (50–60°F) to germinate — warm soil prevents germination. Plants grow as basal rosettes through winter in mild climates before sending up slender stems topped with large, tissue-paper flowers in spring. Iceland poppies are cool-season plants that stop flowering when summer heat arrives; in cold climates treat them as an early-spring annual with fall-sown or indoor-started transplants set out very early. Harvest cut flowers when buds are just beginning to crack open color and sear stem ends in boiling water to maximize vase life.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; needs a long, warm season.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, transplanting once the weather is warm. This dwarf variety features dramatic bronze-red foliage and scarlet seed heads that give a bold tropical effect. It is heat- and drought-tolerant once established and attracts hummingbirds. CAUTION: all plant parts, especially the seeds, are highly poisonous if ingested, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed and nights are warm.
directions: Sow tiny seed on the surface of moist starting mix without covering — light is required for germination. Impatiens are one of the most reliable shade-tolerant annuals and will self-clean most spent flowers. Pinch plants back once after transplanting to encourage compact, bushy growth. Water consistently; wilting in heat is common and plants recover overnight, but chronic drought reduces flowering.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost date; prefers cool conditions for best blooms.
directions: Direct sow shallowly in well-drained soil around the last frost date, as sweet sultan resents transplanting. Airy, softly domed 2 to 3 inch fragrant blooms sway on long, graceful stems and draw bees to the garden. Give plants full sun and average, well-drained soil, and stake in windy sites. Cut flowers just as they open for long-lasting, romantic bouquets, and deadhead to extend the display.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost; can also be fall sown in mild climates.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds, pressing them into the soil since light aids germination, after the last frost. Tall, bushy plants produce an abundance of swirled bicolor blooms that draw butterflies and bees to a pollinator or cutting garden. Coreopsis is quick, easy, and drought-tolerant, thriving in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Deadhead regularly to keep blooms coming through the season, and mass plantings for the best show.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface-sow as seeds need light to germinate.
directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, as light is needed for germination, then transplant after frost. This tall, statuesque plant bears large leaves and drooping clusters of long, tubular white flowers that turn very fragrant in the evening. It shines in a white or moon garden alongside delphiniums or other tall companions. Give it rich, moist soil and part shade in hot climates, and enjoy the perfume from late afternoon into night.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; blooms first year from an early start.
directions: Sow seeds barely covered in spring or late summer; this variety is known to bloom in its first season from an early start. Tall spires of single and semi-double pink, rose, and white flowers evoke a classic 1800s English cottage garden. Provide deep, rich soil and ample moisture for best performance, though established plants tolerate some drought. Stake tall stems in windy sites and allow plants to self-sow to maintain the planting year to year.

plant_note: Start indoors, surface-sowing since light aids germination, or fall sow.
directions: Surface-sow the seed since light aids germination, starting indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost or fall sowing outdoors. Large single and semi-double golden flowers with dark centers stand tall in heat and drought without staking. They are excellent, long-lasting cut flowers and lure butterflies while providing seeds for birds in winter. Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil, and deadhead to prolong the extended bloom season.

plant_note: Direct sow on prepared, weed-free soil in spring; seed can be surface-sown or barely covered at 1/8 inch depth. Fall dormant seeding (after October) is also effective — natural stratification over winter breaks dormancy.
directions: Plant in well-drained, average to dry soil in full sun; indiangrass is one of the four dominant tall-grass prairie grasses, companion to big bluestem, switchgrass, and little bluestem. Its narrow blue-green foliage and elegant golden-yellow seed plumes waving in the fall breeze make it one of the most ornamentally beautiful native grasses. Seed heads emerge in August and September with a distinctive twisted 'awns' that catch the light like bronze silk. Fall color is deep orange-gold, and standing winter stems provide bird seed and structure through snow. Like all warm-season prairie grasses, it is slow to start in spring — do not mistake lateness for death. Cut back to 4–6 inches in early spring annually.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; inkberry is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for wet, acidic conditions but also tolerates moderate drought once established.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun — inkberry is a native evergreen holly shrub of the Atlantic and Gulf coastal plain, growing naturally in bogs, pocosins, pine flatwoods, and moist woodland margins. It tolerates shade, wet soil, and flooding conditions that defeat most other shrubs. The small black berries in late summer and fall are consumed by dozens of bird species including wild turkeys, wood ducks, and bluebirds; bees (especially Melittidae oil-collecting bees) work the small white flowers in spring. It spreads steadily by suckering roots and can form dense, wildlife-shelter colonies over time. Named cultivars include 'Shamrock' (compact, mounding) and 'Densa' (dense form). An outstanding native evergreen for wet, acidic soils where Rhododendron and Kalmia cannot be grown. Plants are dioecious — both male and female plants are needed for berry production.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; also easily started indoors 6 weeks before last frost. Self-sows abundantly once established — remove seedlings where not wanted.
directions: Direct sow in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; inland sea oats is one of the most valuable native ornamental grasses specifically because it thrives in deep shade — a condition that defeats virtually all other ornamental grasses. The flat, bamboo-like leaf blades form a graceful arching clump, but the showstopper is the hanging, oat-like seed clusters that emerge in summer, dangling from slender wiry branches, and persist into winter — turning from green to copper-tan to warm bronze, rattling pleasantly in the wind. It grows naturally along stream banks and in bottomland forests, giving it outstanding tolerance of both shade and periodic wet soils. Self-sows prolifically once established and can become weedy; deadhead if you want to limit spread. The seeds are relished by songbirds in fall.

plant_note: Plant the potted rose in spring after hard frosts; sold as a live plant.
directions: Plant this floribunda rose in spring in a hole amended with compost, keeping the graft at soil level in colder zones. Clusters of richly fragrant purple-plum blooms appear from spring through fall on an elegant upright form, making a bold focal point. Provide full sun, well-drained soil, and consistent moisture, watering at the base to keep foliage dry. Prune in late winter to shape the plant and deadhead spent clusters to encourage repeat flowering.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed, then start indoors; benefits from a chilling period.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in the refrigerator for several weeks, then sow indoors or direct sow in fall for natural chilling. This swamp milkweed blend produces clusters of pink and rose blooms that are irresistible to monarch butterflies and hummingbirds. It grows best in consistently moist to wet soils such as near a pond or rain garden but adapts to average beds. As a host plant for monarch caterpillars, leave foliage intact and avoid pesticides to support the life cycle.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow in spring.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow in spring, barely covering it. Long, wiry stems carry pincushion blooms in white, lilac, and blue shades that are outstanding and long-lasting in bouquets. This tough, fairly drought-tolerant, hardy plant has few pests and blooms over an extended season. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and cut or deadhead frequently to keep flowers coming.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost in warm soil; lift and store over winter where it freezes.
directions: Ivanetti is a classic ball dahlia in deep burgundy-to-purple-black tones, a favorite for cutting thanks to its perfect globe form and strong stems. Plant tubers eye-up in full sun and well-drained soil after the last frost, watering only lightly until growth emerges. Pinch the growing tip early to encourage branching and more blooms, and deadhead through the season. In cold regions dig the tubers after the first frost and store them dry until spring.

plant_note: Plant in fall or early spring; this evergreen blooms very early in the season.
directions: Ivory Prince, a Lenten rose, is among the earliest perennials to bloom, opening upright creamy-white flowers flushed with rose and green in late winter to early spring. Its leathery evergreen foliage stays attractive year-round in partial to full shade. Plant in rich, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist while establishing. All parts are toxic if eaten, so site it away from areas where children or pets graze, and remove old leaves in late winter to showcase the blooms.

plant_note: Plant corms 2–4 inches deep in fall, flat side down; the corm size determines whether the plant produces a male or female flower — larger corms become female and produce bright-red berry clusters in fall.
directions: Plant corms in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade where seasonal moisture is available in spring but soil drains by midsummer. The distinctive hooded spathe (the pulpit) surrounding the upright spadix (Jack) appears in spring, followed by showy clusters of red berries on female plants in late summer. All parts of the plant contain calcium oxalate crystals and are highly irritating if eaten raw; extensive traditional preparation was required by Indigenous peoples who used the corm as food. The plant dies back completely by midsummer; underplant with hostas or ferns to cover the gap.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; can also direct sow in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification.
directions: Plant in cool, consistently moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Jacob's ladder performs best in climates with cool summers and struggles where heat and humidity persist into July. The delicate, ladder-like pinnate foliage is attractive throughout the growing season, and the blue to violet flower clusters appear in late spring. Deadhead spent flower stalks to encourage reblooming and prevent excessive self-seeding. In warmer portions of its range, afternoon shade is essential; the plant naturally occurs in mountain meadows and woodland edges where temperatures remain moderate.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall or stratify and sow in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Jacob's ladder self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions and forms steadily expanding colonies over time.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — Jacob's ladder is a charming native spring wildflower named for its distinctive pinnately compound leaves whose paired leaflets march ladder-like up either side of the central stem, resembling the Biblical Jacob's ladder to heaven. Clusters of nodding, bell-shaped blue-violet flowers appear in April and May on stems 8–16 inches tall, providing valuable early-season nectar for bumblebees and other native bees. It grows naturally in rich, moist, shaded forest and woodland slopes throughout the eastern US, forming loose colonies in appropriate conditions. The foliage remains attractive through summer in cool, moist locations before dying back in late summer. Self-sows reliably and naturalizes gently without becoming invasive. An excellent companion for native ferns, trilliums, and spring ephemerals in the shaded native plant garden.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring; slow to establish but eventually spreads vigorously.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive, well-drained soil in partial shade; Japanese anemone is slow to establish in its first 1–2 years but spreads freely by underground runners once settled. Single or semi-double flowers in white, pink, or deep rose appear on tall, branched stems from late summer through fall when few other perennials are in bloom. Can become invasive in rich, moist conditions — site carefully or contain with buried barriers. Divide in early spring every 3–4 years to control spread or increase the planting.

plant_note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped camellias in fall in zones 7–10; fall planting allows root establishment before the winter bloom season. Set the root ball slightly above grade to ensure excellent drainage — camellias planted too deep quickly decline.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade or filtered sun; camellias are quintessential Gulf Coast and Southeast garden shrubs prized for their large, rose-like flowers in shades of white, pink, red, and bicolor that bloom from fall through early spring — exactly when little else flowers. Protect from harsh afternoon sun and desiccating winter winds, especially newly planted specimens. Apply a 2–3 inch layer of pine bark or pine straw mulch, keeping it away from the trunk. Fertilize with acid-forming camellia fertilizer after flowering ends in spring. Scale and petal blight are the most common issues; treat scale with horticultural oil and improve air circulation to reduce blight. Hundreds of named cultivars exist — choose bloom time (fall, winter, or spring-flowering) based on your frost window.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth begins; avoid late summer or fall division in colder zones, as plants need a full growing season to establish.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; this slow-growing Japanese native produces cascading mounds of arching, bamboo-like foliage that moves gracefully in the breeze. The golden-variegated cultivar 'Aureola' is the most commonly grown and lights up dark corners of the shade garden. Cut back to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges. Growth is slow for the first two years; resist the urge to overcrowd, as mature clumps need their full spacing to show the characteristic cascading habit to best effect. Divide in early spring every 4–5 years.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring after frost or immediately after late-summer bloom; keep roots consistently moist after planting.
directions: Plant rhizomes 2 in deep in rich, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), moisture-retentive to wet soil; Japanese iris excel at pond margins and rain gardens. The enormous flat flowers up to 10 in across appear in early to midsummer in shades of purple, blue, white, and lavender, often with intricate veining. Avoid lime or wood ash near these plants — alkaline soil causes yellowing and poor growth. Divide every 3–4 years in late summer or early spring, replanting healthy outer fans and discarding the woody centers.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring once new growth is visible; avoid hard frosts after planting as new fronds are tender.
directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich soil where it receives dappled or full shade; its silver, green, and burgundy fronds hold the best color with some morning light but will fade or bleach in afternoon sun. Division every 3 years in early spring keeps clumps vigorous and provides plants for naturalizing a shaded bed. Remove spent fronds in late winter before new fiddleheads emerge in spring. Protect newly planted divisions from slug damage with iron phosphate bait, as young fronds are particularly vulnerable.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions 6–12 inches apart in spring or early fall; water well until established. Mulch lightly between plants until coverage is complete.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; pachysandra is the standard choice for dense shade under shallow-rooted trees like maples where most plants fail. The glossy, evergreen whorls of foliage form a uniform 6–8 inch carpet that requires essentially no maintenance once established. Small white flowers in spring are mildly fragrant. Volutella blight can cause patches to brown and die — improve drainage, avoid overwatering, and remove affected plant material promptly. Do not plant in full sun or very dry soil, as foliage will yellow and decline rapidly. Takes 2–3 years from planting to achieve full coverage.

common_name: Javelin Forte Deep Rose Spanish Lavender
plant_note: Plant the live starter after frost danger passes; sold as a live plant.
directions: Plant this compact Spanish lavender in spring in lean, sharply drained soil after frost danger has passed. Deep rose-pink bracts crown purple flowers above classic silver-green foliage on a tidy, mounded plant ideal for containers and borders. Give it full sun, avoid heavy or wet soils, and water sparingly once established since it is drought-tolerant. Shear lightly after the main flush to keep the plant compact and encourage rebloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost; dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow the large seeds 1/2 to 1 inch deep after the last frost, as nasturtiums resent transplanting. This compact jewel-toned blend makes an excellent edging plant and produces edible flowers and leaves with a peppery flavor for salads. Plants are fairly drought-tolerant and actually bloom best in lean soil, since rich soil favors foliage over flowers. Provide full sun to part shade, and grow in a sunny window for winter color indoors.

plant_note: Direct sow seed on the soil surface (seeds need light to germinate) in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade just before last frost; jewelweed self-sows prolifically and typically establishes as a self-sustaining colony once introduced — no further planting is needed after the first year.
directions: Scatter seed on the surface of moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in early spring; jewelweed is a tall native annual of moist woodlands, stream banks, and swampy areas throughout most of North America — one of the few native annuals that thrives in deep shade and moist conditions. The orange, trumpet-shaped flowers are uniquely constructed with a curved spur and are the primary nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds in eastern North America during their southward fall migration. The ripe seed pods burst explosively when touched (hence 'touch-me-not'), flinging seeds up to 3–4 feet. Fresh crushed stems and leaves are widely used as a folk remedy to relieve the itch of poison ivy contact, with some evidence supporting anti-inflammatory compounds in the juice. Jewelweed is a powerful self-sower that naturalizes in moist, shaded areas and requires no care once established.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant in spring.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in a moist medium in the refrigerator before sowing indoors; germination is improved but still irregular. Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; this native prairie plant tolerates occasional wet feet and dry spells once established. Cutting stems back by half in June (Chelsea chop) reduces ultimate height and prevents the need for staking without sacrificing the domed, mauve-pink flower clusters beloved by butterflies. Seedheads are ornamentally interesting into winter and provide food for goldfinches.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; violas prefer cool spring and fall weather.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, or direct sow in cool spring or fall weather. These charming purple, yellow, and white faces bloom prolifically and can poke up above light snow. Though perennial in zones 4-8, they are often grown as annuals since they falter in extended heat. Grow in part shade with moist soil, use them as spring bulb cover, and let them self-sow to return year after year.

plant_note: Plant in fall; fragrant and good for warm climates.
directions: Plant jonquilla daffodil bulbs in fall about six inches deep in well-drained soil and full to partial sun. These fragrant narcissi carry several small golden flowers per stem and tolerate warmer climates better than most daffodils. They naturalize freely and are resistant to deer and rodents. Allow the grassy foliage to ripen fully before removal to build strong bulbs for next year.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as soil warms; leaves emerge after the last frost.
directions: June is an award-winning hosta prized for variegated leaves with golden centers and blue-green margins that brighten shady beds. Give it partial to full shade and rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil; some morning sun deepens the gold coloring. Pale lavender flowers appear in summer on tall scapes. Remove tattered leaves and divide congested clumps in early spring, and protect the tender foliage from slugs.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; seeds need darkness to germinate.
directions: Sow the fine seed in darkness indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Also called painted tongue, this variety bears sprays of velvety, deep-blue, trumpet-shaped flowers from summer until frost. It adds rich depth to mixed containers, borders, and cut arrangements while attracting pollinators. Provide full sun, rich well-drained soil, and support for the somewhat lax stems, deadheading to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil, covering seeds about 1/4 inch, or start indoors a few weeks early. These 4 to 5 inch lime-green and frothy-white blooms dazzle in cottage-garden borders or massed with bachelor's buttons and snapdragons. The edible petals can dress up salads and desserts, and the long stems make superb cut flowers. Grow in full sun with good air circulation to reduce mildew, and cut or deadhead often to keep blooms coming.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow when warm.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow when soil is warm, covering about 1/4 inch. This historic variety, the first white marigold introduced in 1975, produces large creamy-white blooms that are rare in the marigold world. Sturdy plants thrive in full sun and average soil and reward regular deadheading with continuous flowers. Grow near vegetables where they may help deter pests, and cut blooms for long-lasting bouquets.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; violas thrive in cool spring and fall.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, or direct sow in cool spring or fall weather. 'King Henry' forms neat little mounds of richly colored royal-purple flowers ideal for garden edges and containers. Violas love cool weather, making them perfect for spring bulb cover or fall color after annuals fade. Grow in part shade with moist soil, deadhead to prolong bloom, and expect self-sowing in favorable spots.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow in spring.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow in spring, barely covering it. Opulent apricot-toned double blooms stand tall and firm on strong stems, adding elegance to beds and bouquets. China asters make excellent, long-lasting cut flowers and appreciate full sun and rich, well-drained soil. Rotate planting sites each year to avoid soilborne disease, and stagger sowings for a longer season of blooms.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; transplant after frost.
directions: Surface-sow the seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, pressing it in since light aids germination. Papery silvery-rose blooms open into delicate crowns that hold their color beautifully when dried. Well-branched plants reach a good height and supply abundant stems for fresh and everlasting arrangements. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and cut flowers just before they fully open for the best dried results.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; transplant after frost.
directions: Surface-sow the seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, pressing it in since light aids germination. Stately blooms unfurl in layers of soft silvery-white petals for a regal presence in beds and bouquets. Tall, well-branched plants are drought-tolerant once established and provide abundant stems for cutting and drying. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and harvest flowers just as they begin to open for the best dried displays.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; transplant after frost.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This ornamental oregano drapes in cascading layers of papery green and rosy-purple hop-like bracts that catch the light. Drought-tolerant and pollinator-friendly, it excels in containers, bouquets, and the front of sunny borders. Grow in full sun with sharply drained soil, avoid overwatering, and cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; likes cool weather.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. This shorter blend delivers a rainbow of lightly scented blooms on knee-high vines that need little or no support. It is ideal for a short trellis, hanging basket, or the front of a border and delights pollinators. Keep roots cool and evenly watered, and cut flowers regularly to prolong the display before summer heat.

plant_note: Plant nursery shrubs in fall or early spring in a sheltered, partly shaded spot.
directions: This evergreen camellia shrub is best planted from a nursery specimen in fall or early spring in a location with morning sun and afternoon shade, sheltered from harsh wind. It thrives in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, so mulch the shallow roots and avoid planting too deeply. From winter into spring it bears playful blooms of white or soft pink streaked with red, with no two flowers alike. Water regularly during dry spells and prune lightly just after flowering to shape the plant.

plant_note: Plant crown divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; set the crown at soil level and do not bury; lady fern is the most adaptable and widely grown native fern and succeeds in a very wide range of moist, shaded conditions.
directions: Plant in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; lady fern is one of the most beautiful, adaptable, and widely grown native ferns in North America, producing finely divided, arching, bright green to chartreuse fronds in graceful spreading clumps that light up dark garden corners throughout summer. Unlike many ferns it is deciduous, going dormant in winter and re-emerging reliably each spring. It tolerates a wider range of conditions than most ferns — moderately dry soil, some drought once established, and varying light levels from dense shade to dappled sun. Dozens of ornamental cultivars exist, including red-stemmed forms ('Lady in Red'), crested forms, and dwarf selections. It naturalizes readily in moist woodland gardens and combines beautifully with hostas, bleeding heart, and native wildflowers. Division every 3–4 years keeps clumps vigorous.

plant_note: Plant in fall; perennializes well in warm dry climates.
directions: Plant lady tulip bulbs in fall about five inches deep in sharply drained soil and full sun. Unlike hybrid tulips, this dainty species perennializes reliably and naturalizes in warm, dry climates. The slender flowers are white or yellow with a bold red or pink exterior stripe and open flat in sun. Keep the site dry during summer dormancy to encourage the bulbs to return and multiply for years.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; transplant after frost.
directions: Surface-sow the seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This monarda is covered with tiered whorls of lavender-pink flower clusters that attract butterflies, hummingbirds, and bees. The edible petals can replace oregano in savory dishes, be steeped into tea, or float in punch. Grow in full sun to part shade with good air circulation to limit mildew, and deadhead to extend flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; light aids germination so barely cover seed.
directions: This native perennial coreopsis produces masses of golden-yellow, daisy-like flowers above slender, lance-shaped foliage. Press seeds into the soil surface in spring since light helps them germinate, and keep the bed moist until seedlings establish. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, tolerating drought and heat while attracting bees and butterflies. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and let some seed heads mature to feed birds and self-sow.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; most gardeners use purchased transplants due to slow, uneven germination.
directions: Sow seeds after lightly scarifying or nicking the seed coat; soak overnight to improve germination, which is slow and can take 4–6 weeks at 70–80°F. Transplant in full sun after all frost risk has passed; lantana thrives in heat, drought, and poor soil and blooms most freely under mild stress. The multicolored flower clusters change color as they age, giving plants a jewel-like effect; deadhead clusters as they fade to keep new flowers coming. Perennial in zones 9–11 where it can become invasive; grow as an annual elsewhere.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 4–8 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall in mild climates for early spring bloom. Seeds need cold stratification and prefer cool soil to germinate.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep directly in the garden as early as the ground can be worked — larkspur germinates best in cool soil below 65°F and may fail to sprout if the weather turns warm. Do not start indoors, as plants have taproots that do not transplant well. Thin to 9–12 in to promote sturdy stems. Allow some seed to fall for reliable self-sowing in subsequent years.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface in early spring or late fall; needs light and cold.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed in early spring or late fall directly where plants are to grow, since poppies dislike transplanting and need light to germinate. 'Lauren's Grape' produces rich purple single blooms followed by attractive seed pods prized for arrangements and baking. Thin seedlings to give each plant room, and grow in full sun with well-drained soil. Allow some pods to mature and self-sow to bring the poppies back each year.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; loves heat.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant once the weather is warm. 'Lavender Lady' produces abundant papery, clover-like lavender blooms that shrug off extreme heat and drought once established. The long-lasting flowers are excellent for fresh bouquets and dry beautifully for everlasting arrangements. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and pinch young plants to encourage branching and more blooms.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs or root cuttings in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, alkaline to neutral, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; leadplant has an extraordinarily deep root system (to 16 feet) and is extremely drought tolerant once established. Do not overwater.
directions: Plant in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, neutral to slightly alkaline, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; leadplant is a keystone prairie shrub considered by ecologists one of the best indicators of an intact, high-quality tallgrass prairie — its presence signals deep, healthy, undisturbed prairie soil, since its roots penetrate up to 16 feet deep. The silver-gray, pinnately compound leaves (with a lead-gray, canescent coating of dense fine hairs) are beautiful year-round and give the plant its name. The spikes of deep violet-purple flowers with bright orange anthers in summer are exceptional for native bumblebees and specialist bees. It is a nitrogen-fixing legume that improves soil fertility. The combination of extreme drought tolerance, nitrogen-fixing roots, and high bee value makes leadplant one of the most ecologically important prairie shrubs for habitat restoration and dry, sunny gardens.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow when warm.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow when soil is warm, covering lightly. These bushy, compact signet marigolds form dense mounds of lacy, scented foliage topped with profuse single lemon and tangerine blooms. The petite flowers are edible and can be infused into oil or vinegar for salad dressings. Grow in full sun with average soil, deadhead to keep blooms coming, and use them as tidy edging or rock-garden accents.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow when warm.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow when soil warms, covering about 1/4 inch. 'Lemon Drop' bears cheerful sunny-yellow flowers on compact plants perfect for the front of the bed, patio pots, and window boxes. It takes little space in the vegetable garden and may help reduce soil nematodes. Grow in full sun with average soil, and deadhead regularly to encourage continuous bloom through the season.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost once soil warms.
directions: Lemon Queen is a graceful branching heirloom sunflower with soft lemon-yellow petals surrounding dark centers. Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm soil after frost, spacing plants to allow each branching stem room to develop. Reaching five to seven feet, it produces many blooms per plant from midsummer on and is a magnet for bees. Provide full sun and steady moisture, and cut stems freely for long-lasting bouquets.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost; succession sow for continuous bloom.
directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after the last frost, and sow again every 2 to 4 weeks until three months before first fall frost for continuous bloom. Majestic multi-branched plants produce many soft lemon-yellow flowers ideal for cutting all summer. Easy to grow in almost any well-drained soil, these sunflowers are drought-tolerant once established. Give them full sun, and leave late seed heads for birds to enjoy in autumn.

plant_note: Plant rooted stem cuttings or divisions 12–18 inches apart in spring or fall; stems root wherever they contact soil, filling in gaps quickly once plants establish.
directions: Plant in almost any soil in partial to deep shade; lesser periwinkle is one of the toughest shade groundcovers, tolerating dry shade under mature trees once established. The trailing stems root at nodes to form a weed-suppressing mat studded with periwinkle-blue pinwheel flowers in spring, with scattered repeat bloom into fall. Variegated forms such as 'Illumination' and 'Ralph Shugert' brighten dark spaces. Shear plants back by half in early spring to maintain density and remove dead or straggling stems. Note that Vinca minor can spread into natural areas — consider native alternatives (Pachysandra procumbens, Asarum canadense) in gardens bordering woodlands.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; benefits from cool soil to germinate.
directions: Lewis blue flax is a graceful native perennial that opens clear sky-blue flowers each morning atop slender, wiry stems. Sow seeds directly in spring or fall on well-drained soil, pressing them in lightly and keeping the surface moist until they sprout. Once established it is remarkably drought tolerant and thrives in full sun across a wide range of conditions. Allow spent flowers to set seed and it will self-sow to form drifts over time.

plant_note: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in spring as soon as the soil can be worked; they establish quickly and bloom the first season.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep with the flat side down in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; rich or wet soil leads to floppy growth. Liatris is unique in opening its flowers from the top of the spike downward, prolonging the bloom period for 3–4 weeks in midsummer. The purple to rose-pink spikes are outstanding cut flowers and highly attractive to butterflies and bees; stems last 10–14 days in a vase. Clumps multiply steadily; divide every 3–4 years in spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain strong bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil, covering seeds about 1/4 inch, or start indoors a few weeks early. 'Lilliput' produces a never-ending show of vibrant, small double pompon flowers on long stems all summer. Cutting the blooms for bouquets encourages the plants to produce even more, delighting butterflies and bees. Grow in full sun with good air circulation to limit mildew, and deadhead or harvest often to keep it flowering.

plant_note: Plant rhizome clumps or container plants in spring after frost danger passes; set the crown just at soil level in a generous planting hole. In zones 7–8, plant against a south-facing wall and mulch heavily in winter.
directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; agapanthus produces strappy, strap-like evergreen foliage and tall, sturdy flower stems topped by globose umbels of blue, purple, or white trumpet-shaped flowers in June–July that are extraordinarily striking in mass plantings. It is one of the signature plants of Gulf Coast landscapes, thriving in heat, humidity, and coastal conditions with very little care. Clumps become slightly root-bound over time and actually flower more prolifically when congested — do not divide unless absolutely necessary. Deciduous types (A. praecox) are more cold-hardy to zone 7 with protection. Deadhead spent stems for neatness and to prevent abundant self-seeding of named cultivars.

plant_note: Plant pips in fall or early spring in shade.
directions: Plant lily of the valley pips in fall or early spring, setting the rhizomes just below the soil surface in humus-rich, moist shade. The arching stems of nodding white bells are intensely fragrant and spread by rhizomes to form a dense groundcover. It thrives under trees and in woodland gardens and can become invasive, so site it where spread is welcome. All parts are toxic if eaten, so keep it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or early fall; plants establish quickly and begin spreading within the first growing season.
directions: Plant in almost any well-drained soil, from deep shade under dense evergreens to full sun, though the darkest green foliage color develops in partial shade. This evergreen, clump-forming groundcover produces spikes of violet flowers in late summer followed by black berries that persist through winter; it is one of the most reliable plants for covering problem areas under trees where lawn grasses fail. Shear or mow plants to 3–4 inches in late winter before new growth begins to remove tattered winter foliage. Divide congested clumps every 4–5 years to maintain vigor; divisions establish quickly and can be replanted immediately.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost date; lisianthus requires a very long lead time and is challenging from seed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny, pelleted seeds on sterile, fine-textured mix; do not cover as seeds need light and maintain steady 70–75°F. Seedlings grow extremely slowly for the first 8–10 weeks, so patience is essential — do not overwater at this stage as damping off is common. Transplant carefully after last frost into rich, well-drained, alkaline-leaning soil in full sun. The ruffled, rose-like blooms in white, purple, pink, and bicolor make lisianthus one of the finest cut flowers; harvest stems when 2–3 buds per stem have opened.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in spring, or plant divisions in spring in full sun and lean, well-drained soil.
directions: Direct sow in full sun in lean, well-drained to dry soil; little bluestem is a native prairie grass of exceptional ornamental value that thrives in poor, sandy, or rocky soil where it develops the best fall color. Blue-green summer foliage turns brilliant coppery-orange and burgundy-red in fall, and the silver-white fluffy seedheads persist through winter, capturing hoarfrost beautifully. In rich, moist soil plants become floppy and lose their characteristic upright form. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth emerges; divide every 3–4 years if needed.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; prefers cool weather.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. Unlike vining sweet peas, 'Little Sweetheart' is a dwarf bush type with profuse, lightly scented small flowers in a rainbow of colors. Its compact habit suits hanging baskets, patio containers, window boxes, and the front of a border. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut blooms often to prolong flowering before summer heat.

plant_note: Plant small container-grown trees (1–2 gallon) in winter or early spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil; live oak establishes best when young. Large container or balled-and-burlapped trees are slow to re-establish. Plant acorns immediately upon collecting in fall.
directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; live oak is the defining tree of the Gulf Coast landscape, forming magnificent broad, sweeping canopies of twisted horizontal branches draped with Spanish moss, with a single mature tree providing habitat for hundreds of species of insects, birds, and wildlife. The leaves are semi-evergreen in zone 8 (dropping briefly in late winter as new leaves emerge) and evergreen in zone 9–10. It tolerates salt spray, sandy soils, and periodic drought once established and grows steadily — 1–2 feet per year when young. The small acorns are critically important fall and winter food for blue jays, wild turkeys, squirrels, and deer. Plant far from structures and utility lines — mature trees reach 40–60 feet tall with 60–100 foot spread. A heritage landscape tree meant for centuries.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in shallow water (1–4 inches) or saturated soil at pond or stream margins in partial shade once soil warms; requires consistent moisture and will not survive drought.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in shallow water or saturated, slightly acidic soil at pond or stream margins in partial shade; lizard's tail is a distinctive native aquatic perennial of southeastern swamps, stream banks, and freshwater wetlands, producing arching spikes of tiny white fragrant flowers that droop at the tip like a lizard's tail in midsummer, above large glossy heart-shaped leaves. It grows naturally in standing water and at stream margins from Quebec to Florida, and is an excellent choice for pond margins, boggy borders, and rain gardens where its attractive foliage and unusual flowers provide interest through the season. The plant spreads by rhizome to form naturalistic colonies. The fragrant flowers attract many small native bees and beetles. Excellent combined with pickerel rush and blue flag iris for a native wetland plant community.

plant_note: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic soil; loblolly pine is one of the fastest-growing southern pines (2–4 feet per year when young) and establishes readily from small seedlings.
directions: Plant bareroot seedlings or container trees in fall or early spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; loblolly pine is the most common and commercially important tree of the South, but it is also a critical wildlife tree — the seeds are consumed by dozens of bird species including red-cockaded woodpeckers (which nest exclusively in mature pines), white-breasted nuthatches, and pine siskins, while the open structure of mature pine canopy supports a rich understory community of native plants. It grows extremely fast (2–4 feet per year) and provides rapid screening, wind protection, and habitat structure. The three long needles in bundles and heavy cones are distinctive. Susceptible to pine bark beetles when stressed by drought; plant in appropriate moist, sandy soils and avoid planting far outside its natural range.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; long-spurred violet is an Appalachian mountain species that performs best in cool, moist, shaded conditions with good drainage and slightly acidic soil.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — long-spurred violet is one of the most distinctive and elegant of all native violets, immediately recognizable by its unusually long nectar spur — 0.5 to 0.75 inches long, compared to the very short spurs of most violets — and its pale lavender flowers with darker veining and a darker eye. The long nectar spur specifically coevolved with certain long-tongued bees and butterflies that can access the nectar. It grows naturally in cool, moist, rich Appalachian woodland from Pennsylvania south to the Carolinas, preferring the well-drained, acidic, forest soils of mountain slopes and ravine banks. Less adaptable to hot, humid lowland conditions than most native violets. A beautiful specialty violet for cool-climate woodland gardens and native plant collections in zones 4–8, and a host plant for native fritillary butterflies.

plant_note: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil; longleaf pine establishes best from small seedlings planted in fall. Do not fertilize or amend soil — longleaf requires poor, sandy conditions. Mow around young plants to control competing vegetation.
directions: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in sandy, infertile, acidic, extremely well-drained soil in full sun; longleaf pine is one of the most ecologically critical trees in North America, once covering 90 million acres of the southeastern coastal plain but now reduced to less than 3% of its original range — one of the continent's most severe ecological losses. Planting longleaf is an act of ecological restoration. The tree is famous for its 'grass stage' — for 3–7 years, the seedling looks like a grass clump with no visible stem while it develops a deep taproot; then growth accelerates dramatically. Mature longleaf lives 300–500 years, supporting 29 endangered species including the red-cockaded woodpecker, indigo snake, and gopher tortoise. The long needles (12–18 inches), massive cones, and fire-adapted bark make it an outstanding specimen tree in appropriate sandy soils.

plant_note: Soak seeds and sow in cool early-spring soil at the base of a trellis.
directions: Lord Nelson is a richly fragrant heirloom sweet pea with deep, nearly navy-blue blooms on vigorous climbing vines. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil at the foot of a trellis or fence, since sweet peas thrive in chilly starts and fade in heat. Keep the roots cool and evenly moist and pick flowers frequently to keep new buds forming. Provide sturdy vertical support for the five- to six-foot vines.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; nigella dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow in early spring where plants are to grow, since nigella resents transplanting. 'Delft Blue' bears distinctive dual-colored blooms of violet-blue over snowy white with burgundy stamens amid feathery foliage. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow, it is perfect for borders and attracts pollinators, while the ornamental seed pods are prized for arrangements. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and let some pods mature so plants self-sow for next year.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; do not start too early indoors as plants grow quickly and become rootbound.
directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and press lightly — seed needs light to germinate. Direct sowing is preferred as plants grow fast and resent transplanting. Thin seedlings to 18–24 in to allow the dramatic, rope-like crimson tassels to develop fully. The hanging panicles can be cut and hung upside down to dry when they reach full length.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in early spring; also sow in fall in zones 7–10 for spring bloom.
directions: Scatter seeds directly on prepared soil and rake in 1/8 in deep; nigella resents transplanting and germinates best in cool soil. Thin seedlings to 9–12 in to prevent overcrowding — plants are quick to grow and will self-sow freely if allowed to set the inflated seed pods. The blue or white flowers are surrounded by thread-like bracts that give the plant its misty appearance; after bloom the ornamental balloon-like pods are equally attractive and excellent for dried arrangements. Make succession sowings every 3–4 weeks until early summer for continuous bloom.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in a shaded, moist site; division is the most reliable propagation method.
directions: Plant in deep shade to partial shade in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; lungwort is one of the best shade perennials, offering both attractive silver-spotted foliage and early spring flowers. Pink buds open to blue or violet tubular flowers in early spring, often when little else is in bloom, providing nectar for early pollinators. In hot, dry summers the leaves may develop powdery mildew — shear back affected foliage after flowering and new, fresh leaves will emerge. Divide clumps every 3–4 years immediately after flowering to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow outdoors in early spring or fall; nick or soak seeds overnight to improve germination.
directions: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or soak overnight in water to soften the hard seed coat before sowing 1/4 in deep in cool, slightly acidic, well-drained soil. Lupines fix atmospheric nitrogen via root nodules and thrive in lean soils without heavy fertilization — too much nitrogen promotes foliage over flowers. They prefer cool summers; in hot climates, they often perform as cool-season annuals, blooming in late spring and fading by midsummer. Deadhead promptly after the first flowering spike fades to encourage a second flush and prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the soil has warmed and frost has passed; lift and store tubers over winter in cold zones.
directions: Maarn is a classic orange ball dahlia that produces an abundance of round, uniform blooms perfect for cutting. The plants are large and well-balanced, often standing without staking, and they generate plenty of tubers for dividing. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after frost has passed. Cut flowers frequently and deadhead spent blooms to keep the plant producing all season. In zones colder than 8, dig and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter.

plant_note: Sow in darkness indoors; seeds need darkness to germinate.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Above slim, silvery foliage, tiny star-shaped blooms form 2 to 3 inch pincushion-like flowerheads with a light scent that draws pollinators. This Australian wildflower is a soft, fragrant beauty that makes a lovely, airy cut flower. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, provide support in windy sites, and harvest stems for delicate bouquets.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; snapdragons like cool weather.
directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This dwarf snapdragon blend offers a lovely mix of colors that charms in flower beds, borders, containers, and hanging baskets. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so transplant hardened seedlings out around the last frost. Grow in full sun to part shade with well-drained soil, pinch young plants to encourage branching, and deadhead to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; prefers cool, moist conditions.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This mix dazzles with blooms in yellow, orange, pink, and red on plants ideal for borders, containers, and wildflower gardens. Monkey flower thrives in cool, moist conditions and blooms continuously from spring into summer. Grow in sun to part shade, keep the soil consistently moist, and deadhead to keep the colorful show going.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring when soil has warmed; miscanthus establishes best in warm soil and can be slow in cold springs.
directions: Plant divisions in full sun in any well-drained to moderately moist soil; maiden grass forms large, dramatic clumps with arching, green or variegated foliage and showy, feathery silver plumes in late summer and fall. Cut back hard in late winter or early spring before new growth emerges — use pruners or a chainsaw on large clumps; tie the clump with twine before cutting to simplify cleanup. Divide every 3–5 years in spring when the center of the clump dies out. Some cultivars are sterile; check before planting, as seeding types can naturalize aggressively in some regions.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; likes cool weather.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. Mammoth sweet peas boast extra-large, sweetly scented flowers on long stems that are perfect for cutting. Bred from an Early Multiflora Gigantea Spencer type, this blend offers extra drought and heat resistance for a longer season. Provide a tall trellis, keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut flowers frequently to prolong the bloom.

plant_note: Soak and sow in cool spring soil beside a trellis.
directions: This mammoth sweet pea mix delivers large, fragrant blooms in soft rose, pink, lavender, and white on vigorous vines. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil against a trellis, since these cottage-garden climbers love a chilly start. Keep roots cool and moisture steady, and harvest often to encourage a long succession of scented flowers. The five- to six-foot vines need sturdy support to climb.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; likes cool weather.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. This Mammoth selection produces striking, extra-large blooms in deep velvety purple-blue atop long stems ideal for cutting gardens. Bred from Early Multiflora Gigantea, it offers Spencer-type beauty with added drought and heat resistance. Provide a tall trellis, keep roots cool and evenly watered, and cut flowers often to extend flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; needs warm soil and full sun.
directions: This giant heirloom sunflower grows on a single stem to ten or twelve feet and crowns it with a massive golden head up to a foot across. Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm soil after frost, spacing generously so the huge plants have room. Give full sun and steady water, and stake tall stems in exposed sites to keep them from toppling. The abundant seeds are excellent for roasting or feeding birds once heads mature.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after the last frost, spacing plants well to support their large size. This giant grows fast on a single stalk that can reach 12 feet tall and produces one enormous 10 to 12 inch flower head. Not fussy about soil and drought-tolerant, it makes an attractive screen when grown in a tight row. Sow monthly for continual bloom, and leave the seed heads for birds and for harvesting edible seeds.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; this is one of the few viburnums that thrives and fruits in full dry shade under mature trees.
directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; mapleleaf viburnum is exceptional among viburnums for its tolerance of both dry conditions and full shade — characteristics that together make it one of the most useful native shrubs for planting under established deciduous trees where root competition and low light challenge most plants. The maple-like leaves turn brilliant pink to rose-pink to crimson-magenta in fall, some of the most vivid fall color of any shade-tolerant shrub. Flat-topped white flower clusters in late spring attract native bees, followed by small dark blue-black berries (technically edible but not palatable) that are consumed by songbirds. Spreads slowly by rhizome to form naturalistic colonies; one of the best native shrubs for difficult dry shade situations.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in humus-rich, well-drained soil; grown mainly for its foliage.
directions: Marmalade is a coral bells cultivar grown for its ruffled amber, caramel, and rose-tinged foliage that holds color through the seasons. It forms a neat mound and sends up airy flower spikes that attract hummingbirds. Plant in partial shade in rich, well-drained soil, keeping the crown at soil level to prevent rot. Water evenly during dry spells and mulch lightly to keep roots cool. Divide clumps every few years in spring to keep the plant vigorous and refresh its color.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in very early spring in wet, mucky, slightly acidic soil at the edge of a pond, stream, or bog garden; the plant requires consistently wet conditions and will not survive dry summer soils.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in wet to saturated, mucky, slightly acidic soil at the margin of a pond, stream, or bog garden in partial shade to full sun; marsh marigold is one of the earliest spring wildflowers in cold-climate wetlands, producing brilliant golden-yellow waxy flowers like large buttercups in March and April — sometimes pushing through the last snow at the edge of a thawing stream. It is the quintessential cold-climate bog and rain garden plant, growing naturally in springs, bogs, and stream banks from Labrador to North Carolina. All parts are mildly toxic when raw but the flower buds have been used as a caper substitute when pickled. The plant goes completely dormant in summer heat; interplant with other wetland perennials to fill the gap. Not suited to zone 8 and south where summers are too hot.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; marsh violet thrives in the consistently moist to wet conditions of stream banks, pond margins, and rain garden areas where most violets would rot.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — marsh violet is the native violet of wet, shaded environments, growing naturally along stream banks, pond margins, seepage areas, and wet woodland clearings where it produces blue-violet flowers with distinctive darker veining and a white base on the lower petals, held on stems above the characteristic heart-shaped leaves from spring through early summer. It is distinguished from the common blue violet (V. sororia) by its requirement for consistently moist to wet soil and its preference for shaded sites. An outstanding native for rain garden margins, wet woodland gardens, pond edges, and boggy areas in partial to full shade where few flowering plants thrive. Like all violets, a critical larval host plant for native fritillary butterflies. Forms attractive colonies in appropriate moist, shaded conditions by self-seeding and spreading by runners.
