plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall at least 6 weeks before the ground freezes; disturb as little as possible once established.
directions: Plant bulbs 6 in deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil; martagon lilies are unusual in tolerating partial shade and actually prefer dappled woodland conditions. Whorled leaves and pendant, turks-cap-shaped flowers in pink, purple, or white with recurved petals appear in early summer. Martagon lilies are slow to establish and may not bloom for 2 years after planting — once settled they are very long-lived and resent being disturbed. Self-seeding is common; seedlings take 4–7 years to bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost; soak seed to speed germination.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow after the last frost, covering about 1/2 inch. Named for opening in late afternoon, this fast-growing plant flourishes almost anywhere, including hot, windy spots, and tolerates most soils. Fragrant tubular flowers open after 4pm to attract hummingbirds and other pollinators, making it an excellent annual hedge. It reseeds freely and forms tubers that can be dug and stored over winter in cold climates.

plant_note: Set out after frost; treated as an annual in cold zones and a woody perennial in zones 7 and warmer.
directions: Mary Ann is a heat- and drought-tolerant lantana bearing clusters of yellow, orange, and purple-pink flowers from spring through fall. The blooms are a magnet for butterflies and other pollinators while shrugging off humidity and poor soil. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and water sparingly once established, as it thrives on neglect. In zones colder than 7 it is grown as an annual or overwintered indoors. Trim back leggy stems midseason to keep the plant compact and flowering freely.

plant_note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Red Blotch bears extra-large 3.5 inch scarlet-red flowers with dramatic dark blotches on compact, well-branched plants. Pansies love cool weather, so plant them out in early spring or fall for lush, tidy color. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, and deadhead the edible blooms to keep flowering strong.

plant_note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Rose Blotch features soft rose petals with dark centers plus occasional creamy-rose blooms for elegant color. Its compact, spreading habit gives lush coverage with little upkeep, and the edible flowers brighten dishes and drinks. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, planting in early spring or fall and deadheading to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Solar Flare dazzles with red, orange, and yellow blooms and burgundy centers outlined in golden yellow. The color shifts with temperature, showing more red in cool weather and brighter yellow tones as it warms. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, planting in early spring or fall and deadheading to keep the display vivid.

plant_note: Fall sow for natural stratification; cold-treat seed before spring sowing.
directions: This native perennial sunflower towers up to ten feet and lines its stems with golden-yellow blooms in late summer and fall. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring planting, and give it full sun with room to spread since it forms robust colonies. It is drought tolerant once established and provides valuable late-season nectar and seed for pollinators and birds. Cut back or divide clumps periodically to keep the vigorous spread in check.

plant_note: Plant rhizome sections 2 inches deep in early spring; the rhizome spreads aggressively underground to form colonies — give it ample space in a naturalized area.
directions: Plant in moist, rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; this woodland native spreads by horizontal rhizomes to form dramatic colonies of large umbrella-like leaves that carpet the forest floor. The single white flower hidden beneath the paired leaves in spring develops into a yellowish, lemon-scented fruit by midsummer — the only part of the plant that is edible when fully soft and ripe. The roots, leaves, and unripe fruit contain podophyllin, a potent toxin, and should never be consumed. The plant goes dormant and disappears by late summer, so interplant with ferns or other shade perennials to fill the void.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Sow seeds directly into warm, well-drained soil once frost danger passes, barely covering them, or start indoors a few weeks before the last frost. This compact variety produces three-inch deep pink blooms with white-tipped petals, so pinch young plants to encourage bushy branching. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to prolong bloom into fall. Zinnias tolerate heat well and make excellent cut flowers and pollinator draws.

plant_note: Cold stratification improves germination; may be fall-sown.
directions: Chilling the seed for a few weeks before sowing, or fall sowing, improves germination for this hardy perennial. Press seeds onto the surface as light aids germination, and keep soil evenly moist. This strain produces large three-inch flowers with long, graceful spurs in a wide color range, blooming in the second year. It thrives in dappled shade in rock gardens and perennial borders and self-sows readily.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost with cold stratification; or plant divisions in early spring.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist soil in partial shade; tall meadow rue creates an airy, vertical accent with blue-green, columbine-like foliage and billowing clouds of lavender-purple flowers in midsummer. The delicate flowers are held on strong, self-supporting stems and sway gracefully in a breeze. Seldom needs staking if grown in adequate light; divide every 4–5 years in early spring to keep plants vigorous. Excellent in a woodland border or at the back of a shaded perennial bed.

plant_note: Direct sow once soil is warm and frost has passed.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors for an earlier display. This variety produces vibrant scarlet, semi-double to double blooms four to five inches across on long, sturdy stems ideal for cutting. Give plants full sun and good air circulation to reduce mildew, and deadhead to keep blooms coming through frost. A reliable pollinator and butterfly magnet.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; Mexican bush sage blooms in fall and is the showiest late-season salvia for zone 8–11 landscapes.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Mexican bush sage is drought tolerant once established and demands good drainage over fertility. It is a short-day plant that blooms from September through frost, producing velvety purple flower spikes loved by hummingbirds and bumblebees; the later into fall it blooms, the more spectacular the display. Cut the entire plant back to 6–8 in after the last hard freeze in late winter to stimulate dense new basal growth. Propagate easily by dividing the clump in spring or rooting stem cuttings in moist perlite.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date in a sunny, dry site; self-seeds profusely — site away from natural areas.
directions: Sow seeds directly in full sun in lean, very well-drained soil — rich or moist conditions produce weak, floppy plants. The hair-fine, bright green leaves form airy, billowing mounds that catch every breeze, creating a constant motion in the landscape; the foliage turns blonde and golden in summer heat. This grass self-seeds very aggressively and is considered invasive in California and some southwestern states — deadhead before seeds ripen if growing in areas with native grasslands. Cut back hard in late winter before new growth begins.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; in zone 8 it may die back to roots in hard freezes but re-sprouts in spring.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; Mexican sage is a large, bold, shrubby species with broad, deeply veined, highly aromatic leaves and long wands of blue-purple flowers from late summer through fall that attract hummingbirds and bumblebees. It is native to mountain forests and canyon edges in central Mexico at moderate elevation and tolerates partial shade better than most sages, making it useful in the lightly shaded areas of a Gulf Coast or zone 8–9 garden. The foliage has a strong, pleasant herbal fragrance when brushed and is deer resistant. It grows rapidly to a large sub-shrub in one season and may be damaged or killed by hard freezes but typically re-sprouts from the root crown in zone 8. Shear back by half in mid-spring to encourage compact, heavily blooming growth.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes — one of the best heat-loving annuals for attracting butterflies and hummingbirds.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; transplant carefully as Tithonia dislikes root disturbance — use biodegradable pots if starting indoors. Mexican sunflower thrives in heat, poor soil, and dry conditions, making it ideal for back-of-border plantings in Gulf Coast summers when other annuals falter. The vivid orange, 3-inch daisy-like flowers are irresistible to monarch butterflies, swallowtails, and hummingbirds from midsummer through frost. Deadhead regularly by cutting stems (not just the flower head) back to a side shoot to maintain tidy plants and prolong heavy bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Mexican sunflower is a tall, bushy annual covered in vibrant orange-red daisy blooms that are a favorite of monarch butterflies. Sow seeds directly after frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning to give the vigorous plants room to reach up to six feet. It tolerates heat and poor soil well and blooms nonstop from midsummer until frost. Deadhead to keep the flush going, and stake in exposed sites since the branching stems can grow heavy.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in part shade.
directions: Mighty Chocolate Cherry is a tall astilbe with dramatic cherry-red plumes rising above green-burgundy chocolate-toned foliage. It brings bold vertical color to shaded, moist borders and woodland gardens. Plant in partial shade in rich soil that stays evenly moist, since astilbe suffers if it dries out. Water regularly and mulch to conserve moisture and keep roots cool. Cut back spent foliage in late fall and divide clumps every three to four years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Surface sow; needs light and dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow mignonette on the soil surface, as it needs light to germinate and resents root disturbance. The unremarkable greenish flower spikes release an intensely sweet raspberry-like fragrance beloved in Victorian gardens. Grow in full sun to light shade in cool weather and fertile, alkaline soil. Position it near paths and seating where its perfume can be enjoyed, and make successive sowings for a long season.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; resents transplanting.
directions: Sow directly where plants are to grow, as California poppies have a taproot and dislike transplanting. Scatter seed on prepared soil in early spring or fall and press in lightly. This selection bears saucer-shaped, bright orange-red flowers that open in sun and close at night or on cloudy days. Heat- and drought-tolerant once established, it needs little care and often self-sows for years of color.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring where plants will grow; do not transplant.
directions: This California poppy strain glows with deep red-orange, saucer-shaped blooms that open in sun and close at night. Sow seeds directly in early spring where they are to grow, pressing them onto the surface since the taproot resents transplanting. Full sun and lean, well-drained soil suit it best, and it needs little water once established. It self-sows reliably to return and naturalize in gardens and meadows.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed or fall sow for best germination.
directions: Cold-moist stratify the seed for several weeks or sow in fall to break dormancy for this heirloom perennial. Direct sow where plants will grow, as the deep taproot resists transplanting. From summer onward it produces clusters of fiery red-orange flowers, a preferred nectar source for monarchs and other pollinators, followed by ornamental seed pods. Drought tolerant once established, it is best left undisturbed to form a long-lived clump.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow in early spring since this annual dislikes transplanting; successive sowings extend the bloom. Cover seed lightly and keep evenly moist until germination. Airy, fern-like foliage surrounds jewel-toned blue and pastel flowers that attract pollinators and shine in bouquets. The inflated seed pods that follow are prized for dried arrangements; leave some to self-sow for next year.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; resents transplanting.
directions: Sow seed directly onto prepared soil in early spring or fall, since the taproot resents being moved. This blend produces ruffled, saucer-shaped double and semi-double flowers in rose, red, orange, white, and yellow. Heat- and drought-tolerant, it asks little once established and readily self-sows for years of carpeted color. Grow in full sun for the best flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; needs cool soil to germinate.
directions: Direct sow in cool soil in early spring or in fall, as larkspur germinates best at cool temperatures and dislikes transplanting; chilling the seed helps. This variety bears densely packed nine- to twelve-inch spikes of double, ruffled florets in soft greyed lavender and dusty mauve-purple. Provide full sun and support for the tall stems, which make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers. Frost tolerant when young.

plant_note: Plant divisions 2–4 inches apart in spring or fall; in warm climates fall planting takes advantage of cooler temperatures for root establishment before summer heat.
directions: Plant divisions in virtually any soil type — from dry sandy to moderately moist — in full shade to partial sun; this tough Asian native is one of the most versatile shade groundcovers for southern gardens. Once established it smothers weeds, requires no mowing, and remains attractive year-round with its dark, grass-like evergreen foliage and small blue-black berries in fall. Mow or shear the entire planting to 2–3 inches in late winter every few years to remove older, ratty foliage and encourage fresh, uniform growth. Divide overgrown clumps every 4–5 years, replanting at the original spacing to maintain density.

plant_note: Sow in spring or fall; as a biennial it flowers the second year.
directions: Money plant, or silver dollar plant, is grown for its charming purple spring flowers and the translucent, coin-shaped seed pods that follow. Sow seeds shallowly in spring or fall, remembering that as a biennial it forms leaves the first year and blooms the second. It grows well in partial shade with average moisture and self-sows to keep returning. Leave the pods to dry on the plant, then peel the papery outer layers for silvery dried arrangements.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Direct sow one inch deep after frost in a sunny, sheltered site protected from strong wind, given the towering height. Each single-stem plant produces one enormous yellow flower up to eighteen inches across filled with large, tasty kernels. Provide rich soil and steady moisture to support the massive growth, and stake in exposed locations. Leave heads to ripen for edible seeds or to feed birds.

plant_note: Plant tuber-like roots in fall or early spring in a cool, moist, partially shaded site; wear gloves — all parts are highly toxic.
directions: Plant with the tuber-like roots 3–4 in deep in rich, cool, moist, well-drained soil; monkshood is a stately perennial with bold, deeply lobed foliage and tall spikes of deep blue-violet helmet-shaped flowers in late summer to early fall. All parts of the plant are extremely toxic if ingested or if the sap contacts broken skin — always wear gloves when handling and wash hands thoroughly after. It is one of the best late-season blues in the perennial garden and thrives where summers are cool. Divide every 4–5 years in early spring; keep plants where they will not be disturbed by children or pets.

plant_note: Plant corms in spring; naturalizes and can spread widely.
directions: Plant montbretia corms in spring three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. The arching sprays of fiery orange to red tubular flowers in mid to late summer are magnets for hummingbirds. It multiplies rapidly by cormlets and can spread widely, so site it where vigorous growth is welcome. Divide congested clumps every few years and mulch for winter in the colder end of its range.

plant_note: Start indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost after nicking or soaking seed; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Nick or soak seeds in warm water for 12 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep. Moonflower is a vigorous, fast-growing vine that needs a strong trellis or fence. Unlike morning glories, its large, fragrant white blossoms open in the evening and remain open through the night and into the following morning. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers to encourage blooming.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; or nick and soak seed overnight then start indoors 2–4 weeks early.
directions: Nick the hard seed coat with a file or soak in warm water for 8–12 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep to speed germination. Morning glories need a fence, trellis, or other support and are best sown directly at the base of their support. Avoid over-fertilizing, which promotes leaves at the expense of flowers. Blooms open in the morning and close by afternoon on each individual day.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Surface sow the fine seed directly in early spring or fall, as poppies need light to germinate and resent root disturbance. This blend glows in soft shades of pink, white, and purple with crepe-paper petals and contrasting dark centers. Low-maintenance and drought-tolerant, it is stunning massed along borders and self-sows to return each year. Thin seedlings to give plants room.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; mountain laurel blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering and never remove more than one-third of the plant.
directions: Plant in well-drained, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial to light shade; mountain laurel is the state flower of Connecticut and Pennsylvania and one of the finest native flowering shrubs of the eastern woodlands, producing intricate, geometrically precise cup-and-saucer flowers in white, pink, bicolored, and banded forms in late spring. The flowers have a remarkable mechanism — the stamens are bent under tension and spring upright when touched by a bee, catapulting pollen onto the pollinator. All parts of the plant are toxic. It performs best in cool, moist, acidic woodland gardens and benefits from afternoon shade in zone 7–8. Named cultivars like 'Olympic Fire' (deep red buds, pink flowers), 'Elf' (compact dwarf), and 'Galaxy' (burgundy-red buds) offer exceptional garden forms. Mulch heavily with pine bark or oak leaves to maintain soil acidity and moisture.

plant_note: Plant in fall; wear gloves as bulbs can irritate skin.
directions: Plant multiflora hyacinth bulbs in fall about six inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Unlike single-spike hyacinths, this type sends up several looser, fragrant flower stalks per bulb for a naturalistic effect. Wear gloves when handling, as the bulbs can irritate sensitive skin. Deadhead after bloom and let foliage ripen; flower size may diminish over the years as it perennializes.

plant_note: Fall sowing or cold stratification improves germination of this native seed.
directions: This prairie native coneflower carries purple-pink blooms with drooping petals around a spiny central cone from early to midsummer. The seed germinates best after cold, so sow outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, where its deep taproot makes it very drought tolerant once established. It supports bees and butterflies, and its roots have a long history of medicinal use.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks before last frost; nasturtiums prefer cool soil and bolt in summer heat.
directions: Soak large seeds overnight then direct sow 1/2 in deep in poor, well-drained soil. Avoid fertilizing, as rich soil produces lush leaves at the expense of flowers. Deadhead spent blooms to prolong the season, or allow them to set seed and collect for next year. Both flowers and leaves are edible with a peppery flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors 4-6 weeks earlier.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost or start indoors four to six weeks earlier for a head start. This easy garden standard produces single golden-yellow blooms marked with maroon that flower continuously from summer to frost. Fairly drought tolerant, it thrives in full sun and works well as a border, bedding, or companion plant. Deadhead to keep the blooms coming.

plant_note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; extremely slow growing but very long-lived. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; needle palm is the world's most cold-hardy palm, surviving temperatures as low as 0°F to -5°F — making it the only truly cold-hardy palm for zone 7 and one of the outstanding choices for providing a tropical look in surprisingly cold climates. It grows naturally in the understory of moist coastal plain forests and swamp margins in the southeast US (Florida, Georgia, South Carolina) and is adapted to significant shade. It is extremely slow growing (3–6 inches per year) and develops into a clumping, stemless or short-stemmed palm with dramatic fan-shaped leaves. The sharp black needle-like spines on the leaf sheaths deter both deer and humans. Excellent for creating tropical texture in a shaded southern garden.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in fine-textured germination mix at 60–65°F; avoid higher temperatures as heat inhibits germination. Plant outdoors in cool weather in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade where summers are hot. Nemesia blooms prolifically in cool conditions with cheerful two-toned flowers in a huge range of colors; it typically falters in summer heat so shear back and move containers to a cooler spot. Plants may revive and rebloom in fall when temperatures drop again.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in very acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; netted chain fern requires strongly acidic, boggy conditions and will not establish in neutral or alkaline soils.
directions: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; netted chain fern is a native fern of moist, acidic pocosins, pine flatwoods, bogs, and shaded swamp margins throughout the eastern US coastal plain, immediately distinguished from similar chain ferns by the pattern of small, net-like (areolate) veins between the primary leaf veins on sterile fronds. The sterile fronds are pinnate with broadly winged midribs; the fertile fronds look strikingly different — much narrower and almost completely covered by elongated chain-like sori along each midrib. It spreads by rhizome in wet, acid soil to form attractive colonies. An excellent native fern for bog gardens, wet acidic woodland edges, or naturalized areas with consistently wet, acidic soil in the Southeast and coastal plain.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in a damp paper towel in the refrigerator before sowing indoors to improve germination rates. Pinch plants back by one-third in late spring to encourage bushier growth and delay bloom until fall, when their purple flowers are invaluable for pollinators. Tall varieties may need staking or can be grown through a peony cage. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in early spring to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

plant_note: Fall sow for cold stratification; spring sowing works after chilling seed.
directions: New England aster is a tall native perennial that bursts into pink-to-purple blooms from late summer into fall when many flowers have faded. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing, and give plants full sun with room to reach several feet tall. It is a vital late-season nectar source for migrating monarchs and other pollinators. Pinch stems in early summer for bushier growth, and divide clumps every few years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; new Jersey tea has an extremely deep taproot and is very difficult to transplant once established — site it carefully the first time.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; New Jersey tea is a remarkable native shrub with one of the most interesting histories in American botany — the dried leaves were widely used as a tea substitute during the Revolutionary War when British tea was boycotted. The fluffy clusters of tiny white flowers in early summer are among the most important nectar sources in the native prairie and woodland edge, attracting dozens of bee species, skippers, and hairstreak butterflies. The roots fix atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil fertility over time. This shrub is exceptionally drought-tolerant once its deep taproot is established, thriving on rocky, infertile, dry soils where few shrubs succeed. The spring bee value alone makes it an outstanding native garden plant. It resents being moved after establishment, so site it correctly from the start.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for 60 days before sowing, or direct sow in fall and allow natural winter stratification; transplant in spring. Plants benefit from cool stratification and will not germinate reliably from unsratified seed.
directions: Plant in moist, average soil in full sun to partial shade; ironweed is a tall native perennial of moist meadows and streambanks producing intensely saturated royal-purple flower clusters in late summer and fall that are unmatched for pure color saturation in the late-season garden. The flowers are extraordinary butterfly magnets, particularly for monarchs, swallowtails, and skippers fueling up for fall migration. Ironweed is called 'iron' because the stems are so tough and fibrous that they were once used as tying material. To control height in garden settings, cut plants back by half in late June before flowering; this delays bloom slightly and reduces final height from 6–7 feet to 4–5 feet. Self-seeds moderately — deadhead after flowering if not needed.

plant_note: Surface sow seeds 4–6 weeks before last frost (cold stratification for 30 days improves germination) or divide established clumps in spring.
directions: Surface sow seed in moist, average to rich soil in full sun to partial shade; ironweed produces some of the most intensely violet-purple flowers in the late-summer and fall native garden (late August through October), providing critical nectar for migrating monarchs and native bees. Its strong, wiry stems (hence 'ironweed') resist lodging and require no staking. The plant spreads by self-seeding and root division; remove unwanted seedlings in spring when small. Pinch the growing tips once in late spring to encourage a shorter, more branched plant. Excellent when combined with tall goldenrods and asters for a spectacular native fall display.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface sow as light aids germination.
directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost, pressing the fine seed onto the surface since light aids germination. This spicy-scented snapdragon bears dark red blooms that deepen to near-black in fall, with foliage that darkens too. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and set out after hardening off. A bumblebee favorite that makes long-lasting, striking cut flowers.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors for earlier bloom. This custom blend produces large four- to five-inch flowers in a dazzling mix of colors that draw butterflies and pollinators. Plant in masses for the best show, give plenty of sun and airflow to limit mildew, and deadhead to extend flowering. Excellent for cutting.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring, barely covering the rhizome with soil; water gently until established.
directions: This elegant native fern requires deep to dappled shade and reliably moist, near-neutral to slightly alkaline soil; it is particularly stunning near water features where humidity stays high. Plant the shallow, creeping rhizome horizontally just below the soil surface and avoid root disturbance once established. The distinctive fan-shaped, horizontally tiered fronds on dark wiry stems die back completely in winter; mark the location to avoid accidentally digging the dormant rhizome. Divide overgrown clumps in early spring by gently separating rhizome sections, each with at least one growing tip.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; light aids germination.
directions: Surface sow the tiny seed in early spring or fall, as light is needed for germination and plants dislike transplanting. Often grown as a hardy annual or short-lived perennial, it produces chiffon-like blooms in orange, pale pink, yellow, and white that glow when backlit by the sun. Blooming in spring, it is fairly drought tolerant and prefers cool weather. Deadhead to prolong the display.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; blooms on old wood — do not prune until after bloom or you will remove next year's flower buds.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; oakleaf hydrangea is the premier four-season native flowering shrub for shade, offering showy white cone-shaped flower panicles in early summer that fade gracefully to dusty pink and tan and persist on the plant, then brilliant orange-red-purple fall foliage, and finally striking cinnamon-brown exfoliating bark on thick, architectural stems all winter. It is native to shaded ravines and woodland slopes in the Southeast. The shrub blooms on old wood — do not prune unless absolutely necessary, and only immediately after bloom. Named cultivars like 'Snow Queen' (large upright blooms), 'Pee Wee' (compact), and 'Ruby Slippers' (pink flowers) are widely available and all perform well in zone 8–9 gardens.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants bloom in year two from seed but transplants purchased in spring bloom the first year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination. Plant in moist to average soil in full sun; established plants spread aggressively by underground rhizomes and may require annual division to prevent them from overtaking the border. The name refers to the curious behavior of individual florets staying in place when moved along the square stem. The pink to lilac spikes bloom in late summer and fall when pollinators are seeking food before winter.

plant_note: Fall sow for natural cold stratification; spring sow after chilling.
directions: Ohio spiderwort is a hardy native perennial with grassy foliage and clusters of blue to lavender three-petaled flowers that open fresh each morning. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing for best germination. It adapts to full sun or partial shade and average to moist soil, spreading gently to form clumps. Shear back after the first bloom flush to tidy the plant and encourage a second round of flowers.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; tolerates light frost.
directions: Direct sow in early spring, covering seed as darkness aids germination; calendula prefers cool weather and tolerates light frost. This compact variety bears single and double blooms in orange, cream, and yellow tipped with fiery orange. Deadhead often to keep the profusion of blooms going, and pair with blue or purple annuals in containers. The petals are edible and attractive in salads.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; resents transplanting.
directions: Sow seed directly where plants will grow in early spring or fall, since the taproot dislikes transplanting. This heirloom, the state flower of California, produces golden-orange flowers that open in sun and close at night. Drought tolerant and low-maintenance, it attracts pollinators and self-sows freely for naturalized hillsides of color. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained soil.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring; seed requires cold stratification but the cultivar 'Goldsturm' comes true from seed with stratification.
directions: Plant in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Rudbeckia fulgida is the perennial species behind the famous 'Goldsturm' cultivar that revolutionized native plant gardening in the 1980s. Unlike the biennial R. hirta (common black-eyed Susan), this species returns reliably from the crown each year and spreads gradually by short rhizomes to form impressive colonies. The bright orange-yellow flowers with dark brown centers bloom from July through September and attract finches to the persistent seed heads all winter. Divide every 4 years in spring to maintain vigor; plants in rich, moist soil may need staking but grow more compact in lean conditions.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface sow as light aids germination.
directions: Start indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost, pressing seed onto the surface as light aids germination. This variety wows with peachy-orange blossoms brushed with pink above lush green foliage on long stems. Pinch young plants for bushier growth and cut freely for long-lasting bouquets. The blooms are edible and make a whimsical garnish for salads and desserts.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring as soon as the ground can be worked; fall planting is preferred in zones 4–7.
directions: Plant bulbs 6–8 in deep (3 times the bulb diameter) in well-drained, organically rich, slightly acidic soil; apply mulch to keep roots cool. Oriental lilies prefer to have their heads in the sun and their feet in the shade — interplant with low perennials to shade the root zone. The large, intensely fragrant blooms appear in mid- to late summer; deadhead after flowering but allow foliage to remain until it yellows naturally. Avoid overhead watering to reduce the risk of botrytis; stake tall cultivars against wind damage.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring; light aids germination.
directions: Surface sow in early spring, pressing the fine seed onto the soil since light aids germination. This variety forms a low, spreading carpet of fragrant deep purple blooms that flower all season and spill nicely over container edges. Withstanding heat and drought, it is an ideal rock garden and edging plant that draws pollinators. Shear lightly midseason if flowering slows.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seeds need cool soil and germinate best when sown directly outdoors.
directions: Surface-sow seeds in early spring on well-prepared, well-drained soil; seeds need cool temperatures and light, so press firmly without covering. Oriental poppies bloom for a brief but spectacular 2–3 weeks in late spring to early summer with crepe-paper petals in orange, red, pink, or white up to 6 in across. Plants go dormant after bloom leaving a bare spot — interplant with late-emerging annuals like baby's breath or dahlias to fill the gap. Divide clumps in late summer while dormant; established plants dislike being moved during the growing season.

plant_note: Surface sow in cool spring or fall where plants are to grow; needs light and cool soil.
directions: Oriental poppy is a long-lived perennial famous for its huge, silky scarlet blooms with dark purple-black centers in late spring. Surface-sow the fine seeds in cool spring or fall directly where they are to grow, since poppies dislike transplanting and germinate best in cool soil with light. Give full sun and well-drained ground, and expect the foliage to die back after flowering as the plant goes summer dormant. Mark the location so you do not disturb the crown, which resprouts in fall.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall where winters are mild.
directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in early spring as they need light and cool temperatures to germinate; do not cover seeds. Orlaya produces flat-topped umbel flowers with large, irregular outer florets that create a delicate lace effect in pure white, ideal as a cut flower filler. It prefers lean, well-drained soil — overly rich conditions produce lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants self-sow readily where happy, returning reliably if the ground is not disturbed; collect seed or allow them to fall for the following year.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before the ground freezes, for late-spring to early-summer bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times their diameter in sharply drained soil; the large-flowered types like 'Globemaster' can be planted 12 in deep for stability. Foliage often begins to yellow while the flowers are still in bloom — interplanting with perennials helps mask the dying leaves. Deer and rodents avoid alliums, making them a good choice for wildlife-prone gardens. Spherical seedheads are ornamentally interesting and can be dried for arrangements.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil has warmed thoroughly.
directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost at 70–75°F; transplant after frost danger passes into full sun and fertile, well-drained soil — warm soil is essential for strong establishment. Grown as an annual for its striking deep burgundy-purple foliage and the large, cylindrical flower/grain heads that arch gracefully under their own weight in late summer. An excellent anchor plant in tropical-style or mixed annual borders, and the dried seed heads are useful in arrangements. Highly attractive to birds in fall — leave some plants intact for wildlife value.

plant_note: Plant crowns or divisions in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; keep consistently moist until established.
directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in full to partial shade, setting crowns at or just below soil level with sterile fronds pointing outward. The vase-shaped plant spreads by underground stolons to form large colonies over time; divide every 3–4 years to manage spread and rejuvenate growth. Harvest tightly coiled fiddleheads in early spring when they emerge no more than 2–3 inches from the ground and are still tightly coiled; blanch before eating. Avoid dry or compacted soils, as this fern is adapted to stream banks and flood plains.

plant_note: Direct sow spring or fall; biennial that blooms in year two.
directions: Direct sow in spring or fall against a wall or fence that can support the towering spikes; as a biennial it typically blooms the second year. Barely cover the seed and keep moist until germination. This old cottage-garden hollyhock reaches several feet tall with a long bloom period, and the flowers are edible though bland. Stake in windy sites and allow some plants to self-sow for continuous stands.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; tolerates light frost.
directions: Direct sow in early spring, covering seed since darkness aids germination; calendula thrives in cool weather and tolerates light frost. This classic cottage-garden blend bears gold and orange flowers from spring to fall on drought- and heat-tolerant plants. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and save the edible petals for teas, salads, and salves. Grow in full sun for the fullest flowering.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost in cool soil.
directions: Sow Pacific Beauty calendula a quarter inch deep near your last frost date, favoring the cool weather this heirloom loves. It produces a cheerful daisy-like mix of gold, orange, and cream blooms on plants up to two feet tall. The petals are both edible and valued in herbal skin-care preparations. Deadhead regularly for a long season, and let a few blooms set seed to self-sow.

plant_note: Start indoors early; chilling seed improves germination.
directions: Start seed indoors early, chilling it briefly to improve germination for this award-winning heirloom perennial. Set plants in rich, well-drained soil and grow in groups of three or more for impact. Tall, elegant spires pack large blossoms in pink, violet, and many shades of blue; stake the stems against wind. Cut back after the first flush to encourage a second bloom, and provide steady moisture.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; pagoda dogwood needs some protection from afternoon sun in zones 6–7 and is best grown in northern and mountain climates.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; pagoda dogwood is a striking native understory tree distinguished from flowering dogwood (Cornus florida) by its horizontally tiered, pagoda-like branching structure and alternate (rather than opposite) leaves — hence both common names. The flat-topped clusters of small creamy-white flowers in late spring attract many native bee species, and the blue-black berries on red stems in late summer are highly attractive to migrating warblers and thrushes. The plant thrives in cool, moist, shaded conditions and performs best in zones 3–6; it struggles in southern heat and is not suited to zone 8 and south. Fall foliage is an attractive rose-burgundy. The horizontal branching structure is most dramatic in winter silhouette, making it a year-round specimen tree.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in cool soil.
directions: This tricolor painted daisy opens bold three-inch blooms with concentric rings of white, pink, red, or yellow around a dark bullseye center. Sow seeds directly in early spring in cool soil, covering lightly and thinning seedlings once they establish. Grow in full sun with average moisture, and stake if plants grow lanky in rich soil. Deadhead to extend the display and cut stems for cheerful, long-lasting bouquets.

plant_note: Sow in place in early spring for summer color.
directions: Direct sow annual painted daisy in early spring where plants are to bloom. The colorful banded daisies with contrasting central rings make superb long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil, staking taller plants in windy sites. Deadhead frequently to prolong flowering, and the plants often self-sow lightly in favorable conditions.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds in the dark (cover lightly and keep the tray in darkness) at 60–70°F; darkness improves germination for salpiglossis. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the petunia-like flowers with intricate veining in yellow, red, purple, maroon, and bicolor thrive in cool weather. Provide light support with twiggy stakes since plants can flop in wind; pinch tips once for bushier plants. Plants decline in summer heat — treat them as a spring and fall flower in warm climates or plant where afternoon shade moderates temperatures.

plant_note: Plant divisions immediately upon receiving in early spring in very acidic, moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this plant is difficult to establish and should be purchased only from reputable nursery-propagated sources as it is protected in many states.
directions: Plant in moist, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; painted trillium is widely regarded as the most beautiful of the eastern trilliums, producing white flowers with a vivid magenta-pink V-shaped painted mark at the base of each petal in late spring that gives the plant its common name. It grows in coniferous and mixed forest understories with strongly acidic soil, particularly in association with hemlock and pine. It is more demanding of soil acidity than the common great white trillium (T. grandiflorum) and more difficult to establish in the garden. Purchase only nursery-propagated divisions from reputable sources, as wild populations are declining due to collection pressure and deer browsing. Very slow from seed — divisions from established colonies are the only practical propagation method.

plant_note: Fall sowing or cold stratification improves germination of this native seed.
directions: Pale purple coneflower is a native prairie perennial with elegant, drooping lavender-pink petals around a tall spiky cone. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing to break dormancy. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, where the deep taproot makes it very drought tolerant once established. It draws bees and butterflies in bloom and finches to the seed heads, which can be left standing for winter interest.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm, or start indoors.
directions: Direct sow after frost into warm soil, or start indoors a few weeks earlier, pressing the fine seed onto the surface for light. This blend produces large, feathery plumes ranging from golden-yellow to scarlet on long, straight stems that attract pollinators. The multi-branching plants have handsome foliage and love heat. Harvest for fresh bouquets or hang to dry for lasting arrangements.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Surface sow the tiny seed in early spring or fall, as poppies need light to germinate and resent transplanting. Tall, slender stems are topped with two- to three-inch silky double and semi-double blooms that shift from deep burgundy to soft pinkish-red, each striped with silver. Pollinators love them, and no two flowers look alike. Thin seedlings and let some self-sow for next season.

plant_note: Start indoors early in darkness; cool-season bedding plant.
directions: Start seed indoors in darkness eight to ten weeks before setting out, since pansies germinate best without light and prefer cool conditions. This gold-standard performer brightens cool-season gardens with rosy-red blooms and yellow centers on a compact, well-branched habit that resists winter weather. Grow in full sun to part shade in borders, containers, or mass plantings. The flowers are edible and hold up to frost.

plant_note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost for spring planting, or start in late summer for fall transplanting. Plants tolerate hard frost once established.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a moist starting mix; cover the tray with dark plastic as darkness aids germination. Transplant outdoors several weeks before the last frost, as cool weather brings out the richest colors and most abundant bloom. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to prevent seed set, which stops flowering. Plants decline as summer heat arrives; remove and replace with heat-tolerant annuals.

plant_note: Start indoors; cold stratification aids germination.
directions: Start seed indoors, giving it a cold-moist stratification period to improve germination for this first-year-flowering dwarf echinacea. Set plants in full sun and well-drained soil where the vibrant blooms will draw pollinators. Nearly care-free once established and drought tolerant, it makes a fine fresh or dried cut flower. Leave the seed cones standing for winter interest and to feed birds.

plant_note: Plant in fall in a sheltered spot; blooms are heavy.
directions: Plant parrot tulip bulbs in autumn six to eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and a spot sheltered from wind. The flamboyant fringed and twisted petals are streaked and feathered in bold colors, making dramatic cut flowers. Because the large blooms can flop, choose a protected location and stake if necessary. Let foliage die back naturally, and in mild-winter areas chill bulbs before planting.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; parsley hawthorn tolerates more shade than most hawthorns and grows naturally as an understory tree in bottomland forests.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; parsley hawthorn is the most delicate and ornamental of the southeastern hawthorns, distinguished by its exquisitely cut, deeply lobed leaves that truly resemble flat-leaf parsley — giving it an unusually fine, lacy texture among native trees. White flower clusters in spring are followed by small red berries in fall that provide wildlife food. It grows naturally as an understory tree in moist bottomland forests and shaded river banks throughout the Southeast and tolerates shade better than most hawthorn species. The gray-brown bark flakes to reveal attractive smooth orange-tan underbark. An underused native tree that deserves much wider garden use for its ornamental leaf texture and wildlife value.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in poor, sandy, or gravelly soil in full sun; scarify seed by nicking or soaking in hot water for 24 hours before sowing to break dormancy. Self-sows reliably to return each year.
directions: Scarify and direct sow after last frost in lean, sandy, or disturbed soil in full sun; do not add fertilizer or enrich the soil, as partridge pea is a nitrogen-fixer that thrives in poor conditions where competitors cannot crowd it out. The bright yellow flowers in midsummer are the primary pollen source for bumble bee specialist Xylocopa and Megachile species that cannot use other pollen sources. It is also a critical larval host plant for the cloudless sulphur, sleepy orange, and little yellow butterflies. The seed pods provide food for quail, wild turkey, and bobwhite. The plant behaves as a self-sowing annual and colonizes disturbed areas, roadside shoulders, and restoration plantings rapidly.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall; sap and foliage can irritate skin — handle with gloves and avoid contact with eyes.
directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny, gravelly, or rocky, very well-drained site; pasque flower germinates best from fresh seed with natural cold stratification and is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled. Silky, nodding purple, lavender, or white flowers emerge in early spring from the fuzzy, hairy foliage and are followed by fluffy, ornamental seedheads similar to clematis. Perfectly drained, lean, alkaline or neutral soil is essential — clay or continuously wet conditions are fatal. Resents transplanting; sow in situ or plant container-grown specimens when small.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow the large seed about half an inch deep after frost, as nasturtiums dislike transplanting; soaking overnight speeds sprouting. This compact variety bears creamy peach blooms with red centers above rounded leaves. Both flowers and leaves have a peppery, watercress-like flavor that livens up salads. Grow in lean soil and full sun, since rich soil promotes leaves over flowers.

plant_note: Set out dormant or potted plants in spring after hard frost.
directions: Plant this vigorous climber from a potted or bare-root plant in spring, setting it in full sun with rich, well-drained soil at the base of a sturdy trellis or fence. It offers a cascading display of soft pink blooms and is renowned for thriving in tough conditions with minimal care, even shrugging off common rose troubles. Train the long canes horizontally to maximize flowering and water deeply while establishing. A fast grower that rewards patience with abundant bloom.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring, spacing them closely (6–8 inches) for faster coverage; this sedge spreads slowly by stolons to fill in over 2–3 years.
directions: Plant divisions or plugs in dry to moist, acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; this fine-textured native sedge is one of the best lawn substitutes for shaded areas under oak trees and other acid-soil trees where grass refuses to grow. It forms a soft, undulating carpet of arching, deep-green foliage that requires little or no mowing — mow once in late winter if desired for tidiness. Unlike most groundcovers, it tolerates both dry shade under large trees and occasional foot traffic. Plant at 6-inch spacing for coverage within 2 years; once established it spreads steadily by stolons without becoming invasive.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seeds benefit from 4 weeks of cold stratification.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks in damp medium in the refrigerator before sowing indoors for best germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained to dry soil. Penstemon thrives in poor, gravelly soil and is one of the most important native nectar plants for hummingbirds and native bees. The tubular white to pale pink flowers appear in late spring to early summer; deadhead to a basal rosette of leaves after bloom. Excellent drainage is critical — wet winter conditions are the leading cause of plant loss.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist fine-textured mix and provide light at 70–80°F; germination occurs within 7–14 days. Pentas is one of the best butterfly and hummingbird plants for hot, humid gardens, blooming continuously from transplant to frost without deadheading. Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil and fertilize monthly to sustain the heavy flower production. Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy; they recover quickly in heat and resume flowering within 2–3 weeks.

plant_note: Plant bare-root divisions in fall 4–6 weeks before the ground freezes; eyes no deeper than 1–2 in below soil surface.
directions: Plant roots with the pink eyes no more than 1–2 in below the soil surface — planting too deeply is the most common reason peonies fail to bloom. Peonies require 6 weeks of temperatures below 40°F to set flower buds, so they need cold winters and are not suited to zones 9+. Expect minimal bloom the first 1–2 years as the plant establishes; mature plants can live 50+ years with little care. Cut foliage to the ground in fall to remove potential disease inoculum.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Surface sow the fine seed directly in early spring or fall, as these poppies need light to germinate and resent root disturbance. Fully double, three- to five-inch ruffled flowers in many colors rise above soft, blue-green foliage that pollinators adore. Thin seedlings to give each plant room, and leave the decorative seed pods for dried arrangements. Fairly low-maintenance and self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; loves heat.
directions: This zinnia produces cheerful 2 to 4 inch blooms striped and speckled in bright colors, with no two flowers exactly alike. Heat-loving and easy to grow, it brightens beds, borders, and containers until fall frost and makes an excellent cut flower. Sow directly in warm soil or start indoors, and deadhead spent blooms to keep flowering. Give it full sun and good airflow to limit powdery mildew.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then direct sow in early spring at the base of a trellis, fence, or arbor for this everlasting perennial sweet pea. The long, lush trailing vines ramble beautifully over shrubs or serve as a groundcover and return each year. It withstands summer heat well, is fairly drought tolerant, and attracts pollinators, though unlike annual sweet peas the flowers are not fragrant. Note that the seeds are not edible.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained soil; established plants are drought-tolerant.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil — perennial salvia is exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant once established and will rot in soggy conditions. Shear plants back by one-third to one-half after the first flush of deep violet-blue spikes fades to trigger a strong second (and sometimes third) bloom. The aromatic foliage deters deer and rabbits, making it a reliable choice for wildlife-challenged gardens. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to keep them vigorous.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the seed, then direct sow in early spring at the foot of a trellis, as sweet peas relish cool weather and need support to climb. This rainbow blend of large, ruffled flowers in purple, red, rose, scarlet, white, blue, cream, and bicolors carries an exquisite old-fashioned fragrance. More heat resistant than many strains, it still fades in high summer, so sow early. Cut often to keep the fragrant blooms coming.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; this narrow-leaved Mexican zinnia handles hot, dry conditions with ease. It bears bicolored single and double flowers in gold, burgundy, cream, red, and orange that bloom until first frost. Compact and reliable, it suits beds, borders, and containers and needs little water once established. Deadhead to extend the display and draw pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; adapted to hot, dry climates, this species-type zinnia asks little water once established. Its simple, open, single flowers make ideal landing pads for pollinators and self-sow to return year after year. Give full sun and good airflow to limit mildew, and cut or deadhead to keep the airy plants blooming. A charming, drought-tolerant choice for naturalized plantings.

plant_note: Plant tuberous roots in spring; superb cut flower.
directions: Plant Peruvian lily tuberous roots in spring about six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil, handling the brittle roots carefully. The orchid-like flowers in warm oranges, pinks, and reds are among the longest-lasting of all cut flowers. Grow in full sun to light shade with consistent moisture, and pull rather than cut spent stems to stimulate rebloom. In colder zones mulch heavily for winter or lift the roots for storage.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface sow as light aids germination.
directions: Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing the tiny seed onto the surface since light is essential for germination. This compact blend blooms prolifically all summer in red, rose, scarlet, salmon, violet, and white, attracting butterflies and pollinators. Set out after frost in full sun in window boxes, borders, and containers. Deadhead and feed regularly to sustain the heavy flowering.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow tiny seed on the surface of moist starting mix — do not cover, as petunias need light to germinate. Pinch transplants by one-third when planting to encourage branching. Deadhead or shear back leggy plants by half in midsummer to renew vigor and flush of bloom. Feed every two weeks with a balanced liquid fertilizer for best performance.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors 4-6 weeks earlier.
directions: Direct sow after frost or start indoors four to six weeks earlier for this tall African marigold. It produces large, bright yellow, fully double blooms on sturdy stems that make excellent cut flowers. Give it full sun and space to reach its mature height, staking if needed in exposed sites. Deadhead to keep the big, cheerful flowers coming from summer until frost.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in containers of heavy clay soil and submerge in 4–12 inches of water after water temperatures reach 60°F; or plant directly in shallow pond margins.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in shallow water (4–12 inches deep) in clay-loam soil in full sun; pickerel rush is one of the most essential native aquatic plants for pond margins, rain gardens, and wetland restoration, providing blue-violet flower spikes from June through October that are heavily used by bumblebees and long-tongued native bees. The glossy, arrow-shaped leaves provide cover for frogs, herons hunt at the plant's base, and waterfowl eat the seeds. Remove spent flower stalks to encourage rebloom. Divide crowded colonies every 3–4 years in spring, keeping each division attached to a healthy rhizome section.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, acidic, well-drained soil; avoid planting too deep — the crown should be at or just above soil level.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in dappled shade to light sun; the Piedmont azalea is among the most beautiful and fragrant of the native deciduous azaleas, naturally found in woodland edges and along streams from Virginia to Florida and Texas. The pale pink to white flowers with long, exserted stamens appear in early spring before or with the new foliage, giving the plant a delicate, open appearance very different from the dense evergreen hybrid azaleas. Unlike most garden azaleas it tolerates zone 9 Gulf Coast summers well. Prune only after flowering, removing no more than one-third of the plant at once; never shear. Amend soil heavily with compost and pine bark before planting.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in a well-drained mix; germination is reliable at 60–70°F and seedlings grow quickly. Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — scabiosa performs best in cool to moderate temperatures and fades in intense summer heat. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continuous bloom from early summer until fall; flowers are excellent for cutting with long, wiry stems and last 7–10 days in a vase. In zones 7–10 try sowing in fall for earlier spring bloom before heat sets in.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed into a sunny, alkaline-friendly site.
directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost, sowing 1/4 in deep in a light germination mix; transplant outdoors after frost into well-drained, slightly alkaline or neutral soil. The domed, pincushion flowers in rich maroon, lavender, white, pink, and burgundy are magnets for butterflies and superb for cutting. Deadhead consistently to prevent self-seeding and extend the long bloom period from early summer through frost. Avoid rich, wet soils — scabiosa thrives in moderately lean conditions and is drought-tolerant once established.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in spring after soil warms; lift where not hardy.
directions: Plant pineapple lily bulbs in spring about five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil once the ground has warmed. Each stout stem produces a dense spike of starry flowers topped by a tuft of leafy bracts resembling a pineapple. Grow in full sun with regular summer moisture, and it also performs beautifully in large containers. In cold climates lift the bulbs before frost or grow them in pots moved indoors for winter.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring; avoid fall planting in zones 6–7 as plants need a full season to establish before winter.
directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained, lean to average soil; muhly grass is tolerant of sandy soil, heat, humidity, and drought once established and is an outstanding choice for hot, dry southern landscapes. The main attraction is the spectacular cloud of airy, rose-pink flower plumes in September–October that transform the planting into something otherworldly when backlit by low autumn sun. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth begins; avoid heavy organic mulching which can cause crown rot in humid climates. Divide every 4–5 years in spring to rejuvenate large clumps.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in spring after last frost, or in fall in zone 8–10 where they are hardy; the bulbs naturalize quickly and bloom most reliably after summer rain events.
directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; pink rain lily is the most commonly grown zephyr lily in the South, producing soft rose-pink funnel-shaped flowers that emerge just days after summer and fall rainfall — an event that earns them the name 'rain lily.' The bulbs go dormant through winter and dry summers, then spring to life reliably after soaking rains from June through October. In zone 9–10 they may also bloom sporadically through winter with sufficient moisture. Plant in drifts of 20 or more for the most impactful display along path edges, in rock gardens, or at the front of borders. The bulbs naturalize readily and multiply over years into showy colonies. In zones colder than 7, lift and store bulbs dry through winter.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow in spring where plants will grow, as rose mallow dislikes transplanting, and expect blooms in about sixty-five days. The lush plants stand two to four feet tall and cover themselves in pink, hibiscus-like flowers that magnetize pollinators. Give full sun and average soil, thinning seedlings for good air movement. It reseeds readily, so leave a few flowers to set seed for next year.

plant_note: Surface-sow and press in; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Press the tiny seed onto the soil surface without covering it, since light aids germination. This reddish-pink heirloom yarrow forms feathery, aromatic foliage topped with flat clusters of bloom above soft leaves. Once established it is exceptionally drought-tolerant and thrives in lean, well-drained soil in full sun. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong bloom, and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or fence for these quick-growing heirloom vines. Heart-shaped leaves and pinwheel-patterned blossoms open to greet the morning, with a fresh set of flowers each day. Give full sun and support to climb, and avoid overly rich soil, which favors leaves over blooms. Note that morning glory seeds are toxic if ingested.

plant_note: Plant container-grown vines near a sturdy trellis, pergola, or tree trunk in partial to full shade; the vine grows quickly once established but may be slow to emerge in spring.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; pipevine is a vigorous native climbing vine with enormous heart-shaped leaves that creates a dense, lush shade canopy and unusual curved pipe-shaped brown flowers in late spring that lure and temporarily trap small flies as pollinators. It is the essential host plant for the stunning pipevine swallowtail butterfly, which lays eggs exclusively on Aristolochia species — the caterpillars absorb aristolochic acids from the plant, making both larva and adult butterfly toxic to predators. Plant several vines together to provide enough leaf mass for the caterpillars. Train on a sturdy arbor, pergola, or fence; the vine can grow 20–30 feet and covers itself with tropical-looking foliage by midsummer. Cut back hard in late winter to control size.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the seed, then direct sow in spring where plants will grow, as lupines resent transplanting. This compact variety stays just twelve to eighteen inches tall with loose eight-inch spikes in pink, blue, purple, and white, some bicolored. Plant in groups for a splash of color in smaller gardens or containers. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, and deadhead spent spikes to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Sow directly; a top pollinator and self-sowing native.
directions: Direct sow plains coreopsis on the soil surface, as light aids germination. This North American native bears masses of yellow daisies banded in mahogany-red that attract bees and butterflies. It tolerates poor, dry soils and full sun, making it excellent for meadows and low-maintenance borders. Allow some seed to ripen for dependable self-sowing, or deadhead to extend the bloom period.

plant_note: Surface-sow; needs light to germinate. Self-seeds readily.
directions: Scatter seed on prepared soil and barely press it in, as light triggers germination. This easy native annual, also called calliopsis or golden tickseed, opens bright yellow petals marked with burgundy centers from early summer to fall. It thrives in poor, dry soil and full sun, quickly filling meadows and borders with color. Allow some flowers to set seed and it will reliably return the following season.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, deep soil in full sun; plains wild indigo has a very deep taproot and resents disturbance once established — sow where it will remain permanently. Scratch seed lightly and cold-stratify before spring sowing.
directions: Sow scarified seed in fall in well-drained, average to poor, deep soil in full sun — plains wild indigo is a low, spreading prairie legume of the Great Plains and midwestern prairies producing dense racemes of cream to pale yellow pea flowers in early spring, among the earliest native prairie plants to bloom. Unlike the tall blue false indigo (B. australis), plants remain compact and spread broadly rather than upright — the procumbent, mounded form covered with 4-inch cream flower spikes is striking in the spring landscape. A nitrogen-fixing prairie legume with an extremely deep taproot that survives drought and fire. Extremely long-lived once established (decades) but slow to establish from seed. Excellent for prairie garden restoration and native plant meadow plantings in zones 4–9. The inflated black seed pods are interesting ornamentally and rattle when dry. A host plant for specialist bees and several moth species.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plantain-leaf sedge grows naturally in moist, rocky, north-facing woodland slopes in rich, deep soil and requires similar conditions in the garden.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plantain-leaf sedge is the most architecturally distinctive of all the native shade sedges, immediately recognizable by its unusually broad, pleated, dark green leaves (1–2 inches wide) with prominent parallel veins resembling plantain (Plantago) leaves — a completely different aesthetic from typical narrow-leaved sedges. The leaves remain evergreen or semi-evergreen through winter, providing year-round ground-level interest in the woodland garden. Burgundy-red stems bear small, elegant fruiting spikes in early spring before most other plants emerge. It grows naturally on moist, north-facing, rocky woodland slopes in the Appalachians and eastern US. Slower to spread than some native sedges but forms a beautiful, refined groundcover in appropriate conditions. An outstanding accent plant for the front of a shaded border or as a specimen among ferns and hostas.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; keep crown at or above soil level. Do not allow to dry out during establishment.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plumleaf azalea holds a unique distinction among native deciduous azaleas — it blooms in July and August, later than every other native azalea species, and is therefore in bloom when every other azalea in the garden has long finished. The large, brilliant orange-red (rarely yellow) flowers appear on the shrub in midsummer, providing color and hummingbird nectar in a season when the woodland garden often lacks bloom. This is one of the rarest native azaleas in the wild, found only in southwestern Georgia and eastern Alabama, but it is a superb and underused garden plant. It tolerates the heat and partial shade of zone 8–9 summers better than most native azaleas. Mulch heavily with pine bark to maintain soil moisture and acidity.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; cover seed as darkness aids germination.
directions: Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since darkness aids germination and plants tolerate light frost. This dwarf mix averages sixteen to twenty-four inches and blooms in blue, purple, pink, red, and white, making it great for containers. Drought tolerant once established, it flowers from spring into summer and attracts pollinators. Deadhead to extend bloom and let some flowers set seed to self-sow.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost after surface-sowing and cold-stratifying at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, sterile mix and cold-stratify at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks to break dormancy, then move to 65°F for germination. Grow on at cool temperatures (55–60°F) since heat causes etiolation; transplant into partial shade in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. Primroses bloom best in cool, moist conditions — they are outstanding spring plants but typically go dormant in summer heat. Divide crowded clumps after bloom in spring and replant divisions in refreshed, compost-amended soil.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost; excellent long-lasting cut flower.
directions: Plant pompon dahlia tubers after the last frost about four to six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. The small, perfectly spherical, tightly quilled flowers are outstanding long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun, stake the stems, and pinch young plants to encourage more blooms. Deadhead regularly and, in cold zones, dig and store the tubers over winter after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface after last frost when soil is warm; seed needs warmth and light to germinate.
directions: Scatter seed on the surface of sandy or gritty soil and press lightly — do not cover seed, as light is needed for germination. Portulaca is one of the most drought- and heat-tolerant annuals available, thriving in poor, dry soil where other flowers fail. Blooms open in full sun and close at night or in cloudy weather; modern varieties stay open longer. Self-sows readily in warm climates.

plant_note: Plant at least one male and three to five female shrubs for berry production; match male and female cultivar bloom times. The male plant 'Red Escort' is widely used with female cultivars of this species.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; possum haw is a native deciduous holly of the Southeast naturally found in stream margins, swamp edges, and moist bottomland forests, valued for the brilliant orange-red berries that adorn bare branches all winter long after leaves drop in fall. It is an excellent alternative to winterberry (I. verticillata) for zone 8–9 gardens where winterberry can struggle with heat. Like all hollies, it is dioecious — both male and female plants are required for berry set, in a ratio of at least one male per five females. Cedar waxwings, robins, bluebirds, and mockingbirds rely on the persistent berries throughout winter. Named female cultivars like 'Warren's Red' and 'Fineline' are widely available.

plant_note: Surface sow seed in fall (needs light to germinate; do not cover seed) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie alumroot is more drought-tolerant and sun-tolerant than the woodland alumroot species and thrives in the open prairie garden.
directions: Surface sow seed in fall (light required for germination) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade — prairie alumroot is the native Heuchera of midwestern prairies and prairie openings, producing airy wands of tiny greenish-white flowers on slender stems above mounds of rounded, shallowly lobed, evergreen to semi-evergreen leaves. While the garden coral bells (H. sanguinea) and its many modern hybrids are more familiar in cultivation, prairie alumroot is a tough, drought-tolerant native species offering excellent ecological value — it is an important nectar source for hummingbirds and long-tongued native bees and is the host plant for specialist Heuchera-feeding insects. More drought-tolerant and sun-hardy than the woodland-origin Heuchera species, making it better suited to drier prairie-garden settings. Evergreen rosettes add winter interest.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to moist, deep prairie soil in full sun; prairie blazing star is the tallest of the common blazing stars and requires a site with good moisture in summer to achieve its full 4–5 foot flowering height.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep prairie soil in full sun — prairie blazing star is the most dramatic of the native liatris species, producing the tallest and most densely packed flower spikes — 3–5 feet tall — of dense magenta-rose florets that open from the top of the spike downward (a reversal of the usual bottom-to-top progression) in midsummer.
