Like all liatris species, the flowers are intensely attractive to monarch butterflies (which use them heavily during fall migration), native bumblebees, and a wide array of specialist prairie bees. It grows naturally in the wet to mesic tallgrass prairies of the Midwest, requiring better soil moisture in summer than the drier-site liatris species. The corms (thickened stem bases) survive prairie fires, drought, and cold with equal ease. An outstanding focal-point perennial for prairie gardens and naturalistic plantings where maximum height and drama are needed.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify seed at 35–40°F for 60 days before spring sowing. Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun.
directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep or direct sow in well-drained, average to dry soil in full sun; prairie blazingstar is one of the tallest blazingstars, producing dense, 2–3 foot spikes of bright rose-purple flowers from July through August that open from the top of the spike downward — the reverse of most flowering spikes. It is one of the premier late-summer nectar plants for monarch butterflies preparing for fall migration, as well as a magnet for native bees and swallowtails. The grass-like, basal foliage is unobtrusive early in the season; plants emerge from small corms that multiply slowly over the years. Excellent cut flower. Tolerates clay soil better than many prairie plants and adapts well to average garden conditions.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seeds for 60–90 days then sow in spring, or direct sow in fall for natural stratification; first bloom occurs in the second or third year.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed or plant transplants in deep, well-drained, preferably rocky or clay loam soil in full sun; this long-lived prairie plant grows a massive, 6-foot-deep taproot that anchors it against drought and enables it to survive prairie fire. The basal rosette of enormous, rough-textured heart-shaped leaves — up to 18 inches across — is distinctive all season, while the wiry branched stems carry yellow composite flowers to 8 feet in mid- to late summer. It is extremely slow to establish, requiring 3–4 years to first bloom, but once rooted it is virtually indestructible and may live for decades. Do not transplant established plants as the taproot cannot be severed without killing the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; very slow to establish from seed — divisions or plugs reach ornamental size much faster.
directions: Sow seeds in full sun in lean, well-drained, sandy or rocky soil after last frost; prairie dropseed is a slow-growing native that takes 2–3 years from seed to develop into an ornamental clump. The extremely fine-textured, emerald-green foliage forms a perfect arching fountain shape, and open, airy flower panicles in late summer emit a distinctive fragrance of warm popcorn or cilantro. Fall color is a beautiful tawny orange-gold and the foliage persists attractively through winter. Once established it is extremely drought- and heat-tolerant and virtually maintenance-free.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring after cold stratification for 30 days, or sow in fall for natural stratification; plants may rebloom in fall in cool climates.
directions: Direct sow cold-stratified seed in early spring in lean, well-drained, sandy to gravelly soil in full sun; prairie phlox is more drought tolerant and heat tolerant than the commonly planted P. divaricata and grows in dry prairie soils and open woodland edges across the eastern US. The fragrant pink to lavender flower clusters bloom in late spring and attract swallowtail butterflies and native bee species. The downy (pilosa) stems give the plant a soft texture throughout the season. Shear back by one-half after bloom to prevent disease and encourage fresh growth; plants in rich soil or shade are more susceptible to powdery mildew. Excellent combined with native grasses in a dry meadow or prairie planting.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie rose is the only native climbing rose in the Midwest and East and is remarkably adaptable to difficult sites.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie rose is the only native climbing rose of the Midwest and Great Plains, producing clusters of beautiful deep pink, single, 2-inch flowers in early to midsummer on long arching canes that trail or climb through shrubs and fences. The flowers attract native bees including leafcutter bees and mason bees; the red hips persist into winter providing food for mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, and other fruit-eating birds. Unlike most garden roses, prairie rose requires no spraying, no deadheading, and no special care — once established it is remarkably drought-tolerant and adaptable. It blooms only once in summer (it is not repeat-blooming) but the display is spectacular. The 3-leaflet leaves give it a distinctive character among roses. An excellent native rose for naturalistic gardens, fence lines, and erosion control on slopes.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; or start indoors in late winter with 8–10 weeks of cold moist stratification.
directions: Sow seeds in fall and allow winter cold to naturally stratify them, or cold-stratify indoors for 8–10 weeks before spring sowing; plant in lean, well-drained to dry soil in full sun. The nodding, burgundy-pink flowers in mid-spring are followed by the spectacular feathery, smoke-like seed plumes that give the plant its name and provide ornamental interest for weeks. Prairie smoke is a rugged, drought-tolerant native wildflower that thrives in rocky or sandy soils where more demanding plants struggle. It spreads slowly into a weed-suppressing groundcover in open, sunny areas.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie spiderwort self-sows readily and naturalizes in prairies and open areas. The three violet-blue petals have a distinctive triangle formation typical of all Tradescantia species.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade — prairie spiderwort is a Great Plains native Tradescantia closely related to the more widely grown Ohio spiderwort (T. ohiensis), producing the same distinctive three-petaled violet-blue to pink-purple flowers whose delicate petals last only a single morning before shriveling, but with new buds opening daily over many weeks in spring and early summer. Like all spiderworts, the clear blue-violet flowers are striking when massed, creating a wave of cool color in the spring garden. Prairie spiderwort is generally more compact and drought-tolerant than Ohio spiderwort, reflecting its origin on drier Great Plains prairies. Foliage goes dormant and yellows in midsummer heat — plant with later-emerging perennials to fill in. An excellent native for dry, sunny to partly shaded prairie gardens and informal settings in zones 3–9.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes.
directions: Sow seed directly after the last frost, one-half inch deep in warm, well-drained soil. This branching native annual produces many small yellow flowers with reddish-brown centers on graceful stems. It is highly drought-tolerant and thrives on the lean soils of open meadows and restoration plantings. Bees feed on the blooms while finches and other birds harvest the ripe seed in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 10 weeks early; slow to establish.
directions: Start purple bell vine indoors ten weeks before the last frost, surface sowing the fine seed and providing warmth. This delicate climber produces dangling flowers with rosy-pink parasol-shaped calyces and dark tubular petals. Grow on a light trellis or in hanging baskets in full sun to partial shade with rich, moist soil. It is grown as a tender annual in most climates and blooms until frost.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–8 weeks improves germination rates significantly. Transplant after last frost into well-drained soil — established plants tolerate drought well. Flowers typically appear in year 2 from seed; deadhead spent blooms to extend the season or leave seedheads for birds. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify for 30 days to improve germination; often blooms the second year from seed.
directions: Give seed a month of cold-moist stratification, then sow in spring for the strongest stands. This hardy native perennial bears purple petals around a raised, coppery cone from summer into fall. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil where it will tolerate heat and drought once established. Leave the seed heads standing over winter to feed songbirds and encourage self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil warms, or cold stratify seed for 4 weeks to improve germination.
directions: This native heirloom is a long-lived perennial that blooms for a month or more from midsummer, with pinkish-purple petals surrounding a large dark cone. Sow seed in spring after the last frost, or cold stratify beforehand to break dormancy. Space plants a foot or more apart in well-drained soil and full sun to part shade. Once established it is drought tolerant and self-sows. Leave spent cones standing to feed goldfinches through fall and winter.

plant_note: Direct sow after danger of frost has passed; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: This trailing variety carries deeply veined petals in shades of purple fading to lavender and dusty rose, making it ideal for hanging baskets or as a flowering ground cover. Sow the large seeds directly where they are to grow once the soil has warmed, planting about half an inch deep. Avoid rich soil and heavy feeding, which produces leaves at the expense of blooms. It is fairly drought tolerant once established. Both the peppery blossoms and leaves are edible and add a colorful kick to salads.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants after last frost; this cultivar does not come true from seed and is propagated by division.
directions: Plant after last frost in full sun and very well-drained soil; purple fountain grass is grown as an annual north of zone 9 for its arching burgundy-red foliage and showy, rose-tinged plumes from midsummer through frost. It thrives in heat and drought once established but requires consistent moisture the first few weeks after transplanting. In frost-free climates it is a perennial that can become invasive — avoid planting near natural areas and remove flower heads before seeds ripen. Bring container-grown specimens indoors before frost to overwinter as a houseplant in colder zones.

plant_note: Surface sow seed 4–6 weeks before last frost in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; seeds need light to germinate. Can also sow in fall for natural stratification. Plants are dioecious — both male and female plants produce flowers, but only females set seed.
directions: Surface sow seed in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; purple meadow rue is one of the tallest and most architecturally striking native shade perennials, producing airy sprays of lavender-purple to white flowers on dark purple stems in late spring to early summer that rise above bold, blue-green, aquilegia-like foliage. It grows naturally in moist prairies, stream banks, and open woodland edges across the Midwest and Great Plains, tolerating light shade well. The flowers are pollinated by wind and native bees. The plant provides excellent vertical structure in the middle-to-back of a woodland edge border and combines beautifully with native ferns and woodland wildflowers. It spreads slowly by rhizome and self-seeding to naturalize in favorable conditions.

plant_note: Scarify and cold-stratify seed; inoculating with legume rhizobia aids nitrogen fixation.
directions: Lightly scarify and cold-stratify the seed, then sow into a firm, weed-free seedbed in full sun. This native legume fixes nitrogen and produces cone-shaped clusters of vivid purple flowers in early to mid-summer. Its deep taproot makes it very drought-tolerant and slow to establish, so be patient in the first year. It is a superb choice for prairie restoration, pollinator plantings, and improving lean soils.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; soak seed overnight to speed germination.
directions: This unusual heirloom prized since 16th-century England bears nodding, tear-shaped purple bracts on blue-green foliage that pairs beautifully with bronze and deep-purple plants. Soak seed overnight, then sow directly into the garden in early spring for the strongest plants. It thrives in heat and makes a long-lasting cut flower. Give it well-drained soil and full sun. Plants often self-sow to return the following year.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: This blend produces bright, papery globe-shaped blooms in warm sunrise tones that add bold color and unique texture to the summer garden. Start seed indoors about six to eight weeks before the last frost, as germination is slow and heat-loving. Transplant into full sun after the soil warms, where the plants tolerate heat and drought with ease. The more you cut, the more it blooms, making it excellent for fresh and dried arrangements. Pollinators are drawn to the long-lasting flowers all season.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seed germinates best in cool soil and darkness.
directions: Pure white lacy umbels top plants reaching two feet or more, creating an airy, romantic filler for cut-flower bouquets. Sow seed directly in cool early-spring or fall soil and cover lightly, as it germinates best in darkness. Plants produce one strong flush of blooms beloved by pollinators and beneficial insects. Give them full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Succession sow for a longer supply of the delicate white flowers.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost; safflower has a taproot and dislikes transplanting.
directions: This ornamental safflower opens creamy yellow and matures to deep saffron orange, giving evolving color and thistle-like texture through midsummer. Sow seed directly where plants are to grow, since the taproot resents disturbance. Sturdy upright stems make excellent fresh and dried cut flowers. Full sun and lean, well-drained soil suit it best, and it is quite drought tolerant. The edible petals can be used as a saffron substitute.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in consistently moist soil; this plant grows best with reliable moisture and will struggle without it.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, rich, slightly acidic soil in full sun to light shade; queen of the prairie is a magnificent native for rain gardens, pond edges, and any low area with reliable summer moisture. The enormous clouds of fluffy, peach-pink flowers on tall stems in midsummer are among the most dramatic native perennial displays available and attract native bees in large numbers. The plant spreads by rhizome to form large colonies and is not suited to small gardens or dry sites. Divide every 4–5 years in spring; each division should include a generous section of rhizome with multiple crowns. Cut back in late fall after frost.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: This tall standout carries an extraordinary lime-and-orange color blend never before seen in a zinnia, with long-lasting blooms on sturdy stems. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season areas. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and cut often to prolong bloom. Full sun and good air circulation help prevent powdery mildew. The flowers attract pollinators and make exceptional cut flowers all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: Sturdy plants produce long-stemmed blossoms in a rich lime-and-red blend all summer, drawing in pollinators. Sow seed directly into warm soil after the last frost for fastest growth. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep the plants blooming into fall. Provide full sun and space plants for airflow to reduce mildew. The colorful, edible petals make a pretty garnish for salads and desserts.

plant_note: Start indoors and surface sow; seed needs light to germinate. Transplant after frost.
directions: Grown for its dazzling foliage in white, bronze, gold, copper, yellow, pink, red, and purple, this blend brightens shady spots where flowers struggle. Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the soil surface since it needs light to sprout. Transplant after all danger of frost into partial shade and rich, moist soil. Pinch out flower spikes to keep the leaves vibrant and the plants bushy. It also makes an excellent houseplant to add color through winter.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or start indoors; often blooms the first year from an early start.
directions: In late spring a blue-green mound of foliage is covered with heavily fringed, fragrant, edible blooms in white, pink, lilac, and rose that butterflies adore. Sow seed directly in the garden in spring, or start indoors early for first-year flowers. Give plants full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Shear after the first flush to encourage a repeat bloom. The lacy flowers make charming, sweetly scented cut arrangements.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors; seed needs light and warmth to germinate. Transplant after frost.
directions: Start seed indoors on the soil surface under bright light and warmth, as it needs light to sprout. Grown for its painted foliage rather than flowers, this mix shows a wide range of leaf colors and patterns. Move plants outdoors only after all frost danger passes, choosing a spot with partial shade and consistent moisture. Pinch growing tips regularly and remove flower spikes to keep foliage bushy and vivid; it can be overwintered indoors as a houseplant.

plant_note: In mild-winter climates (zones 8–10) plant corms in fall for late-winter/spring bloom; in cold climates start indoors and transplant after last frost.
directions: Soak corms in cool water for 4 hours before planting, then set them claw-side down about 2 in deep in well-drained, fertile soil. Ranunculus thrives in cool temperatures (45–65°F) and will decline when heat arrives, so timing is critical. In zones 9–10 plant October through January for blooms from February through May; provide afternoon shade in the warmest areas. After foliage yellows, allow corms to dry in situ or lift, cure, and store in a cool dry location for replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall for natural cold stratification; transplants poorly due to deep taproot.
directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny prairie or meadow garden in lean, well-drained soil; rattlesnake master develops a deep taproot and resents being moved after establishment. The dramatic, yucca-like basal rosette of spiny blue-green leaves is topped by tall branched stems bearing small, spiny, globe-shaped white flower heads in midsummer that are highly attractive to native bees. It is extremely drought-tolerant and actually performs better in poor, sandy or rocky soil than in rich garden beds. The sculptural seedheads provide winter interest and are prized for dried arrangements.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; fresh seeds planted immediately after collecting in fall also establish reliably. All parts of the plant are toxic — the seeds especially so.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; red buckeye is one of the finest native shrubs for the shaded garden, producing upright panicles of brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers in April and May precisely timed to the return of ruby-throated hummingbirds from migration. The flowers are visited almost exclusively by hummingbirds and are among the most important early-season hummingbird plants available. It grows naturally as a forest understory shrub throughout the Southeast and performs exceptionally well in zone 8–9 Gulf Coast gardens in partial shade. The glossy, palmate compound leaves are attractive all season but the plant goes fully dormant in summer heat — mark its location and interplant with later-season companions. The large, mahogany-colored seeds are toxic to humans and livestock.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: Each medium bloom showcases deep, sultry red petals around a vivid yellow center on compact, deer-resistant plants only six to ten inches tall. Sow directly after the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early for a quicker show. These marigolds thrive in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Deadhead regularly to keep them flowering until frost. Their compact size makes them ideal for edging beds, borders, and containers while drawing in pollinators.

plant_note: Surface-sow on prepared soil in early spring or fall; needs light and does not transplant well.
directions: Scatter the fine seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall, as it needs light and resents transplanting. This iconic Flanders poppy opens tissue-paper scarlet petals with dark centers in late spring and early summer. Grow it in full sun and well-drained soil where its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant. Let flowers form seed pods and it will self-sow generously for years to come.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep and maintain even moisture at 65–75°F until germination. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — good drainage is critical, especially in winter, as plants rot in waterlogged conditions. The torch-like flower spikes transition from red or orange in bud to yellow as the florets open, creating a two-toned effect that attracts hummingbirds throughout summer and fall. In zones 5–6 tie the leaves together and mulch deeply around the crown before the first hard freeze to protect the growing point.

plant_note: Plant fresh samaras in fall as soon as they ripen (they lose viability quickly) or plant container trees in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; red maple is one of the most adaptable native trees but performs best in moist, slightly acidic soil.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — red maple is one of the most abundant and ecologically important trees in eastern North America, occurring naturally from Canada to Florida in an extraordinary range of conditions including swamps, dry ridges, and ordinary upland sites. It is also one of the first trees to bloom each spring, producing dense clusters of tiny red flowers in February–March before leaves emerge that provide critically important early-season pollen for mining bees emerging from overwintering. The brilliant red fall color — rivaling sugar maple in many individual trees — and the fast growth (2–3 feet per year) make it the most commonly planted shade tree in the eastern US. The samaras ripen in spring (unlike most maples which ripen in fall) and are quickly eaten by squirrels, grosbeaks, and finches. Tolerates flooding, clay, and a wide pH range.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: This color-shifting marigold is deep burgundy in cool weather, then develops splashes of yellow as temperatures warm and reverts to solid burgundy when it cools again. Sow directly after the last frost or start indoors for earlier blooms. Full sun and well-drained soil produce the sturdiest, most colorful plants. Deadhead often to prolong flowering through the season. It makes a striking garden hedge and attracts pollinators.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Plant Red Runner tubers 4-6 inches deep once soil warms, laying each tuber horizontally with the eye pointing up. This decorative variety produces dark romantic-red blooms on strong stems well suited for cutting. Pinch the growing tip at 12 inches to encourage bushier plants and more flowers. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter. Water deeply once sprouts emerge and avoid overwatering newly planted tubers.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in late summer or fall; do not bury too deeply as shallow planting promotes better bloom. The bulbs have an unusual growth cycle — never remove foliage as it builds the bulb's energy reserve for next year's bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, moist to average soil in partial shade to full sun; red spider lily (originally from China and Japan, long naturalized in the American South) is famous for its 'surprise' appearance — naked stalks push up abruptly from bare ground in September with no foliage, opening into clusters of brilliant crimson flowers with long arching stamens, then disappearing as quickly, followed weeks later by winter-to-spring foliage that dies back in summer. Plant along path edges, under deciduous trees, or in massing where the unusual bloom timing creates a dramatic seasonal event. Do not disturb established colonies — they bloom best when crowded. The bulbs are toxic; plant away from areas where children might dig. A beloved heirloom bulb of Southern gardens.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, slightly acidic, deeply humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; never dig trilliums from wild populations — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The rhizome is easily damaged; plant with care at the same depth as grown.
directions: Plant nursery-grown rhizome divisions in fall in deeply humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — red trillium (T. erectum) is one of the most widespread and variable of all trilliums, producing maroon to dark red (rarely white or pink) three-petaled flowers in mid-spring on erect stalks above a whorl of three broad, mottled leaves. The flower has a distinctive unpleasant odor (resembling carrion) that attracts blow flies and carrion beetles as pollinators. Despite the malodorous flowers, it is a beautiful and ecologically important native wildflower of eastern woodland, associated with rich, mesic forests from Canada to the Appalachians. The seeds are dispersed by ants (a process called myrmecochory) that carry the elaiosome-bearing seeds underground and leave them near established ant colonies. Like all trilliums, red trillium takes 7 years to flower from seed; purchase only nursery-grown, propagated plants — never collect from wild.

plant_note: Attach small clumps to the rough bark of live oaks, southern magnolias, or other large trees by tying gently with jute twine; keep moist until established. Can also grow on old wooden fences or moss-covered rocks.
directions: Attach small clumps to the rough bark of live oaks, magnolias, cypress knees, or mossy rocks in partial to full shade; resurrection fern is a fascinating native epiphytic fern that grows without soil, anchoring itself to bark and extracting water and nutrients from rain and air. Its extraordinary survival ability allows it to lose up to 97% of its water content and appear completely dead and shriveled in drought, then revive to lush green within hours of rain — earning its dramatic common name. In its natural habitat the bark surface of mature live oaks is often entirely carpeted with this fern, and in rain events the transformation from brown crisp desiccation to vivid green is remarkable and occurs overnight. Establish by tying moist clumps to rough bark in spring; once attached it will spread slowly across the bark surface over time and requires no further care.

plant_note: Set tubers out after the last spring frost when the soil is warm.
directions: Rip City bears dramatic dark red-black blooms on long, easy-to-cut stems from early in the season. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained soil after frost danger passes, and stake the plants early since the tall stems benefit from support. This variety is a strong tuber producer, so plants can be divided at season's end. Deadhead spent flowers to keep new blooms coming through fall. Lift tubers before hard frost in cold-winter regions.

plant_note: Plant after frost when soil reaches about 60 F.
directions: Robin Hood produces large salmon-orange blooms whose saturation shifts through the growing season, making each flower slightly different. Because the big blooms are heavy, stake or corral this variety early so stems stay upright. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in loose, fertile, well-drained soil once frost danger has passed. Feed with a low-nitrogen fertilizer to favor flowers over foliage. Cut blooms freely to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; plants usually bloom the second year.
directions: This perennial painted daisy, also called Persian daisy, produces bright long-stemmed blooms that make excellent cut flowers and pair beautifully with columbine and delphinium. Sow seed in spring in full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Plants typically flower in their second season and attract pollinators. Divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Some people may experience contact dermatitis, so handle the foliage with care.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost when soil has warmed.
directions: Rock Run Ashley is a smaller smoky-salmon dahlia that blends beautifully in mixed bouquets and is a favorite with florists. Plant tubers horizontally 4-6 inches deep after the soil warms, spacing plants about 12-18 inches apart. Pinch young plants to promote branching and more cut stems. Keep soil evenly moist during bloom but let it dry slightly between waterings to prevent tuber rot. Harvest flowers in the cool of the morning for the longest vase life.

plant_note: Plant tubers outdoors once the danger of frost has passed.
directions: Rockstar is a dark anemone-type dahlia with distinctive foliage that blooms continuously through the season and holds well in the vase. Plant the tuber 4-6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil after frost, positioning the eye upward. Provide full sun and support tall stems as needed. Deadhead regularly and cut blooms often to keep the plant productive. In cold climates dig and store tubers before the first hard freeze.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; cold stratification improves germination.
directions: The state flower of Colorado, this heirloom columbine bears long-lasting pale violet and white blossoms above delicate blue-green, fern-like foliage. Sow seed in early spring or fall and expose it to a cold period, as chilling greatly improves germination. It is at home in part shade and moist, well-drained soil. Plants bloom in their second year and self-sow readily. Leave a few seed heads to naturalize the patch over time.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; seed benefits from cold stratification.
directions: This long-lived heirloom penstemon sends up exquisite blue-violet flower spikes that bloom for about a month in early summer and attract pollinators. Sow seed in fall or early spring and give it a cold, moist period to break dormancy. Provide full sun and lean, sharply drained soil, as it is drought tolerant once established. Evergreen basal rosettes carry the plant through winter. Leave the satiny brown seedpods for interest and self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; plants may bloom the first fall from an early sowing or establish and bloom their second year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained soil; germination is fast and reliable. Plant in full sun in poor to average, very well-drained soil — rose campion thrives under dry, sunny conditions that would stress many perennials. The intensely magenta-crimson flowers glow against the woolly, silver-white felted stems and leaves, which are ornamental throughout the growing season. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years) but self-sow abundantly so colonies persist indefinitely; thin or transplant self-sown seedlings to prevent overcrowding.

plant_note: Scarify hard seed coat before sowing to speed germination.
directions: Nick or soak the hard seed coat before sowing to encourage even germination in warm soil. This native hibiscus produces large, pink, tropical-looking blooms and thrives in moist to wet ground. Give it full sun and steady water, making it ideal for pond edges, rain gardens, and low, damp spots. The plant dies back in winter and returns from the root, drawing bees and hummingbirds each summer.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.
directions: Cold-stratify the seed for about a month, then sow into moist soil in spring. Also called swamp milkweed, this native perennial bears clusters of fragrant dusty-rose flowers atop narrow-leaved stems. Unlike common milkweed it tolerates wet ground, making it perfect for rain gardens and pond margins in full sun. It is an essential Monarch host and nectar source, so avoid pesticides and let caterpillars feed on the foliage.

plant_note: Plant divisions in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade; this plant requires reliable moisture and will stress in drought. Blooms 2–3 weeks earlier than the white species (Chelone glabra).
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; rose turtlehead produces deep rose-pink inflated flowers from midsummer into fall, blooming 2–3 weeks earlier than the similar white turtlehead and offering richer color. It grows naturally along stream banks, wet meadow margins, and boggy woodland edges from Maryland south into the Southeast. Bumblebees that are heavy enough to force open the closed flower mouth are its primary pollinators. Plant in groups of three or more for best visual impact. Divide clumps every 4 years in spring to maintain vigor; cut to the ground after hard frost. Excellent in rain gardens and wet borders where the reliable summer moisture it needs is naturally available.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: Abundant four- to five-inch rosy pink blooms range from dusty rose to warm pink, evoking an old-fashioned cottage garden when planted in drifts. Sow seed directly into warm soil after the last frost. Cut and deadhead frequently to keep the plants producing into fall. Full sun and good spacing for airflow help avoid powdery mildew. Pollinators, including butterflies, love the long-lasting flowers.

plant_note: Set out nursery plants in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: This hardy evergreen shrub produces clusters of soft pink, trumpet-shaped blooms in spring above lush foliage. Plant it in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and site it in dappled shade or morning sun. Keep the shallow roots cool and evenly moist with a mulch of pine bark or leaves. Avoid deep planting, as the crown must stay near the surface. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; surface sow as seed needs light to germinate.
directions: This sweetly scented alyssum forms low mats covered in tiny rose-pink flowers in mini-rosettes, perfect for rock gardens, walls, paths, and container edges. Sow seed directly in early spring, pressing it onto the surface since it needs light to germinate. Full sun to light shade and well-drained soil keep it blooming. Shear lightly if it fades in summer heat to spur a fresh flush. The mildly grassy, mustardy flowers are edible and draw in pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral, clay to loam soil in full sun; rosinweed is a deep-rooted prairie plant that takes 2–3 years to bloom but becomes virtually indestructible once established — site it where it can remain permanently.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun — rosinweed is the most compact and garden-adaptable of the native Silphium genus, producing bright yellow, 2–3 inch daisy-like flowers from late summer through early fall on branching stems 3–5 feet tall. Like all silphiums, rosinweed is a deep-rooted prairie species with extraordinary drought and heat tolerance once established — the deep taproot (sometimes 12 feet deep in mature specimens) accesses moisture unavailable to shallow-rooted plants. The rough, sandpaper-textured opposite leaves are distinctive. The flat, disc-shaped seeds are highly attractive to goldfinches, which perch on the sturdy stems to extract them. It is being researched as a potential perennial grain crop because the seeds have a high oil content. More compact and less rank than compass plant or prairie dock, making it more suitable for smaller prairie garden settings. Extremely long-lived — prairie specimens are known to be over 100 years old.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in mild climates; clarkia dislikes transplanting.
directions: Graceful branching stems carry a profusion of delicate pink, white, and salmon blooms from spring into summer, swaying in the breeze. Sow seed directly where it is to grow, as clarkia resents root disturbance. It prefers cool weather, so sow in early spring or in fall where winters are mild. Give it full sun to part shade and lean, well-drained soil. Pollinators love the flowers, which also last well when cut.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in early spring, spacing at 8–12 inches for coverage within 2 years; tolerates very dry shade once established.
directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; rosy sedge is one of the finest native groundcovers for dry shade under mature deciduous trees, where it forms dense tufts of arching, fine-textured, glossy green foliage. It is not a spreader in the invasive sense — each clump slowly expands but does not colonize aggressively — making it well-suited to a refined shade garden. The plant tolerates drought better than Pennsylvania sedge (C. pensylvanica) and handles the dense root competition of established trees. Self-sows lightly once established to fill gaps over time.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; succession sow for continuous cut flowers.
directions: Sow seed one inch deep directly in the garden after the last frost once soil has warmed. This heirloom variety produces deep burgundy-red, velvety petals around dark chocolate centers on sturdy five- to six-foot stems. Give it full sun and moderate water, staking in windy sites to protect the tall stalks. Cut blooms just as they open for long-lasting arrangements, or leave heads to feed pollinators and birds.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; sunflowers grow best sown where they will bloom.
directions: This stunning true-red sunflower blooms on multi-branching plants, offering a dramatic accent in beds, borders, and bouquets. Sow seed directly into warm soil after frost, spacing plants closer together for longer stems and smaller flowers ideal for cutting. Full sun and steady moisture produce the best display. Cut stems just before the flowers fully open for the longest vase life. Successive sowings extend the bloom season into fall.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seed for 60 days before spring sowing; corms can also be planted in spring at 2 inches deep.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed or plant corms in sharply drained, lean soil in full sun; this native Liatris blooms later than L. spicata, providing purple-pink flower spikes in late August through September when other prairie plants are winding down. It is more drought tolerant and heat tolerant than the common spike gayfeather, making it particularly well-suited to hot, dry gardens and the southern edges of its range. The open, loose flower arrangement (compared to the denser L. spicata) is preferred by some native bee species. Monarch butterflies and native bumblebees are especially attracted to this plant during their late-summer movements and foraging.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; soak seed first.
directions: The Royal series offers a majestic mix of colors with larger flowers and better heat tolerance for a longer bloom period than most sweet peas. Soak the seed, then sow directly in very early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, since they love cool weather. Provide a trellis, fence, or netting for the climbing vines. Keep them well watered and cut frequently to prolong flowering. The fragrant blooms lure pollinators and perfume the house when brought indoors.

plant_note: Surface-sow; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Press the tiny seed onto the soil surface without covering, since it needs light to sprout. This heirloom sweet alyssum forms a low, spreading carpet of honey-scented violet-purple blooms. Grow it in full sun to light shade as an edging, container filler, or ground cover among vegetables to draw beneficial insects. Shear lightly after the first flush to encourage a fresh round of bloom into cooler weather.

plant_note: Plant crowns at soil level in early spring in consistently moist soil; ideal near ponds or streams where roots can access reliable moisture.
directions: Plant in wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; the royal fern has a naturally wide distribution across bogs, stream margins, and wet woodlands and is among the largest and most impressive of hardy ferns. The broadly compound fronds have a distinctive open, airy appearance unlike the typical fern, and the fertile frond tips turn rusty brown with spores in summer. Provide consistently wet to saturated soil and avoid any drought stress, particularly in the first two years; mulch heavily with leaf mold to retain moisture. Divide the massive, fibrous root mass in early spring every 7–10 years.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: This Fleuroselect award winner bears deeply colored three-inch blooms that open red-wine and age to dusty rose, striking against white flowers and silver foliage. Sow directly after frost into average, well-drained soil, as rich soil produces foliage at the expense of flowers. Full sun and modest water suit it best; it is drought tolerant once established. Deadhead to prolong bloom or leave a few seed heads to self-sow. Butterflies flock to the flowers, which also cut well.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; can start indoors 3-4 weeks early.
directions: Grown by Thomas Jefferson at Monticello, this ornamental vine dazzles with pinkish-purple flowers and shiny dark-purple pods against purple-tinged foliage. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, providing a sturdy fence or trellis for the fast, twining stems to climb. Full sun brings out the richest pod color. Keep it evenly watered through the heat of summer. Note that the raw pods and seeds are toxic and should be treated as ornamental only.

plant_note: Plant small tuber clusters in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; rue anemone is a spring ephemeral that dies back completely by early summer — mark its location to avoid disturbing it during summer dormancy.
directions: Plant small tuber clusters in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — rue anemone is one of the smallest and most delicate of all eastern woodland spring ephemerals, producing clusters of white to pale pink (rarely deep pink) five-petaled flowers on slender stems only 4–9 inches tall in early spring, just as the forest canopy begins to leaf out. The leaves closely resemble those of meadow rue (Thalictrum), explaining both the common name and the former genus placement. Like all true spring ephemerals it dies back completely by early summer, leaving no trace above ground. It is a slow-growing but extremely long-lived native that gradually forms a charming colony when well-sited. Named varieties like 'Shoaf's Double Pink' and 'Betty Blake' (double white flowers) are available from specialty native plant nurseries. An outstanding native for the shaded wildflower garden, best combined with later-emerging foliage plants to fill in after it goes dormant.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; nick or soak the hard seed coat to aid germination.
directions: This 1937 English selection offers an array of colors on tall dramatic spikes that shine when planted in masses or mixed borders. Nick or soak the hard seed coat, then sow directly in spring or fall, as lupines dislike transplanting their long taproot. They prefer full sun, cool summers, and well-drained, slightly acidic soil. In zones 4 to 7 they are short-lived perennials; in warmer areas grow them as annuals. Remove spent spikes to encourage more blooms and support pollinators.

plant_note: Scarify and soak seed before sowing; resents root disturbance so direct sow when possible.
directions: Nick and soak the hard-coated seed overnight, then sow where plants are to grow since lupines dislike transplanting. This perennial hybrid sends up tall spikes densely packed with pea-like flowers in blue, purple, pink, yellow, and white. Plant in full sun and cool, well-drained soil; as a legume it fixes nitrogen and improves the ground. Cut spent spikes to encourage rebloom and let a few set seed for next season's plants.

plant_note: Plant in spring after last frost in lean, well-drained soil; established plants are extremely drought-tolerant.
directions: Plant in poor to average, sharply drained soil in full sun — Russian sage declines quickly in fertile or moist soil and is highly susceptible to root rot in clay. Cut plants back hard in early spring (to about 6 in) just as new basal growth emerges; do not cut back in fall as the woody framework helps protect the crown. The airy silver stems and lavender-blue flowers create a haze of color from midsummer through fall and are beloved by pollinators. Deer avoid this plant due to its strongly aromatic foliage.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; surface sow as seed needs light.
directions: This Asian wildflower has an unusual spiky habit and rosy color that adds welcome contrast to the landscape and long-lasting cut and dried flowers. Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant into full sun and well-drained soil after frost. It tolerates heat and dry conditions once established. Harvest stems when most of the tiny flowers have opened for the best dried bouquets.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost when the soil has warmed.
directions: Salmon Runner is a well-balanced salmon-colored dahlia that produces abundant cut flowers without needing staking. Plant the tuber 4-6 inches deep in loose, fertile soil after the last frost, eye facing up. This sturdy variety stands on its own but still benefits from pinching to increase stem count. Water deeply and consistently once growth is established, and cut blooms often to prolong flowering. Lift and store tubers over winter in regions colder than zone 8.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in well-drained, acidic soil slightly above grade; fall planting allows root establishment before the autumn and winter bloom season begins.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade to full sun; sasanqua camellias bloom in fall and early winter, earlier than Camellia japonica, and are more sun-tolerant and heat-hardy, making them the better choice for Gulf Coast gardens in zones 8–9. Single, semi-double, or double flowers in white, pink, red, and lavender provide vivid color from October through January when little else is in bloom. Unlike japonicas, sasanquas can be trained as espaliers against walls, used as hedges, or even grown as small specimen trees. Apply pine-bark mulch and acid-forming camellia fertilizer in spring. Petal blight and scale are the main concerns; improve air circulation and treat scale with horticultural oil in late winter.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; saw palmetto transplants from containers fairly well but is very slow to establish from small plants. The underground root system is ancient and extensive in wild specimens.
directions: Plant in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — saw palmetto is one of the most ecologically important shrubs in the southeastern US, forming the dominant understory layer across millions of acres of Florida and Gulf Coast pine flatwoods and scrub. Individual plants are estimated to live 500–700 years, maintaining an enormous root system that stores carbohydrates and re-sprouts after fire within days. The cream-white flower clusters produce small black berries that are a primary food source for black bears, white-tailed deer, gopher tortoises, and more than 100 bird species. The berries are also harvested commercially as an herbal supplement for prostate health. Saw palmetto tolerates partial shade, salt, drought, and periodic flooding — it is one of the most resilient and valuable native plants for Gulf Coast habitat gardens. The saw-toothed petioles give it its name.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; light cold stratification aids germination.
directions: This southwestern native perennial reaches up to thirty inches and bears deep red flowers that stand out against rich green foliage in pollinator gardens. Sow seed in early spring or fall and give it a cool period to improve germination. It thrives in moderately dry, well-drained soil and full sun to part shade. Once established it is quite drought tolerant. Deadhead to prolong bloom and leave some seed to naturalize.

plant_note: Direct sow in place; short-lived, so succession sow.
directions: Broadcast scarlet flax seed thinly on prepared soil in early spring and rake in lightly. The brilliant red saucer-shaped flowers dance atop wiry stems and are a favorite in wildflower mixes. Because individual plants are short-lived, make successive sowings for continuous color through the season. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil with minimal supplemental water.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; tap-rooted and resents transplanting — sow where plants will remain.
directions: Sow seeds directly in well-drained, even poor, sandy or gravelly soil; sea holly develops a deep taproot and strongly dislikes being transplanted after establishment. The metallic steel-blue stems, bracts, and thimble-shaped flower heads appear in midsummer and hold their color and structure well into fall. Drought-tolerant once established, sea holly declines in rich, moist soil — lean, infertile conditions produce the best blue coloration. Cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements before flowers fully open to preserve the vivid blue color.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: These charming cosmos have fluted, seashell-shaped petals in white, pink, rose, and crimson that make an excellent cut flower. Sow seed directly after the last frost into average, well-drained soil, since rich soil reduces flowering. They prefer neglect over pampering and become drought tolerant once established. Deadhead to extend the show or leave seed heads to self-sow. Full sun keeps the tall plants sturdy and blooming into fall.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; division is easily done by digging and separating rooted stems.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil — even poor, gravelly soil suits sedum perfectly; overly rich or moist soil causes floppy, open-centered plants. Tall sedums may flop in midseason; pinch once in late spring or grow through a support ring to keep them upright. Leave the russet seedheads standing through winter as they are attractive and provide structure in the dormant garden. Divide every 3–4 years in spring by separating the fleshy crown sections.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: This distinguished zinnia produces elegant double three- to five-inch blooms in a dreamy, rare salmon-pink on strong, long stems. Sow directly into warm soil after the last frost for fast growth. Cut and deadhead often to keep the plants flowering all summer. Full sun and airflow between plants help prevent powdery mildew. The edible petals are loved by pollinators and brighten the cutting garden.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: This tall, showy blend produces large three- to four-inch white, pink, rose, and crimson flowers ideal for wildflower plantings and cutting. Sow directly after frost into average, well-drained soil, as lean conditions bring more bloom. Cosmos are drought tolerant and need little attention once up. Full sun keeps the plants upright and flowering; stake in windy sites. An excellent butterfly lure that self-sows for the following year.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; sensitive fern spreads vigorously by rhizome in moist conditions and can colonize large areas quickly — site it where spreading is welcome.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist conditions; sensitive fern is one of the most distinctive and bold native ferns of eastern North America, producing large, broadly triangular, undivided-looking fronds with wide, rounded lobes that give it an unusually coarse texture compared to the typically finely cut fern frond. It gets its name from its extreme sensitivity to frost — the fronds collapse at the first light freeze in fall. A separate fertile frond with clusters of bead-like sporangia remains standing through winter and is an attractive structural element in dried arrangements. It spreads readily by rhizome in moist conditions and is excellent for naturalizing wet, shaded areas along stream banks, pond margins, and bog gardens. The coarse, bold foliage provides excellent contrast with fine-textured shade plants.

plant_note: Start indoors; nick or soak the hard seed coat and keep warm. Transplant after frost.
directions: This novelty rapidly folds its fern-like leaves when touched, exposing spines as a defense, delighting children and adults alike. Nick or soak the hard seed and start it indoors in warmth, then grow on as a houseplant or transplant outdoors after frost. Give it bright light, full sun to part shade, and consistently moist, well-drained soil. It is frost tender and typically grown as an annual or potted plant outside the tropics. Handle sparingly to enjoy repeated leaf-folding without stressing the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow in very early spring or fall; seed germinates best in cool soil and darkness.
directions: Tall spikes of light and dark blue blossoms make a compelling backdrop for beds and bouquets and reseed readily. Sow seed directly in very early spring or in fall, as it needs cool soil and darkness to germinate and dislikes transplanting. Larkspur blooms in the cool weather of late spring and early summer. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, and stake in exposed sites. All parts are toxic if eaten, so site it away from grazing animals.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; surface sow as seed needs light to germinate.
directions: This spectacular perennial sends up multiple graceful spikes of white, pink, deep rose, violet, and purple flowers that attract butterflies. Sow seed in spring or fall, pressing it onto the soil surface since it needs light to germinate. It is fast growing and fairly drought tolerant, excelling in dry landscape gardens or mass plantings. Give it full sun and sharply drained soil. Cut spent spikes to encourage rebloom and use the flowers in arrangements.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist potting mix and keep at 60–70°F for germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained soil. Shasta daisies often bloom lightly the first year from seed and fully the second year — named cultivars from division or plugs bloom more reliably in year 1. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage repeat blooming from midsummer into fall. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring, discarding the woody center and replanting vigorous outer sections.

plant_note: Surface-sow and press in; needs light to germinate. Usually blooms the second year.
directions: Press seed onto the soil surface without burying it, since light aids germination. This classic perennial produces crisp white petals around bright yellow centers on strong stems, and the flowers are edible. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, deadheading regularly to extend the summer bloom. Divide crowded clumps every two to three years in spring to keep plants healthy and floriferous.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring after soil has warmed to at least 65°F; space clumps 36–48 inches apart. In zone 8, mulch heavily in winter to protect the rhizome from killing frosts.
directions: Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; shell ginger is a stunning tropical-looking perennial for Gulf Coast gardens, growing rapidly to 6–10 feet with broad, fragrant, cane-like stems and large lance-shaped leaves. In late spring and summer, mature canes produce cascading clusters of porcelain-white flowers with yellow and pink markings that resemble shells before opening — extraordinarily fragrant and beautiful. Only second-year canes flower; cut spent canes to the ground after bloom to make room for new growth. The variegated form ('Variegata') has gold-striped foliage and is slightly smaller. Plants freeze to the ground in zone 8 frosts but re-emerge from hardy rhizomes in spring with deep mulch protection.

plant_note: Surface sow on cool soil; do not cover the fine seed.
directions: Scatter the tiny Shirley poppy seed on the surface of cool, moist soil in very early spring, or sow in fall in mild regions. Do not cover the seed, which needs light and cold to germinate well. The delicate crepe-paper flowers in pink, red, and white are classic cottage and meadow plants. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow, and let some pods ripen for self-seeding.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall, pressing lightly but not covering since it needs light to sprout. This 1880s heirloom bears delicate two-to-three-inch blossoms on slender, nodding stems that dance in the breeze. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow where they are to grow and thin gently. As a carefree annual it readily self-sows, making it ideal for naturalized areas and meadow plantings. Water sparingly once established, as these plants tolerate lean, dry conditions.

common_name: Shock Wave Purple Tie Dye Petunia
plant_note: Start indoors early and surface sow; seed needs light. Transplant after frost.
directions: This petite petunia bears one-and-a-half to two-inch variegated purple flowers with tie-dye patterns that shift with temperature and light, so no two blooms match. Start the tiny seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. Its compact, mounded, spreading habit makes it ideal for hanging baskets and containers. Feed regularly and pinch to keep it full and blooming all season.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; sunflowers grow best sown where they will bloom.
directions: Yellow-tipped, bronze-red petals and contrasting purple-tinged stems make this well-branched sunflower a striking cut flower. Sow seed directly into warm soil after frost, spacing closer for longer stems suited to bouquets. The blooms are pollenless, so they will not shed on tabletops. Give the plants full sun and steady moisture. The edible petals add a bittersweet pop of color to salads.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in the garden for natural cold stratification; seeds require at least 60 days of cold moist conditions before they will germinate in spring. Division of established crowns is the faster propagation method.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; the plant is a spring ephemeral that blooms in early spring with distinctive nodding clusters of swept-back petals in white to pink with a yellow ring at the center. After flowering and setting seed in late spring, the foliage yellows and the plant goes fully dormant by midsummer — mark the location well and interplant with later-emerging shade perennials. Plants grown from seed typically require 2–3 years to produce their first bloom. Naturalize in groups of a dozen or more for the most impactful display in a woodland garden.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; shooting star is a spring ephemeral that dies back completely by early summer — mark planting location to avoid disturbing the dormant roots during summer dormancy.
directions: Plant in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade — shooting star is one of the most spectacular native spring wildflowers, producing umbels of 3–20 nodding flowers whose swept-back petals (white to lavender to deep magenta) and prominent yellow anther cone pointing downward create an unmistakable shooting-star silhouette. The flowers are buzz-pollinated exclusively by bumblebees, which grip the anther cone and vibrate at a specific frequency to release the pollen. A spring ephemeral of open woodland, prairies, and moist slopes, shooting star goes completely dormant by early summer and must be marked to avoid disturbing the underground corms. It grows slowly and persists for decades in appropriate conditions. Requires good drainage despite needing moisture in spring — summer-wet conditions cause rot. Outstanding for naturalizing in moist, open woodland gardens with hostas, ferns, and other spring wildflowers.

plant_note: Surface-sow; needs light to germinate. Spreads vigorously by runners.
directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it in lightly, as light encourages germination. Commonly called pink ladies, this spreading native perennial opens delicate pink, cup-shaped flowers over a long season. It thrives on neglect in full sun and dry, poor soil, spreading readily by runners to cover slopes and difficult sites. Give it room to roam or contain it, and expect a carefree groundcover loved by bees and butterflies.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.
directions: Cold-stratify the seed for about a month before sowing in spring for reliable germination. This native perennial produces large, fragrant clusters of soft pink star-shaped flowers on upright stems with broad, fuzzy leaves. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil where it tolerates drought and spreads gradually by rhizomes. It is a vital host for Monarch and Queen butterfly caterpillars, so keep it pesticide-free.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall, or cold stratify seed 4-6 weeks before spring sowing.
directions: This robust heirloom perennial hosts monarch butterflies and draws many pollinators with clusters of star-shaped blooms above green to silvery foliage. Sow seed in fall or cold stratify it for several weeks before spring sowing to break dormancy. It grows best in full sun and well-drained soil and is drought tolerant once established. Its deep roots dislike transplanting, so sow it in place. Leave seed pods to spread the patch and support monarch populations.

plant_note: Sow shallowly and cover; seed germinates best in darkness.
directions: Sow seed lightly covered, since this annual phlox germinates best in darkness. It produces a colorful blend of five-petaled, lightly fragrant blooms on low, spreading plants. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, sowing in early spring or fall in mild climates for the best bloom. Deadhead to prolong flowering and enjoy the bright carpets it forms in beds, borders, and containers.

plant_note: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Shumard oak establishes faster than most oaks and tolerates brief flooding, making it an excellent street and yard tree in the South.
directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Shumard oak is the premier large native oak of the Gulf Coast and Deep South, growing faster than most oaks (2–3 feet per year when young) while developing into a large, long-lived specimen tree with spectacular deep red-to-scarlet fall color — one of the best oaks for fall color in zone 7–9 where many trees fail to color reliably. It tolerates brief flooding and clay soils better than many oaks, making it an excellent choice for urban and suburban landscapes in the South. The deeply lobed leaves with pointed tips are similar to pin oak but without the drooping lower branches. The acorns provide important fall food for turkeys, deer, and numerous bird species. An outstanding Gulf Coast street tree, shade tree, and wildlife tree.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as soon as plants can be purchased; also self-seeds moderately in moist, shaded sites.
directions: Plant in partial to full shade in cool, humus-rich, moist but well-drained soil; brunnera is one of the best foliage plants for shade, particularly the silver-variegated cultivars like 'Jack Frost.' Delicate sprays of forget-me-not blue flowers appear in early to mid-spring above the large, heart-shaped leaves. In hot or dry conditions the leaves may scorch — consistent moisture and afternoon shade are essential in warmer climates. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in early spring; variegated cultivars must be divided rather than grown from seed to maintain their markings.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins, or in late summer after flowering; set rhizomes just below soil surface.
directions: Plant rhizomes just 1–2 in below the soil surface in moisture-retentive, slightly acidic soil; unlike bearded iris, Siberian iris prefers consistently moist conditions and tolerates wet spots. Graceful, narrow foliage forms attractive clumps all season, topped by elegant blue, purple, or white flowers in early summer. Siberian iris are very long-lived and rarely need dividing — only do so when the center of the clump dies out or blooming diminishes, typically every 5–8 years. Division is best done immediately after flowering or in early fall; replant promptly and water well.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs in fall; naturalizes in lawns.
directions: Plant Siberian squill bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in sun to partial shade. The nodding, intensely blue star-shaped flowers appear very early in spring and provide crucial forage for early bees. It naturalizes freely in lawns and under deciduous trees, forming sheets of blue over the years. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in full sun and lean, well-drained soil; do not fertilize as this is a plant of lean, poor soils.
directions: Direct sow in early spring in lean, well-drained, rocky to sandy soil in full sun; side oats grama is the official Texas state grass and one of the finest warm-season native grasses for ornamental use, with slender upright stems bearing distinctive oat-like seed heads all arrayed on one side of the stem in a graceful line. It establishes quickly from seed, tolerates drought and heat extremely well once established, and provides excellent winter structure with its bronze-tan seed heads. Mow or cut back once in late winter before new growth begins. Excellent for low-maintenance meadow plantings, slopes, and bird-supporting landscape borders. The seed is harvested by sparrows, finches, and native buntings.

plant_note: Direct sow on the surface of prepared soil in spring; sideoats grama germinates readily without stratification in warm soil. Also excellent as a fall-dormant sown grass — broadcast seed from October through December for natural spring germination.
directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; sideoats grama is the official state grass of Texas and one of the most widely adapted and drought-resistant native grasses in North America. It is called 'sideoats' for its distinctive seed heads, which hang in a row of small oat-like florets all on one side of the arching seed stalks from July through September, creating a charming, delicate appearance in late summer. The deep blue-green foliage turns orange-red in fall. Sideoats is one of the few native grasses that performs reliably in the extreme heat and drought of the Southern Plains, making it invaluable for Texas and Gulf Coast gardens with dry, alkaline soil. An excellent low-maintenance lawn substitute or turf replacement on slopes.

plant_note: Start indoors and surface sow; seed needs light. Transplant after frost.
directions: Grown for its striking silvery-white, felted foliage, this plant adds contrast and texture as edging, in moon gardens, and as bouquet filler. Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. It is drought tolerant and may overwinter in mild climates when mulched. If allowed to flower, the yellow blooms attract butterflies.

plant_note: Direct sow after danger of frost has passed; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: This blend blooms in single flowers of scarlet, gold, orange, and yellow on long, trailing stems that can be trained up a trellis or fence. Sow the large seeds directly where they are to grow once the soil warms, planting about half an inch deep. Keep the soil lean, as rich conditions favor leaves over flowers. It is drought tolerant once established and thrives in full sun to part shade. Both the peppery blossoms and leaves are edible and add a watercress-like kick to salads.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.
directions: Give seed a month of cold-moist stratification, then sow in spring. This dependable native perennial bears clouds of small lavender-blue, daisy-like flowers in late summer and fall. Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil where its smooth blue-green foliage resists disease. It provides critical late-season nectar for bees and migrating butterflies as other flowers fade.

plant_note: Direct sow cold-stratified seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to gravelly, thin soil in full sun to partial shade; smooth coneflower is a Federally Threatened species adapted to disturbed, thin-soil openings in cedar-hardwood woodland — it requires good drainage and open mineral soil.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to gravelly soil in full sun to partial shade — smooth coneflower is a Federally Threatened native coneflower of the Appalachian piedmont and Blue Ridge foothills, declining primarily due to habitat loss and canopy closure over its characteristic thin-soil, semi-open woodland and roadside habitats. It is distinguished from the more common purple coneflower (E. purpurea) by its smooth (not rough) stems and leaves and its adaptation to disturbed, thin, rocky soils — often in partially shaded situations unlike most other Echinacea. The pale to deep rose-purple ray flowers and prominent central cone are characteristic of the genus and equally attractive to pollinators and beneficial for gardens. Growing this species supports its conservation by maintaining cultivated populations and seed banks. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants from reputable native plant nurseries.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; this hydrangea blooms on new wood — cut it back to 12 inches in late winter for the largest, most vigorous flowers.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; smooth hydrangea is the native parent of the enormously popular cultivars 'Annabelle' and 'Incrediball', which have transformed shade gardens with their enormous round white flower heads up to 12 inches across. Unlike oakleaf hydrangea, smooth hydrangea blooms on new wood — cut all stems to 12–18 inches in late winter for the strongest, most floriferous performance. It grows naturally along stream banks, woodland edges, and cliff faces across the eastern US and tolerates more shade and competition than most shrubs. If flower heads become too heavy for stems, cut back less severely the following year. Pollinators of many species use the tiny fertile flowers at the center of the flower heads.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep, in moist but well-drained soil; ideal for naturalizing in damp meadows.
directions: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep and 3–4 in apart, in organically rich, moisture-retentive but not waterlogged soil; perfect for damp meadow or pond-edge plantings. The nodding, checkerboard-patterned bells in deep purple, white, or pink appear in mid-spring and are among the most intricately patterned flowers in the spring garden. Naturalizes freely in damp grassland — avoid mowing until foliage has died back completely in early summer. Once established the bulbs multiply steadily; divide congested clumps in early fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist starting mix and do not cover — seed needs light to germinate. Pinch seedlings once to promote multiple spikes. Transplant to the garden a few weeks before the last frost, as snapdragons thrive in cool conditions and can tolerate a light freeze. After the primary spike blooms, cut it off to encourage a second flush from side branches.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost has passed and soil is warm.
directions: Snoho Doris is a tall, productive dahlia bearing salmon-coral blooms with uniquely patterned petals on very long, easy-to-harvest stems. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained soil after the last frost, and provide light support for the tall plants. Pinch the central shoot early to encourage more branching and stems. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continued blooming into fall. A great choice for beginning dahlia growers thanks to its reliable production.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last spring frost when soil warms.
directions: Snoho Jojo is a productive, short-statured dahlia with smoky mauve blooms carried on long stems that are excellent for cutting. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil once frost danger passes. The compact plant produces cut-worthy stems from the start of the season. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep blooms coming through fall. In cold climates, lift the tubers before the first hard freeze and store them in a cool, dry place.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall; naturalizes and resists squirrels.
directions: Plant snow crocus corms in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil and full to partial sun. This early species crocus naturalizes rapidly in lawns and borders and is more squirrel-resistant than large Dutch crocus. The slender lavender flowers are among the first blooms of late winter, providing vital early forage for bees. Leave the foliage undisturbed until it withers to allow the corms to multiply.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost; euphorbia dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Crisp white-edged bracts look hand-painted and give a luminous, frosted glow even in summer heat, brightening beds and contrasting with deeper greens. Sow seed directly around the last frost, since this euphorbia resents transplanting. It thrives in full sun to part shade and average, well-drained soil and tolerates heat and drought. Wear gloves, as the milky sap can irritate skin and eyes. The variegated foliage makes a fine cut accent when the stem ends are seared.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs in fall, 3 in deep; alternatively plant 'in the green' (just after bloom) for better establishment.
directions: Plant bulbs 3 in deep in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees or in a shaded border; snowdrops are one of the first bulbs to bloom, often in January or February. They establish most successfully when transplanted 'in the green' — lifted and divided just after blooming while foliage is still active. Clumps multiply steadily and benefit from division every 3–4 years right after flowering to prevent overcrowding. Allow foliage to die back naturally; avoid mowing naturalised areas until leaves have fully yellowed.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early.
directions: Delicate ivory pincushion blooms with frilled petals appear continuously from early summer to fall on sturdy two-foot stems ideal for cutting. Sow seed directly around the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early. Provide full sun and well-drained soil and deadhead often to keep the honey-scented flowers coming. Bees and butterflies are drawn to the blooms. The strong stems make it a reliable cut-flower workhorse.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; soft shield fern requires good drainage and will not tolerate waterlogged soil, but needs consistent moisture during its growing season.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — soft shield fern is considered by many gardeners the most beautiful of all the commonly grown shade ferns, producing extraordinarily finely divided fronds with a uniquely soft, feathery texture that distinguishes it from coarser ferns. The fronds are twice-pinnate with neatly rounded, finely crenate pinnules that give the whole frond an almost lacy appearance.
