Many named cultivars exist with even more divided fronds ('Divisilobum Group'), cristate (crested) tips, or plumose forms. Unlike many Polystichum species, soft shield fern is semi-evergreen in zone 7–9, holding its fronds through winter to provide year-round interest. The soft, mounding clumps combine beautifully with hostas, astilbe, and woodland wildflowers. Excellent for the shaded border in zones 5–9.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions horizontally 1–2 inches deep in spring before or just as growth emerges; space 18 inches apart. Fall planting is also effective where winters are mild.
directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Solomon's seal is one of the most graceful native woodland perennials, producing arching stems lined with paired oval leaves and pendant white bell-shaped flowers in late spring that hang below the foliage like small lanterns. Bluish-black berries follow in late summer and are highly attractive to birds. The foliage turns clear yellow in autumn before the stems die back completely. Plants spread slowly by rhizome to form dignified colonies; transplant divisions in spring or fall. Giant Solomon's seal (P. commutatum) grows to 5–6 feet and is equally effective in larger shade gardens.

common_name: Something Old Something New Morning Glory
plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; nick or soak the hard seed coat first.
directions: This blend pairs nostalgic favorites like sky-blue Heavenly Blue with newer starred varieties for a mix of climbing morning glory flowers. Nick or soak the hard seed, then sow directly into warm soil after frost near a fence or trellis for the twining vines to climb. Give the plants full sun and average soil; overly rich soil favors foliage over flowers. Water regularly to keep the vines vigorous through summer. Note the seeds are toxic if eaten, so keep them away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil at the water's edge or in shallow water up to 4 inches deep; fall planting allows roots to establish before spring bloom.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil at the margin of a pond, stream, or rain garden in partial shade to full sun; southern blue flag is the native wetland iris of the coastal South and Southeast, growing naturally in swamps, freshwater marshes, wet meadows, and pond margins from Virginia to Florida. The violet-blue flowers veined with white and yellow in late spring are extraordinarily beautiful and important for early bumblebees and native bee species large enough to pollinate the structured flowers. The bold, sword-like leaves provide vertical structure in water gardens. Plant where the rhizome can stay moist — unlike bearded irises, this species must not dry out. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. A superb companion to pickerel rush, arrowhead, and swamp lily in water garden designs.

plant_note: Plant in fall in zones 8–10 for best establishment before bloom; in zones 7–8 spring planting after bloom is also effective. Set root ball 1–2 inches above the surrounding soil grade and backfill with native soil amended with pine bark.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–5.5) soil amended with pine bark in partial shade to morning sun with afternoon shade; Southern Indica azaleas are the classic massive-flowering shrubs of Deep South gardens, producing spectacular displays of white, pink, red, lavender, or salmon flowers in spring that completely cover the plant. These are large, fast-growing plants — give them full mature-size spacing rather than planting them too close. They are much more heat- and root-rot tolerant than Northern azaleas and are specifically bred for Gulf Coast conditions. Mulch deeply with pine straw to maintain moisture and soil acidity. Prune immediately after flowering ends in spring, as next year's buds set on summer growth. Lace bugs are the primary pest — treat with systemic insecticide in late spring.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; southern magnolia tolerates a range of soils but performs best in fertile, moist, slightly acidic conditions.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; southern magnolia is the defining icon of the Deep South, a massive evergreen tree with leathery, glossy dark green leaves (with rusty-brown undersides) and enormous, intensely fragrant white flowers up to 12 inches across that open from May through September — among the largest flowers of any native North American tree. The tree is native to the coastal plain from North Carolina to Texas and is the state flower and tree of Mississippi and Louisiana. Compact cultivars like 'Little Gem' (20–25 feet, repeat-blooming) and 'Brackens Brown Beauty' make it accessible for smaller gardens. The cone-like fruit with brilliant red seeds is highly ornamental and provides important food for gray squirrels, turkeys, and songbirds. Plant away from foundations, driveways, and structures to allow for its eventual large size.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in any well-drained to moist soil in any light condition from full sun to deep shade; this is one of the most adaptable native plants for the South.
directions: Plant in any soil — wet or dry, acidic or alkaline, rich or poor, sun or deep shade — wax myrtle tolerates virtually every condition in zone 7–11 once established and is arguably the Gulf Coast's most versatile and valuable native shrub. The aromatic, evergreen foliage provides year-round screening and privacy; both male and female plants produce tiny waxy blue-gray berries that encrust the branches in fall and winter, providing essential food for yellow-rumped warblers, tree swallows, and many other bird species that can digest the high-fat wax. The root nodules fix atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil over time. Prune heavily at any time to maintain a desired size, or allow it to develop naturally into a multi-trunked small tree 15–20 feet tall. The aromatic leaves have traditional use as an insect repellent.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes readily in shade.
directions: Plant Spanish bluebell bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, well-drained woodland soil in partial shade. The upright stems carry many bell-shaped blue, pink, or white flowers in mid to late spring. It naturalizes vigorously beneath trees and shrubs and can spread aggressively, so site it thoughtfully. Allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally to feed the bulbs for next season.

plant_note: Soak and nick seed; start indoors 6 weeks early.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seeds overnight, then start Spanish flag indoors six weeks before the last frost. The vigorous vine produces one-sided sprays of tubular flowers that shade from scarlet through orange to creamy yellow. Provide a strong trellis in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, transplanting after all frost danger passes. Hummingbirds and butterflies flock to the blooms, which continue until first frost.

plant_note: Sow after frost danger passes in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: This South African annual thrives in hot, dry sites and forms a low, spreading carpet of succulent foliage topped with neon 1.5-inch daisy-like blooms. Direct sow into fast-draining soil after the last frost, barely covering the fine seed and keeping it lightly moist until germination. Give plants full sun; flowers open in bright light and close at night or under clouds. Space plants so they can knit together along borders, between pavers, or in rock gardens. Avoid overwatering, as soggy roots quickly rot in this drought-loving species.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring; seed benefits from cool soil and light to germinate.
directions: Sparky produces charming double and semi-double blooms in warm apricot tones with burgundy undersides on compact 8-to-10-inch plants. Scatter the tiny seed on the soil surface in early spring, pressing it in without covering since it needs light to sprout. Poppies resent transplanting, so direct sow where they are to grow and thin seedlings to prevent crowding. Drought-tolerant and deer-resistant once established, this variety draws bees and butterflies to the garden. Let a few blooms go to seed and it will often self-sow for the following year.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; tolerates cool weather.
directions: This vigorous, late-flowering Spencer type bears fragrant ivory ruffled blooms 1 to 2 inches wide that resemble delicate confections. Sow seed directly in early spring in rich, well-drained soil, providing a trellis or netting for the climbing vines to grab. Nicking or soaking the hard seed coat overnight speeds germination. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and harvest flowers often to keep the plants producing through the season. Its slightly shorter stems and late bloom time extend the sweet pea season for intimate bouquets.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable; likes cool roots.
directions: Princess Elizabeth blends peachy salmon and soft cream tones in romantic, lightly fragrant blooms ideal for cottage-style beds and vertical gardens. Direct sow in early spring in cool, fertile soil, soaking or scarifying the seed first to improve sprouting. Set up a sturdy trellis for the vigorous vines and keep the soil consistently moist. Frequent cutting encourages the plant to produce armfuls of blooms over a longer window. Plant in full sun with cool roots, mulching to buffer summer heat.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; cold stratification for 4 weeks improves germination of seed.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks before sowing; established divisions establish more reliably than seed-grown plants and can be done in spring or fall. Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, average to rich soil; plants tolerate wet conditions and make excellent rain garden plants. The three-petaled flowers in purple, blue, pink, or white open only for a single morning but are produced in rapid succession over many weeks. Shear the entire plant to 8 in after the main bloom flush to stimulate fresh foliage and a second round of flowers in fall.

plant_note: Surface-sow; light aids germination. Short-lived but self-sows.
directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it in, as light helps it germinate. Also called dotted horsemint, this native produces tiered whorls of yellow flowers spotted with purple, set off by showy pink bracts. It thrives in full sun and sandy, dry soil, and its aromatic foliage deters deer. A biennial to short-lived perennial, it self-sows freely and is a magnet for bees, wasps, and other beneficial pollinators.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or early fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; space 12–18 inches apart for quick coverage.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, avoiding heavy clay that stays waterlogged in winter; spotted deadnettle forms a quick-spreading mat of silver-marked foliage that suppresses weeds effectively under trees and shrubs. The small pink, white, or purple flowers appear in late spring and continue sporadically through summer. Cut plants back hard in midsummer if they become leggy or stressed by heat to encourage a flush of fresh foliage. The silver-splashed leaves of popular cultivars like 'White Nancy' and 'Beacon Silver' brighten dark corners more effectively than most groundcovers. Divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; surface-sow as light aids germination.
directions: Cold-stratify the seed for a month and surface-sow, since light improves germination. This tall native perennial produces large, domed clusters of fragrant rose-pink flowers from August into September. Grow it in full sun to part shade and consistently moist to wet soil, ideal for rain gardens and pond edges. Its towering stems are a butterfly favorite and add bold structure to the back of the border.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; spreading sedge colonizes slowly by long stolons to form a low groundcover that is easy to control if needed.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; spreading sedge is a low-growing native sedge of eastern woodland floors, distinguished by its unusually wide leaves (up to 3/4 inch wide, broad for a sedge) that are a beautiful glaucous blue-green color with a matte, almost frosted surface. The prostrate to arching leaves spread loosely to form a lax groundcover very different in character from the more upright native sedges — relaxed, natural-looking, and graceful. It spreads slowly by long stolons to form a loose, open mat. The cultivar 'Hobb' (sold as 'Blue Bunny') offers especially pronounced blue-green foliage and is widely available in the native plant trade. An outstanding groundcover for the shaded woodland garden in zone 4–8, especially effective paired with native spring wildflowers, ferns, and bleeding heart.

plant_note: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in woodland soil; they self-sow freely once established and naturalize to form large colonies.
directions: Plant small, deep-set corms in fall in moist, humus-rich woodland soil under deciduous trees where the plant receives full spring sun before the canopy leafs out; this is one of the earliest spring ephemerals, producing delicate pink-striped white flowers that carpet the forest floor in March and April. The plant goes completely dormant by late spring, so interplant densely with ferns or hostas to cover the void. The tiny underground corms are edible — tasting like a mild, chestnut-flavored radish — but harvesting kills the plant; admire rather than harvest from naturalized colonies. Plant in masses of at least 50 corms for a natural effect, as single plants look sparse.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Spring Green is an eye-catching celosia standing about 40 inches tall with fresh lime-green plumes that thrive through late summer heat. Start seed indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost, barely covering it and keeping soil warm around 75 degrees F for best germination. Transplant into full sun after frost danger passes, spacing plants for good airflow. The long, sturdy stems make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers and attract pollinators. Deadhead or harvest regularly to keep the display coming through the growing season.

plant_note: Start indoors in cool conditions; transplant in early spring or fall.
directions: Midnight Glow features rich velvet-purple petals with glowing yellow centers that fade into deep purple edges, producing large 3-inch blooms on uniform, well-branched plants. Start seed indoors in cool conditions and dark until germination, then grow on in bright, cool light before transplanting. Pansies thrive in cool, short-day weather, making them ideal for early spring and fall displays. Set plants in full sun to part shade in rich, moist soil and deadhead to prolong bloom. The edible flowers make a colorful garnish for salads and desserts.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; foliage smells of onion when crushed.
directions: Plant spring starflower bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The fragrant, star-shaped, pale blue flowers appear over grassy foliage that smells faintly of onion when bruised. It naturalizes readily in mild climates, forming spreading clumps in rock gardens and borders. Keep the site dry during summer dormancy and divide crowded clumps every few years.

plant_note: Plant pea-sized yellow tubers (which resemble corn kernels, giving the plant its name) 1–2 inches deep in fall in groups for a natural display; interplant with hostas or ferns.
directions: Plant the small, yellow, corn-kernel-like tubers 1–2 inches deep in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in full to partial shade; squirrel corn is a spring ephemeral closely related to Dutchman's breeches (D. cucullaria) but with rounded, heart-shaped flowers instead of pantaloon-shaped ones, and it has a hyacinth-like fragrance that D. cucullaria lacks. The finely dissected gray-green foliage and delicate white flowers appear in March–April then disappear completely by early June. The plant produces its namesake yellow tubers rather than the white grain-like tubers of its close relative. Entirely toxic — do not confuse with any edible plant. Forms small colonies over time as tubers multiply.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; huge seed heads dry beautifully.
directions: Plant star of Persia bulbs in fall about six inches deep in sharply drained soil and full sun. Each stem bears an enormous globe up to ten inches across of metallic star-shaped violet florets in late spring. The dried seed heads persist for weeks and are prized for arrangements. Site it in a hot, dry spot and let the bulbs bake during summer dormancy to ensure reliable return.

plant_note: Direct sow; drought tolerant and heat loving.
directions: Direct sow star of the veldt in warm, well-drained soil after frost danger passes. This South African annual bears glowing orange and apricot daisies that open in bright sun and close on cloudy days. It thrives in heat and lean soil with minimal water, making it excellent for dry sunny beds and containers. Deadhead spent blooms and avoid overwatering, which can cause rot.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost in full sun.
directions: Starflower opens delicate pale blue and white blooms, but its real magic is the spherical seedhead covered in papery, striped cups each cradling a five-pointed dark star. Direct sow in spring around the last frost, lightly covering the seed and keeping it evenly moist until seedlings emerge. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil. Cut blooms for fresh arrangements or leave them to mature into the ornamental seedheads prized for dried bouquets and crafts. The flowers draw bees and butterflies through summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after all frost danger passes in warm soil.
directions: State Fair Mix is a pollinator magnet producing immense flowers dominated by reds and purples with orange, pink, white, and yellow scattered throughout. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning seedlings to give these tall plants room to branch. Zinnias grow quickly and bloom heavily until fall frost when kept deadheaded or cut. The long, sturdy stems make outstanding cut flowers for arrangements. Provide good airflow and water at the base to reduce foliar disease.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix; seeds come in a papery casing and germination is slow. Statice prefers cool conditions early in the season. Harvest flower stems for drying when 75% of the florets on each branch have opened. The papery calyces — which hold their color dried — are the ornamental part; the true petals are tiny and brief.

plant_note: Seed benefits from cold stratification; transplant seedlings in spring.
directions: Sea holly brings striking architectural form with steel-blue thistle-like flower heads and spiny bracts that shimmer in moonlight. Seed germinates best after a period of cold, moist stratification, so chill sown flats or sow outdoors in late fall. Transplant carefully, as the deep taproot dislikes disturbance, into lean, well-drained soil in full sun. Drought-tolerant once established, it blooms profusely through summer and provides winter interest with long-standing seedheads. Cut stems dry beautifully for everlasting arrangements.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as young plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix — light aids germination, so cover with only the thinnest layer of vermiculite if at all. Stock thrives in cool weather (55–65°F nights) and the fragrance is intensified by cool temperatures. Transplant to the garden in early spring; plants begin to decline when daytime temperatures consistently exceed 80°F. Cut flowers when one-third of the buds on the spike are open for the longest vase life.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant after last frost.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist medium at 35–40°F for 4–6 weeks before sowing indoors; germination is slow but improved by stratification. Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; Stokes' aster is native to the American Southeast and tolerates heat and humidity well but dislikes wet winter conditions. The large, cornflower-like blooms in blue, lavender, or white appear from early summer to fall and are excellent for cutting. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; plants spread slowly and are well-behaved in the border.

plant_note: Biennial; sow in spring or summer for bloom the following year.
directions: Canterbury bells, also called bellflower, is an elegant, tall biennial that makes a dramatic backdrop for shorter annuals and perennials. Surface sow the fine seed, which needs light to germinate, and grow seedlings on before transplanting into rich, moist, well-drained soil. As a biennial, plants form a leafy rosette the first year and send up their cup-and-saucer bloom spikes the second season into early summer. Site in full sun to part shade and stake tall stems in windy spots. The nectar-rich flowers attract bees and other pollinators.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and press lightly — light aids germination, so barely cover seed. Strawflowers tolerate drought and poor soils remarkably well. For drying, harvest stems when the central button of each head is still closed, before the outer bracts fully open; they will continue to open as they dry. Deadhead garden plants regularly to maintain continuous bloom.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in late winter or early spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, cool, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; striped maple is a cool-climate understory tree that requires protection from afternoon sun and heat in zone 6+ and will not thrive in zone 7 and warmer.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, cool soil in partial to full shade — striped maple is one of North America's most beautiful native understory trees, instantly recognizable by the striking vertical white-and-green striping on the young bark (giving it the alternate name 'snakebark maple' or 'moosewood'). The large, three-lobed leaves turn butter yellow in fall. It is a true understory specialist that grows naturally in the shade beneath taller hardwoods in cool, moist, acidic mountain forests — it is shade-tolerant enough to regenerate in nearly complete shade. Moose browse the bark in winter (giving the common name). Not suited to zone 7 and warmer, where the heat and humidity are too much for this cool-climate species. An outstanding ornamental tree for the shaded garden in zone 3–6, particularly in New England, the upper Midwest, and the Appalachian highlands. The ornamental bark is visible year-round and is spectacular.

plant_note: Plant container trees in spring or fresh seed in fall in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, clay to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; sugar hackberry is exceptionally adaptable to difficult soil conditions including alkaline soils, clay, and brief flooding.
directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade — sugar hackberry is the primary Celtis species of the Gulf Coast and Deep South, better adapted to the heat, humidity, and occasional flooding of zone 7–9 than the common hackberry (C. occidentalis). The distinctive warty, corky-gray bark is unmistakable and one of the most interesting bark textures of any native tree. Small orange to purple-black berries in fall are among the most important food sources for migrating songbirds — more than 50 species consume the berries, including yellow-rumped warblers and hermit thrushes. The tree is the exclusive larval host for the hackberry emperor butterfly and American snout butterfly, two distinctive native butterflies wholly dependent on Celtis species. It tolerates urban conditions, compacted soils, drought, and alkaline pH remarkably well. A keystone wildlife tree for the Gulf Coast landscape.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Summer Lovin bears a mix of creamy lemon-tipped petals with burgundy or strawberry-pink centers, often several color variations on a single plant. Direct sow the large seed after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil and full sun, planting about an inch deep. The tall, branching stems are pollenless and stiff, perfect for clean cut-flower bouquets. Thin seedlings to give plants room and stake if needed in exposed sites. Harvest often to encourage more blooms across the summer.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; tolerates wet soils.
directions: Plant summer snowflake bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in sun to partial shade. Despite its name it blooms in mid to late spring, bearing nodding white bells tipped with green on arching stems. Unusually among bulbs it tolerates heavy, wet soils and naturalizes along ponds and in damp borders. Leave the strappy foliage to ripen fully, and the clumps will slowly increase over the years.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify seed; taproot resents transplanting.
directions: Sundial lupine, also called wild lupine, is a heirloom perennial native to the eastern U.S. and a vital host plant for the Karner blue butterfly. Scarify or cold-stratify the hard seed and direct sow in fall or early spring, since the deep taproot dislikes transplanting. Grow in full sun in lean, sandy, well-drained soil where it naturalizes in wildflower gardens and meadows. Its blue flower spikes appear in late spring to early summer and draw many pollinators. Avoid rich soil and excess water, which this drought-tolerant native does not need.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost when soil is warm; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for extended bloom.
directions: Direct sow seed 1 in deep after the last frost date, thinning to final spacing once seedlings reach 6 in tall. Sunflowers prefer poor-to-average soil and resent transplanting due to a taproot. Single-stemmed types bloom once; branching varieties produce multiple heads if cut regularly. Harvest seed heads when the back of the head turns yellow-brown and seeds feel firm.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; good for containers due to compact habit.
directions: Sow seed one inch deep after the last frost once soil warms. This dwarf heirloom grows just two to three feet tall, producing dozens of fluffy, double golden-yellow pom-pom blooms. Its compact, branching habit suits containers, borders, and small gardens, and the sturdy stems make fine cut flowers. Give it full sun and moderate water, and deadhead to keep new blooms coming through the season.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; soak or nick seed to speed germination.
directions: Sunrise Blend offers a colorful assortment of trumpet blooms that flutter against vigorous heart-shaped foliage from midsummer to the first fall frost. Soak or nick the hard seed coat, then direct sow after the last frost near a trellis, fence, or arbor for the climbing vines to scale. The flowers open in early morning and may stay open on cloudy or cool days. Grow in full sun in average soil, avoiding overly rich ground that favors leaves over flowers. Note that the seeds are poisonous if eaten, so keep them away from children and pets.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; loves heat and dry soil.
directions: Sunshine Blend gazania is a heat- and drought-loving groundcover in warm colors from yellow to mauve, with lush low foliage and blooms held high on their stems. Start seed indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost, covering lightly since darkness aids germination, then transplant after frost danger passes. Grow in full sun in sandy, well-drained soil where the daisy-like petals unfurl in sunshine and close under clouds. Water sparingly once established, as this tough plant tolerates drought and poor soil. Deadhead to keep the color coming through summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Sunspot delivers large sunflower heads on surprisingly dwarf 18-to-24-inch stalks, making it a favorite for kids and small gardens. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings so each plant has room to develop its outsized bloom. The short stature makes dramatic cut flowers and container plantings, and the heads later provide seeds for birds. Provide steady moisture while young and full sun throughout the season.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; transplant after frost.
directions: Salpiglossis, or painted tongue, produces velvety, trumpet-shaped flowers with striking metallic coloration and intricate veining in a range of jewel tones. Start seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost, covering it fully since the seed needs darkness to germinate, and keep soil warm. Transplant after frost into rich, well-drained soil in full sun with shelter from strong wind. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and stake the somewhat lax stems. Deadhead regularly and enjoy the blooms as long-lasting cut flowers.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in consistently moist to wet, acidic soil; do not allow to dry out during establishment. Do not plant too deeply — keep the crown at or above soil level.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to light sun; swamp azalea is the most fragrant of all native North American deciduous azaleas, producing intensely clove-spice-scented white flowers (often pink-flushed) in late June through July, extending the native azalea bloom season well into summer after all other species have finished. It grows naturally in swamps, bogs, stream banks, and wet woodland margins from Maine to Florida, and tolerates standing water better than any other Rhododendron. The plant spreads slowly by rhizome and root sprouts to form a colony; purchase container-grown propagated plants only. Mulch with pine bark or pine needle litter to maintain soil acidity and moisture. Never allow to dry out in summer.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade; this plant grows naturally in very acidic, boggy, waterlogged conditions and will not thrive in alkaline or neutral soils.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp cyrilla (also called titi or leatherwood) is a distinctive multi-season native shrub of coastal plain bogs, swamp margins, and pocosins in the Southeast, producing long racemes of small white flowers in June and July that are intensely fragrant and among the most important summer nectar sources for native bees and the basis of the highly prized 'titi honey' produced by southeastern beekeepers. The semi-evergreen leaves turn brilliant orange-red in fall before dropping, and in warm winters may persist through spring. The plant is adapted to very acidic, poorly drained soils where it has little competition. An outstanding specimen for bog gardens, rain gardens, and acid pond margins in zone 7–9.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in late summer to early fall in moist to wet, acidic soil at the water's edge or in shallow water; fall planting allows roots to establish before winter dormancy and spring bloom.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; this native species is the parent of the beloved Louisiana iris hybrid group, growing naturally in swamps, bayou margins, ditches, and wet flatwoods of the Gulf Coast from Florida to Texas. The large, elegant blue-violet flowers (occasionally varying to pink or white) in spring are extraordinary among native wildflowers for their sophisticated form and vivid color. Louisiana iris hybrids have expanded the color range to include burgundy, yellow, white, pink, and bicolors — if you want color variety, seek out named Louisiana iris hybrids from specialty nurseries; for native restoration, stick with the true species. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. Plant at pond margins, in rain gardens, or in any consistently wet spot.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs with the neck just at or above soil level in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade; does not tolerate dry conditions. Excellent for pond margins and rain gardens.
directions: Plant large bulbs with the neck at or just above soil level in consistently wet to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; swamp lily is a stunning native bulb of the Gulf Coast, Florida, and Caribbean, growing naturally in freshwater swamps, bog margins, stream banks, and wet ditches. It produces large, fragrant, spider-like white flowers with narrow strap petals in summer and into fall — often repeatedly after summer rain events — on upright stalks among bold, strap-like evergreen leaves. Plant at pond margins, in consistently moist rain garden hollows, or in large containers kept in saucers of water on the patio. The bold foliage and exotic flowers provide a tropical look in Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens. Divide crowded clumps every 4–5 years by removing and replanting offsets.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall (seeds need 30-day cold stratification) or cold-stratify indoors and start in late winter; transplant to the garden in spring. Unlike butterfly weed, swamp milkweed germinates more reliably after stratification.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, average soil in full sun to partial shade; swamp milkweed is an essential monarch butterfly host plant that tolerates wet conditions where common milkweed struggles, making it ideal for rain gardens, pond margins, and low-lying areas. The bright rose-pink to mauve flower clusters bloom from June through August and are heavily visited by monarchs, swallowtails, and bumble bees. Unlike other milkweeds, it does not spread aggressively by rhizomes and stays in well-behaved clumps, making it one of the best milkweed species for garden planting. Leave seedpods on through winter for wildlife interest and natural reseeding. The white-flowered cultivar 'Ice Ballet' is also excellent.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in moist to wet soil near water features; this is the rose for wet, shaded areas where others cannot survive.
directions: Plant in consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade near ponds, streams, or rain gardens; unlike most roses this eastern native tolerates waterlogged soil and grows naturally in swampy margins and along stream banks. The fragrant, single pink flowers in early summer attract native bees, and the bright red rose hips that follow persist through winter, providing critical food for robins, mockingbirds, and cedar waxwings. Prune only to remove dead wood; the arching, prickly canes can be tied to a support or left to form a dense, impenetrable thicket with high wildlife value. Highly disease resistant in moist conditions where other roses suffer.

plant_note: Nick or soak seed in warm water 24 hours before sowing indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes when soil has warmed. Alternatively, direct sow in very late spring. Plants emerge late — do not assume they are dead until June.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, rich soil in full sun; swamp rose mallow produces the largest flowers of any perennial hardy to zone 4 — dinner-plate sized blooms up to 10–12 inches across in white, pink, red, or bicolor from July through September. It is native to wetlands, marshes, and stream margins throughout eastern North America and thrives in rain gardens and pond edges where few other plants bloom so dramatically. Plants emerge very late in spring — sometimes not until late May or early June — so mark their location to avoid digging them. Numerous cultivars with red, pink, white, and bicolor flowers are available (Luna series, Disco Belle, Summerific series). Cut stems to the ground in winter after they die back.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full to partial sun; plants can also be started from divisions in spring for faster bloom.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; swamp sunflower is arguably the most spectacular of the native sunflowers for fall bloom, producing hundreds of bright yellow flowers on tall multibranching stems from October through frost — often when almost nothing else is blooming. It grows naturally in wet pine flatwoods, swamp margins, and roadside ditches across the coastal South and is perfectly suited to Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens where the late season heat and moisture support its growth. The plant spreads aggressively by rhizome and self-seeding; give it space or divide annually in spring. Monarch butterflies and native bees mob the flowers. Pinch tips twice in early summer for a shorter, more compact plant.

plant_note: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; swamp white oak tolerates extended flooding and clay soils better than most oaks and is an excellent choice for wet, low-lying areas.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; swamp white oak is the premier large native oak for wet, clay-heavy, or periodically flooded sites — conditions where most other oaks decline. The exfoliating, peeling bark on the upper branches is a distinctive and attractive identification feature, revealing lighter bark beneath. The acorns are large, borne in pairs on long stalks (2–4 inches), and are among the most important fall-winter food for wood ducks (which nest in cavities of old trees), turkeys, deer, and dozens of other species. Fall color is yellow to bronze. The tree develops into a large, rounded specimen 50–60 feet tall with a broad, open crown. Its tolerance of wet, clay, compacted, and temporarily flooded conditions makes it an outstanding choice for low areas of the landscape, stream buffers, and rain gardens where it can manage stormwater.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 weeks early; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start Swan River daisy indoors about six weeks before the last frost for earliest bloom. The dainty, fragrant daisies in blue, mauve, and white cover mounding plants ideal for edging, containers, and hanging baskets. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding overly rich conditions that soften growth. Shear lightly after the first flush and feed lightly to keep the plant blooming through summer.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; alyssum tolerates light frost and prefers cool weather.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface and press gently — light aids germination; just barely cover if needed to prevent the seed from blowing away. Sweet alyssum may slow or stop blooming in peak summer heat; shear plants back by half and they will rebound strongly when temperatures cool in early fall. The honey-scented flowers attract beneficial insects throughout the season.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring; avoid bare-root transplanting as magnolias resent root disturbance. Site carefully as the roots are shallow and wide-spreading.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, acid soil in full sun to partial shade; this native magnolia naturally inhabits swampy lowlands and stream banks from Massachusetts to Florida and is one of the best large shrubs or small trees for wet areas in the southern garden. The creamy-white, lemon-scented flowers appear over a long season from late spring through summer, and the semi-evergreen to evergreen leaves (depending on winter severity) have a striking silver underside. In the Gulf South it can reach 30 feet as a multi-stemmed, nearly evergreen tree; in colder zones it is smaller and more deciduous. The red fruit clusters provide fall and winter food for songbirds and small mammals.

plant_note: Direct sow outdoors 4–8 weeks before last frost; seeds prefer cool soil (55–65°F) and young plants tolerate frost.
directions: Nick or soak seeds overnight before sowing 1 in deep in cool, enriched soil at the base of a trellis. Sweet peas perform best during cool weather and decline as summer heat arrives — sow as early as possible. Cut flowers frequently, as even a few fading blooms left on the plant will trigger seed set and halt blooming. Provide sturdy support from the start, as vines climb to 4–6 ft.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; often blooms the second year as a biennial.
directions: Sweet Rose Magic delights with flowers that open white, shift to soft pink, and mature into rich rose, creating a tricolor display on a single stem. Start seed indoors and barely cover it, or sow outdoors in late spring, transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. As a biennial it typically forms foliage the first year and blooms the second, though it may flower the first season from an early start. Grow in full sun to part shade and deadhead for repeat bloom. The clustered flowers are lightly clove-scented and excellent for cutting.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 weeks early for cut flowers.
directions: Start sweet scabious indoors about six weeks before the last frost, or direct sow once soil warms. The domed pincushion flowers in deep maroon, pink, and white are long-lasting cut flowers and pollinator magnets. Grow in full sun and fertile, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil, staking tall stems if needed. Harvest or deadhead frequently to keep the plants producing new blooms all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; does not transplant well due to taproot.
directions: Sow seeds directly in a sunny, well-drained location in early spring; thin to 9–12 in apart as seedlings emerge. The fragrant, fringed, powder-puff flowers in shades of pink, purple, yellow, and white are excellent for cutting, with a pleasant, musky sweet scent. Plants prefer cool weather and will begin to decline as summer heat intensifies — make a second sowing in late summer for fall bloom in warm climates. Successive sowings every 3–4 weeks in spring extend the flowering season through early summer.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; in mild climates can also be sown in early spring. Spreads vigorously by stolons and self-seeds freely once established.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to light morning sun; in zone 8–9 this European violet stays semi-evergreen in winter and begins flowering as early as December in mild years, providing critical early nectar for emerging queen bumblebees. The fragrant violet or white flowers are fully edible and prized for crystallizing, flavoring syrups, and garnishing desserts and cocktails; the leaves are also edible raw or cooked as greens. The plant spreads energetically by stolons and self-seeds, naturalizing quickly under deciduous trees and shrubs. Remove unwanted seedlings annually if spread needs to be managed in a formal garden.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant can go out 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants are frost-tolerant.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; sweet William is technically a biennial that flowers in its second year, but modern annual varieties bloom the first year from an early indoor start. The domed, fragrant flower clusters in vivid shades of red, pink, white, and bicolor appear in late spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters promptly to extend the blooming period; plants self-sow readily to maintain a colony from year to year.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil; avoid alkaline soils which cause chlorosis. Choose sterile (fruitless) cultivars like 'Rotundiloba' if the spiky seed balls are undesirable.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; sweetgum is one of the most common and beautiful deciduous trees of the eastern US, producing perhaps the most spectacular and varied fall color display of any native tree — individual trees vary from pure yellow to orange to red to deep burgundy-purple, and many trees display all these colors simultaneously on the same tree. The star-shaped leaves are distinctive and beautiful. The 'gumballs' (spiky spherical seed capsules) are the tree's only liability — the seeds provide important food for finches and small rodents but the fallen capsules can be a nuisance underfoot. Sterile cultivars like 'Rotundiloba' produce no seeds and have rounder leaf lobes. The tree grows 1–2 feet per year and is exceptionally adaptable to a wide range of conditions. The aromatic resin (storax) has traditional medicinal and fragrance uses.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; sweetleaf is not commonly available in nurseries — look for it from native plant specialists and botanic gardens.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; sweetleaf (also called horse sugar) is a native shrub or small tree of moist woodland slopes and stream banks in the southeastern US, best known for three remarkable characteristics: the leaves have a distinctly sweet taste (hence both common names), deer browse the sweet foliage voraciously; the bright yellow flower clusters in late winter to early spring appear before most other shrubs and provide crucial early-season nectar for queen bumblebees emerging from overwintering; and it is the exclusive larval host plant for Henry's elfin butterfly, a small, understated but increasingly rare butterfly that depends entirely on this plant for its caterpillars. Underused in horticulture, sweetleaf deserves much wider planting for its early nectar value and butterfly support.

plant_note: Start indoors in cool, dark conditions; transplant in early spring or fall.
directions: This Swiss heirloom is the parent of most modern pansies, producing low, bushy plants covered in showy, large, velvety flowers. Start seed indoors in cool conditions and darkness, then grow seedlings on in bright light before transplanting in early spring or fall. Pansies thrive in cool weather and make a fine companion to spring bulbs. Grow in full sun to part shade in rich, moist soil and deadhead to extend bloom. The edible petals garnish soft cheeses and baked goods.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Strawflowers live up to their name, with stiff, papery bracts that look like colored straw and never wilt, making them the classic everlasting cut flower. Surface sow the seed indoors since light aids germination, then transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. Swiss Giants produce extra-large double blooms in bright shades on long, sturdy stems. Cut flowers just before they fully open and hang them upside down to dry for lasting arrangements. Deadhead spent blooms to keep plants flowering, and enjoy the pollinators they attract.

plant_note: Sow indoors and cover; seed germinates best in darkness and cool conditions.
directions: Start seed indoors covered and in darkness, keeping the soil cool for best germination. This heirloom mix produces large, velvety, edible blossoms in blue, purple, yellow, orange, and burgundy. Pansies love cool weather, so transplant in early spring or fall and grow in full sun to part shade with steady moisture. Deadhead often and they will bloom prolifically until summer heat or hard freezes arrive.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in spring; warm soil (above 60°F) promotes best germination.
directions: Sow seeds in full sun after last frost in average to poor, well-drained soil; switchgrass is extremely adaptable and tolerates clay, sand, wet or dry conditions, and even brief flooding — a true workhorse native grass. The airy, branching panicles of tiny seeds in late summer create a misty, luminous effect, and many cultivars develop spectacular red, burgundy, or orange fall color. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter; divide every 3–4 years in spring when the clump center dies out. Named cultivars like 'Shenandoah' and 'Prairie Fire' offer predictably strong red fall color.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall (cold stratification improves germination) in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; tall bellflower is a biennial that blooms in its second year — establish a planting from seed in two successive years to ensure continuous bloom thereafter.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — tall bellflower is a striking native biennial of eastern forest understories that can reach 3–6 feet tall when in bloom, producing a long terminal raceme and lateral branches covered with flat, five-lobed blue-violet flowers that open wide (unlike the nodding, bell-shaped flowers of most bellflowers) throughout summer. It is one of the most shade-tolerant native wildflowers capable of producing impressive flowering stems in quite deep shade. The long, curved styles and protruding stamens give the flowers a distinctive star-burst appearance. The plant is a true biennial — forming a rosette in year one, blooming and setting seed in year two, then dying. Self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions to maintain a naturalized colony. An outstanding native plant for creating a vertical accent in the shaded native garden in zones 3–8.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; seed needs darkness to germinate.
directions: Bachelor's button, also known as cornflower, is a drought-tolerant, low-maintenance annual with flowers great for cutting or drying and edible petals for garnishing salads. Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since it needs darkness to germinate, and thin seedlings once up. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil. This tall blend self-sows readily, naturalizing for another crop of flowers the following year. Deadhead or harvest often to prolong the bloom period and support pollinators.

plant_note: Surface sow in spring as seed needs light to germinate; division of established clumps in early spring is more reliable and faster.
directions: Scatter seed on the surface of average, well-drained soil in full sun; tall goldenrod is the single most important plant for migrating monarch butterflies and late-season native bees, providing nectar during the critical fall migration period. Despite its reputation for causing hay fever (the actual culprit is ragweed, which blooms simultaneously), goldenrod pollen is heavy and insect-pollinated, not airborne. The plant spreads aggressively by rhizome in fertile soil; divide every 2–3 years to maintain vigor, using divisions to fill new areas. Excellent companion for ironweed (Vernonia), asters, and Joe-Pye weed in a late-season native garden.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors; snapdragons tolerate cool weather when hardened off.
directions: This tall blend of snapdragons adds classic charm to gardens and cut-flower arrangements, and its blooms are a bee magnet as bumblebees climb right into the mouths. Surface sow the tiny seed indoors since light aids germination, and grow seedlings on before transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so set out hardened plants a bit before the last frost and provide late-afternoon shade in hot regions. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and stake the tall spikes if needed. The edible flowers make a pretty garnish, and deadheading prolongs the display.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moist soil in partial shade; tall thimbleweed self-sows readily and naturalizes in appropriate dry to moist woodland conditions without being invasive.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moist soil in partial shade — tall thimbleweed is an upright, woodland-edge native anemone whose white, 1–1.5 inch flowers are followed by the plant's most distinctive feature: elongated, thimble-shaped seed heads that are covered in cottony white fibers (resembling a thimble wrapped in white fluff) and persist well into winter, providing long-lasting ornamental value. The flowers attract solitary native bees and small beetles. It grows naturally in dry to moist open woodland, woodland edges, and clearings throughout the eastern and central US, tolerating the dry shade beneath dense tree canopies better than most native wildflowers. The persistent cottony seed heads are useful in dried arrangements. More drought-tolerant than most anemone species. An excellent and underused native for dry, shaded woodland garden settings.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 1–2 weeks; often blooms from seed the first year.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist towels for 1–2 weeks at 35–40°F, then sow indoors at 65–75°F; germination remains erratic over 14–28 days. Plant in full sun in poor to average, well-drained soil; this South American native is drought-tolerant and naturalizes readily once established. The wiry, branching stems carry clusters of small purple flowers from midsummer through frost at an unusual height that allows it to weave transparently through other plants. Grown as an annual north of zone 7, it self-sows prolifically even in cold climates and seedlings replace killed parent plants.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; thrives in heat.
directions: Direct sow tassel flower after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil. The small brush-like scarlet and orange flowers rise on wiry stems above tidy rosettes, adding fine texture to arrangements. It thrives in heat and full sun and blooms freely all summer with little care. Deadhead to prolong flowering and allow occasional self-sowing for future plants.

plant_note: Plant in fall; includes paperwhites for forcing.
directions: Plant tazetta daffodil bulbs in fall six inches deep in well-drained soil, or force paperwhite types indoors in pebbles and water. These clustered, intensely fragrant narcissi are well suited to mild-winter gardens and bloom early. Give them full sun and let the foliage die back naturally outdoors. Forced bulbs are generally discarded after flowering, while garden bulbs naturalize in suitable zones.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Teddy Bear produces fluffy, bright-yellow puffball blooms 3 to 5 inches across on a compact, multi-branching plant 24 to 36 inches tall. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings to give each plant room to branch and form its many pompom flowers. It works beautifully as a short hedge and makes a fabulous, long-lasting cut flower. Water steadily while young and enjoy the double blooms all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow cold-stratified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, thin, rocky, cedar glade soil in full sun; Tennessee coneflower is adapted to the extreme conditions of limestone cedar glades and requires excellent drainage — it will rot in clay or wet soils.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, thin rocky soil in full sun — Tennessee coneflower is a Federally Threatened native species known from only a handful of cedar glade sites in Davidson and Rutherford counties of central Tennessee, where it grows in the extremely shallow, rocky, alkaline soil over limestone bedrock that characterizes these rare ecosystems. It is distinguished from other coneflowers by its upward-reflexed (rather than drooping) ray petals, giving the flower a more open, daisy-like appearance. The species was removed from the federal endangered species list in 2011 after recovery efforts increased its wild populations — a conservation success story. It performs well in gardens as a drought-tolerant, heat-hardy perennial for well-drained, sunny, alkaline to neutral soils in zones 4–9. Growing this species in cultivation helps maintain genetic diversity and reduces collection pressure on wild populations. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants.

plant_note: Plant tubers outdoors after the last frost when the soil is warm.
directions: Terracotta produces romantic terracotta-bronze blooms with wavy petals on long stems, making it an outstanding cut flower and a strong tuber producer. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil once frost has passed, laying them horizontally with the eye up. Provide full sun and stake tall plants to keep the long cutting stems upright. Cut flowers frequently and deadhead to encourage continuous bloom into fall. Dig and store the tubers before hard frost in cold climates.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors; seed needs light to germinate. Transplant when hardened off.
directions: Start seed indoors on the soil surface under light, since it needs light to germinate. This mix produces tall, upright spikes of ruffled, nectar-rich blooms in vivid colors. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so transplant in early spring for strong stems and abundant flowers. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and cut spikes regularly for long-lasting bouquets and repeat bloom.

plant_note: Sow in fall; scarify seed and inoculate with rhizobia for best results. Blooms the following spring.
directions: Nick or soak the hard seed coat and sow in fall so plants establish for spring bloom. The state flower of Texas, this native annual lupine forms spikes of striking blue flowers tipped with white. Grow in full sun and dry, well-drained soil where, as a legume, it fixes nitrogen and needs little care. Let plants set seed to naturalize, and expect the strongest displays after a cool, moist winter.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in alkaline, rocky, well-drained soil; extremely slow-growing but extremely long-lived. Scarify seeds before sowing to aid germination.
directions: Plant in alkaline to neutral, rocky or caliche, sharply drained soil in full sun; this aristocratic native evergreen shrub of the Texas Hill Country and desert Southwest is prized for its intensely fragrant violet-purple flower clusters that smell like Concord grape candy and appear in early spring. It is extremely slow-growing (6–12 inches per year), drought tolerant once established, and virtually pest and disease free. The distinctive woody seed pods contain bright red seeds that are highly toxic if eaten. Give it an excellent drainage and poor alkaline soil — rich, moist conditions cause root rot. An excellent foundation or specimen plant for Gulf Coast and zone 8–9 gardens where winter temperatures stay above 10°F.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; seed germinates in darkness.
directions: The Bride stands out among bachelor's buttons for its unusual snowy-white, frilly blooms and captivating honey fragrance. Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since darkness aids germination, and thin the seedlings once established. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil where this drought-tolerant annual needs little care. Frequent cutting or deadheading keeps the plants producing their pollinator-friendly flowers, which draw bees and butterflies. Leave a few flowers to set seed and it will often self-sow.

plant_note: Plant the potted or bare-root shrub in spring after frost danger passes.
directions: The Champion Sunblush is an easy-care flowering rose shrub with brilliant, sun-kissed petals suited to both beginner and experienced gardeners. Set the plant in full sun in rich, well-drained soil, digging a hole wide enough for the roots and watering in well. Provide consistent moisture at the base to keep foliage dry, and mulch to conserve water and moderate soil temperature. Prune in late winter to shape the shrub and remove dead or crossing canes, and feed in spring as growth begins. Hardy in zones 5 to 9, it rewards steady care with repeat blooms through the season.

plant_note: Plant nursery stock in spring after the ground thaws; can also plant in early fall.
directions: The Rising Sun redbud is a small deciduous tree prized for new growth that emerges in fiery apricot, orange, and gold before aging to lime and green. Site it in rich, well-drained soil where it receives morning sun and some afternoon shade in hot climates. Rosy-pink pea-like flowers cover the bare branches in early spring before the leaves unfurl. Water deeply during the first two years to establish the root system, then supplement only in dry spells. Prune lightly after flowering to shape the canopy.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the second year.
directions: The Watchman appears nearly black from a distance but reveals a rich purplish-burgundy cast up close on towering flower spikes. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, barely covering it, since this biennial usually forms a rosette the first year and blooms the second. Grow in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and stake the tall spikes in windy locations. The dramatic towers make an excellent backdrop for pink, red, yellow, or white flowers and attract bees and butterflies. Let some flowers set seed and it will readily self-sow for future years.

plant_note: Surface sow seed in early spring or start transplants; threadleaf coreopsis is more reliably propagated by division than seed — divisions establish quickly in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade.
directions: Grow in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade — threadleaf coreopsis is the most finely textured and perhaps the most garden-adaptable of all native coreopsis species, producing a season-long display of bright golden-yellow daisy flowers above delicate, needle-like whorled foliage from early summer through fall frost. Unlike most coreopsis, the thread-like leaves create an exceptionally fine-textured mound that contrasts beautifully with bold-leafed perennials. It spreads steadily by rhizome to form wide, weed-suppressing clumps and thrives in poor, dry soils where richer garden perennials struggle. Named cultivars 'Moonbeam' (pale yellow), 'Zagreb' (bright gold, compact), and 'Golden Gain' are widely available and extremely garden-worthy. Deadheading is optional — the plant reblooms regardless, but removing spent flowers extends peak flowering. One of the longest-blooming native perennials available.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall; space 5-6 ft apart for a solid privacy screen.
directions: Thuja Green Giant is a fast-growing evergreen conifer widely used as a living privacy hedge, adding several feet of dense, rich green foliage each year. Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil, spacing plants 5 to 6 feet apart for a continuous screen. Water regularly the first season to establish deep roots; once mature it tolerates heat, humidity, and brief drought. Its naturally pyramidal habit rarely needs pruning, though it takes shearing well if a formal shape is desired. It resists deer browsing and most pests.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.
directions: Thuja Virginian is a compact arborvitae hybrid that offers the dense evergreen screening of Green Giant in a more manageable size for smaller yards. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and water consistently through the first growing season to establish. Its tight, upright pyramidal form makes an excellent privacy hedge or specimen without heavy pruning. Mature plants tolerate heat and humidity and stand up to deer better than many evergreens. Mulch the root zone to conserve moisture and moderate soil temperature.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Thumbelina is a charming dwarf zinnia and 1963 All-America Selections winner, producing abundant cheerful blooms in a wide range of colors on compact plants. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning seedlings for good airflow. Its small stature suits containers, edging, and the front of borders. Deadhead spent blooms to keep flowers coming through summer and into fall. Water at the base to reduce foliar disease, and enjoy the pollinators the flowers attract.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in spring in a hot, sunny location.
directions: Thunderstruck Coral Boom is a fast-growing crape myrtle that produces large panicles of vivid coral-pink blooms from early summer into fall. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; more sun means more flowers and better mildew resistance. Its multi-season interest includes attractive exfoliating bark in winter and colorful fall foliage. Prune in late winter to remove crossing branches and shape the canopy, avoiding heavy topping. Water regularly while establishing, after which it becomes notably heat- and drought-tolerant.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in a warm, sunny spot.
directions: Thunderstruck Rumblin' Red is a crape myrtle celebrated for its fiery red summer blooms and strong year-round interest. Site it in full sun in well-drained soil, where abundant light maximizes flowering and helps prevent powdery mildew. The dark-tinged foliage and smooth exfoliating bark add appeal beyond the long bloom season. Prune lightly in late winter to maintain a tree-like structure rather than topping it hard. Once established it is quite tolerant of heat and dry conditions, needing supplemental water only in extended droughts.

plant_note: Sow in fall or early spring; a reliable wildflower for meadows.
directions: Scatter tidy tips seed on cool, moist soil in fall or early spring and rake in lightly. This California native produces cheerful yellow daisies tipped in white that attract native bees and butterflies. It thrives on lean soil with minimal water once established, making it ideal for wildflower meadows and drought-tolerant plantings. Allow some flowers to set seed for reliable self-sowing in future seasons.

plant_note: Plant bulbs after all frost danger passes in spring, once soil has warmed above 60°F.
directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; each exotic three-petaled flower lasts only one day but new buds open in succession over weeks. The vivid blooms come in yellow, orange, red, pink, and white, typically with a heavily spotted or blotched center cup. Plant in groups of a dozen or more for a showy display from midsummer into fall. In zones colder than 8, dig bulbs after first frost, dry thoroughly, and store in paper bags in a cool, frost-free location.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; propagates from stem bulbils.
directions: Plant tiger lily bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The recurved orange flowers heavily spotted with black nod in mid to late summer atop tall sturdy stems. This vigorous heirloom produces small dark bulbils in the leaf axils that can be planted to grow new bulbs. It is a known carrier of viruses, so plant it well away from other prized lilies.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade.
directions: Tilt-A-Swirl is a bigleaf hydrangea whose mophead blooms shift through pink, red, and green as the season progresses, with flower color influenced by soil pH. Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil where it gets morning sun and shade during the hottest part of the day. Keep the soil consistently moist, especially in summer, as this species wilts quickly when dry. It blooms on old and new wood, so avoid heavy pruning; simply remove spent flowers and dead stems in late winter. Amend soil with aluminum sulfate for bluer tones or lime for pinker ones.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Tiny Tim is a compact, honey-scented sweet alyssum that stays covered in tiny flowers all season with no need to shear back. Surface sow the seed in early spring since light aids germination, pressing it into the soil without covering. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil where it forms a fragrant, spreading groundcover. It withstands heat and drought while drawing beneficial insects and pollinators. Use it to edge beds, tuck between pavers, or spill from containers and hanging baskets.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; ideal for containers or small beds.
directions: Tiny Tower is a very slow-growing dwarf Alberta spruce that keeps a naturally dense, narrow pyramidal shape without shearing, making it perfect for small gardens, entryways, and containers. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and provide good air circulation to discourage spider mites, its main pest. Water regularly the first two seasons and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. In hot climates give it some afternoon shade to prevent needle scorch. Its compact evergreen form also serves as a living holiday tree.

plant_note: Soak seed overnight and sow deep; resents transplanting so direct sow.
directions: Soak the large seed overnight and sow about half an inch deep directly in the garden after frost. This dwarf, non-trailing variety produces a cheerful mix of yellow, red, and orange blooms held above the foliage. Both the peppery leaves and flowers are edible, making it a favorite for salads and companion planting. Grow in full sun and lean soil, since rich ground yields lots of leaves but fewer flowers.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins; they establish quickly and bloom in their first season from divisions.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; toad lily is an elegant late-season bloomer that produces exotic-looking, orchid-like flowers in white, purple, or lilac with dark spots from late summer through fall. The arching stems with clasping leaves create a graceful, architectural presence throughout the season. Consistent moisture is essential — plants decline in dry soil; mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and temperature. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to manage the slowly spreading clumps.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; never collect toadshade from wild populations — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The stalkless (sessile) flower sits directly atop the three mottled leaves.
directions: Plant nursery-grown divisions in fall in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — toadshade trillium is one of the most distinctive of the eastern native trilliums, recognizable immediately by its sessile (stalkless) dark maroon flower that sits directly upon the three large, dramatically mottled leaves without an intermediate stalk. The heavily mottled, silver-and-green leaves are among the most beautiful of any native spring wildflower, providing ornamental interest even before the flowers open. A wide natural range stretches from New York west to Kansas and south to Georgia, growing in rich, moist hardwood forests and along stream banks. Like all trilliums, toadshade takes 7 years to bloom from seed — always purchase nursery-propagated plants and never collect from wild populations. The flowers attract small flies and beetles as pollinators; the seeds are dispersed by ants. An outstanding native wildflower for the shaded woodland garden.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant after frost into part shade.
directions: Tom Thumb Blend is a compact, shade-loving balsam standing 12 to 16 inches tall with bushy plants that come alive with violet, white, and rose double flowers. Start seed indoors and barely cover it, keeping soil warm, then transplant after frost into part shade and rich, moist soil. Bumblebees and hummingbirds are drawn to the array of blooms tucked along the stems. Keep the soil consistently moist, as balsam wilts quickly when dry. Its tidy size makes a charming centerpiece for shady beds and containers.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; thrives in heat.
directions: Torch is a genuine heat lover, setting the dog days of summer ablaze with sizzling 3-inch orange flowers on a large, multi-branching plant for the back of the border. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, barely covering the seed. It tolerates infertile soil, drought, and neglect, so avoid overwatering and rich ground. The tall stems benefit from spacing for airflow and may need staking in exposed sites. Its nectar-rich blooms are irresistible to butterflies and other pollinators.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist soil; tolerates wet sites well.
directions: The Tri-Color Dappled Willow is a fast-growing deciduous shrub whose new spring growth emerges in variegated green, white, and soft pink, with coral-red stems adding winter interest. Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile soil; it thrives in damp spots where many shrubs struggle. Prune hard in late winter to encourage a fresh flush of the brightest variegated foliage. Water generously, as willows are thirsty plants that dislike drying out. Grow it as a rounded shrub in a mixed border or shear it into a formal shape.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil; tolerates wet ground.
directions: This is the standard, tree-form version of the tri-color dappled willow, grafted atop a single trunk to create a lollipop-shaped canopy of green, white, and pink variegated leaves. Plant in full sun to light shade in consistently moist, fertile soil where it can show off its cascading foliage. Prune the head in late winter to keep it dense and to renew the most colorful new growth. Keep the soil evenly moist, as willows demand regular water and tolerate wet feet. Remove any suckers that sprout below the graft to preserve the tree form.

plant_note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry-to-average sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; triplet lily is dormant and completely dry in summer — do not water during summer dormancy or the corms will rot.
directions: Plant corms in fall in very well-drained, dry-to-average, sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade — triplet lily is a spectacular western native bulb of California and Oregon grasslands and open woodlands, producing umbels of 15–30 funnel-shaped blue-violet to purple flowers (2 inches long) on 18–24 inch stems in late spring to early summer. The flowers are among the bluest of all native American wildflowers and provide important nectar for native bumblebees and long-tongued bees. The plant is entirely summer-dormant — it emerges in early spring, blooms, and then disappears as summer approaches, requiring dry summer conditions for healthy corm development (do not irrigate in summer). Excellent for naturalizing in dry, warm-summer climates in well-drained rocky or sandy soils — it multiplies steadily by corm offsets in appropriate conditions. An outstanding native bulb for Mediterranean-climate and dry western gardens.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall 6-8 inches deep before ground freezes.
directions: Plant Triumph tulip bulbs in fall about six to eight inches deep and four to six inches apart in well-drained soil. This mid-season class offers sturdy stems and classic egg-shaped blooms in a vast color range, excellent for beds and cutting. Provide full sun and allow the foliage to yellow naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb. In warm climates pre-chill bulbs for ten to fourteen weeks before planting, or treat them as annuals.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–11 it acts as a short-lived perennial and blooms from spring through hard frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm starting mix and transplant after frost danger has passed; tropical milkweed establishes quickly and blooms in its first season unlike most native milkweeds. It is an important monarch host plant and nectar source throughout the Gulf Coast growing season, but in zone 9–11 where it does not freeze back, cut plants to 6 in in fall and winter to disrupt the overwinter buildup of Ophryocystis elektroscirrha (OE), a protozoan parasite that accumulates on non-dormant milkweed. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming, or allow seedpods to dry and open for easy self-sowing. The red-and-yellow bicolor flowers distinguish it from native milkweeds at a glance.

plant_note: Direct sow on soil surface (light aids germination) after last frost, or start indoors 6 weeks earlier; in zone 9–11 it self-sows freely and behaves as a naturalized perennial.
directions: Scatter seed lightly on the soil surface after last frost in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; this Gulf Coast native thrives in heat and drought where cultivated salvias fail, and it self-sows so freely in zone 9 that it naturalizes into a permanent colony. The brilliant red flower spikes bloom from spring through first frost and are irresistible to ruby-throated hummingbirds; white-flowering varieties attract additional butterfly species. Deadhead lightly to prolong bloom, but allow some seed to set for naturalization. It tolerates poor, sandy soil and partial shade better than many salvias, making it ideal for difficult spots in a Gulf Coast landscape.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring; never let dry out.
directions: Plant trumpet lily bulbs in fall or early spring about eight inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil. The tall stems carry many large, powerfully fragrant trumpet-shaped flowers in midsummer and often need staking. Give them full sun with cool, shaded roots and never allow the bulbs to dry out completely. Leave stems standing until they yellow so the bulbs can store energy for the following year.

plant_note: Plant container vines near a very sturdy support structure in spring; trumpet vine is vigorous and heavy — it will pull down lightweight trellises. Control root suckers by mowing or digging promptly, as the vine can spread aggressively.
