directions: Plant near a very sturdy fence, masonry wall, pergola, or large tree in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil — trumpet vine is extraordinarily adaptable and drought tolerant; the large orange-red trumpet flowers appear from June through September and are among the most important native nectar sources for ruby-throated hummingbirds in the eastern US. The vine climbs by aerial roots and is very vigorous — growing 20–30 feet and sending up root suckers widely around the base. Control by mowing suckers, cutting back hard annually, and avoiding planting near the foundation of structures where the roots can damage masonry. The sap causes skin irritation in many people. Despite its aggressive character, trumpet vine is exceptional in rural settings where it can be allowed to cover a fence, outbuilding, or dead tree. The contact dermatitis risk from sap is real; wear gloves when pruning.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall; this cool-season grass is best divided after it finishes its main growth flush in late spring.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; unlike most ornamental grasses that demand full sun, tufted hair grass performs beautifully in partial shade and is one of the best cool-season grasses for lightly shaded woodland garden borders. The fine-textured, arching clumps of deep-green foliage are attractive through the growing season, and the airy, golden-bronze flower panicles that appear in midsummer shimmer and move in the slightest breeze. Cut the clump back to 4–6 inches in late winter before new growth begins. This grass thrives in cool, moist conditions and may go partially summer-dormant in hot, humid climates.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall when soil temperatures drop below 55°F, about 4–8 weeks before hard frost.
directions: Plant bulbs pointed end up at a depth of 3 times their height, typically 6–8 in deep, in well-drained soil — standing water will cause rot. In warmer zones (7–8), pre-chill bulbs for 6–8 weeks in the refrigerator before planting to simulate the cold period they need. Most hybrid tulips perform best in year 1; replanting fresh bulbs annually is common practice in mild climates. Allow leaves to die back naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb.

plant_note: Plant in spring in deep, moist, well-drained soil with room to grow large.
directions: The tulip poplar is a fast-growing native shade tree that reaches towering heights, with distinctive four-lobed leaves and cup-shaped, greenish-yellow tulip-like flowers in late spring. Give it a large, open site in full sun with deep, moist, well-drained soil, as its extensive root system needs room. Water regularly for the first few years to establish; mature trees are fairly self-sufficient. Its rapid growth makes it excellent for quick shade, though the soft wood benefits from a sheltered location. Foliage turns clear golden-yellow in autumn.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; an extremely tough Texas native that thrives in heat, part shade, and dry soils where few flowering shrubs will grow.
directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in partial to full sun; Turk's cap is one of the most shade-tolerant flowering natives for the Gulf South and performs well under the canopy of live oaks. The distinctive red flowers never fully open, forming a twisted turban shape, and are a top hummingbird nectar source from summer through fall. Small red fruits that follow are edible and mildly sweet — birds devour them quickly, and new plants sprout readily from seeds they drop. Prune back to 12–18 in in late winter; in zone 7–8 the top dies back but the roots are reliably hardy to about 10°F.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after last frost in any well-drained soil; this is one of the few true shade-tolerant flowering shrubs that performs in deep shade while also thriving in full sun. Dies to the ground in hard frost zones but re-sprouts vigorously.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to deep shade; Turk's cap is one of the South's most versatile and valuable flowering shrubs, producing bright red nodding flowers with tightly spiraled petals that never fully open (resembling a Turkish fez) from summer through frost — and in zone 9–10, flowering nearly year round. It is outstanding in dry shade under large trees where little else will bloom, and is equally at home in full blazing sun. Ruby-throated and rufous hummingbirds are its primary pollinators and mob the flowers from summer through migration. In zone 8 it dies back to the roots in hard freezes but re-sprouts reliably in spring and reaches full size by July. Do not overwater; it tolerates considerable drought once established.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in early spring at 4–6 inch depth, in groups of 3–5 for best effect; handles more shade than most lilies and prefers consistently moist soil.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to light sun; Turk's cap lily is the tallest native eastern lily, and in optimal conditions can reach 8 feet with dozens of richly spotted orange reflexed (turkscap-shaped) flowers per stem in July–August. Unlike cultivated Asiatic or Oriental lilies, it grows naturally in wet meadows and forest edges and requires consistently moist soil throughout the growing season. It tolerates more shade than most lilies and is valuable in the lightly shaded, moist native garden border. Ruby-throated hummingbirds are primary pollinators. The foliage and bulbs may be toxic to cats; keep away from where cats roam.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in a sunny, well-drained spot.
directions: Tuscarora is a vigorous crape myrtle that bears long-lasting panicles of hot coral-pink flowers from midsummer into fall. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil to maximize bloom and reduce the chance of powdery mildew. Its smooth, mottled cinnamon bark and orange-red fall color extend the interest well past the flowering season. Prune only lightly in late winter to preserve its graceful vase-shaped structure rather than cutting it back hard. Established plants tolerate heat and drought, needing extra water mainly during long dry stretches.

plant_note: Surface-sow; seed needs light to germinate. Often blooms the second year.
directions: Press seed onto the soil surface without covering, since light is needed for germination. Also called Carpathian bellflower, this compact perennial forms neat mounds covered in violet-blue, upward-facing bells. Grow it in full sun to part shade and cool, well-drained soil, ideal for rock gardens, edgings, and containers. Shear after the first bloom flush to tidy the plant and encourage a second round of flowers.

plant_note: Start indoors well before last frost; slow to germinate.
directions: Tweedia, or blue milkweed, offers rare true-blue, aquamarine star-shaped flowers with lavender buds and older blooms adding extra flair. Start the slow-germinating seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost in warm soil, and transplant out after frost danger passes. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where the sweetly scented flowers draw pollinators. Unlike common milkweeds it stays compact, making it well suited to containers and cut-flower use. Handle the stems with care, as the milky sap can irritate skin.

plant_note: Plant divisions or purchased plants in early spring in humus-rich woodland soil; purchase only from nursery-propagated sources as this plant is slow to propagate and protected in many states.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline woodland soil in full to partial shade; this rare, choice native woodland plant is named for Thomas Jefferson and named for its unique paired leaf halves that look like butterfly wings emerging from a single petiole. The pure white flowers appear briefly in early spring before the leaves expand, lasting only a few days before petals fall, and should be watched closely to catch them at their peak. The plant produces remarkable urn-shaped seed capsules with a hinged lid that opens to release seeds — one of the most distinctive seed vessels in North American flora. Extremely slow from seed, requiring 3–5 years to bloom; purchase propagated divisions from reputable nurseries.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; umbrella magnolia grows naturally as an understory tree in moist ravines and shaded slopes and tolerates deep shade better than most flowering trees.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; umbrella magnolia is a distinctive native understory tree of the Appalachians and Southeast named for the pattern of its enormous leaves — 12–24 inches long — arranged in a dramatic whorl around the branch tips like the spokes of an umbrella, creating a striking bold-textured tropical effect in the shaded garden. The large creamy-white flowers (6–10 inches) in late spring have an unusual, slightly unpleasant fragrance but are still attractive from a distance. Red cone-like fruit clusters in late summer and fall are ornamental and eaten by songbirds. It grows naturally as a subcanopy tree in moist, acidic ravines and tolerates shade better than most Magnolia species, making it one of the few flowering trees that performs well in partial to light shade. The extraordinarily large leaves and exotic texture make it an excellent specimen in shaded woodland gardens.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost when soil has warmed.
directions: Valley Porcupine is a spiky, pink-and-white cactus-type dahlia that is both an excellent cut flower and a reliable tuber producer, making it a great choice for beginners. Plant tubers horizontally about 4 to 6 inches deep after the soil warms in spring, with the eye facing up. Site in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and stake tall plants to support the heavy blooms. Pinch the growing tip when plants are about a foot tall to encourage bushier growth and more flowers. In cold zones, lift and store the tubers indoors over winter after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost when soil is warm.
directions: Valley Rust Bucket is a smaller red-orange dahlia whose warm autumn tones blend beautifully with bronze and copper companions in the cutting garden. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil once frost danger has passed. Stake the plants and pinch the central shoot early to promote branching and a fuller flush of blooms. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep the plant producing through the season. Lift the tubers before hard freezes in colder regions and store them in a cool, dry place for replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Vanilla Ice brings a creamy, light-yellow color to the garden on multi-branched plants about 5 feet tall bearing sweet 3-to-5-inch flowers. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings so the branching plants have room to develop their many blooms. Practically care-free, these sunflowers provide months of color and lovely cut flowers, and are striking in a moonlit garden. Water steadily while young and enjoy the pollinators and birds they attract.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; branching habit produces many blooms.
directions: Sow seed one inch deep directly in the garden after the last frost once soil warms. This heirloom bears velvety, multi-toned blooms in mahogany, bronze, and burnt orange on branching five- to seven-foot stems. Its many pollen-rich flowers make excellent cut material and feed bees throughout summer and fall. Give it full sun and moderate water, staking tall plants in exposed sites to prevent lodging.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or spring in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; vernal witch hazel tolerates moist soil and periodic flooding along streams better than most other witch hazels.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade — vernal witch hazel is the earliest-blooming native Hamamelis species, opening its strap-petaled flowers in January through March while the plant is still dormant, even in the coldest conditions of zone 4–5. The flowers come in shades from pale yellow to orange to deep brick-red (dark orange-red in the commonly grown form) and have a sweet, spicy fragrance. Unlike the common witch hazel (H. virginiana), which blooms in fall, H. vernalis blooms in late winter and is the best native species for early-season pollinator value — providing nectar for early queen bumblebees and native mining bees on warm winter days. It is native to the gravel bars and rocky streambanks of the Ozarks and tolerates moist to wet conditions. The fall foliage is brilliant orange-yellow.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained soil; excellent drought tolerance once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; speedwell is notably drought-tolerant and performs poorly in soggy or heavy clay conditions. The upright spikes of blue, pink, or white flowers bloom from late spring into summer and are excellent for cutting. Deadhead by removing spent spikes back to a lateral bud or leaf to extend the flowering period and prevent excessive self-seeding. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor and an attractive mounding habit.

plant_note: Plant divisions or slips 18–24 inches apart in rows along the contour of a slope in full sun; vetiver is sterile and does not self-sow, making it non-invasive in spite of its vigorous growth. Established plants tolerate drought, flooding, and extreme heat.
directions: Plant slips or divisions along contour lines on slopes in full sun in any well-drained soil; vetiver is the world standard for low-cost erosion control on steep slopes — a tropical grass from South Asia with an extraordinary root system that penetrates 6–10 feet straight down (not laterally) and holds soil with remarkable efficiency against both water and wind erosion. The World Bank promotes vetiver hedgerow systems in developing countries as the most effective and affordable slope stabilization technology available. The aromatic roots are distilled to produce vetiver essential oil, a base note used in hundreds of fine perfumes. Unlike most grasses used for erosion control, vetiver is sterile and non-invasive — it produces no viable seed and spreads only by division. In zone 8–11 it is an outstanding low-maintenance erosion control plant for steep slopes, stream banks, and road cuts.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the following spring.
directions: Victoria Blue is a darling, compact forget-me-not with petite, star-shaped blue flowers perfect for the front of a shade garden or alongside spring bulbs. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, covering it lightly since darkness aids germination. As a biennial or short-lived perennial it often blooms the spring after sowing and then self-sows to return. Grow in part shade in moist, rich soil where it heralds spring with its cheerful color. Keep the soil evenly moist and let plants naturalize for a lasting display.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the following spring.
directions: Victoria Pink is a compact forget-me-not with petite, star-shaped pink flowers that attract pollinators and pair beautifully with spring bulbs. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, covering it lightly since darkness helps germination. As a biennial or short-lived perennial it typically blooms the spring after sowing and self-sows to return year after year. Grow in part shade in moist, rich soil, keeping it evenly watered. Let plants naturalize at the front of shady beds for a soft, spreading carpet of color.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant several weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild freezes.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a cool, moist starting mix; darkness during germination is beneficial. Violas are more petite and heat-tolerant than pansies, often self-sowing prolifically in the garden. Deadhead to extend flowering, or allow seed to set for naturalized self-seeding the following year. They shine in early spring containers and border edges.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost in full sun.
directions: Violet Queen is a clary-type annual salvia grown for its showy, long-lasting violet bracts rather than its tiny true flowers, with aromatic foliage and blooms. Direct sow around the last frost in full sun and average, well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. It blooms non-stop through summer; simply remove spent spikes to encourage continual color. Drought-tolerant once established, it mixes well with bright annuals in beds and borders. The colorful bracts also dry nicely and draw hummingbirds, butterflies, and other pollinators.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a shaded, moist site; or plant divisions/transplants in early spring.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, shaded to partly shaded woodland soil; Virginia bluebells are a spring ephemeral that goes dormant by early summer, disappearing completely underground. The clusters of pink buds open to sky-blue, nodding tubular flowers in early to mid-spring and are among the most beautiful of native wildflowers. Mark the location of clumps so they are not accidentally disturbed; interplant with hostas, ferns, or other perennials that will fill the gap when bluebells go dormant. Divide crowded clumps or transplant in early fall when dormant.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), wet to moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Virginia chain fern tolerates deeper standing water than most ferns and can colonize the margins of swamps, bogs, and wet ditches.
directions: Plant in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), wet to moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Virginia chain fern is a robust, twice-pinnate native fern of Atlantic coastal plain bogs, swamps, and wet pine flatwoods — larger and more dramatic than the similar netted chain fern, with bipinnate fronds reaching 2–5 feet in saturated acidic soil. The sori are arranged in the chain-like pattern that names the genus, running in long lines parallel to the midrib. It tolerates standing water around the root zone and is one of the best native ferns for establishing in swampy, waterlogged, acidic conditions where few other plants thrive. Spreads steadily by rhizome to form bold colonies in appropriate wet, acid conditions. An important structural plant in wetland restoration and naturalistic bog gardens in the Southeast and coastal plain from New England to Florida.

plant_note: Plant container vines in spring near a structure, tree, or fence; the vine clings by adhesive suction-cup pads that do not penetrate surfaces or damage mortar. Do not confuse with poison ivy (three leaflets); virginia creeper has five.
directions: Plant in virtually any soil — wet or dry, rich or poor, sandy or clay — in full sun to deep shade; Virginia creeper is one of the most versatile native vines, blanketing fences, walls, trees, and arbors in deep shade where few other plants grow, and producing a stunning curtain of brilliant crimson-scarlet-burgundy fall color that is among the most vivid of any plant in the North American flora. The small dark blue-black berries that follow in fall are an essential food for migrating thrushes, waxwings, bluebirds, and woodpeckers. It clings by adhesive pads (not invasive roots), making it appropriate on stone and brick. The sap and berries are toxic to humans and can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals; the similar five-leaflet compound leaf distinguishes it from three-leaflet poison ivy. Prune aggressively in late winter to control size.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; plants establish quickly and begin blooming the following spring. Direct seeding is possible but takes longer — divisions are the preferred propagation method.
directions: Plant in moist to average, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia spiderwort is a native wildflower of open woods and roadsides producing three-petaled flowers in shades of blue-violet to purple, pink, or white that open in the morning and close by early afternoon. The strap-like, grass-like foliage is bold and attractive in the spring garden. After flowering, plants go semi-dormant in midsummer heat and may look tatty — cut back hard to 4–6 inches to encourage a flush of fresh foliage. Plants self-seed prolifically and spread by clump division; thin as needed. The closed flowers and young shoot tips are edible in salads, and the plant has a long history of use by eastern woodland peoples.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall or transplant tiny corms 2–3 inches deep in autumn; plants require winter cold to break dormancy and will bloom the following spring. Once established, they self-seed to naturalize freely.
directions: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade beneath deciduous trees; Virginia spring beauty is a spring ephemeral wildflower that carpets the woodland floor with pink-striped white flowers in early spring before tree canopies close. The narrow, fleshy leaves and small corms are edible — the corms taste like a mild potato and were gathered by Indigenous peoples of eastern North America. Plants go fully dormant by late spring, leaving bare soil where they grew; interplant with ferns, hostas, or other summer-active groundcovers to fill the gap. Self-seeds freely once naturalized — scatter seed right after it ripens in late spring to establish new colonies.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; this shrub tolerates transplanting well and establishes quickly. Fall planting is excellent in zones 7–9 where winters are mild.
directions: Plant in moist, average to wet soil in full sun to full shade; Virginia sweetspire is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for difficult garden situations, tolerating wet soil, dry clay, deep shade, and drought once established. Fragrant white flower racemes in June are attractive to butterflies and bees, and the fall foliage turns spectacular shades of orange, red, scarlet, and purple — often the best fall color of any shade-tolerant shrub. The cultivar 'Henry's Garnet' is the most widely grown, with especially long flower racemes and outstanding crimson fall color. Plants spread by suckers to form thickets; remove suckers to maintain a single-stem form or allow them to colonize a slope or wet area as a groundcover.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring for late spring color.
directions: Direct sow viscaria thinly in early spring where plants are to bloom. The dainty rose, blue, and white five-petaled flowers cover slender stems and suit cottage gardens and cutting beds. It appreciates full sun and well-drained soil and blooms quickly from seed. Thin seedlings for good air movement and deadhead to prolong the flowering period.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a shaded, moist location.
directions: Vision In Pink is a shade-loving perennial astilbe that sends up soft pink, feathery flower plumes above mounds of deep green, finely cut foliage in summer. Plant in partial to full shade in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; it will not tolerate dry conditions. Divide clumps every few years in spring to maintain vigor and increase your planting. Cut back spent plumes or leave them for winter interest, and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. It is an excellent choice for woodland gardens, shaded borders, and near water features.

plant_note: Plant small container trees in winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to wet soil; water oak is fast growing for an oak (2–3 feet per year when young) and establishes readily from planting.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically wet soil in full sun; water oak is one of the most common and fast-growing native oaks of the coastal South, easily identified by its distinctively variable leaf shape — three-lobed duck-foot leaves, paddle-shaped leaves, and narrow elliptic leaves can all appear on the same tree. It grows naturally in bottomland forests, wet stream banks, and moist upland soils from New Jersey to Texas and is among the fastest-growing oaks in the South (2–3 feet annually). The very small, nearly round acorns produced in enormous quantities beginning at 10–15 years are critically important for wood ducks, mallards, wild turkeys, squirrels, and deer. Water oak is smaller than live oak (40–70 feet) and better suited to residential landscapes where space is limited. It typically lives 60–80 years.

plant_note: Plant corms after last frost in spring for summer bloom; in mild-winter climates (zones 9–10) plant in fall.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in fertile, well-drained soil; watsonia closely resembles gladiolus and thrives in similar conditions. The tall, sword-like foliage produces elegant spikes of tubular flowers in shades of pink, red, coral, white, and bicolor throughout summer. In frost-free climates corms multiply into large colonies and can become invasive — divide every few years to manage spread. In colder zones lift corms after frost kills the foliage, cure, and store in a cool, dry, frost-free location.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost due to slow growth; transplant after frost danger has fully passed.
directions: Sow dust-fine seed on the surface of moist, sterile starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed needs light to germinate. Start early as wax begonias are slow growers before transplant size. These self-cleaning plants rarely need deadheading, blooming continuously from spring through frost. Bronze-leaved cultivars tolerate more direct sun than green-leaved types.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; stake to control form.
directions: The Weeping Norway Spruce is a striking evergreen with dramatic cascading branches clothed in rich, dark green needles, ideal as a living sculpture or accent. Its ultimate size and shape depend on how it is staked, so train a central leader upright for a tall specimen or let it sprawl as a ground-hugging form. Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil and water regularly during establishment. It is hardy and cold-tolerant, needing little pruning beyond removing damaged wood. Give it space to let the weeping branches drape naturally.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist ground, away from pipes and foundations.
directions: The Weeping Willow is a graceful, fast-growing shade tree with long, sweeping branches that trail nearly to the ground and sway in the slightest breeze. Plant in full sun in moist to wet soil, ideally near a pond or stream where its thirsty roots have ample water. Keep it well away from septic lines, drains, and foundations, as the aggressive roots seek out moisture. Water deeply and regularly, especially in dry spells and during establishment. Prune in late winter to remove crossing or damaged branches and to raise the canopy as it matures.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil in full sun.
directions: The Weeping Yoshino is a flowering cherry with gracefully cascading branches that in spring become a fountain of delicate, blush-white blossoms before the leaves emerge. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and provide good air circulation to limit fungal issues. Water regularly during establishment and mulch the root zone, keeping mulch away from the trunk. Prune only lightly right after flowering to maintain the weeping shape, as cherries are prone to disease through large cuts. It makes a spectacular focal point in a lawn or beside a patio.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; fringetrees are dioecious — female plants produce ornamental black berries if a male is nearby, though both sexes bloom spectacularly.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; white fringetree is one of the most spectacular spring-flowering native plants in North America, producing masses of drooping, fringe-like, pure white flower panicles (each petal strap-shaped and narrow, like white fringe) that completely cover the plant in late spring, appearing briefly after the leaves emerge. The effect of a fully bloomed fringetree — a large, billowing cloud of pure white flowers — is extraordinary. Female plants produce dark blue-purple olive-like berries in fall that are important food for birds; the leaves turn clear yellow in fall. Remarkably adaptable — tolerating partial shade, moderate drought, clay soils, and a very wide hardiness range (zone 3–9). One of the most valuable and versatile native large shrubs or small trees for the American garden, yet widely underplanted. Slow to establish but very long-lived.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant in spring into well-drained soil.
directions: White gaura brings airy elegance with long, delicate stems and butterfly-like white blooms that dance in the breeze above the foliage. Start seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant into full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant once established, so avoid soggy ground and heavy feeding. Cut back spent flowering stems to encourage a fresh flush of bloom through summer and fall. Hardy in zones 5 to 9, it adds graceful height and motion to beds and borders.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; cool temperatures aid germination.
directions: White Lace produces pure-white, lacy flowers in stunning 3-to-5-inch clusters on strong stems and a compact, bushy habit that suits gardens large and small. Direct sow in early spring or fall, as the seed benefits from cool temperatures and darkness to germinate. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil. This long-lasting cut flower is striking massed or intermingled with colorful blooms. Deadhead to prolong flowering, and let some seed drop to encourage self-sowing.

plant_note: Plant acorns immediately upon collection (they cannot be stored dry) in fall in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; white oak develops a very deep taproot and should be planted small and allowed to establish — transplanting large trees is difficult.
directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container-grown small trees in spring in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; white oak is arguably the most ecologically important tree species in eastern North America, supporting more species of caterpillars (534 species of moths and butterflies) than any other native tree genus — a keystone species for the entire eastern food web. Individual trees live 300–600 years and develop into majestic, wide-spreading specimens with deeply furrowed ash-white bark and a broad, irregular crown. The sweet acorns (low tannin, suitable for direct consumption by wildlife and humans) ripen annually and provide critical food for deer, turkeys, wood ducks, blue jays, and dozens of other species. The spectacular crimson, orange, and burgundy fall color rivals the maples. Plant from acorn when possible; the taproot develops early and transplanting large specimens is extremely difficult. A tree for generational thinking.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; white prairie clover has a deep taproot and does not transplant well once established — direct seeding in fall or early spring produces the best results.
directions: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; white prairie clover is one of the most valuable native prairie legumes, distinctive for its unique cylindrical flower spikes — the tiny white flowers open progressively upward from the base in a narrow band that travels up the spike over 2–3 weeks, creating an unusual effect quite different from any other native wildflower. The flowers are exceptionally attractive to native bees including specialist Dalea bees (Hesperapis and Protandrena species) that collect pollen exclusively from Dalea plants. It is a nitrogen-fixing legume that enriches the soil and is drought-tolerant and long-lived once established. The deep taproot provides winter hardiness and drought resistance. An outstanding native plant for dry, sunny prairie and meadow gardens, and one of the best plants for specialist native bee support.

plant_note: Surface sow seed in spring in partial to full shade; plants establish readily and self-sow to naturalize. Not appropriate near livestock — the plant's toxins pass through cow's milk and historically caused 'milk sickness'.
directions: Surface sow seed or plant divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; white snakeroot fills a critical niche as a shade-tolerant late-season bloomer, producing clouds of white fluffy flowers from August through October when pollinators most need late-season forage in shaded areas. It naturalizes freely by self-seeding under trees where few other plants bloom in fall. Note that all parts of the plant are toxic to livestock and humans — the toxin tremetol accumulates in milk of cows that graze on it, historically causing 'milk sickness' in settlers. Do not plant near pastures or where children might graze on it; it is safe as an ornamental in a garden setting.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; biennial that may flower the second year.
directions: White Swan clary sage dazzles with luminous white bracts rising in graceful tiers along each stem for a clean, sculptural summer look. Direct sow in spring in full sun and well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. Though the true flowers are tiny, the papery bracts hold their color beautifully through heat. This drought-tolerant biennial often forms foliage the first year and blooms the second, then may self-sow. Cut the long stems for fresh or dried arrangements and enjoy the pollinators the plant attracts.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for best germination; transplant in spring.
directions: White Swan is a graceful white-flowered coneflower selected from the native purple echinacea, with blooms that attract butterflies and seed heads that feed birds. Cold-stratify the seed for a few weeks, then start indoors and transplant into full sun and well-drained soil. This hardy perennial is drought-tolerant once established and needs little care. Leave the cone-like seed heads standing for winter interest and to support birds. Divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous, and enjoy the pollinator activity all summer.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early fall, 2–3 inches deep, with the growing tip pointing up; handle carefully as divisions are slow to recover.
directions: Plant in deep, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil beneath a deciduous canopy where leaf litter accumulates naturally; trilliums are adapted to the nutrient cycling of the eastern forest floor and resent disturbance once established. The plant emerges, blooms, and sets seed all within about six weeks in spring before dying back completely to the ground — mark the location carefully so the dormant rhizome is not accidentally disturbed. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants, as wild-collected trilliums are protected in many states and transplant very poorly. Division for propagation should be attempted only on large, multi-year clumps, as the plant needs 7–10 years from a small piece to reach flowering size.

plant_note: Plant divisions in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade; this plant requires reliable moisture throughout the growing season and will not tolerate drought.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; white turtlehead grows naturally in stream banks, wet meadows, and swamp margins across eastern North America. The inflated, snapdragon-like white (sometimes flushed pink) flowers appear in late summer to fall, providing late-season nectar for bumblebees that are heavy enough to force their way inside the closed flower mouth. It is also the primary larval host plant for the Baltimore checkerspot butterfly, a declining species that depends specifically on this plant. The plant spreads slowly by rhizome. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage additional blooming.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or scarify and cold-stratify seed for 60 days before spring sowing; slow to establish from seed, blooming in 3–4 years.
directions: Direct sow in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white wild indigo grows more slowly than the more common blue Baptisia australis but is better adapted to the drier, hotter conditions of the southern Great Plains and Southeast. The white flower spikes appear in early summer, followed by inflated black seed pods that rattle in the wind and provide winter interest. Like all Baptisia species it develops a large, deep taproot that makes transplanting of established plants impossible; site it carefully and leave it undisturbed once established. It is a larval host plant for wild indigo duskywing and Zarucco duskywing butterflies. Fixes atmospheric nitrogen.

plant_note: Surface-sow and press in; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Press the fine seed onto the soil surface without burying it, as light aids germination. This native heirloom yarrow bears flat clusters of white flowers above soft, feathery, aromatic foliage. Extremely drought-tolerant once established, it thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Deadhead to prolong bloom and divide clumps every few years; the flowers are valued in medicinal and dye gardens and dry well for arrangements.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; plants spread steadily by rhizome and can naturalize in appropriate moist woodland settings. Do not confuse with purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria), an invasive wetland plant — this is a completely different, well-behaved native species.
directions: Direct sow in early spring or plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; whorled loosestrife is a charming native woodland wildflower of eastern North America, recognizable by its distinctive architecture — the lance-shaped leaves are arranged in precise whorls of 4 around the stem, and small five-petaled yellow flowers with a red center arise from the axil of each leaf in the whorl, creating a very regular, geometric flowering pattern in early summer. It is in no way related to the invasive purple loosestrife (Lythrum) — this is a well-behaved native Lysimachia that spreads slowly by rhizome in appropriate moist, acidic woodland conditions. It provides early-summer pollen and floral oils for specialist native bees in the family Macropis. A lovely, understated wildflower for the naturalized woodland garden.

plant_note: Plant rooted stem cuttings or divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil; division of established mats in fall is also successful.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, slightly moist, well-drained soil in partial to light shade; this native phlox carpets the eastern woodland floor in pale lavender-blue in mid-spring, flowering before the tree canopy closes overhead and providing early nectar for bumblebee queens and phlox moths. After bloom, the stems sprawl and root at the nodes to spread the colony gradually, forming a semi-evergreen groundcover. Shear lightly after flowering to encourage denser growth and possible rebloom in fall. Do not confuse with creeping phlox (P. subulata), which needs full sun — this species will fail in those conditions.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall and allow natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify seeds for 60 days in damp paper towels in the refrigerator before spring sowing.
directions: Sow seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; this prolific self-seeder quickly naturalizes to form colonies of heart-shaped leaves and violet-blue spring flowers. Both the flowers and young leaves are edible and nutritious — the flowers make beautiful garnishes and violet leaf tea has long been used traditionally. After the showy spring flowers, the plant produces inconspicuous cleistogamous flowers near the soil all summer that set abundant seed; do not confuse these seed capsules with aphid infestations. The plant spreads generously by seed and rhizome — ideal for a naturalized meadow or wild shade garden but potentially aggressive in small formal beds.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in a shaded bed, barely covering seed; cold stratification for 60 days improves germination. Division of established crowns in early spring is faster.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; this native of eastern woodland edges blooms in mid-spring with lavender-pink five-petaled flowers, then develops distinctive long-beaked seed capsules that fling seeds explosively when ripe. The deeply cut, hand-shaped foliage often turns attractive shades of red and orange in fall before dying back. The root has medicinal tannins and has been used historically as an astringent. Divide established clumps every 4–5 years in early spring to maintain vigor; transplants establish readily and begin blooming in their first or second year.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep and 4–6 inches apart in groups of 10 or more in fall; the bulbs need a cold period to bloom. Avoid planting in wet soils that stay saturated in summer when bulbs are dormant.
directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wild hyacinth is the smaller, more shade-tolerant of the eastern native camas species, growing naturally in moist prairie remnants, open woodland edges, and meadows from Ontario south to Texas. The pale blue-violet flowers on slender stems appear in mid-spring and are among the first native wildflowers to bloom, providing pollen for early native bees before most flowers have opened. Plant in large drifts (25+ bulbs) for the best visual effect. The foliage dies back completely by early summer, so mark plant locations and interplant with later-emerging perennials to fill the gap. The bulbs are edible — they were an important food source for Native American peoples — but require proper preparation.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade; one of the few native evergreen shrubs that performs in partial to full shade in the Southeast.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; wild olive is the native North American member of the same genus as the prized Asian fragrant sweet olive (O. fragrans), and though its white flowers in late spring are smaller and less flamboyant, they carry a delicate, sweet fragrance and are borne abundantly on large shrubs or small trees in woodland settings. It grows naturally in moist, shaded coastal plain forests and ravine forests of the Southeast, making it well-suited to garden use under tall trees where it provides refined, glossy-leaved evergreen structure year-round. The small, dark blue berry-like drupes ripen in fall and provide wildlife food. Slow growing but extremely long-lived; excellent as a foundation shrub or informal hedge in shade.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; wild petunia self-sows prolifically in appropriate conditions and can naturalize in dry, open areas — a welcome attribute in a native meadow but worth noting in a more formal setting.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained, dry to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — wild petunia (Ruellia humilis) is a native prairie perennial that produces a continuous succession of lavender to pale violet, 1.5-inch funnel-shaped flowers remarkably similar in appearance to garden petunias through summer and into fall, making it a genuinely ornamental native for dry, sunny or partly shaded settings. Individual flowers last only a day, but new ones open continuously over many weeks on the low, hairy, sessile-leaved plants. It grows naturally in dry, open prairies, rocky glades, and open woodland edges. Unlike tropical Ruellia (Mexican petunia, R. simplex), native wild petunia is not invasive — it self-sows modestly and is well-behaved in most gardens. Excellent for rock gardens, dry slopes, and native plant gardens. An important nectar source for bumblebees and long-tongued native bees.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in sandy, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; this plant grows naturally in dry, sandy upland soils and pine woods — do not plant in heavy clay or rich, moist soils.
directions: Direct sow in dry, sandy, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; wild petunia is a delicate native wildflower of the dry sandy pine flatwoods and upland forests of the Southeast, producing lavender-blue tubular flowers resembling petunias (though not related) throughout the growing season from spring through fall. It grows naturally in infertile, dry conditions where it avoids competition from more aggressive plants. The trumpet-shaped flowers are visited by hummingbirds, sphinx moths, and small native bees. Plant in rock gardens, in sandy soils under pines, or at dry woodland margins; it will not tolerate wet or clay soils. It self-sows reliably and naturalizes into small colonies in ideal conditions. In zone 9–10 it may behave as an evergreen short-lived perennial.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify indoors and spring-sow after last frost.
directions: Sow in lean to average, well-drained soil in full sun; wild quinine is a sturdy native prairie plant that tolerates heat, drought, and clay once established. The flat-topped clusters of small white button flowers appear in midsummer and persist for many weeks, drawing in a wide diversity of native bees, wasps, and beetles. The button-like, persistent seed heads provide architectural interest into fall and winter. A reliable, long-lived plant that requires virtually no maintenance once established in a sunny meadow or native plant garden.

plant_note: Scarify seeds by nicking or soaking in hot water for 24 hours before sowing after last frost; can also be started indoors 4 weeks earlier for transplanting.
directions: Scarify and direct sow in fertile to average, moist to well-drained soil in full sun; wild senna is a bold-textured native perennial with large, compound leaves and bright yellow flowers in midsummer that are structurally perfect for specialist bumblebee species that sonicate (buzz-pollinate) the flowers to release pollen. It is also the larval host plant for cloudless sulphur and sleepy orange butterflies. The persistent seed pods provide food for birds through winter. Cut back to the ground in late fall or early spring; the plant regrows vigorously from the crown each year. Develops a substantial taproot and is difficult to transplant once established; select your site carefully.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in spring or fall; this native stonecrop takes easily from even small stem sections laid on moist soil.
directions: Plant cuttings or plugs in rocky, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; this is one of the very few native sedums that thrives in shade, growing naturally on shaded stream banks and moist cliff faces where other plants struggle. The flat, succulent rosettes of three-part leaves form a dense groundcover, and the starry white flowers in spring attract native bees. Unlike most sedums, it prefers consistent moisture and will not thrive in hot, dry, sunny locations. Propagate by division or stem cuttings any time during the growing season; pieces root readily when placed in contact with moist soil.

plant_note: Plant small container trees (1–2 gallon) in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil; willow oak transplants better than most oaks and establishes quickly. Plant acorns immediately upon collecting in fall.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; willow oak is one of the most graceful native oaks, producing narrow, willow-like leaves unlike any other oak, giving the tree an unusually fine-textured, feathery appearance in the landscape. It grows naturally in bottomland forests and moist uplands across the coastal South and is one of the most widely planted native oaks as a street tree for its tolerance of urban conditions, moist soils, and relatively fast growth rate (1–2 feet per year). The tiny acorns produced in enormous abundance beginning at 10–15 years provide critical fall and winter food for wood ducks, mallards, wild turkeys, squirrels, and songbirds. Fall color is not as vivid as red oak but pleasant golden-yellow. Very long-lived and ultimately a large landscape tree.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot or a container that can move indoors in cold zones.
directions: Wilma Goldcrest is a lemon cypress with glowing golden-green foliage that releases a fresh citrus scent when brushed, and a narrow, upright form perfect for tight spaces. Grow it in full sun to keep the foliage brightest, in fast-draining soil that never stays waterlogged. In containers it stays around 3 to 5 feet, while in the ground in mild climates it can reach 10 feet. In zones colder than 7, grow it in a pot and bring it indoors near a bright window over winter. Water when the top inch of soil dries, and avoid soggy roots.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring in moist, fertile soil in partial shade; plants establish readily and spread by rhizome and self-seeding, naturalizing aggressively once happy. Do not plant in small gardens without containment.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wingstem is a bold, tall native wildflower of moist woodland edges and stream banks with distinctive narrow wings running down the length of the stem (giving it the common name), topped with loose clusters of raggedy yellow flowers in August through October that are outstanding late-season nectar sources for bees, wasps, and butterflies. The plant is one of the most shade-tolerant of the tall native yellow-flowered composites, blooming reliably in significant shade where few similar plants perform. It spreads aggressively by rhizome and should be given a naturalized area of its own or managed by division. The persistent seed heads are eaten by goldfinches and other seed-eating birds through fall and winter.

plant_note: Plant the potted or bare-root climber in spring after hard frosts.
directions: Winner's Circle is a vigorous climbing rose with bright, cherry-red double blooms and a strong, upright habit that flowers spring through fall. Plant the potted or bare-root rose in spring in full sun and rich, well-drained soil near a sturdy trellis, arbor, or fence to support the canes. Tie in the long canes as they grow and train them horizontally to encourage more blooms. Water at the base to keep foliage dry, mulch to conserve moisture, and prune in late winter to shape and remove old wood. Hardy in zones 4 to 10, it rewards care with repeat flushes of color.

plant_note: Plant tubers in fall as soon as available — they dry out quickly; soak in water for 24 hours before planting.
directions: Soak the small, hard tubers in water for 24 hours before planting to rehydrate them; plant 2 in deep under deciduous trees or shrubs as soon as received in late summer or early fall. Bright yellow cup-shaped flowers surrounded by a green ruff appear in late winter, often alongside snowdrops, before any leaves emerge on overhead trees. Winter aconite self-seeds freely and slowly forms large, cheerful carpets if left undisturbed. Best planted where they can stay permanently; they strongly dislike being moved.

plant_note: Plant at least one male and three female shrubs for berry production; one male can pollinate up to five females. Match the male cultivar's bloom time to the female's — choose male cultivar recommendations from the nursery that match your female selection.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; winterberry is a spectacular native deciduous holly that produces brilliant red (or yellow or orange in some cultivars) berries that encrust every branch after leaf drop in fall, providing dazzling winter interest from October through February when birds finally strip them. It grows naturally in swamps, stream banks, and wet woodland margins and performs best with consistent moisture. This species is dioecious — you must plant both male and female plants, matching cultivars that bloom at the same time, to get berries. Named female cultivars like 'Winter Red', 'Sparkleberry', and 'Red Sprite' (compact) are widely available; match them with the appropriate male. Mulch to maintain soil moisture and acidity.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix without covering; they need light to germinate at 70–75°F. Torenia is one of the best flowering annuals for shaded and part-shaded spots in hot, humid climates where impatiens are lost to downy mildew. The bicolored, snapdragon-like flowers in purple, pink, white, or yellow bloom profusely without deadheading throughout summer. Keep soil consistently moist, as plants wilt quickly when dry but recover readily; fertilize monthly with a balanced liquid feed.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in early spring or late fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; the plant transplants well from container stock but is slow to establish from seed. The species blooms October–December; vernal witch hazel (H. vernalis) blooms in late winter.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; American witch hazel is the latest-blooming native shrub, producing clusters of fragrant, spidery yellow flowers on bare branches from October through December — often while the leaves are still falling around the flowers. It is a stunning sight in the November woodland garden when nothing else is in bloom. Growing naturally as a multi-stemmed understory tree in moist woodland slopes and ravines across eastern North America, it tolerates full shade but blooms more heavily with some dappled light. The bark and leaves are the botanical source of witch hazel astringent. Allow it to grow naturally as a large multi-stemmed specimen; it resents heavy pruning. The seed pods pop open explosively to disperse seeds.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes in fall in humus-rich shade.
directions: Plant wood anemone rhizomes in fall just below the surface in cool, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade. The starry white spring flowers carpet the ground before deciduous trees leaf out, then the plant goes dormant by summer. It spreads slowly by creeping rhizomes to form a lovely ephemeral groundcover. Keep the soil moist during growth and mark the location, as the foliage disappears entirely in summer.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in dry to average, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; woodland sunflower spreads by rhizome to form large colonies and is best used where aggressive naturalization is welcome — it is exceptionally effective at naturalizing along dry woodland edges and under oak canopies.
directions: Sow seed in fall or divide in spring in dry to average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade — woodland sunflower is one of the very few perennial sunflowers genuinely adapted to partial shade, producing 2–3 inch yellow daisy flowers in mid-to-late summer on upright stems 3–6 feet tall in conditions where most sunflowers refuse to bloom. It grows naturally in dry, open woodlands, woodland edges, and clearings throughout the eastern US and is especially common along the edges of oak woodland, thriving in the dry shade beneath the oak canopy that challenges most sun-loving perennials. It spreads vigorously by rhizome — use this to advantage in naturalizing dry, shaded areas with low-maintenance native cover and late-season pollinator value. The seeds are eaten by goldfinches and other seed-eating birds. An outstanding native for filling the challenging dry, part-shade niche.

plant_note: Plant container vines or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; woolly Dutchman's pipe is the southeastern-native species of Aristolochia, hardier and better adapted to Gulf Coast conditions than A. macrophylla.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun with a sturdy support structure; woolly Dutchman's pipe is the southeastern US native species of Aristolochia, distinguished from A. macrophylla by its dense woolly coating of soft hairs on the leaves and stems — a beautiful textural detail. Like all native Aristolochia species, it is the exclusive larval host plant for the pipevine swallowtail butterfly (Battus philenor), one of the most spectacular black-and-iridescent-blue native butterflies in eastern North America. A single established vine supports dozens of caterpillars each season. The unusual curved, yellowish-green to brown pipe-shaped flowers are insect-trapping devices that temporarily trap small flies for pollination. Twining stems reach 10–20 feet and provide rapid screening in partial shade. Highly recommended for butterfly gardens in zones 5–9, particularly in the Southeast where this species is native.

plant_note: Surface sow seed 4–6 weeks before last frost without covering; seeds need light to germinate. Can also divide established clumps in spring. Self-sows reliably and spreads by rhizome.
directions: Surface sow seed in moist, average to rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; wrinkleleaf goldenrod is notable among goldenrods for its significant shade tolerance and preference for moist to wet conditions, growing naturally in wet meadows, stream margins, and lightly shaded bottomlands. The arching stems of yellow flowers in September and October provide critical late-season nectar for migrating monarchs, native bees, and late-emerging moths in shaded settings where most other nectar plants don't grow. The cultivar 'Fireworks' is named for its particularly graceful, arching flower sprays and is the most widely available selection. Spreads by rhizome and self-seed into naturalizing colonies; divide every 3 years in spring to maintain vigor. An excellent choice for moist, partly shaded rain gardens and woodland borders.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Wyns New Pastel is a tall, vigorous dahlia bearing big pink-and-yellow blended blooms all season on a well-branched, hedge-like plant. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after the danger of frost has passed. Because of its height, provide sturdy staking early and pinch the central shoot to encourage branching. Cut flowers freely and deadhead to keep new buds forming through summer and fall. In cold climates, dig and store the tubers over winter, replanting them the following spring.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in full sun; blooms quickly from seed.
directions: Xanthos is a breakthrough soft-yellow cosmos, an award winner that is early to flower on well-branched, shorter plants ideal for containers and the front of the border. Direct sow after the last frost in full sun and average, well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. It thrives in lean soil, so avoid overfeeding, which produces foliage at the expense of blooms. Deadhead or cut flowers often to keep the plant producing through summer. Drought-tolerant once established, it draws bees, butterflies, and other pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; prefers to be direct sown as transplants disturb its taproot.
directions: Direct sow after last frost in full sun with lean, well-drained soil; xeranthemum is one of the easiest everlastings to grow and tolerates poor, dry conditions well. The papery, daisy-like flowers in shades of lavender, pink, purple, and white are naturally self-preserving and air-dry on the stem. Harvest stems for drying when flowers are about two-thirds open; hang upside down in a warm, airy location for 2–3 weeks. Successive sowings 2–3 weeks apart can extend the harvest season into early fall.

plant_note: Plant fresh (unsoaked) nuts in fall in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, well-drained soil in partial shade; unlike most trees, yellow buckeye performs best in partial shade, especially afternoon shade in zone 7–8. The fresh nuts lose viability rapidly — plant within weeks of collection.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, deep, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade — yellow buckeye is unusual among trees in that it actually performs better in partial shade than full sun, making it one of the best large native trees for the shaded garden. Native to rich mountain forests of the southern Appalachians, it produces beautiful upright clusters of yellow flowers in spring that attract hummingbirds and long-tongued native bees, and large smooth seed pods containing 1–3 mahogany-brown nuts with pale patches — striking ornamental fall fruit. The palmate compound leaves of 5 leaflets are among the first to emerge in spring and first to drop in fall (often by August in drought). It tolerates understory conditions and is frequently found growing as a subcanopy tree beneath tulip poplar and basswood. The nuts are toxic to humans but consumed by squirrels. Often considered the most beautiful of the North American buckeyes.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seed in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing; once established the plant self-sows prolifically.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained, gritty soil in partial shade; yellow corydalis is a European native that has naturalized in North American gardens and thrives in wall crevices, shaded rock gardens, and the base of stone steps where drainage is perfect. Once established it self-sows freely and colonizes shaded hard-to-plant areas, blooming with small tubular yellow flowers nearly continuously from spring through fall in cool climates. The finely divided, gray-green foliage is attractive even out of bloom. Remove unwanted seedlings while small; established plants have taproots that resist transplanting.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container vines in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; yellow passionflower is the most shade-tolerant of the native Passiflora species and naturalizes readily in shaded woodland edges and fence lines in zone 6–9.
directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; yellow passionflower is the most shade-tolerant native Passiflora species and is far less aggressive than maypop (P. incarnata), making it more suitable for smaller gardens and shaded areas. The flowers are smaller than other passionflowers but share the same intricate, layered structure — creamy yellow-green petals and a distinctive corona of filaments — and are visited by long-tongued native bees for nectar. Like all native Passiflora, yellow passionflower is a host plant for the larvae of gulf fritillary, variegated fritillary, and zebra longwing butterflies. The small dark purple fruits are eaten by birds. It grows naturally along shaded woodland edges, thickets, and roadsides throughout the southeastern US and is one of the best native vines for adding butterfly-host value to a shaded fence or trellis.

plant_note: Plant small corms (bulbs) 2–3 inches deep in fall; or sow fresh seed immediately after collection in late spring, as seed viability drops quickly. Corms are the practical starting method — bulbs begin blooming in 2–3 years vs. 4–7 from seed.
directions: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil beneath deciduous trees; yellow trout lily is among the most charming spring ephemerals of the eastern forest floor. The single mottled brown-and-green leaf (resembling a brook trout's back) appears in early spring, followed by a solitary nodding yellow lily flower on plants old enough to bloom. Young plants form non-blooming single-leaf colonies for 4–7 years before maturity. Plants go completely dormant by early June, leaving no trace; mark their location to avoid disturbance. The fleshy corms are edible and were eaten by Indigenous peoples, though harvesting from wild populations is unethical. Ideal naturalized en masse under deciduous trees to create a spectacular spring carpet.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this plant establishes quickly and spreads by rhizome to form a dense colony. Tolerates periodic flooding.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial to full shade; yellowroot is a low, colony-forming native shrub of Appalachian stream banks and moist woodland slopes, grown for its ferny, compound foliage that provides attractive, fine-textured groundcover in shade, its drooping panicles of small maroon-purple flowers in early spring, and its outstanding orange-gold fall color. The roots and inner bark are vividly yellow from the alkaloid berberine (also found in goldenseal), historically used as a natural yellow dye and for medicinal purposes. As a groundcover, it spreads vigorously by rhizome to form weed-suppressing mats under trees, on moist slopes, and along shaded stream banks. One of the best native low-maintenance groundcovers for moist shade.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and well-drained soil.
directions: The Yoshino cherry is the classic flowering cherry famed for its spring canopy of fragrant, blush-white blossoms that open before the leaves emerge. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil, giving it plenty of room for its broad, spreading crown. Water regularly while young and mulch the root zone, keeping the mulch off the trunk. Prune sparingly and only just after bloom, since cherries are susceptible to disease through large wounds. It provides light shade in summer and golden-bronze foliage in fall, making it a beloved landscape specimen.

plant_note: Plant in fall or spring in partial shade with acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: Yuletide is a sasanqua camellia that blooms in late fall and early winter, opening bright single red flowers with showy golden stamens against glossy evergreen foliage. Plant in partial shade in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil; sheltering it from harsh afternoon sun and drying winds protects the blooms. Keep the soil consistently moist but never soggy, and mulch to conserve moisture and maintain acidity. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the shrub. Its winter color makes it a standout for foundation plantings, hedges, and holiday-season interest.

plant_note: Sow outdoors around the last spring frost; tolerates light frost and can also be sown in late summer for fall bloom.
directions: Direct sow seeds about a quarter inch deep once the soil is workable in spring, or start indoors a few weeks before the last frost. This cultivar produces bronzy-orange petals that fade to soft pink over a maroon-backed, yellow-centered flower. Deadhead regularly to keep the edible blooms coming and prevent early fade in summer heat. Petals are edible and make a colorful addition to salads or as a garnish. Calendula self-sows readily and thrives in cool weather.

plant_note: Surface sow or plant divisions in spring; this is the only goldenrod species that thrives in shade and can be naturalized under trees where sun-loving goldenrods fail.
directions: Surface sow or plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; unique among the goldenrods, this species naturally grows in the understory of deciduous eastern forests and tolerates deep shade that would kill other goldenrods. The common name refers to the distinctly zigzagging (flexicaulis) stem. Yellow flower clusters bloom in late summer to fall, providing late-season nectar in shaded areas where other late bloomers are absent. Spreads moderately by rhizome to form colonies but is far less aggressive than sun-loving goldenrods. Divide clumps in spring every 3–4 years.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed; zinnias resent cold, wet ground.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost about a quarter inch deep, or start indoors a few weeks early for an earlier show. This scabiosa-flowered blend yields 2 to 2.5 inch double, semi-double, and occasional single blooms in soft pastel shades where no two flowers are alike. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching and more stems for cutting. Cut or deadhead frequently to keep the plants productive all season. Provide good air circulation to reduce powdery mildew.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil temperature is above 65°F; also easy to start indoors 4–6 weeks early.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep directly in the garden once frost risk is past and soil is warm. Pinch seedlings at 6 in tall to promote branching and a fuller plant with more stems for cutting. Cut flowers frequently to the next set of leaves to stimulate continuous bloom through summer. Avoid overhead watering to reduce powdery mildew; water at the base.

plant_note: Sow directly after the last frost; successive sowings every couple of weeks extend the bloom season.
directions: Direct sow about one inch deep after the last frost once soil has warmed. This branching, pollenless-style florist sunflower blooms roughly two months from sowing, bearing golden-yellow 4 to 6 inch flowers on tall, sturdy stems ideal for bouquets. Space plants closer for single-stem cut flowers or wider to encourage branching and more blooms. Its long vase life makes it a favorite for the backyard florist. Stake in windy sites to keep the tall stems upright.

plant_note: Start indoors about 6 weeks before the last frost, then transplant out once frost danger has passed.
directions: Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant after the weather warms, or direct sow in warm regions. Also called Monarch of the Veldt, this heat- and drought-tolerant South African annual produces 3 to 4 inch daisy-like flowers above silver-tinged foliage from midsummer to fall. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, and avoid overwatering as it prefers drier conditions. The blooms attract bees, birds, and butterflies. Flowers close at night and on cloudy days.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; abiu is cold-sensitive and requires a frost-free year-round climate.
directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; abiu is closely related to sapodilla and canistel and shares their preference for warm, humid conditions with regular moisture. Trees are self-fertile and heavy bearers once established; grafted selections begin fruiting in 2–4 years versus 7+ for seedlings. The smooth golden-yellow skin of ripe fruit conceals a translucent, gelatinous, caramel-flavored flesh — eat by cutting in half and scooping; avoid getting the sticky latex near the lips if the fruit is slightly underripe. Harvest when the skin has turned fully golden-yellow and the fruit yields gently to thumb pressure, typically in late summer through fall.

plant_note: Plant container-grown or grafted stock in spring after all frost danger; in zone 9 site against a south-facing wall for extra warmth.
directions: Acerola produces cherry-like fruits with one of the highest natural concentrations of vitamin C of any food plant — up to 65 times that of an orange. The shrubby tree is self-fertile and can bear multiple times per year in truly frost-free climates; it is very sensitive to cold below 30°F and root systems are damaged below 28°F. Plant in well-drained, slightly acid to neutral soil and fertilize lightly with a balanced fertilizer three times per year. Fruit ripens very quickly from green to red and is extremely perishable — harvest when fully red and process (freeze, juice) within 24–48 hours for maximum vitamin C retention.

plant_note: Plant in the warm, wet tropics; strictly frost-free.
directions: The African mango, or bush mango, is a large tropical tree grown for its mango-like fruit and especially its oil-rich seed kernel, called dika nut. It demands a hot, humid, frost-free climate with abundant moisture and deep, fertile soil. Plant in full sun with plenty of room, as mature trees become tall and broad. Trees are slow to bear from seed, so grafted stock fruits sooner; harvest the fallen fruit and dry the prized kernels.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter before bud break; almonds bloom very early and need a frost-free spring.
directions: Almonds bloom earlier than almost any other tree fruit and are severely damaged by late spring frosts, which is why commercial production is limited to Mediterranean-type climates. Most varieties require a cross-pollinator — plant at least two compatible cultivars (Non-Pareil with Carmel or Price, for example). Trees thrive in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soils and are drought-tolerant once established. Harvest when the hulls split open and the shells dry and harden, typically in late summer; spread nuts to dry for two weeks before storing.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in early spring in rich, moist, well-drained, acidic soil.
directions: American beech is monoecious and wind-pollinated, but nut production is highly variable and irregular — large mast crops occur every 2–5 years, driven by weather patterns; at least two trees (ideally a grove) dramatically improves mast yield in any given year. The triangular beechnuts are sweet and nutritious, high in fat and protein, and valued by both wildlife and people; they are best eaten raw or lightly roasted and are traditionally used for oil extraction. Beech is extremely sensitive to soil compaction and grade changes around the root zone — construction damage is the primary killer of mature trees in urban settings. Plant in deep, moist, well-drained, acidic soil away from construction zones and plan for very long-term production: significant nut crops typically begin 10+ years after planting.

plant_note: Plant fresh nuts or container-grown seedlings in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, deep soil in full sun; American chestnut requires two or more trees for cross-pollination to set nuts. Consider blight-resistant hybrid trees from the American Chestnut Foundation for greater long-term survival.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, deep soil in full sun — the American chestnut was once the dominant tree of the eastern forest, comprising 25% of all trees from New England to the Deep South and providing the primary mast crop sustaining deer, turkeys, bears, and passenger pigeons across millions of acres. The introduction of chestnut blight (Cryphonectria parasitica) from Asia in the early 1900s killed an estimated 4 billion trees within 50 years in the most catastrophic ecological disaster in American history. The root system of most trees survives and sends up shoots that grow to 10–20 feet before dying of blight — these sprouts can produce a small crop of nuts. The American Chestnut Foundation has developed blight-tolerant hybrid trees (about 94% American chestnut genetics) and restoration planting is actively underway. Planting American chestnut seedlings or blight-resistant hybrids is a meaningful conservation act; the trees grow fast, produce sweet nuts, and support 125 species of caterpillars.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container-grown shrubs in spring; plant at least two different clones (not two of the same named cultivar) for good pollination and maximum fruit set.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; American elderberry is one of the most productive native fruiting shrubs, bearing enormous flat-topped flower clusters in early summer that are irresistible to pollinators, followed by drooping clusters of small dark purple-black berries in late summer used for syrups, jellies, wine, and immune-support preparations. Plant at least two genetically distinct clones for better cross-pollination and heavier fruit set. The shrub spreads by root suckers to form large thickets; remove suckers promptly to control spread. All parts of the plant except fully ripe, cooked berries and flowers should be considered toxic when consumed raw. Cut all canes to 12 inches in late winter every 3–4 years to rejuvenate; older canes are pithy and produce poor crops.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container shrubs in early spring before leafout; two or more plants are needed for cross-pollination and nut production.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; this native thicket-forming shrub tolerates a wider range of conditions than European hazelnut and is resistant to Eastern filbert blight. Plant at least two different seedling-grown plants together, as this species is self-incompatible and requires cross-pollination for nut set. Nuts ripen in September–October, enclosed in leafy husks; harvest when the husks begin to loosen and dry nuts in a single layer before storing. The multi-stemmed habit can be managed by removing the oldest canes at the base every few years.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container-grown shrubs in spring; cross-pollination is required for nut production — plant at least two genetically distinct individuals within 20 feet. Nuts from stratified seed can be sown in fall for spring germination.
directions: Plant in well-drained to average moist soil in full sun to partial shade; American hazelnut is a native multi-stemmed shrub that produces small, sweet hazelnuts enclosed in fringed husks in late summer — identical in flavor to the European filbert but better adapted to eastern North American conditions. The catkins (male flowers) are showy in late winter before any leaves emerge, providing early nectar for bees. Leaves turn yellow-orange in fall. Squirrels are formidable competitors for the nuts — harvest when the husks begin to turn brown and bring indoors to cure.
