plant_note: Direct sow after soil is warm; thrives in heat.
directions: Black-eyed peas are a heat-tolerant cowpea grown for their nutritious seeds and their ability to fix nitrogen and improve soil. Direct-sow after the soil warms to 70F, planting an inch deep and thinning to 3-6 inches apart, since the plants dislike transplanting. They tolerate heat and drought well, so water moderately and avoid overly rich soil, which favors foliage over pods. Harvest young pods for fresh snap peas or leave them to dry on the plant for shelling and storage.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; cowpeas are more heat- and drought-tolerant than common beans.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in warm, well-drained soil; black-eyed peas tolerate poor, sandy soils and summer drought better than most vegetables. Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen, as they fix their own from the atmosphere. Harvest immature pods at 3–4 in for snap use, or allow pods to swell fully and turn pale tan for fresh shell peas; dry the remaining pods completely for storage as dried beans.

plant_note: Sow early spring and fall; germinates in cool soil, bolts in heat.
directions: Bloomsdale is a cold-hardy heirloom spinach with heavily crinkled, deep-green leaves and a sweet, mild flavor that stands up well in cool weather. Direct-sow in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked and again in late summer, since seed germinates poorly in warm soil. Thin plants to 4-6 inches apart and harvest outer leaves cut-and-come-again, or cut whole plants before hot weather triggers bolting. In mild zones a fall planting under cover can overwinter for an early spring harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable, or again in late summer.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep in cool soil in early spring or late summer, thinning to give each plant room. This dependable open-pollinated variety from the early 1800s has crinkled, savoyed leaves and can be harvested as tender baby greens in as little as 28 days. It performs well in the ground or in containers and tolerates part shade. Cut outer leaves as needed or harvest whole plants before warm weather triggers bolting.

plant_note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.
directions: Separate bulbs into cloves at planting and set each clove pointed end up, 2 inches deep, in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes. This Turban-type hardneck forms large, plump bulbs with violet-streaked wrappers and 8 to 10 easy-peel cloves. Mulch after planting to protect roots over winter, and cut off the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into the bulbs. Harvest when lower leaves brown, then cure in a dry, airy place before storing.

plant_note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed; a drought-tolerant heirloom flour corn.
directions: Sow seeds about 1 inch deep in blocks of at least four short rows rather than a single long row to ensure good wind pollination. This Hopi heirloom tolerates dry, hot conditions better than most corn, so avoid overwatering once established. Allow the ears to dry fully on the stalk before harvesting the deep-blue kernels for grinding into flour or cornmeal.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; an heirloom storage pumpkin with slate blue-gray skin.
directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in hills once the soil is thoroughly warm, thinning to the two strongest vines per hill. Give these sprawling plants plenty of room and a steady supply of water and compost to size up the deeply ribbed fruits. Cure the blue-gray pumpkins in a warm, dry spot after harvest for long winter storage and sweet, dense flesh.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; a compact bush type producing stringless green beans without support.
directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep directly in the garden once frost has passed, since beans resent transplanting. These compact bushes need no trellis and set a heavy, concentrated crop of straight, tender pods. Pick often while the pods are young and stringless to encourage continued production, and consider a second sowing for a fall harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep directly in warm soil, since beans dislike transplanting and rot in cold ground. This variety produces a very large concentrated crop of round, 6 inch pods on 16 to 18 inch bushy, disease-resistant plants. Because it crops all at once, sow in successions two weeks apart for a steady supply. Harvest pods while young and tender before the seeds swell, and pick frequently to encourage more pods.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep at the base of a sturdy trellis or pole in warm soil once frost danger has passed. Vigorous 5 to 8 foot vines are high-yielding and produce a bounty of 5 to 7 inch, white-seeded pods excellent fresh, canned, or frozen. Provide strong support before the vines begin to climb. Harvest pods while young and tender, picking every few days to keep the vines producing over a long season.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil, planting in blocks of several short rows rather than one long row to ensure good pollination. This sugar-enhanced hybrid produces 8-inch ears packed with 16 rows of juicy golden kernels that hold their sweetness well after harvest. Sturdy stalks carry a strong disease package for consistent yields. Feed with nitrogen and water deeply during tasseling and ear fill, and harvest when silks brown and kernels are plump and milky.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date in spring; for fall harvest, sow 6–8 weeks before first frost.
directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and thin to 6–12 in depending on whether you want baby or full-sized heads. Provide consistent moisture and feed lightly with nitrogen; drought stress and heat both trigger premature bolting. Harvest baby bok choy at 4–6 in tall by cutting at the soil line, or allow full-sized types to reach 12–18 in. The whole plant is edible—cut at the base and the plant may re-sprout for a second harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or summer; this variety tolerates heat for extended sowing.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in fertile soil and thin to about 4 inches apart, or grow in containers. This baby bok choy tolerates heat, so you can sow it through summer, harvesting at about 4 inches tall for delicate, crisp, mild-flavored leaves. The uniform green plants are an indispensable stir-fry ingredient and also good fresh or marinated. Keep soil evenly moist to keep growth tender, and harvest whole heads while young for the best texture.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil; a vigorous vining gourd best grown on a sturdy trellis.
directions: Soak the hard seeds overnight and sow an inch deep after the soil is thoroughly warm to speed slow germination. Train the aggressive vines up a strong trellis or arbor so the bottle-shaped fruits hang and develop an even shape. Harvest young fruits for cooking, or leave them on the vine to fully mature and dry for crafts and birdhouses.

plant_note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost and transplant after frost danger passes; direct sowing is also possible once soil is warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in warm mix or directly in warm soil; nick the seed coat or soak overnight to speed germination. Bottle gourd is a vigorous, tropical vine that requires a sturdy trellis or fence and very warm conditions to thrive. Harvest immature fruits when they are 6–8 inches long (or the desired shape depending on variety) for vegetable use — they are delicate and best cooked; allow fruits to mature fully and dry on the vine if growing for use as a gourd vessel. Pollination requires both male and female flowers to be open simultaneously; hand-pollinate in the morning if fruit set is poor.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This classic heirloom, thought to date to the late 1800s, has distinctive potato-leaf foliage and produces massive 16 to 24 ounce pink fruits with rich, old-fashioned flavor. The indeterminate vines need a tall, sturdy stake or trellis and benefit from suckering for airflow. Water evenly at the base to reduce cracking, and harvest fruit when fully colored and slightly soft.

common_name: Brandywine Red & Yellow Blend Pole Tomato
plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This blend pairs Red Brandywine, with its rich, well-balanced flavor, and golden Yellow Brandywine, which is sweet-tart, both producing large fruits often near a pound and sometimes close to two. The indeterminate vines need a tall, sturdy support and benefit from pruning for airflow. Water evenly to prevent cracking, and harvest each color when fully ripe and slightly soft.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix after nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F; plants establish rapidly in warm weather.
directions: Plant cuttings in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade; this low-growing groundcover from Brazil spreads to fill in bare areas beneath shrubs and fruit trees, providing a living mulch while yielding edible leaves. The slightly succulent, wavy-edged leaves are harvested young and used raw in salads or briefly cooked like spinach, with a mild, pleasant flavor free of bitterness. It is extremely productive in the subtropics, rooting wherever stems contact moist soil. Take cuttings in early fall to overwinter indoors in a sunny window, or treat as an annual in zone 8 with fresh transplants each spring.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Sow seed just 1/8 inch deep and barely cover, since lettuce needs light to germinate in cool soil. This variety holds its pretty leaves high above the core for easy cutting and forms compact heads that yield well in a small space. Strong bolt resistance extends the harvest, and it resists downy mildew, lettuce leaf aphid, and Fusarium wilt. Keep soil evenly moist, provide afternoon shade in warm weather, and harvest outer leaves or the whole head as needed.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in cool soil.
directions: Quinoa is an ancient, nutritious South American grain, and this rainbow blend adds striking seed-head colors of hot pink, burgundy, red, orange, yellow, white, and green. Direct sow in early spring in cool soil, in full sun and well-drained ground. The tender young leaves can be cooked like spinach, while the protein-rich seeds are harvested when the plants dry down. Rinse the harvested seeds well to remove the naturally bitter saponin coating before cooking. Give the tall plants room, as they can reach 4 to 6 feet.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost once seedlings are stocky.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in starter mix and keep at 70–75°F until germination, then grow on in bright light to prevent leggy transplants. Set out 2–4 weeks before last frost and fertilize with a nitrogen-rich feed at planting and again when heads begin to form. Harvest the central head while florets are still tight and dark green, before any yellow shows. Cut with a few inches of stem to encourage side-shoot production after the main head is removed.

plant_note: One of the easiest microgreens; sow densely on a moist tray and blackout for two to three days.
directions: Broadcast broccoli seed thickly across an evenly moist tray and cover for the first couple of days to encourage strong, uniform germination. Once shoots stand up, uncover and give them bright light so the stems stay short and sturdy. Keep the medium consistently moist and provide gentle airflow to prevent damping-off. Harvest the crisp, mild greens at the cotyledon stage in about a week for the sweetest flavor and best texture.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring crop; sow again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep and thin to 4–6 in; rapini grows quickly and is best as a cool-season crop because heat intensifies bitterness and accelerates bolting. Side-dress with nitrogen once plants are established to encourage the leafy shoot growth that is the primary harvest. Harvest when flower buds are visible but before they open fully, cutting the top 6–8 in of the shoot with leaves included. Repeated cuts extend the harvest, as side shoots continue to develop after the main stem is removed.

plant_note: Grown indoors year-round in a jar or sprouting tray; not sown in garden soil.
directions: Rinse seeds and soak overnight, then drain and spread in a sprouting jar or tray kept out of direct sun. Rinse and drain two to three times daily to keep sprouts moist but never standing in water. Harvest tangy, nutrient-dense sprouts in four to six days once the first true leaves show and greening begins. Refrigerate harvested sprouts and use within a week for the best crunch and peppery bite.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out; a cold-hardy drumhead cabbage for fall and winter harvest.
directions: Start seeds indoors about six weeks before setting out sturdy transplants, or direct sow in summer for a fall crop. This heirloom forms dense, flattened drumhead heads that tolerate hard frosts and store well. Keep the soil evenly moist and feed with nitrogen to build large heads, and harvest after cool weather sweetens the leaves.

plant_note: Start indoors 14–18 weeks before first fall frost so plants mature in cool autumn weather.
directions: Sow 1/4 in deep indoors under bright lights; transplant into deeply prepared soil and stake tall plants against wind. When sprouts at the base reach marble size, remove the growing tip (topping) to concentrate energy and speed maturation of the remaining sprouts. Harvest sprouts from the bottom of the stalk upward once they are 1–2 in across and tight. Flavor peaks after exposure to a hard frost.

plant_note: Use unhulled seed and soak overnight; grown indoors on trays year-round.
directions: Soak the unhulled buckwheat seed overnight and rinse it before spreading it densely on a moist tray. Cover for a few days while the heart-shaped cotyledons push up, then expose to bright light and mist as needed. Keep an eye out for seed hulls clinging to the leaves and mist them off before harvest. Cut the tender, lightly nutty greens once the cotyledons have fully opened, generally around a week to ten days.

plant_note: Plant sets or transplants 2–4 weeks before last frost; onions from sets mature faster and are simpler for home gardeners.
directions: Press sets or transplants 1 in deep with the pointed tip facing up in rich, well-drained, loose soil. Onion bulb size is determined by the number of leaves at the time of bulbing: each leaf corresponds to a ring, so healthy top growth is essential throughout spring. Reduce watering once the necks begin to soften and tops start to fall over naturally. Cure harvested bulbs in a warm, airy spot for 2–3 weeks before moving to dry, cool storage.

plant_note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked; succession sow for continuous greens and roots.
directions: This French heirloom is prized for its deep burgundy, almost black foliage that rivals chard or spinach in the salad bowl. Sow seed directly about half an inch deep and thin seedlings to three to four inches, using the thinnings as baby greens. Harvest tender leaves young for color and flavor, and pull the sweet dark-red roots at one to three inches across. Keep soil evenly moist to prevent woody roots and toughening of the greens.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in deep, raised beds or containers; roots can reach 3 feet and demand well-prepared deep soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in soil loosened to at least 18 inches; raised beds filled with light, compost-rich mix make harvest far easier than attempting to dig clay or compacted soil. Thin to 4–6 inches once seedlings are established. Gobo roots are harvested at 18–24 inches long and about 3/4 inch in diameter, typically 120 days after sowing; use a spading fork to loosen the soil alongside the root before pulling to prevent breakage. Scrub roots and slice thinly for kinpira and stir-fries; soaking cut pieces in acidulated water prevents browning.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant; also excellent as a fall crop timed to mature in cool weather.
directions: This purple sprouting type forms a modest central head, then rewards patient growers with abundant tender side shoots. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before the last frost and set transplants eighteen to twenty-four inches apart in rich, consistently moist soil. Purple broccolis can be finicky, so keep them well fed and cool for the best flavor and antioxidant-rich color. Harvest the main head first to encourage a long flush of flavorful, tender side shoots.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after the last frost when soil is reliably warm; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for a continuous harvest.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in well-drained soil once nighttime temperatures stay above 50°F. Avoid overhead watering once flowers appear to reduce bean rust and fungal issues. Pick pods when they snap cleanly and the seeds inside are still small; regular harvesting extends production. Inoculating seed with rhizobium before planting boosts nitrogen fixation and yield.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil; a giant hard-shelled gourd needing a long season and strong support.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and sow an inch deep once frost danger is well past, giving this long-season gourd every warm day it can get. Provide a very sturdy trellis or let vines run on the ground with straw beneath the developing fruit. The enormous round gourds can reach three to five feet in circumference and dry into durable containers and craft shells.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant; succession sow every two to three weeks and grow in cool weather to avoid bolting.
directions: A vigorous butterhead beloved since 1963 for its buttery flavor and melt-in-your-mouth texture around a creamy yellow heart. Sow seed shallowly, barely covering it, since lettuce needs light to germinate well. Its compact heads are ideal for containers and single-serve salads, and tolerate a bit more heat than most butterheads. Keep soil evenly moist and provide afternoon shade in warm weather to delay bolting and bitterness.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; an AAS-winning Bibb lettuce with good heat tolerance.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, barely covering them, and keep the surface moist during cool spring or fall weather. This Bibb-style butterhead forms loose rosettes with a tender, sweet yellow heart and holds up better to heat than most lettuces. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut the whole head, and succession sow every few weeks for a steady supply.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; a classic tan-skinned winter storage squash.
directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil, planting in hills and thinning to the strongest plants. Let the long vines sprawl and keep them well watered while the smooth, tan-skinned fruits with deep orange flesh develop. Harvest when the rinds are hard and cure them in a warm spot for a week or two to improve flavor and storage life.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost; count back from first fall frost to ensure enough days to maturity.
directions: Sow 2–3 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in fertile, well-drained soil and thin to the strongest vine. Butternut is a long-season crop, so calculate the days to maturity against your first fall frost date before planting. The vines spread widely; allow 8–10 feet of run per plant. Harvest when the skin has turned uniformly tan and the stem dries out fully; cure for 10–14 days in a warm location before storing.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 4 weeks before last frost for spring crop, or start midsummer for fall harvest.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep indoors and harden seedlings off gradually before setting out into cool soil. Feed transplants with a balanced fertilizer at planting and side-dress with nitrogen when heads begin to size. Keep soil moisture consistent, as uneven watering causes heads to split. Harvest when the head feels firm and solid when squeezed; a light frost sweetens the flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant after the soil warms; a blocky heirloom sweet bell.
directions: Start seeds indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and keep them warm for even germination. Transplant out only after nights stay reliably warm, spacing plants so air moves freely between them. The thick-walled, blocky fruits ripen from green to sweet deep red; harvest at either stage and support heavily laden plants if needed.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant only after soil warms and all frost danger passes.
directions: A classic bell pepper since 1928, valued for its large, blocky, uniform fruits that are perfect for stuffing. Start seed indoors eight weeks before the last frost and keep soil warm for reliable germination. Transplant after nights stay above 55 degrees, spacing plants eighteen to twenty-four inches apart. Green peppers left on the plant ripen to sweet red with even higher vitamin C, so harvest at whatever stage you prefer.

plant_note: Sow as early as the ground can be worked in spring; tolerates light frost and thin, dry soils where most oilseeds fail.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seed at 5–8 lb/acre and tamp firmly; camelina requires light for good germination and should not be buried more than 1/4 in deep. It matures in under 100 days and tolerates drought and cold better than any other oilseed crop, making it a fine spring or overwintered autumn oilseed in short-season gardens. Harvest when 80% of the pods are golden and dry — thresh by beating bundled stalks into a bucket, then winnow. The oil is exceptionally high in omega-3 fatty acids and has a pleasant nutty flavor for culinary use.

common_name: Candy Cane Chocolate Cherry Sweet Pepper
plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; excellent in patio containers.
directions: An ornamental edible with variegated foliage and striped fruit that ripens from green-and-white to chocolate-red and cream. Start seed indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and keep warm to encourage germination. Its compact, showy habit makes it a standout in patio containers and balcony gardens. Harvest peppers green for a crisp bite or let them fully stripe and color up for sweeter flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; can start indoors 2–3 weeks early.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, fertile hills after all frost danger has passed, planting 4–5 seeds per hill and thinning to 2. Reduce watering as fruits near maturity to concentrate sugars in the flesh. Ripe cantaloupes emit a sweet fragrance at the blossom end and slip easily from the vine with gentle pressure; this is called full-slip harvest. Avoid overwatering late in the season, which dilutes flavor and can cause splitting.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil warms; succession sow for a long harvest.
directions: A high-yielding French filet-style bush bean producing dark green, stringless pods five to six inches long with excellent flavor. Sow seed directly one inch deep after the soil warms, since beans resent cold, wet ground. Its strong disease resistance and productivity make it a reliable succession crop for a long harvest window. Pick pods regularly while young and slender to keep the plants producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; a fast white-stemmed bok choy prone to bolting in heat.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly in cool spring or late summer weather, as heat and stress push this brassica to bolt. Thin plants to give each room to form crisp white stems and tender green leaves. Keep the soil consistently moist for fast, mild growth and harvest whole heads when they reach full size, ideal for stir-fries and soups.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant; also grows well as a fall crop.
directions: A compact, pointed cabbage weighing just one and a half to two pounds, ideal for small households and small gardens. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before the last frost and transplant twelve to eighteen inches apart. Its tightly wrapped, ornamental conical heads shrug off weather and pests while protecting the tender interior. Keep evenly watered and harvest when heads feel firm and full for the sweetest flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; provide sturdy support for this indeterminate vine.
directions: An award-winning heirloom slicer with ten to twelve ounce fruits that are purplish-brown outside and brick-red within. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant after nights warm. As an indeterminate vine it needs a tall stake or cage and regular pruning of suckers for airflow. Water deeply and evenly to prevent cracking, and let the fruit fully color for its rich, complex flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes, spacing widely as plants become very large.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in large cells or pots and grow on at 65–70°F; transplant into deeply prepared, fertile soil with plenty of compost. In late summer, blanch the stalks by gathering them upright, wrapping loosely in cardboard or burlap, and tying for 4–6 weeks to produce pale, tender, mildly bitter stalks. Harvest in late autumn by cutting the whole plant at the base, then trim the central stalks for cooking. In Zones 7–10 cardoon is perennial; cut old growth to the ground in early spring and topdress with compost to stimulate regrowth.

plant_note: Direct sow only; carrots do not transplant well. Keep soil surface moist until germination.
directions: A colorful mix of red, purple, yellow, and orange carrots, each carrying its own nutrients and sweet flavor. Sow seed directly a quarter inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the surface consistently moist during the slow germination. Thin seedlings to two to three inches so roots can size up evenly. Harvest young for baby carrots or at full size, and note that colors sweeten with a light frost.

plant_note: Direct sow 3 weeks before to 2 weeks after last frost; succession sow every 3 weeks.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the surface consistently moist until sprouting, which is slow. Thin seedlings to 2–3 in so roots can size up. Harvest when shoulders reach desired diameter; flavor sweetens after a light frost.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked; peas thrive in cool spring weather.
directions: A snap pea with thick, juicy, three-inch pods eaten whole, raw or lightly cooked. Sow seed directly one inch deep as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring. The short, roughly thirty-inch vines are largely self-supporting but benefit from a low trellis. It resists powdery mildew and pea enation, so harvest pods frequently while plump and glossy to keep them coming.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; an heirloom white-skinned pumpkin for decor and baking.
directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil in hills, thinning to the two best vines each. Give the plants space and steady moisture to develop the smooth, ghostly white rinds and sweet orange flesh. Harvest when the stems harden and cure in the sun to firm the skin for both fall displays and baking.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season areas.
directions: A petite ornamental pumpkin producing pure-white fruits four to four and a half inches across, perfect for painting and displays. Sow seed directly one inch deep once the soil is warm, or start indoors briefly in short-season climates. Give the vines room to run and keep soil evenly moist while fruit sizes up. Harvest when the skin is hard and the stem corks, leaving a length of stem attached for longer storage.

plant_note: Plant 8–10 inch stem cuttings (stakes) horizontally 4–6 inches deep or at a 45-degree angle after soil warms to 65°F; upright planting produces fewest tubers while horizontal planting maximizes yield.
directions: Plant stem cuttings (stakes) horizontally 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; cassava is drought tolerant once established and produces in poor soils where other root crops fail, making it one of the world's most important calorie crops. All varieties contain some cyanogenic glycosides — sweet varieties are low enough for safe eating after cooking, while bitter varieties require extensive washing and fermentation to detoxify. Never eat raw cassava. Harvest tubers when 8–18 months old by digging carefully to avoid breaking the fragile roots; they must be used within a few days of harvest or frozen, as they deteriorate rapidly. In zone 9, the above-ground plant freezes back but roots may survive with heavy mulch.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant for a fall crop; sprouts sweeten after frost.
directions: Start seeds indoors and set out sturdy transplants so the long season finishes as cool fall weather arrives. This 1940s heirloom forms compact, dwarf stalks packed with large, tightly wrapped sprouts. Keep the plants well fed and watered, and wait to harvest until a few frosts have sweetened the sprouts from the bottom of the stalk upward.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant about 4 weeks before last frost.
directions: Sow 1/4 in deep in well-drained starter mix 6–8 weeks before transplanting; cauliflower is sensitive to temperature swings and nutrient deficiencies. When the developing curd is about the size of a golf ball, gather outer leaves over it and secure with a rubber band to blanch for white varieties. Harvest when the head is firm, tight, and 6–8 in across before the curds begin to separate. Consistent soil moisture and boron-sufficient soil prevent hollow stem and poor curd formation.

plant_note: Flavor sweetens after frost; also spring-sow for summer harvest.
directions: Also known as Tuscan or lacinato kale, this Italian heirloom bears puckered blue-black strap leaves used in soups and braises. Start indoors and transplant, or direct sow, spacing plants 15 inches apart in fertile soil. Harvest lower leaves first, working upward and leaving the growing crown intact for continuous production. Cold weather concentrates sugars, so the best-flavored leaves come after the first hard frosts. Watch for cabbage worms in warm spells and cover with row fabric if needed.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant after all frost danger passes.
directions: A prolific blend of pencil-thin cayenne peppers used fresh, dried, ground, or strung into a decorative ristra. Start seed indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and keep warm for good germination. Transplant after nights stay above 55 degrees and support heavily laden plants if needed. Let fruits ripen to red for full heat, and dry them whole or grind for homemade red pepper flakes.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant after warm-up; a slender hot pepper for drying and sauces.
directions: Start seeds indoors about eight weeks ahead, keeping them warm to germinate reliably. Transplant into a sunny bed once nights are warm, and expect long, slender 5 to 6 inch pods that ripen bright red with lasting heat. Harvest peppers green or red and string the red ones to air-dry for grinding into cayenne powder.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix, maintaining soil temperature above 75°F for prompt sprouting. Set transplants out once the garden is fully warm and side-dress with a balanced fertilizer once plants begin flowering. Thin-walled cayenne fruits dry exceptionally well; allow them to redden fully on the plant before harvesting for drying. String harvested peppers in a warm, airy location or use a dehydrator to preserve them.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant; tolerates heat better than most greens for a long season.
directions: A rainbow-stemmed chard in red, magenta, orange, pink, yellow, and white beneath glossy dark green leaves. Sow the seed clusters half an inch deep and thin seedlings to six to twelve inches apart. It tolerates both light frost and summer heat, making it productive from spring through fall. Harvest outer leaves regularly and the plant keeps generating new growth from the center all season.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed when plants are 3–4 in tall.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist starting mix — they need light to germinate — and barely cover with vermiculite; seedling growth is extremely slow for the first 6 weeks. Transplant into rich, consistently moist soil and remove the lower side shoots throughout summer to encourage bulb development. Celeriac bulbs are at their best after light autumn frosts, which deepen the nutty flavor. Harvest before the ground freezes hard; trim roots and leaves and store in slightly moist sand in a cold root cellar.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger passes and soil has warmed slightly.
directions: Surface-sow or barely cover tiny seed with 1/8 in of moist mix; celery germinates slowly and needs consistent warmth and bright light from the start. Transplant into deeply prepared, rich soil and provide consistent, abundant moisture throughout the season, as drought quickly causes tough, stringy stalks. Hill soil around the base or use collars to blanch stalks to a paler color and milder flavor if desired. Harvest entire plants by cutting at soil level when stalks are 12–18 in tall and firm.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; can also be started indoors 4 weeks early and transplanted out.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep or start indoors and transplant at 4–5 weeks; celtuce is grown primarily for its swollen, asparagus-like central stalk rather than its leaves, though young leaves are also edible. The stalk is ready when it is 1–1.5 inches in diameter and 12–15 inches tall; peel away the outer layer to reveal the crisp, mild interior. Keep soil consistently moist and rich in nitrogen for the fastest stalk development. Harvest before the plant bolts, as heat causes bitterness and pithy texture in the stalk.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 4 weeks before transplanting in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; soak seeds 24 hours before planting to improve germination. Provide a trellis or other support for climbing.
directions: Direct sow or transplant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade with a support to climb; Ceylon spinach is the red-stemmed form of Malabar spinach, producing striking bright-red vining stems, dark green to purplish leaves, and decorative dark reddish-purple berries. Like green Malabar spinach (B. alba), the leaves are thick, succulent, and mild-flavored when young, used fresh in salads or cooked as a spinach substitute in stir-fries, soups, and curries throughout tropical Asia. The red-stemmed form is highly ornamental — it can be used as a colorful edible trellis plant or allowed to cascade over a fence. It thrives in heat that wilts true spinach and is particularly productive in Gulf Coast summer gardens. In zone 9–11 it overwinters as a short-lived perennial. Tolerates partial shade better than most summer leafy greens.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; stake or cage this indeterminate vine.
directions: An heirloom indeterminate cherry tomato named for horticulturist Alan Chadwick, bearing heavy trusses of sweet, crack-resistant one to two ounce fruits. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights warm. Provide a tall trellis or cage and prune suckers to keep the vigorous vines manageable. Water evenly and pick the glossy red fruits regularly to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; a fast heirloom round red radish for spring and fall.
directions: Sow seeds a half inch deep directly in the garden and thin to about an inch apart for well-formed roots. These fast radishes are ready in under a month, producing round, cherry-red roots with crisp, mild white flesh. Keep the soil evenly moist to avoid pithiness and pungency, and sow in succession during the cool ends of the season.

plant_note: Direct sow; a stocky heirloom carrot that performs well in heavy soils and stores long.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly in loose soil and keep the surface consistently moist through the slow germination period. This stocky, tapered variety about four to five inches long tolerates heavier and clay soils better than long carrots. Thin seedlings to prevent crowding and harvest as needed, storing the sweet, red-cored roots in a cool place for winter use.

plant_note: Start indoors two to three weeks early or direct sow in warm soil; melons dislike root disturbance.
directions: A true French cantaloupe too fragile to ship commercially, with smooth skin, light green stripes, and intensely fragrant creamy-orange flesh. Start seed indoors in peat pots two to three weeks before setting out, or direct sow once soil is thoroughly warm. Give vines full sun, warmth, and steady moisture while fruit develops. Harvest at full slip when the fruit detaches easily from the stem and the aroma turns rich and sweet.

plant_note: Needs consistent heat; use black mulch in cooler climates.
directions: This intensely fragrant French melon has smooth gray-green skin and deep orange, aromatic flesh. Start seed indoors three weeks before transplanting to gain a head start in short-season areas, handling the sensitive roots gently. Grow on black plastic mulch with row cover early to trap warmth, removing cover at flowering for pollinators. Reduce watering as fruit ripens to concentrate sugars. Harvest when the fruit turns yellowish, smells sweet, and slips easily from the vine.

plant_note: Start indoors two to three weeks early or direct sow in warm soil; needs a long, hot season.
directions: A classic oblong watermelon with greenish-gray rind and sweet red flesh, bred by the USDA for disease resistance to Fusarium wilt and anthracnose. Start seed indoors in peat pots or direct sow once soil is warm, giving the sprawling vines plenty of room. Water consistently while fruit sizes up, then ease off as melons approach ripeness to concentrate sweetness. Harvest when the ground spot turns creamy yellow and the tendril nearest the fruit dries.

plant_note: Propagate by 12–18 inch stem cuttings stuck directly into moist soil in spring; unlike most cuttings, chaya cuttings establish easily even without rooting hormone. Plant in a site with full sun and well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Plant stem cuttings in well-drained to sandy soil in full sun; chaya is a vigorous, heat-tolerant tropical shrub native to the Yucatan that produces enormous crops of large, spinach-like leaves year-round in frost-free climates. The leaves are exceptionally nutritious — very high in protein, calcium, iron, and carotenoids — but must be cooked for at least 5 minutes before eating, as raw leaves contain hydrocyanic glycosides that dissipate completely with cooking. Once cooked, leaves taste like a mild, slightly earthy spinach and can substitute in any cooked greens recipe. Chaya is extraordinarily productive: a single mature bush can yield over 10 pounds of leaves per week in warm weather. It tolerates drought, poor soil, and neglect better than almost any other vegetable.

plant_note: Start indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost by placing an entire fruit on its side in a pot of moist mix; transplant after all frost danger is past.
directions: Plant the whole fruit (it is the seed) on its side with the broad end slightly angled down, barely covered with moist soil; sprouting begins from the narrow end. Provide a very strong trellis as a single mature vine can reach 30 feet and produce dozens to hundreds of fruits per season. Harvest fruits when they are 3–6 inches long and the skin is still smooth and light green; older fruits become pithy and hard. In Zones 9–11, chayote is perennial and will produce an edible tuberous root as well as fruits; in cooler zones, grow as an annual by starting fresh each spring.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; stake or cage this indeterminate vine.
directions: A cross of the heirlooms Cherokee Purple and Carbon that delivers rich purple-beefsteak flavor earlier, with more fruit and fewer cracks. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights warm. The indeterminate vines need a tall cage or stake and periodic pruning for airflow. Water deeply and evenly, and harvest the ten to twelve ounce fruits when fully colored and slightly soft for peak flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; an indeterminate green beefsteak from the Cherokee Purple line.
directions: Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once the soil warms. This indeterminate heirloom, bred from the Cherokee Purple lineage, produces large fruits that ripen green with an amber blush and firm, juicy flesh. Stake or cage the tall vines, water evenly to prevent cracking, and judge ripeness by a slight softening since the color stays green.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; stake or cage this indeterminate heirloom.
directions: A century-old heirloom said to trace to the Cherokee people, prized for dusky purple-red fruits balancing sweetness with a hint of smoke. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant once the soil warms. This indeterminate vine needs sturdy support and regular pruning to keep it productive from summer to fall. Water consistently to reduce cracking and harvest when the shoulders stay dusky and the fruit yields slightly.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; an indeterminate dusky-rose heirloom beefsteak.
directions: Start seeds indoors roughly six weeks before the last frost and set transplants deeply once nights are warm. This indeterminate heirloom yields large, dusky rose-purple beefsteaks with dense, meaty flesh and a rich, smoky-sweet flavor. Provide strong support and even watering, and harvest when the shoulders take on their characteristic dusky hue and the fruit yields to gentle pressure.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; an AAS-winning fast round red radish.
directions: Sow seeds a half inch deep and thin promptly so the roots have room to round out. This AAS-winning heirloom matures in about three weeks with bright cherry-red skin and crisp, mild white interiors. Keep the bed moist and harvest as soon as the roots size up, since letting them linger in warm weather turns them woody and sharp.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; succession sow every one to two weeks for continuous harvest.
directions: A fast, easy Holland heirloom producing round, bright-red radishes with mild, crisp white flesh in about a month. Sow seed directly half an inch deep and thin to about an inch apart for well-formed roots. Keep soil evenly moist and grow in cool weather to avoid pithy, overly pungent roots. Harvest promptly when roots reach about an inch across, since they turn woody if left in the ground too long.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; ideal for hanging baskets and containers.
directions: A compact, cascading cherry tomato bred for containers and hanging baskets, spilling clusters of bright red one-and-a-half-inch fruits. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights warm. Its trailing habit is ornamental and needs little staking, making it ideal for balconies and patios. Keep container soil evenly moist and harvest the abundant fruit throughout the season.

plant_note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes; harvest the following summer.
directions: A hardneck heirloom from the Republic of Georgia with purple-streaked bulbs and nine to ten easy-to-peel cloves of rich, mellow flavor. Plant individual cloves pointed end up about two inches deep in fall, a few weeks before the ground freezes, and mulch for winter protection. Snap off the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into bulb formation. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place before storage.

plant_note: Sow after last frost; chia is a short-day plant that flowers in fall, so it is best suited to climates with a long frost-free autumn.
directions: Surface-sow seed on a firm, moist seedbed and barely press in, as chia seed is mucilaginous and will stick to the surface when wet; thin plants to 18–24 in once seedlings emerge. Chia does not respond well to transplanting and should be direct-sown where it will grow. The plant flowers in autumn as day length shortens and produces whorled spikes of small purple flowers followed by seed-filled calyces; harvest stalks when the calyces begin to dry and before birds consume the seed. Place harvested stalks head-first into paper bags to finish drying, then shake out seed and screen away debris; seeds are rich in omega-3 fatty acids, calcium, and fiber.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; a compact bushy edamame with large green beans.
directions: Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm soil after frost, as soybeans dislike transplanting. The compact, bushy plants set clusters of plump pods that fill with large, sweet green beans. Harvest the whole plant when most pods are full but still bright green, then boil the pods briefly and pop out the tender edamame.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost date; chickpeas prefer a cool start but need warm, dry weather to mature properly.
directions: Sow chickpeas 2 in deep in light, well-drained soil with a neutral to slightly alkaline pH; they perform poorly in heavy, waterlogged ground. The plants produce distinctive sticky, glandular hairs on stems and pods that can cause a mild skin reaction in sensitive individuals, so harvest with gloves. Pick pods when they are green and plump for fresh eating, or leave plants to dry fully in place and harvest entire plants when 90% of pods have turned tan. Shell and sun-dry for a day before long-term storage.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; squash resents root disturbance.
directions: An early, heavy-yielding summer squash on upright, compact two-foot bushes with moderate powdery mildew resistance. Sow seed directly one inch deep once the soil is warm and settled. The open, upright habit makes harvesting easy and improves airflow around the plants. Pick fruit young and tender, checking daily at peak season since squash size up quickly.

plant_note: Grown indoors year-round in a jar or tray; not sown in garden soil.
directions: Rose-colored radish sprouts deliver a spicy bite popular in Chinese and Japanese cooking, from raw salads to sushi. Soak seed briefly, then drain and spread in a sprouting jar or tray out of direct sun. Rinse and drain two to three times daily until the sprouts stand an inch or two tall with open leaves. Harvest in four to six days and refrigerate, using within a week for the freshest peppery flavor.

plant_note: Plant tubers 2–3 inches deep and 6 inches apart in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in early spring; Chinese artichoke spreads readily by tuber and can be difficult to eradicate once established — plant in a contained bed.
directions: Plant tubers 2–3 inches deep in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Chinese artichoke produces the most visually distinctive tubers in the vegetable garden — small (1–2 inch), cream-white, segmented underground stems that resemble tiny white caterpillars or coiled seashells. Known in French cuisine as 'crosnes' (from the French town where they were introduced in the late 19th century), they have a crisp, sweet, delicate flavor similar to water chestnuts, used raw in salads, lightly stir-fried, or pickled. The plant is in the mint family and spreads by tubers — contain it or plan for its spread. Harvest in fall after frost kills the tops, when the tubers are at their crisp best. The tubers discolor quickly after harvesting; use immediately. Tolerates partial shade, making it useful in a shaded food garden where few vegetables thrive.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant; grows in spring and fall and tolerates some heat.
directions: Also called gai lan, this Asian green offers tender flowering stems, juicy stalks, and leaves with a sweet, slightly bitter flavor. Sow seed directly half an inch deep and thin to six to ten inches apart. It is more heat tolerant than regular broccoli, so it can be sown across a longer season. Harvest the whole plant or cut the main stem to encourage tender side shoots before the flower buds fully open.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant; an aromatic thin-stalked heirloom celery for cooking.
directions: Start the tiny seeds indoors, pressing them into the surface since they need light to germinate, and keep them evenly moist. Transplant into rich, damp soil and grow through the cooler parts of the season. This variety forms slender, hollow, intensely aromatic stalks used more as a seasoning than a raw snack, so cut stalks as needed for stir-fries and soups.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 3–4 weeks before last frost as plants can handle light frost.
directions: Sow tiny seeds on the surface of moist starting mix and press lightly without covering; seeds need light to germinate and are very slow — allow 2–3 weeks. Chinese celery produces slender, hollow stems and abundant leaves with intense celery flavor far more pungent than Western stalk celery; it is used primarily as an herb and flavoring rather than eaten in large quantities raw. Transplant into moisture-retentive, fertile soil and keep evenly moist throughout the season, as drought causes bitterness and premature bolting. Harvest outer stalks continuously once plants are established; the leaves are the most intensely flavored part.

plant_note: Plant tuber sections or bulbils in spring after last frost once soil reaches 60°F; established crowns are hardy to zone 5.
directions: Plant tuber sections or aerial bulbils (small potato-like growths that form in the leaf axils) 2–3 inches deep in deep, loose, stone-free soil; the main tuber can grow 3 feet straight down, so deep, well-prepared beds are essential. Provide a trellis or fence immediately as this vigorous twining vine can reach 15 feet in a season. Harvest main tubers in late fall after the vine dies back, being careful to dig deeply and extract the entire tuber without breaking; the secondary bulbils can also be harvested and eaten or saved for replanting. The raw tuber is one of the few yams that can be eaten uncooked and grated — called nagaimo in Japanese cuisine — where its distinctive gluey texture is prized.

plant_note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked; succession sow through summer.
directions: This Italian heirloom reveals concentric pink-and-white rings when sliced, and the rings show best when eaten raw or lightly cooked. Sow the multigerm seed clusters half an inch deep and thin seedlings to 3 inches once true leaves appear. Keep soil consistently moist to prevent woody, zoned roots and to encourage tender growth. Harvest when roots reach 1.5 to 2 inches across for the sweetest flavor. The greens are also edible and can be cooked like chard.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant after warm-up; a sweet bell ripening chocolate brown.
directions: Start seeds indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and keep them warm for good germination. Transplant into full sun once nights stay warm, and allow the blocky fruits to ripen fully from green to glossy chocolate brown for the sweetest flavor and thickest walls. Support laden plants and harvest regularly to keep new fruit setting.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; stake or cage this indeterminate vine.
directions: A prolific indeterminate cherry tomato bearing one-inch purplish-red fruits that add sweetness to salads and pastas. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights warm. The vigorous vines set long trusses of six to eight crack-resistant fruits, so give them a tall trellis and prune for airflow. Water evenly and harvest when the fruit turns dusky red for the fullest flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; stake or cage this indeterminate vine.
directions: An extraordinarily productive cherry tomato with elongated, deep-red fruits striped in green and brown and a rich, sweet flavor. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights warm. Provide a tall cage or trellis for the vigorous indeterminate vines and prune suckers for airflow. Water consistently and keep picking, as the fruit keeps coming all season long.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost date in spring, or 6–8 weeks before first fall frost; best in cool weather but tolerates mild warmth better than other brassicas.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in rows, thinning to 6–10 inches; plants can be sown more densely and thinned progressively, using the thinnings as greens. Harvest the central flower stalk when buds are just beginning to show yellow but before flowers open, cutting 6 inches down to leave side shoots. The stems, leaves, and flower buds are all edible; successive harvesting keeps production going for several weeks. Even soil moisture and cool temperatures produce the sweetest, most tender stalks.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; needs a long, warm season.
directions: The French heirloom Rouge Vif d'Etampes, a deeply ridged, flattened pumpkin twelve to twenty-four inches across weighing fifteen to twenty pounds. Sow seed directly one inch deep in warm soil and give the vigorous vines ample room to sprawl. Keep soil evenly moist while fruit develops and set ripening fruit on a board to prevent rot. Harvest when the rind hardens to a vivid red-orange and the stem begins to cork.

plant_note: Choose day-length-appropriate strain for your latitude.
directions: These flat, disc-shaped Italian onions are prized for roasting whole and caramelizing due to their high sugar content. Start seed indoors ten weeks before last frost and transplant seedlings when pencil-thick. Keep beds weed-free since onions compete poorly, and water steadily until tops begin to fall. Stop watering once necks soften and leaves yellow, then lift and cure in a dry airy place. Well-cured bulbs store for several months in a cool spot.

plant_note: Start true seed indoors, then transplant; unusual for a potato grown from botanical seed.
directions: A rare potato grown from true botanical seed, producing a diverse mix of round and fingerling tubers in rose-gold and red shades. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant seedlings after hardening off. Hill soil or mulch around the stems as they grow to keep developing tubers covered and prevent greening. Harvest once the tops die back and cure the small, flavorful tubers before storage.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; okra loves heat and dislikes cold soil.
directions: An improved 1980 selection of the classic Clemson okra, bearing dark green, grooved, straight, spineless pods with full-bodied flavor. Soak seed overnight and sow directly one inch deep once the soil is warm, since okra sulks in cold ground. The tall, hollyhock-related plants bear hibiscus-like blooms and thrive in summer heat. Pick pods young at two to four inches every day or two, as they toughen quickly if left on the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow into hot soil; an AAS-winning spineless okra for heavy yields.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and sow an inch deep only after the soil is truly warm, since okra sulks in cold ground. This 1939 AAS winner grows into tall, productive plants that set spineless pods over a long season. Pick the pods young at about three inches every couple of days, as they toughen quickly and frequent harvest keeps the plants producing.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; squash resents root disturbance.
directions: An Italian heirloom zucchini with long, cylindrical dark-green fruit striped in lighter green, best picked around eight inches. Sow seed directly one inch deep once the soil is warm and settled. The bush-type plants are productive, so harvest fruit young and often before they grow seedy past twelve inches. Keep soil evenly moist and watch for squash bugs and vine borers common to summer squash.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; an Italian heirloom bush zucchini with striped ribbed fruit.
directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil, planting a few per hill and thinning to the strongest. This compact Italian bush type produces slender, ribbed fruits striped in light and dark green with firm, flavorful flesh. Harvest zucchini young at six to eight inches and pick often, since a single missed fruit quickly grows into a marrow and slows the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant around last frost date in spring; for fall, direct sow 8–10 weeks before first frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 in deep in fertile soil and thin to 18–24 in; collards are heat-tolerant among brassicas and can produce well into summer in the South. Fertilize with nitrogen every 4–6 weeks to maintain vigorous leaf production on these tall, long-season plants. Begin harvesting lower, fully expanded leaves once plants are 10–12 in tall, always leaving the top growing cluster intact. Frost sweetens the leaves substantially; plants may overwinter in zones 7–9.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; a compact determinate ideal for pots and grow bags.
directions: A compact determinate hybrid bred for containers, producing red beefsteak-style tomatoes of six to eight ounces in about seventy days. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant into large pots or grow bags after nights warm. Its tidy habit needs only light support and suits small spaces and balconies. Keep container soil evenly moist and feed regularly, since potted tomatoes are heavy feeders.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; an early, heat-tolerant bush snap bean.
directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep directly in the garden after the last frost, as beans dislike root disturbance. This early, adaptable variety sets heavy crops of crisp, flavorful pods and tolerates both cooler and warmer conditions than many bush beans. Harvest the tender pods young and pick frequently to keep the plants productive over several weeks.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil warms; succession sow for a long harvest.
directions: A stringless favorite since 1949 that produces early, in about fifty days, and tolerates heat and powdery mildew better than most. Sow seed directly one inch deep after the soil warms, as beans rot in cold, wet ground. Its heat tolerance and disease resistance make it dependable where other varieties struggle. Pick pods young and often to keep the plants setting new beans.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant; also grows well as a fall crop.
directions: A Danish heirloom from 1909 that became the standard for round, early cabbages, forming tight six-to-eight-inch heads of three to four pounds. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before the last frost and transplant twelve to eighteen inches apart. Its uniform, quick-maturing heads sit compactly on short stems, saving garden space. Keep evenly watered and harvest when heads feel solid to avoid splitting.

plant_note: Direct sow in late summer or fall; a very cold-hardy salad green also called lamb's lettuce.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly in cool soil in late summer or fall, since this green germinates poorly in heat. The small, spoon-shaped leaves form tight rosettes that shrug off hard frosts and can be harvested through winter under a light cover. Cut whole rosettes at the base for their mild, nutty flavor, and let the very cold-hardy plants stand in the garden as a living winter salad supply.

plant_note: Direct sow only; carrots do not transplant well. Keep soil surface moist until germination.
directions: A striking carrot with bright purple skin and orange interior, reviving a color grown for over a thousand years. Sow seed directly a quarter inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the surface moist through the slow germination. Thin seedlings to two to three inches so the roots can size up evenly. Harvest when roots reach full size and enjoy the sweet flavor and extra antioxidants in the purple skin.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: This Italian heirloom zucchini bears ribbed, mottled fruit with a firm texture and rich, nutty flavor. Harvest young at 4 to 8 inches for the best tenderness, or let fruit reach up to 18 inches for cooking. Pick often to keep the compact plants producing all season. The abundant male blossoms are also excellent stuffed or fried. Water evenly and give plants room for good airflow to reduce powdery mildew.

plant_note: Ribbed Italian heirloom prized for flavor over sheer yield.
directions: This ribbed Italian heirloom zucchini has firm, richly flavored flesh and produces attractive star-shaped slices. Direct sow in warm, fertile soil and give the large, somewhat sprawling plants plenty of room. It yields fewer fruit than hybrids but each has superior nutty flavor and holds up well when grilled. Its abundant male blossoms are excellent for stuffing and frying. Harvest fruit young at 6 to 8 inches for the best texture and to keep production going.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out after the last frost.
directions: A classic Italian heirloom, this indeterminate variety produces deeply ribbed, rich-red fruit of 6 to 8 ounces with an intense, old-world flavor balancing sweetness and acidity. The thick, meaty walls make it ideal for sauces and paste. Provide sturdy staking or a tall cage since vines grow long and heavy. Prune suckers for larger fruit and better airflow. Feed and water consistently to prevent blossom-end rot and cracking.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost when soil is reliably above 60°F; avoid planting in wet, cold soil which causes seed rot.
directions: Sow seeds 1–1.5 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil; cranberry beans can be grown as a bush or pole type depending on variety. Harvest at the fresh shell stage when pods are colorfully streaked with red and cream and beans inside are plump but not yet dry — this is the classic fresh borlotti stage prized in Italian cooking. For dried beans, leave pods on the plant until they are papery and rattle when shaken, then pull entire plants and cure under cover before threshing. Regular watering during pod fill prevents shriveled beans.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier for an earlier harvest; in frost-free zones sow any time of year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade after soil warms to 70°F; the deep burgundy-red, deeply lobed foliage is ornamental throughout the season, making this one of the most attractive edible plants for a zone 9–11 garden. The young leaves have a tart, cranberry-sorrel flavor excellent in salads, wraps, and as a garnish; the fuchsia flowers are also edible and make a vivid cranberry-colored tea. In zone 9–11 it grows as a perennial but blooms only in short days, producing flowers in late fall; in cooler zones treat as a warm-season annual for leaf harvest. Cut back by half in late summer to promote fresh tender growth.

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after frost danger passes.
directions: This determinate variety bears cylindrical, cream to pale-yellow fruit best picked at about 3 inches when they resemble small sausages. The smooth, buttery, mild flavor is excellent eaten fresh or simmered into pale sauces. The compact bush habit suits containers and small beds and needs little staking. Because it sets fruit over a concentrated window, it is well suited to canning batches. Keep watering steady to avoid cracking.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors 3 weeks early in short-season areas.
directions: This heirloom watermelon produces round, 6 to 11 pound fruit about 10 inches across with sweet, creamy pale-white flesh under a thin, delicate rind. Its relatively short season makes it a good choice for northern gardens, especially with a black plastic mulch to warm the soil. Handle the fragile rind gently at harvest. Water deeply but reduce moisture as fruit ripens to concentrate sweetness. Give vines ample space to sprawl.

plant_note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant out around the last frost.
directions: This award-winning eschalion produces oblong, 4 to 5 inch bulbs with coppery-pink skin and rosy-purple inner rings, combining the fruity sweetness of shallots with the easy peeling of onions. Start seed indoors early since it needs a long season to size up. Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Keep beds weed-free and evenly watered until tops begin to fall, then cure bulbs in a dry, airy spot. Stored properly they keep for months.

plant_note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes for a summer harvest.
directions: This heat-tolerant Creole-type garlic has bright pinkish-red wrappers and yields the largest bulbs in mild-winter regions, with a distinctive shallot-like flavor. Plant single cloves pointed-end up, 2 inches deep and 4 to 6 inches apart, in fall in loose, fertile, well-drained soil. Mulch after planting, keep evenly watered in spring, and stop watering as the lower leaves brown. Harvest when several lower leaves have died back, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy spot before storing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: This large spring radish reaches 1 to 2 inches across with a crisp white interior and a mild flavor perfect for salads and sandwiches. It grows fast, making it a great choice for children and impatient gardeners. Sow directly in cool weather since heat causes it to bolt and turn pithy. Thin seedlings early to give roots room to swell. Keep soil evenly moist for tender, uncracked roots.

plant_note: Direct sow into hot soil; a heat-tolerant heirloom with striped rind and red flesh.
directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in hills only after the soil is thoroughly hot, since watermelons demand warmth. Give the long vines ample room and steady water while the fruits size up, then ease off watering as they near ripeness to concentrate sugars. The 20 to 25 pound striped melons are ripe when the ground spot turns creamy yellow and the tendril nearest the fruit dries.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil, or start indoors early in short seasons.
directions: A high-quality home-garden standard, this variety weighs about 20 to 25 pounds with bright crimson, sweet flesh. With a little care it can be grown in northern climates, especially using black plastic mulch and starting seeds indoors. Provide rich, well-drained soil and consistent moisture during vine growth and fruit set. Ease off watering as melons ripen to boost sugar. Harvest when the ground spot turns creamy yellow and the tendril nearest the fruit dries.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant after warm-up; a mild sweet Italian frying pepper.
directions: Start seeds indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and keep them warm to germinate evenly. Transplant into full sun once nights are reliably warm, and harvest the thin-walled, 3 to 6 inch fruits pale green for frying or let them ripen red for extra sweetness. Pick regularly to keep the plants setting new peppers through the season.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil warms to at least 60°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and maintain soil at 80°F for best germination; grow seedlings in bright light to prevent stretching. The cubanelle produces thin-walled, tapered fruits 4–6 inches long that ripen from pale yellowish-green to red; they are nearly sweet with very mild heat and excellent for frying, stuffing, and fresh eating. Harvest green fruits at 65 days or leave on the plant to ripen red for sweeter flavor. Plants are prolific producers; side-dress with a balanced fertilizer midseason to maintain yield.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; a vining plant bearing grape-sized cucumber-like fruits.
directions: Start seeds indoors a few weeks before the last frost, as the tiny seeds germinate slowly, and transplant once nights are warm. Train the delicate vines up a trellis or netting where the abundant fruits are easy to spot and pick. Harvest the grape-sized, watermelon-patterned fruits when firm for a crisp cucumber flavor with a citrusy tang, eaten fresh or pickled.

plant_note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost and transplant after frost danger passes; cucamelon dislikes root disturbance so use biodegradable pots.
directions: Sow 1/4 inch deep in warm mix indoors and pot on without disturbing the root ball when transplanting; cucamelon develops a large tuberous root that can overwinter in frost-free climates. Train the delicate vines up a trellis or netting and keep soil consistently moist for best fruit set. Harvest the grape-sized fruits when they look like tiny watermelons and feel firm — they become seedy and bitter if left too long. The flavor is a pleasant combination of cucumber and lime; pick frequently to maintain heavy production.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature is at least 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in hills or rows once soil has warmed to at least 65°F. Cucumbers are high feeders; amend beds with compost and side-dress with nitrogen once vines begin running. Train vines onto a trellis to improve airflow and make harvest easier. Pick slicing types at 6–8 inches and pickling types at 2–4 inches to keep plants productive and prevent bitterness from over-ripe fruit.

plant_note: Long-season winter squash; some resistance to vine borers.
directions: This heirloom winter squash bears large crook-necked fruit with sweet flesh traditional in Southern pies. Direct sow several seeds per hill in warm soil, thinning to the two strongest plants. Give the vigorous vines ample room to ramble, as they can run 12 feet or more. Cushaws show useful resistance to squash vine borers compared with pumpkin types. Harvest when the rind hardens and the vine dies back, then cure in a warm spot to sweeten and improve storage.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; a Danish heirloom with long cylindrical roots for uniform slices.
directions: Sow the clustered seed about a half inch deep and thin the seedlings so each root has room to elongate. This Danish heirloom produces smooth, cylindrical roots six to eight inches long that yield many uniform slices from a small footprint. Keep the soil evenly moist for tender, sweet, deep-red flesh, and harvest before the roots grow oversized and coarse.

plant_note: Long roots grow partly above soil; hill lightly to keep them covered.
directions: This Danish heirloom produces long cylindrical roots that yield uniform slices, ideal for pickling and canning. Sow seed half an inch deep in loose, stone-free soil so the elongated roots can develop straight. Thin to 3 inches and keep the beds evenly moist to prevent zoning and cracking. As roots push upward, draw a little soil over exposed shoulders to prevent greening. Harvest when roots reach about 6 inches long and 2 inches thick.

plant_note: Direct sow in late summer for fall harvest; a long white heirloom radish that also loosens soil.
directions: Sow seeds a half inch deep in loose, deep soil, ideally in late summer for a fall crop that avoids bolting. Thin seedlings generously so the long white roots can grow straight and uniform. Keep the bed evenly moist and harvest the mild, crisp roots before hard freezes; the deep taproots also help break up and aerate compacted ground.

plant_note: Best sown 8–10 weeks before the first autumn frost; long roots develop best in cooling soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 in deep in deeply tilled or raised beds; daikon roots can extend 18 in or more and will fork or bend in compacted soil. Thin seedlings to 6 in early for best root development and use thinnings as microgreens. Harvest at 12–18 in length before hard freezes, though a light frost mellows the sharp pungency. Daikon is also used as a tillage radish cover crop to break up hardpan.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring as soon as soil is workable; carrots resent transplanting.
directions: This late-19th-century heirloom offers strong color, high yield, and uniform, tapered roots that perform well even in heavy soils thanks to their vigorous tops. Sow seed thinly directly in the garden and keep the surface consistently moist until the slow seeds sprout. Thin seedlings to prevent crowding and forked roots. It interplants nicely with onions. Loosen and deeply cultivate the bed before sowing for the straightest roots.

plant_note: Direct sow once soil can be worked; sow again in mid to late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Danvers 126 is a dependable heirloom that produces tapered, deep-orange roots even in heavier or imperfect soils. Loosen the bed deeply and remove stones so roots stay uniform, then sow seed thinly about a quarter inch deep. Keep the surface consistently moist during the slow germination period, and thin seedlings to a couple inches apart once they establish. Harvest at roughly 70 to 80 days when shoulders reach three-quarters of an inch, and hold roots in cool storage for winter use.

plant_note: Start indoors for spring, or direct sow in mid-summer for a fall and winter crop.
directions: This extra-cold-hardy lacinato-type kale features striking purple midribs against blue-green, savoyed leaves that deepen in color during cool fall weather. It withstands frost better than most dinosaur kales, and flavor sweetens after a light freeze. Grow in full sun to partial shade in rich, moist soil. Harvest outer leaves as needed to keep plants producing. It overwinters well in mild regions for early spring greens.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil is warm and settled.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil after last frost, placing 2–3 seeds per hill and thinning to one. Delicata is relatively compact for a winter squash, making it suitable for smaller gardens. The cream-and-green striped skin becomes creamy yellow with green stripes as it matures. Harvest when the background color shifts from white to creamy yellow and the skin resists puncture with a thumbnail.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: This 1894 heirloom develops oblong fruits with hard cream skin striped in green and sweet, tender flesh. Sow seed an inch deep in warm soil, spacing plants generously to give the semi-vining habit room to run. Provide steady moisture through fruit set and mulch to conserve water and suppress weeds. Harvest when the skin hardens and the stripes deepen, then cure fruits in a warm spot for a week or two to improve storage and flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant out only after nights stay warm.
directions: This blend of mini sweet snack peppers ripens to red, yellow, and orange, all prized for crunchy, juicy texture and vibrant color. Start seed indoors early since peppers need a long warm season. Harden off carefully and transplant only after soil and nights are reliably warm. Plants are productive and well suited to containers and small beds. Pick regularly once fruit colors up to encourage continued set.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring.
directions: This early Dutch heirloom shelling pea charms with pink blooms and vivid purple pods on compact, productive vines. Sow seed an inch deep as soon as the soil is workable, since peas thrive in cool spring weather. Provide a short trellis or brushy support to keep the vines tidy and the pods clean. Harvest the purple pods while plump and shell out the sweet green peas promptly, as flavor is best right after picking.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall harvest.
directions: Detroit Dark Red is a classic heirloom prized for deep crimson roots, tender texture, and sweet flavor that holds up for canning and storage. Sow seed about half an inch deep in loose, well-drained soil, keeping the bed evenly moist to aid the multi-seeded clusters. Thin seedlings to three or four inches apart so roots size up well, and use the thinnings as tender greens. Pull roots at two to three inches for the best eating quality, and hold larger roots in cool, damp storage over winter.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall harvest.
directions: A dependable heirloom, this beet forms tender, deep-crimson roots of 2.5 to 3 inches that stay smooth rather than woody even when grown large. The nutritious greens are excellent cooked like chard or spinach. Because each seed is a cluster, thin seedlings to one per spot for well-formed roots. Sow in cool weather and keep evenly watered to prevent zoning and cracking. Perfect for roasting, pickling, or grating raw into salads.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: This golden heirloom beet offers vibrant orange roots with a mild, buttery, non-bleeding flavor and edible greens. Sow seed half an inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the surface moist until the seedlings emerge. Thin to three or four inches apart so roots develop fully, and harvest greens sparingly along the way. Roast, steam, or eat roots raw, and store surplus in a cool, humid spot for winter keeping.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: A beloved heirloom with sweet, non-bleeding orange roots and nutritious edible greens that stay mild whether raw or cooked. Direct sow seed clusters half an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil, keeping moisture steady during germination. Thin seedlings to a few inches apart and remove weeds so roots can swell evenly. Harvest at golf-ball to tennis-ball size for tender eating, and cure larger roots for cool storage.
