plant_note: Start indoors early; germination is slow and needs steady warmth.
directions: The classic orange habanero delivers intense heat with a fruity, tropical sweetness that shines in salsas and hot sauces. Start seed indoors well ahead of the last frost, as germination is slow and demands consistent warmth. Transplant into full sun only after nights are reliably warm, and the productive plants do well in containers. Handle the ripe pods with gloves and harvest regularly once they turn deep orange.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This large bell pepper ripens from green to glowing orange with thick, sweet, juicy walls. Pick fruit at either stage to boost overall production and add color to meals. Start seeds indoors well ahead of your last frost, then set sturdy transplants into warm soil. Stake heavily laden plants and water evenly to prevent blossom-end rot.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost, or start indoors 3 weeks early.
directions: Orangeglo is a beloved heirloom watermelon with brilliant orange flesh and a crisp, exceptionally sweet, tropical flavor. The large oblong fruits ripen on vigorous vines over a long, warm season. Direct sow into warm, well-drained soil in full sun after all frost danger, giving the vines ample room to run. Water consistently while fruit develops, then reduce watering as the melons near ripeness to concentrate sugars.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as soon as it can be worked.
directions: This snow pea produces some of the largest, crispest, sweetest flat pods you will grow, on vines that stay under 30 inches and need little support. It carries strong resistance to powdery mildew, Fusarium wilt, and pea enation mosaic virus. Sow directly in cool spring soil and keep evenly moist. Harvest pods young and flat, before the seeds swell, to keep plants producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Oregon Sugar Pod II is a productive snow pea bearing crisp, sweet, flat pods that are delicious raw or lightly cooked. The compact vines resist common pea diseases and crop heavily over the cool season. Direct sow in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, and provide short netting or twiggy supports. Pick pods young and often, before the peas inside swell, to keep the plants producing.

common_name: Oregon Sugar Pod II Snow Pea
plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as soon as it can be worked.
directions: This heavy-yielding snow pea bears large numbers of 4 inch flat pods on manageable 28 inch vines that resist mildew, pea enation virus, and leaf roll. Sow directly in cool spring soil and keep the bed moist. A short trellis or brush keeps vines upright and pods clean. Pick pods while flat and tender, before the peas inside develop, for the best flavor.

common_name: Orient Wonder Pole Yard Long Bean
plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; loves heat and humidity.
directions: Also called Chinese long beans, this dark green pole variety produces smooth, slender pods that stay tender because the seeds develop slowly. It tolerates weather swings from cool and dry to hot and humid better than common beans. Provide a tall, sturdy trellis for the vigorous vines. Harvest pods young at 12 to 18 inches, picking often to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Start mid to late summer for fall and winter color; frost intensifies the hues.
directions: Ornamental kale, a flowering cabbage, forms compact rosettes of frilly leaves in pink, purple, cream, and white that light up cool-season beds and containers. Start seed in mid to late summer so plants color up as temperatures drop, since the vivid hues develop with cold. Give full sun and steady moisture for full, dense rosettes. Frost only deepens the color, making this a standout for fall and winter displays; the leaves are edible but grown mainly for looks.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Oxheart Pink is a vigorous indeterminate heirloom producing large, heart-shaped fruits with smooth skin, deep pink flesh, and a firm, meaty texture with few seeds. The dense flesh makes it excellent for sauces as well as fresh slicing. Start seed indoors before the last frost and transplant into full sun in warm soil, planting deeply. Provide strong support for the heavy fruit and keep watering even to reduce cracking and blossom-end rot.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This Italian heirloom, also known as Cuore di Bue, bears heart-shaped, meaty fruits that are dense with few seeds and cook down into a robust sauce. Being indeterminate, the vines grow tall and need staking or caging. Start seeds indoors, transplant into warm soil, and water evenly to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot. Prune suckers lightly to improve airflow and fruit size.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil warms.
directions: This early, thin-skinned Spanish pepper is a classic tapas appetizer, mostly mild but with the occasional fiery pod for a game of Spanish roulette. Pick fruits small at 1 to 1.5 inches for the best texture and flavor. Start seeds indoors early and transplant into warm, sunny beds. Harvest frequently while green, blistering the pods in hot oil for the traditional dish.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix at 78–82°F; transplant into fertile, well-drained soil with consistent moisture. Padrón peppers are the Spanish tapas classic — small, thin-walled, and mostly mild, but a percentage of fruits become significantly hot (especially those left to grow larger or harvested later in the season). Pick pods when they are 1.5–2 inches long and still green for the mildest flavor; fried in olive oil and salted, they are traditionally served as pimientos de Padrón. Consistent watering during fruit set prevents irregular heat and blossom drop.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; plant in blocks for good pollination.
directions: This colorful open-pollinated sweet corn was bred from a cross of Luther Hill sweet corn and Painted Mountain flour corn, giving it kernels in many hues and adaptation to short, cool seasons. Sow in warm soil in blocks of at least four rows so wind pollinates the ears well. Keep evenly watered, especially as tassels and silks form. Harvest in the milk stage when kernels are plump and juicy.

plant_note: Sow in mid to late summer so heads form in cool fall weather.
directions: Palla Rossa is an Italian radicchio-type chicory that forms tight, round heads of deep burgundy leaves with crisp white ribs and a pleasantly bitter flavor. It grows best as a fall crop; the cool weather colors up the heads and mellows their bitterness. Direct sow in mid to late summer in full sun and thin to give each plant room to head up. Harvest when heads feel firm, and a light frost only improves flavor and color.

plant_note: Sow in summer for a fall crop; color and heads develop best in cool weather.
directions: This radicchio forms firm red heads whose color and flavor develop best in cool fall weather, and it overwinters well in zone 4 and warmer. The mildly bitter leaves sweeten after frost. Sow in mid to late summer for heading in autumn, keeping soil evenly moist. Cut heads at the base, and if cut correctly the plants may regrow a second flush.

plant_note: Round roots tolerate heavy or shallow soils where long types fail.
directions: This round French heirloom carrot forms golf-ball-sized roots that thrive in clay or rocky ground where long carrots struggle. Sow seed thinly and shallowly, keeping the surface damp until the slow seedlings emerge. Thin to 2 inches to allow the little globes to plump up evenly. Because roots are short, they mature quickly and are excellent for containers. Harvest young at about 1 to 1.5 inches across for the sweetest, most tender roots.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; sow again in late summer for fall.
directions: This classic romaine, developed by the USDA and Clemson around 1950, forms upright 10 to 12 inch heads with crunchy sweet leaves and a buttery green heart. It shows good heat tolerance and vigor. Sow shallowly in cool soil and keep evenly moist for tender leaves. Harvest whole heads or pick outer leaves, and offer light shade in summer heat to delay bolting.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall.
directions: Parris Island is a classic romaine (cos) heirloom forming large, upright heads up to a foot tall with firm green outer leaves and tender, blanched hearts. It shows good heat resistance for a lettuce, making it dependable into early summer. Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer, keeping soil consistently moist. Give full sun in cool weather, and harvest whole heads when firm or take outer leaves as needed.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed 2–4 weeks before the last frost; parsnip seed has a very short viability window so always use seed from the current season.
directions: Sow 3 seeds per station 1/2 in deep in very deeply dug, stone-free soil and thin to the strongest seedling; long taproots will fork in compacted ground. Parsnip germination is notoriously slow and erratic — sowing radish seed in the same row acts as a quick-germinating row marker. Leave roots in the ground through autumn frosts, which convert starches to sugars for a sweeter harvest. Wear gloves when handling foliage in bright sun to avoid phototoxic sap burns.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil warms.
directions: The fresh chilaca pepper dries into the dark, wrinkled pasilla that is essential to traditional mole and adds depth to enchilada sauces and salsas. Heat runs a mild 1,000 to 2,000 Scoville units. Start seeds indoors early and set transplants into warm soil in full sun. Let pods ripen to dark brown for drying, or use them fresh, harvesting regularly to keep plants productive.

common_name: Patio Choice Yellow Bush Cherry Tomato
plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This compact determinate bush tomato is ideal for containers and small spaces, yielding abundant sweet half-ounce yellow cherry tomatoes. Its tidy habit needs little staking, making it good for patios and mixed pots. Start seeds indoors, transplant after frost, and water evenly to keep fruit from splitting. Pick ripe tomatoes often to encourage a steady flush.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Patio Pride is an early, compact heirloom shelling pea that grows just eight to twelve inches tall, making it ideal for containers, window boxes, and small raised beds. Despite its small stature it produces a generous early crop of sweet peas. Direct sow in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, sowing thickly since the dwarf plants support each other. Harvest promptly once pods fill for the best flavor, and enjoy a quick turnaround for succession planting.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as soon as it can be worked; great for containers.
directions: This early snap pea is a 2017 All-America Selections winner, ready in about 40 days on compact plants perfect for containers. It yields sweet, uniform 2 to 3 inch pods that are tender when picked young. Sow directly in cool soil for a quick spring or fall crop and keep evenly moist. Harvest pods when plump but still crisp to keep the vines producing.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature is at least 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil in hills of 2–3 seeds, thinning to the strongest plant. Consistent moisture at the root zone keeps the bush compact and productive throughout the season. Pattypan's distinctive flying-saucer shape makes sizing easy. Harvest when fruits are 2–3 inches across for the most tender flesh; over-mature fruits become tough and seedy.

plant_note: Soak peas overnight before sowing thickly; shoots can be cut and regrow once.
directions: Soak the pea seed overnight so it germinates quickly and evenly, then spread it thickly across a moist tray. Keep the seeds covered under weight for a couple of days to encourage strong, upright shoots, then bring them into bright light. Water from below to keep the dense mat hydrated without inviting rot. Harvest the sweet, tender pea shoots at three to six inches tall; many growers get a second cutting from the same tray.

plant_note: Sow thickly in cool soil or trays; harvest young as tender shoots.
directions: Harvested young, pea shoots are a tender, versatile green for salads, sandwiches, soups, and stir-fries. Sow the seeds thickly in a container or garden bed and keep them evenly moist. Snip the shoots with scissors when a few inches tall, cutting above the lowest leaves so plants can regrow for a second cutting. They grow quickly in cool conditions and tolerate part shade.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks after last frost; peanuts need a long, frost-free growing season of at least 120 days.
directions: Shell peanuts and plant seeds 2 in deep in loose, sandy, well-drained soil; heavy clay prevents proper peg penetration and pod development. After flowering, keep the area around plants free of weeds and loosen surface soil lightly so fertilization pegs can easily enter the ground. Harvest when foliage begins to yellow and the inner shell veins turn dark, by lifting the entire plant with a fork. Cure plants upside-down in a warm, airy location for 2–3 weeks before removing pods.

plant_note: Sow thickly and do not thin; crowding keeps bulbs small.
directions: Tiny pearl onions are grown deliberately crowded so each bulb stays small and round for pickling and boiling. Direct sow densely in a wide band and skip thinning, since competition limits bulb size. Keep the bed evenly moist and weed-free during the short growing period. Harvest when tops yellow and fall, then cure the little bulbs before storing or pickling. Blanching the cured bulbs in hot water makes peeling far easier.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; needs a long season to mature.
directions: This Pennsylvania heirloom is a long-necked butternut type with rich, smooth flesh and excellent keeping quality. The elongated necks are nearly solid flesh, ideal for roasting and soups. Give the vigorous 6 to 8 foot vines full sun and plenty of room to sprawl. Cure harvested fruit in a warm, dry spot to deepen flavor and improve storage life.

plant_note: Direct sow at last frost date or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier; seeds require light to germinate so press onto the soil surface without covering.
directions: Press seeds onto warm, moist soil surface without covering, as light aids germination; cold stratification in the refrigerator for one week before sowing improves germination rates. Perilla is a heat-loving herb-vegetable used extensively in Japanese and Korean cuisines — red types as a colorant and flavoring, green types as a wrap and garnish. Harvest individual large leaves from the base of the plant upward, or cut the whole plant back by half to promote bushy regrowth. Pinch flower spikes as they emerge to extend the leaf harvest and prevent aggressive self-seeding.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; the biennial keeps producing into fall.
directions: Perpetual spinach is actually a leaf chard grown for tender, mild leaves that eat like true spinach but far more heat- and bolt-resistant. As a cut-and-come-again crop it produces for months, and being biennial it can carry over for a second season in mild areas. Direct sow in spring in full sun to partial shade and keep evenly watered. Harvest outer leaves regularly and the plant will keep pushing out new growth well into fall.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost; keeps producing through the season.
directions: Despite its name this thin-ribbed Italian heirloom is a Swiss chard grown for its tender, spinach-like leaves, and it is slow to bolt in hot weather. Sow directly and thin seedlings, then harvest outer leaves regularly so the plant keeps producing new growth for months. Keep the soil evenly moist and feed occasionally for lush leaves. It tolerates both spring and fall conditions and mild frost.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–6 weeks after last frost; in zone 9–11 pigeon pea grows as a woody short-lived perennial and may produce for 3–5 years before replanting.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in well-drained, low-fertility soil; pigeon pea is a deep-rooted drought tolerant shrub that fixes nitrogen and does not require fertilization. It grows into a woody perennial in frost-free zones and produces pods over an extended season rather than all at once. Harvest green pods when plump for fresh shelling or allow to dry on the plant for dried dal. Prune back to 2–3 ft after the main harvest season to stimulate a second flush of growth and pods.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and transplant to fertile, well-drained garden soil once the risk of frost has fully passed. The heart-shaped, thick-walled fruits ripen from green to deep red and are sweeter and more aromatic than most bell peppers, with a rich, almost spicy fragrance that is distinct to the type. Harvest at full red for fresh use, or roast and peel for preserving in oil — pimento is the classic stuffing for green olives. High yields from compact plants make this a productive choice for containers or small beds.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This high-yielding beefsteak ripens to yellow-orange marbled with red streaks running through the meaty, low-acid flesh. Being indeterminate, the vines grow tall and need strong staking or caging. Start seeds indoors, transplant into warm soil, and water consistently to reduce cracking on the large fruit. Prune suckers lightly to boost airflow and support ripening of these big tomatoes.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after all frost danger.
directions: Pink Banana is a large heirloom winter squash producing elongated, pink-skinned fruits with sweet, tender orange flesh on vigorous vines that can run fifteen feet. The big fruits store well for months in a cool, dry place. Direct sow into warm, rich soil in full sun after frost, giving the sprawling vines plenty of space. Harvest when the rind hardens and skin turns pink, then cure the squash before storing.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; needs a long warm season.
directions: This heirloom banana-type squash grows 18 to 24 inches long with smooth, velvety skin and sweet flesh good for side dishes and pies. Give the sprawling vines full sun and plenty of space, and sow in warm soil after frost. Water steadily while fruit sizes up, then reduce as the rind hardens. Cure harvested squash in a warm, dry place to sweeten the flesh and extend storage.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; tolerates heat and drier conditions.
directions: These compact 2 foot cowpea plants need no staking and produce prolifically even in drier conditions than most beans. Also called crowder peas, they are nutritious, high in fiber and protein, and pair well with cooked greens. Sow directly in warm soil after frost and give them full sun. Harvest pods when the peas have filled out, shelling for fresh use or leaving to dry for storage.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; grow to full maturity for dry beans.
directions: Pinto is a heat- and drought-tolerant bush bean grown as a dry bean, producing pods of speckled beans on upright plants with short runners. Direct sow after frost into warm soil in full sun, and let the pods dry fully on the plant. Water moderately during flowering and pod set, then ease off as pods mature. Harvest when pods are brown and brittle, then shell and dry the beans further for storage.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and provide bottom heat around 80°F to speed germination. Transplant to fertile, well-drained soil once nights are consistently above 55°F. Water deeply but infrequently to encourage deep rooting and reduce disease pressure. Harvest dark green fruits at 4–5 inches long for fresh use, or allow to turn red and dry them for ancho chiles.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil once frost has passed.
directions: This dependable slicing cucumber yields dark green, straight 7 to 8 inch fruits on plants that resist mildews, anthracnose, scab, and angular leaf spot. Its disease package makes it reliable in humid climates. Sow in warm soil and trellis the vines to keep cucumbers straight and clean. Water consistently to avoid bitterness, and pick often to keep production going through a long season.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; erect a trellis or pole structure before sowing to avoid disturbing roots later.
directions: Plant seeds 1 in deep at the base of a sturdy trellis or teepee of poles set 6–8 ft tall, spacing 3 seeds per pole. Vines twine naturally but may need early guidance toward supports. Harvest pods before seeds bulge prominently for the tenderest texture. Pole beans produce over a longer season than bush types, so keep picking to prevent pod maturation from stopping new flower set.

plant_note: Direct sow at or up to 2 weeks after last frost once soil reaches at least 60°F. Isolate from sweet corn by at least 250 feet or time plantings to avoid simultaneous silking.
directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in blocks of at least 4 rows for proper wind pollination, isolating from sweet corn to prevent cross-pollination that ruins eating quality. Side-dress with nitrogen at knee-height and again when tassels appear. Leave ears on the stalk past eating stage until husks are completely brown and papery; pull husks back and hang ears in a dry, ventilated spot for 4–6 weeks until kernels release from the cob easily. Kernels are ready to pop when moisture content drops to around 14%.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Bred by Wild Boar Farms, this true-yellow beefsteak has a sweet flavor with citrus notes and slightly flattened 8 to 12 ounce fruits. Being indeterminate, the vines grow tall and benefit from sturdy staking. Start seeds indoors, transplant into warm soil, and water evenly to prevent cracking. Let fruit ripen fully to yellow for the sweetest flavor, picking regularly to sustain production.

plant_note: Plant seed pieces 2–4 weeks before last frost when soil reaches 45°F; in warm climates a second planting is made in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Plant certified seed potatoes cut to egg-sized pieces (each with 1–2 eyes) 4 in deep in trenches spaced 30 in apart. Hill soil up around the stems as they grow, keeping tubers covered to prevent greening from light exposure. Water consistently and watch for late blight in humid weather — infected foliage should be removed promptly to protect tubers. Harvest new potatoes when plants flower, or cure full-season types for a week at room temperature before long-term storage.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall 4–6 weeks before hard freeze in zones 5–9; in zones 3–4, plant as early as possible in spring.
directions: Plant individual bulbs 1 inch deep in well-drained, fertile soil — large bulbs planted in fall will multiply into a cluster of 8–12 medium onions, while small bulbs tend to produce fewer but larger bulbs. Potato onions are a multiplier onion type that propagates entirely by bulb division, forming a cluster of bulbs in the same way shallots do; no seed is needed or typically produced. Allow tops to fall over and begin to yellow before harvesting the entire cluster; cure in a warm, airy spot for 2–3 weeks. Save the smallest bulbs from the cluster for replanting, perpetuating the variety indefinitely without seed purchases.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; also sow in late summer for fall and winter harvest.
directions: This blend mixes Dwarf Blue Curled, Dwarf Siberian Improved, Lacinato, and Red Russian kale for a range of colors, textures, and flavors. Harvest baby greens in about 25 days or let plants size up for full leaves. Sow directly in cool soil and thin as they grow, picking outer leaves so plants keep producing. Frost sweetens the flavor, making it a great fall and winter crop.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; sow again in late summer for fall.
directions: This heirloom leaf lettuce dating to at least 1881 forms large, loose green leaves with bronze-red margins and an upright habit. Slow to bolt with good heat tolerance, it offers a tender texture and mild sweet flavor. Sow shallowly in cool soil and keep evenly moist. Harvest outer leaves as a cut-and-come-again crop, and shade lightly in summer heat to prolong the harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as soon as it can be worked.
directions: This heavy-producing heirloom shelling pea, also called Laxton's Progress #9, fills each 5 inch pod with 7 to 9 plump, sweet peas. Sow directly in cool spring soil and keep evenly moist for good pod fill. The compact vines need little support. Harvest when pods are rounded and full but still bright green, shelling promptly for the freshest flavor.

plant_note: Sow after last frost; proso millet is the shortest-season grain available to home gardeners, maturing in as little as 60 days.
directions: Drill or broadcast seed 1/2 in deep at roughly 20–25 lb per acre in home-scale rows and thin to 4–6 in; proso millet germinates quickly in warm soil and produces an open panicle of seed in just 60 days, making it practical even in short-season gardens above zone 4. Harvest entire stalks when the lower seeds are hard and the panicle begins to droop; hang bundles upside-down over a tarp for 2 weeks to finish drying. Thresh by beating bundles against the inside of a barrel and winnow outdoors; the hull is loose and comes free with light rubbing in warm water. Proso millet is gluten-free and has the lowest water requirement of any grain crop.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; tolerant of cool soil for early sowings.
directions: Provider is an early, dependable bush snap bean valued for germinating well in cooler soil and adapting to a wide range of climates. It sets straight, stringless, tender pods with good flavor in a heavy early flush. Direct sow after frost, or slightly earlier than most beans since it tolerates cool soil, in full sun. Pick pods young and often to keep the plants producing over several weeks.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; germinates in cooler soil than most beans.
directions: This early, high-yielding bush bean produces straight, stringless pods and resists bean common mosaic virus, pod mottle virus, and mildew. It germinates in cooler soil than other beans, so it can go out a bit earlier. Sow directly where plants will grow, since beans dislike transplanting. Pick pods young and slender, harvesting often to keep the plants setting new beans.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost; time planting to mature before the first fall frost.
directions: Sow 3–4 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in deeply worked, compost-rich soil once soil temperature is at least 65°F, then thin to the 2 strongest seedlings. Large-vined types need 6–10 feet of space per plant; choose bush types for smaller gardens. Place a piece of cardboard or straw under developing fruits to prevent rot. Harvest when the shell is fully orange, the rind is hard throughout, and the stem has begun to dry and cork.

common_name: Pumpkin on a Stick Ornamental Eggplant
plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil warms.
directions: This 1800s ornamental eggplant produces clusters of small, ribbed fruits that look like tiny pumpkins, prized for fresh and dried fall arrangements. Grow it like eggplant, starting seeds indoors early and transplanting into warm, sunny soil. The fruits ripen from green to orange-red on sturdy branching plants. Cut stems when the little fruits color up, and hang to dry for lasting decorations.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after nights stay warm.
directions: Purple Beauty is a striking heirloom bell pepper with thick-walled, four-inch fruits that ripen from green to a deep glossy purple, then to red if left longer. The crisp, mild flesh is as ornamental as it is tasty. Start seed indoors well before the last frost and transplant into full sun once nights are warm. The compact plants suit containers; harvest at the purple stage for color or leave to sweeten fully.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil warms.
directions: This bell pepper shifts from creamy lime to deep purple and finally red, with a contrasting lime-green interior that is both ornamental and tasty. The compact, very early plants adapt to many climates. Start seeds indoors and transplant into warm soil in full sun. Pick peppers at the purple stage for color or let them redden for sweetness, and water evenly to prevent blossom-end rot.

plant_note: Keep seedbed constantly moist during slow germination.
directions: These anthocyanin-rich carrots range from purple skin with orange cores to solid purple throughout. Sow the fine seed a quarter inch deep in deep, loose, stone-free soil and never let the surface dry during germination. Thin seedlings to 2 to 3 inches so roots develop without forking or crowding. Purple pigment intensifies in cool weather, so fall crops often show the deepest color. Harvest when shoulders reach three-quarters of an inch and store in cool, humid conditions.

plant_note: Consistent cool weather prevents buttoning; avoid heat stress.
directions: The vivid purple curds come from anthocyanins and do not require blanching like white types. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before transplanting and set plants out in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Keep growth steady and unchecked, since any stress from heat, drought, or transplant shock can cause premature small heads. Harvest while the head is still tight and dome-shaped, before the curds begin to separate. The color softens somewhat when cooked but stays vibrant if steamed briefly.

plant_note: Sow 2 weeks after last frost into warm soil; a quintessential Gulf Coast staple that thrives in heat and humidity that discourage northern legumes.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep directly into warm, well-drained soil — purple hull peas rot in cold, wet ground, so wait until soil consistently exceeds 65°F. Inoculate seed with cowpea rhizobium for maximum nitrogen fixation and avoid excess fertilizer, which promotes foliage over pods. Harvest pods when the purple color intensifies and the peas feel plump inside, shelling immediately or refrigerating; over-mature pods turn tan and the peas starch up quickly. Succession-plant every 3 weeks through summer for a continuous harvest; a second planting in late July produces a heavy fall crop in the Gulf South.

plant_note: Sprout indoors year-round in a jar or tray; no field planting needed.
directions: These purple kohlrabi sprouts add eye-popping color and crunch to salads and sandwiches with a classic brassica flavor and spicy note. Sprout the seeds indoors in a jar or shallow tray, rinsing and draining two to three times daily. They are ready to eat in four to seven days once the shoots stand an inch or so tall with tiny leaves. Keep them moist but drained to avoid mold.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer; succession sow.
directions: Purple Plum is an eye-catching heirloom radish with deep purple skin and crisp, mild white flesh in round roots about an inch and a half across. It grows fast in cool weather and holds its quality well without turning pithy. Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer, thinning to give roots room to swell. Keep soil evenly moist for the crispest, mildest radishes, and succession sow every couple of weeks.

plant_note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked in spring; succession sow every 1-2 weeks.
directions: Direct sow 'Purple Plum' seeds 1/2 inch deep and thin seedlings to about 1-2 inches apart for well-formed, violet-skinned roots. This fast-maturing radish is ready in roughly a month and stays crisp and mild even as bulbs enlarge. Keep soil evenly moist to prevent pithiness and cracking, and harvest promptly once roots reach about 1 inch across. Tolerant of cool weather and containers, it thrives in full sun to partial shade.

plant_note: Overwintering type; sow midsummer for spring harvest in mild zones.
directions: This hardy overwintering broccoli produces many small purple spears in early spring rather than a single central head. Start seed indoors in mid to late summer and transplant in early fall so plants establish before cold sets in. Provide steady moisture and rich soil, and mulch heavily to protect crowns through winter in zones 6 and 7. Harvest spears when buds are tight and cut regularly to encourage continued side-shoot production. Exposure to winter chill is what triggers the abundant spring flush.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: Start purple tomatillo seeds indoors 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant into warm soil in full sun. Plant at least two seedlings to ensure cross-pollination, since tomatillos are largely self-incompatible. The sprawling plants benefit from staking or caging; harvest fruit when the papery husk splits open and the berry turns deep violet for the sweetest flavor. Keep watering consistent through fruit set to encourage a heavy crop.

plant_note: Sow in mid summer for a fall harvest; flavor sweetens after frost.
directions: American Purple Top rutabaga produces large five- to six-inch roots with purple shoulders and smooth ivory flesh that is sweet, mild, and fine-textured. Grown as a fall crop, it stores exceptionally well for winter use in mashes and stews. Direct sow in mid summer so roots mature in cool weather, and thin generously to size up. A light frost sweetens the roots; harvest before the ground hard-freezes and store in a cool, humid place.

plant_note: Sow in late summer for fall roots, or early spring for a quick crop.
directions: Purple Top is the classic heirloom turnip, forming smooth white roots with bright purple shoulders plus tender, nutritious greens. Both roots and tops are edible, giving a dual harvest from one planting. Direct sow in early spring for a fast crop or in late summer for sweeter fall roots, thinning to give roots room. Keep soil evenly moist and harvest roots young at two to three inches for the mildest flavor.

plant_note: Sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall harvest.
directions: Direct sow 'Purple Top White Globe' 1/2 inch deep and thin to 3-4 inches apart for smooth, globe-shaped roots with purple shoulders and white bottoms. This classic heirloom is dual-purpose: harvest young greens for cooking and pull roots at about 3 inches across before they turn woody. Keep soil consistently moist for tender, sweet roots, and grow in cool spring or fall weather. Turnips store well in a cool, humid place through winter.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall.
directions: Purple Vienna is a cool-season heirloom kohlrabi forming deep purple bulbs above the soil with crisp, tender, mildly sweet flesh similar to a turnip. Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer, thinning to give the swelling stems room. Keep growth steady with even moisture, since stress can make the bulbs woody. Harvest when bulbs reach two to three inches across for the best texture, before they turn fibrous.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or in mid to late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Sow 'Purple Vienna' kohlrabi 1/4 inch deep and thin to 4-6 inches apart so the purple, edible swollen stems develop cleanly. The bulbs are best harvested at 2-3 inches in diameter while crisp and sweet; larger bulbs turn tough and fibrous. Grow in the cool weather of spring or fall in full sun, keeping moisture even to avoid cracking. Both the bulb and young leaves are edible raw or cooked.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost onto the soil surface; needs warmth and light.
directions: Purslane is a heat-loving, drought-tolerant green with succulent leaves rich in omega-3 fatty acids and a bright, lemony flavor. The low, spreading plants make an edible ground cover and thrive in hot, dry conditions where other greens fail. Direct sow after frost onto the soil surface, pressing seed in lightly since it needs warmth and light to germinate. Pinch tips regularly to keep leaves tender and encourage bushy, continuous growth.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; germination is erratic in cool soil below 65°F.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds or press them 1/8 inch into warm, well-drained soil; do not overwater as seeds rot easily in cool, wet conditions. Golden or green cultivated purslane is much larger and less bitter than its weedy relative, with thick, succulent stems and paddle-shaped leaves. Harvest stem tips 5–6 inches long when plants are lush and before flower buds form; the plant regrows readily after cutting. Purslane is one of the few plant sources of omega-3 fatty acids and is excellent raw in salads or lightly sautéed.

plant_note: Start indoors or sow directly once soil is warm; well suited to indoor container growing year-round.
directions: 'Quick Snack' is a compact, parthenocarpic cucumber bred for 6-8 inch pots on a sunny windowsill, counter, or balcony. Because it sets fruit without pollination, it produces well indoors with no bees needed; keep it in bright light and evenly moist soil. Harvest the smooth, dark green fruits young at 1.5-2.5 inches long for the best sweet, crisp flavor and to keep the plant producing. Feed lightly and provide a small trellis or let vines trail over the pot rim.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost date in spring; quinoa bolts in midsummer heat, so timing for a cool finish is critical in most climates.
directions: Broadcast or sow seed 1/4 in deep in firm, well-drained soil and thin to 12–18 in when seedlings are 3–4 in tall; quinoa is highly drought-tolerant once established and prefers cool, dry weather through seed fill. The colorful seed heads (red, orange, or cream) are ready to harvest when the seed falls easily from the panicle when rubbed — typically after the first light fall frost has dried the plant. Cut the seed heads into paper bags, dry for 1–2 weeks in a warm airy space, then thresh by rubbing heads between hands and winnow away the chaff outdoors in a breeze. Rinse seed thoroughly before eating to remove bitter saponins from the seed coat.

plant_note: For tight, colorful heads, sow in midsummer so radicchio heads up during cool fall weather, 10–14 weeks before first frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep and thin to 10–14 in; radicchio produces loose, green summer foliage before forming its characteristic red and white tight head as days shorten and temperatures fall. Cut back summer foliage to about 1 in when plants look tired and cool temperatures arrive; new heads will form from the crown within 3–4 weeks. Harvest compact heads by cutting at the base once they feel firm and the color is deep burgundy. Hard frost may damage outer leaves but the inner head often survives.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; succession sow every 10 days for continuous harvest, stopping when summer heat sets in.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 in deep in cool, well-drained soil at 10-day intervals for a steady supply. Radishes bolt quickly in heat or long days, so spring and fall are the prime windows. Thin to 2 in as soon as seedlings are up to prevent overcrowding, which causes lanky tops and small or misshapen roots. Pull spring types at 3/4 to 1 in diameter before they become hot and pithy.

plant_note: Among the fastest microgreens; sow densely and blackout only one to two days.
directions: Radish is one of the fastest and most forgiving microgreens, germinating within a day or two of dense sowing on a moist tray. Keep the blackout period short, then give bright light to develop sturdy stems and, in some varieties, a pink or purple blush. Mist regularly and provide airflow to keep the vigorous growth clean. Harvest the crisp, boldly peppery shoots in about a week at the cotyledon stage.

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after frost danger passes.
directions: This color-coded blend produces cherry tomatoes in orange, yellow, brown, red, and green, mixing mostly indeterminate pole types with one determinate. Start seeds indoors about 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply into warm soil in full sun. Provide sturdy stakes or tall cages for the vining plants and pinch suckers to keep them manageable. Harvest the sweet, bite-sized fruits as each color ripens for a colorful, all-summer supply.

plant_note: Sow directly in loose, deep soil; carrots resent transplanting.
directions: This F1 hybrid produces white, yellow, orange, and coral roots from a single sowing, each 7-9 inches long, uniform and conical. Direct sow 1/4 inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the surface consistently moist during the slow germination period. Thin seedlings to 2-3 inches apart so roots size up evenly, and harvest this early-season Nantes x Imperator type once shoulders reach full color. Cool weather sweetens the flavor.

plant_note: Sow in early spring once soil is workable; a second sowing in late summer extends fall harvests.
directions: Soak the corky seed clusters overnight to speed germination, then sow half an inch deep directly in the garden. Thin seedlings to 6-12 inches, since each cluster can produce several plants. The vivid red, pink, yellow, orange, and white stems hold their color best in full sun with steady moisture. Harvest outer leaves regularly on a cut-and-come-again basis and the plant will keep pushing new growth well into fall.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed immediately after collecting in late summer, or purchase bulbs and transplant in fall; seed requires a warm period followed by cold stratification and will not germinate until the second spring. Growing from bulbs is far faster, yielding harvestable plants in 3–5 years vs. 5–7 from seed.
directions: Plant in deep, rich, moist, humus-laden soil under deciduous hardwoods; ramps are spring ephemerals of the eastern North American forest floor, emerging early when trees are still bare before going fully dormant by midsummer. Both the broad, elliptic leaves and the pungent garlicky-oniony bulbs are edible and among the most sought-after wild foods in Appalachian cuisine. Harvest no more than one leaf per plant, leaving the bulb to regenerate; never harvest more than 10% of any patch to prevent depletion. Plants are notoriously slow from seed — 5–7 years to first harvest — but once established form long-lived, self-seeding colonies. Purchase ethically sourced bulbs and replant promptly in autumn.

plant_note: Transplant divisions or plant fresh seed in fall under deciduous hardwood canopy; ramps are woodland ephemerals that emerge in early spring and die back by early summer.
directions: Plant bulb divisions 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich woodland soil under deciduous trees that provide deep summer shade; ramps are native North American woodland plants and require the ephemeral light window of early spring before tree leaf-out. Seed germinates slowly and erratically, often taking 18 months for full emergence after a double dormancy period requiring warm then cold stratification; vegetative division of established clumps is the most reliable propagation method. Harvest sparingly — take no more than one leaf per plant or one bulb in three, leaving the colony to regenerate — as over-harvesting wild populations is a significant ecological concern. The entire plant (broad flat leaves, small white bulb, and stems) carries a strong, complex garlic-onion flavor and is celebrated as a spring delicacy across Appalachia.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer; heat causes premature bolting.
directions: Rapini, or broccoli raab, is grown for its tender stems, leaves, and small budding florets with a pleasantly bitter, peppery flavor. Direct sow 1/4 inch deep and thin to 4-6 inches apart, growing in the cool weather of spring or fall to avoid rapid bolting. Harvest the whole plant or cut budding shoots just before the small yellow flowers open, when flavor is best. Fast to mature, it can be succession sown for repeat harvests.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost; tolerates summer heat better than root radishes.
directions: Unlike ordinary radishes, 'Rat's Tail' is grown for its abundant slender, edible seed pods borne above ground on tall 2-5 foot plants. Direct sow 1/2 inch deep, thin to 6-12 inches apart, and give the plants room and support as they grow large. Harvest the crisp, spicy pods young at 3-5 inches long while still tender, picking often to keep new pods forming. Heat tolerant, it produces through summer when other radishes bolt.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Set up a sturdy trellis, pole, or teepee before sowing, as these vigorous vines climb 6-10 feet. Plant seeds one inch deep once soil is reliably warm, spacing them a few inches apart at the base of each support. The heat-tolerant, drought-resistant vines bear green pods streaked with purple that fade when cooked. Pick pods young for tender snap beans, or leave them to dry on the vine for storage as the plump beans mature.

common_name: Red & Yellow Pear Blend Pole Cherry Tomato
plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: This heirloom blend combines 'Red Pear' and 'Yellow Pear', indeterminate vines bearing small, firm, pear-shaped fruit with sweet, mild, low-acid flavor. Start seeds indoors about 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply into warm, full-sun beds. The vigorous vines need tall stakes or cages and regular pinching of suckers. Harvest the bite-sized fruits all summer for snacking, salads, pickling, or preserves.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant a few weeks before the last frost; also sows well in mid-summer for a fall crop.
directions: Start seeds indoors six weeks before setting out, then transplant hardened seedlings into rich, well-drained soil. This compact heirloom forms dense, deep-purple heads around 3-4 pounds and tolerates cold well, making it a good choice for tight spaces and raised beds. Keep moisture even to prevent the round heads from splitting as they firm up. Harvest when heads feel solid, and enjoy the crisp, colorful leaves fresh, in slaws, or lightly cooked.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant a few weeks before the last frost, or sow in summer for fall.
directions: 'Red Acre' forms solid, deep-red heads early on compact plants, making it a strong pick for small gardens and containers. Start seeds indoors about 6 weeks before setting out, then transplant into rich, full-sun soil spaced 12-18 inches apart. Keep moisture steady to prevent splitting and harvest heads once firm and about 5-7 inches across. This cool-season variety offers good storage and resistance to yellows.

plant_note: Tiny warmth-loving seed; do not presoak, sow on the surface of moist medium.
directions: Scatter the very fine amaranth seed on the surface of a moist tray without burying it, and keep the medium warm to trigger germination. It grows more slowly than brassicas and prefers extra heat, so be patient during the first week. Mist gently to keep the delicate seedlings from drying out while avoiding standing water. Harvest the vivid crimson, mild-sweet microgreens once the cotyledons color up, usually around two weeks, for an elegant gourmet garnish.

plant_note: Start indoors early; this is an intermediate-day onion best for mid latitudes.
directions: 'Red Amposta' is an intermediate-day onion producing large, reddish-purple globes with sweet, snappy flavor and attractive red-and-white rings. Start seeds indoors 10-12 weeks before the last frost, then transplant seedlings 3-4 inches apart in fertile, full-sun soil. Keep the shallow-rooted plants weed-free and evenly watered until the tops begin to fall over. Cure bulbs in a dry, airy spot before storing.

plant_note: Start indoors well before the last frost and transplant once nights stay warm.
directions: Sow seeds indoors about six weeks before your last frost, then transplant deeply, burying much of the stem to encourage strong roots. These indeterminate Amish heirloom vines are large and sprawling, so provide tall stakes or heavy cages for support. The rosy-red, deeply lobed beefsteak fruits are sweet, low-acid, and best eaten fresh in slices. Water consistently and mulch to reduce cracking and blossom-end rot on the big fruits.

plant_note: Time transplants so sprouts mature in cool fall weather; a light frost sweetens the flavor.
directions: Start seeds indoors and transplant into firm, fertile soil, giving each plant plenty of room and support in windy sites. This striking heirloom produces purple-red sprouts along tall stalks with a nutty-sweet flavor that deepens after frost. The long season means patience is required, so plan the planting date backward from your first fall frost. Harvest sprouts from the bottom up as they firm to about an inch, and the color intensifies with cold weather.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil a few weeks after the last frost; soaking seed overnight speeds sprouting.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and sow one inch deep only after soil is thoroughly warm, since okra hates cold. This 1988 All-America Selections winner grows into tall five-foot plants with deep-red stems and burgundy pods that add ornamental appeal. Harvest pods young, at about three to four inches, snipping every day or two to keep the plants productive and the pods tender. The rich color can fade with cooking but the plants remain handsome all season.

plant_note: Start indoors or direct sow once soil is thoroughly warm.
directions: 'Red Burgundy' is an ornamental yet productive okra with deep crimson pods, red stems, and yellow flowers loved by bees. Soak seeds overnight before sowing into warm soil, and give plants full sun and warmth to thrive. Harvest the tender pods frequently at about 6 inches long, since they toughen quickly and regular picking keeps the plant producing. The pods are excellent in soups, gumbo, stews, or fried.

plant_note: Short-day type best for southern gardeners; start early and transplant while cool.
directions: Start seeds indoors two to three months before transplanting, as onions need a long lead time. This short-day variety forms bulbs when days reach 10-12 hours, so it suits southern and mild-winter gardens best. Set transplants a few inches apart in loose, fertile soil and keep them weed-free and evenly watered. The glossy red bulbs reach about four inches with sweet, mild white flesh ringed in pink, and are best used fresh rather than for long storage.

plant_note: Small legume seed grown like alfalfa; sow densely on moist trays or as sprouts.
directions: Sow the small clover seed densely and evenly across a moist tray, or grow it in a sprouting jar with regular rinsing. It germinates quickly and grows fine, upright stems, so uncover early and provide bright light. Mist frequently to keep the delicate mat hydrated without souring. Harvest the mild, fresh-tasting green shoots after about a week to ten days at the cotyledon stage.

plant_note: Grown indoors as sprouts in a jar year-round, not planted in the garden.
directions: Red clover sprouts are grown indoors in a jar or sprouting tray, offering a crisp, mild flavor and easy-to-hull seed coats similar to alfalfa. Soak seeds several hours, then rinse and drain two to three times daily in indirect light. Harvest in about 5-7 days once the sprouts show tiny green leaves, then refrigerate. They add crunch and nutrition to sandwiches, salads, and green drinks.

plant_note: Direct sow; well suited to heavy or shallow soils where longer carrots fail.
directions: 'Red Cored Chantenay' produces thick, blunt, 6-inch roots that reliably size up even in heavy or shallow soils where longer carrots struggle. Direct sow 1/4 inch deep and keep the seedbed moist through the slow germination, then thin to 2-3 inches apart. The heavy-yielding, split-resistant roots are excellent for juicing, canning, freezing, and cooking, and they store well. Cool weather deepens their sweetness.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer; hot weather intensifies pungency and prompts bolting.
directions: 'Red Giant' bears big, bold maroon leaves with lime-green veins that are as ornamental as they are tasty in the garden or a container. Direct sow 1/4 inch deep and thin to 6-12 inches apart, or harvest crowded plants young as baby greens. The mustardy, horseradish-like flavor sharpens in older leaves and hot weather, so pick young for milder greens. Grow in cool spring or fall weather for the best texture.

plant_note: Start indoors with bottom heat well ahead of the last frost; this crop needs a long warm season.
directions: Start seeds indoors early with consistent warmth, since habaneros germinate slowly and need a long, hot season to ripen. As tender perennials they can be grown year-round outdoors only in frost-free zones. Transplant into full sun after nights stay warm, and support the branching plants as the crimson pods develop. These are among the hottest habaneros with a fruity flavor, so handle the ripe red pods with gloves when harvesting for salsa and hot sauce.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors with heat mats; transplant only when soil and nights are warm.
directions: Sow seeds indoors eight or more weeks before transplanting, using bottom heat to coax the slow-germinating seeds along. This heirloom Capsicum chinense produces bright red, extremely hot, fruity pods perfect for salsas and Caribbean cooking. Move plants outdoors only after the weather is settled and warm, spacing them for good air circulation. Ripen fruits fully to deep red for maximum heat and flavor, wearing gloves whenever you handle them.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil warms to 65°F; in short-season areas, start indoors 3 weeks before transplanting.
directions: Sow 2–3 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in compost-enriched soil and thin to one or two vines per hill once established. Red Kuri is a teardrop-shaped Cucurbita maxima with vivid orange-red skin and nutty, chestnut-flavored, dry flesh that is among the finest of all winter squash. Harvest when skin is deep orange-red and hard; the stems will be beginning to dry and the skin will resist a thumbnail. Cure for 10–14 days at room temperature before storing; fruits keep well for three to four months under cool, dry conditions.

plant_note: Direct sow once soil is warm, or start indoors 3 weeks early.
directions: 'Red Kuri', also called Orange Hokkaido, yields teardrop-shaped 3-4 pound fruits with smooth, sweet, chestnut-like flesh. Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil and give the vigorous vines plenty of room to sprawl in full sun. Keep watering steady while fruit develops, then cure harvested squash to build sweetness and storage life. Cut fruit once the rind hardens and the stem dries, leaving a stub of stem attached.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil is consistently warm; the heat-season replacement for spinach in tropical and subtropical gardens.
directions: Scatter seeds thinly on the soil surface — they need light to germinate — then press gently and keep moist; thin seedlings to 6 inches apart, using thinnings as baby greens. Leaves are brilliantly colored in shades of red, purple, and green and are mildly flavored, used in stir-fries, soups, and as a cooked green throughout South and Southeast Asia. Harvest outer leaves or cut entire young plants at 6–8 inches tall to encourage regrowth; plants that are allowed to flower and set seed will readily self-sow for the following year. This species thrives in the same hot, humid conditions that cause true spinach to bolt immediately.

plant_note: Direct sow 3–6 weeks before last frost for spring; sow again 6–8 weeks before first fall frost for the best-flavored autumn harvest.
directions: Scatter seeds thinly and cover with 1/4 inch of soil in a cool, moist seedbed; germination is rapid in cool conditions. Red mizuna has the same deeply-cut, feathery leaf form as green mizuna but with vivid burgundy-red stems and leaf coloring that intensifies in cool weather, making it as ornamental as it is edible. Harvest outer leaves continuously once plants reach 4 inches, or cut the entire plant 1 inch above the base to allow regrowth; flavor is peppery and nutty, similar to green mizuna but with slightly more bite. Fall sowings consistently outperform spring plantings in flavor and color intensity as frost sweetens the leaves.

plant_note: These yardlong beans crave heat; direct sow into warm soil well after frost.
directions: Provide a tall, sturdy trellis before sowing, as these vigorous yardlong vines climb high and love the heat. Direct sow one inch deep once soil is warm, and give the plants full sun for the best production. The striking burgundy pods grow 16-22 inches long and are stringless and tender when picked young. Harvest often while pods are pencil-thin to keep the vines cropping through the warm months.

plant_note: Sow only after soil has warmed thoroughly; cold soil rots seed.
directions: Provide a sturdy 7-8 ft trellis before sowing, as vines climb aggressively and produce deep burgundy pods up to 18 inches long. Direct sow two weeks after last frost when soil reaches at least 70 F, planting seeds 1 inch deep. Harvest pods young and tender at pencil thickness for best flavor and to keep plants productive. Pick every two to three days during peak season to prevent pods from becoming fibrous. Feed lightly with a balanced fertilizer since heavy nitrogen reduces pod set.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant near the last frost, or sow in summer for a fall crop.
directions: 'Red Power' forms uniform, 1.5-2 pound pointed heads of tightly wrapped magenta leaves on a compact, upright plant ideal for small gardens. Start seeds indoors about 6 weeks before setting out and transplant into rich, full-sun soil. Keep moisture even to prevent splitting and harvest once heads feel firm. The sweet, mild flavor and striking color make it excellent raw in salads, stir-fried, or braised.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: 'Red Pride' is a determinate bush tomato that yields a heavy set of firm, 10-ounce red fruits on vigorous, compact plants. Start seeds indoors about 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply into warm, full-sun soil. Its strong disease-resistance package covers Alternaria stem canker, gray leaf spot, Fusarium wilt, and Verticillium wilt, making it low-fuss. A short cage or stake supports the concentrated fruit load; harvest when fully red.

plant_note: Sow in early spring and again in late summer; hot weather encourages bolting.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, barely covering them, since lettuce needs light to germinate and prefers cool soil. This Cos-type heirloom forms upright heads of sturdy, crisp leaves in a rich burgundy that fades to pale green at the heart. Keep the soil evenly moist and provide some afternoon shade in warm regions to delay bolting. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut whole heads once they are full and firm.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer; flavor sweetens after fall frosts.
directions: Direct sow half an inch deep in cool soil, then thin seedlings to about a foot apart for full leaf development. This cold-hardy heirloom has flat, oak-leaf-shaped leaves with purple-red stems and a tender, mildly sweet flavor. Harvest outer leaves continuously and the plants keep producing tender greens over a long season. A touch of frost improves the sweetness, so it is an excellent overwintering green in milder zones.

plant_note: Tender enough for baby-leaf salad harvest as well as mature use.
directions: This oleracea-type kale has flat, frilly gray-green leaves with purple stems and is notably tender and sweet. Direct sow half an inch deep and thin to 12 inches, or harvest thinnings as baby greens. It tolerates both heat and cold better than curly kale, making it a good four-season crop. Snap off outer leaves as needed and the plant will keep pushing new growth from the center. A light frost improves its mild, sweet flavor.

plant_note: Sow thickly for cut-and-come-again baby greens; grows indoors year-round.
directions: Grown as baby greens, 'Red Russian' kale delivers tender, flat, purple-veined leaves loaded with vitamins A and C plus calcium and iron. Sow seeds thickly in shallow rows or trays and harvest by cutting leaves at about 3-4 inches tall, allowing regrowth for repeat cuttings. It grows quickly indoors any season or outdoors in cool weather, tolerating light frost. Snip young for salads or a quick saute.

plant_note: Sow in succession from early spring; heat tolerant enough to extend into warm weather.
directions: 'Red Sails' is a loose-leaf lettuce with soft, buttery, bronze-red leaves that resist bitterness even in warm weather. Sow seeds shallowly, barely covering them since light aids germination, and thin to 8-12 inches for full heads. Harvest baby greens in 2-3 weeks or full heads in about 6 weeks, or pick outer leaves for a continuous supply. Its heat tolerance and slow bolting make it a reliable choice for extended harvest.

plant_note: Sow successively from early spring; this heat-tolerant type resists bolting better than most.
directions: Scatter seeds thinly and press lightly into the soil surface, as they germinate best with light. This loose-leaf heirloom forms large open rosettes of frilly burgundy-red leaves with a crisp, mild flavor. It is notably heat-tolerant, so it holds longer than many lettuces before bolting in warm weather. Harvest leaves individually on a cut-and-come-again basis for a steady supply of baby or full-size greens.

plant_note: Long-day Italian type; best where summers have long daylight.
directions: This elongated torpedo-shaped Italian onion has a mild, sweet flavor ideal for fresh eating and grilling. Start indoors and transplant early, giving each bulb room to elongate in loose, fertile soil. Maintain even moisture through bulbing to develop full size, then taper off as tops lodge. Because it is a fresh-use onion, it stores only a few weeks compared with storage types. Harvest when most tops have fallen and cure briefly before use.

plant_note: Direct sow after the soil warms; needs a long, warm season.
directions: 'Red Warty Thing', descended from the 1897 heirloom 'Victor', bears bumpy, thick, red-orange 10-20 pound pumpkins with fine-textured, flavorful flesh. Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil and give the sprawling vines ample space in full sun. Water deeply and feed the heavy-feeding plants through the long season. The thick rind lets these fruits store for many months and doubles as striking fall decor.

common_name: Red and Yellow Blend Biquinho Chile Pepper
plant_note: Start indoors early with bottom heat; transplant well after frost.
directions: These small, teardrop 'little beak' Brazilian peppers are tangy, sweet, and smoky with only a whisper of heat, ripening from green to red or yellow. Start seeds indoors 8 weeks before the last frost with warm soil, as germination is slow, and transplant only when nights stay warm. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and light feeding; the compact plants do well in containers. Harvest fully colored pods for fresh eating, pickling, or drying.

plant_note: Start indoors or direct sow; also excellent as a fall crop for cold-sweetened leaves.
directions: 'Redbor' is a strikingly ruffled, deep purple kale that is both ornamental and delicious, with color and curl intensifying as temperatures drop. Start seeds indoors 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow, spacing plants 12-18 inches apart in full sun. Extremely cold hardy, plants sweeten in fall and can survive hard freezes for late-season harvest. Pick outer leaves as needed, letting the center keep producing.

plant_note: Plant divisions as soon as soil is workable in early spring; each division should have at least one large bud.
directions: Plant root divisions 3–4 in deep in deeply dug, compost-enriched soil, setting buds just at the soil surface. Do not harvest any stalks in the first year, and take only a few in year two so the crown can establish a deep root system. From year three onward, harvest stalks when they are 12–18 in long and 3/4 in thick by pulling and twisting at the base; remove any flower stalks that appear immediately to prevent weakening the crown. Note that only the stalks are edible—the leaves contain high concentrations of oxalic acid and are toxic.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 75°F; requires sustained heat to produce well.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in warm soil after last frost and provide a trellis or fence immediately, as vines climb vigorously by tendrils. Ridge gourd differs from smooth luffa in having ten prominent sharp ridges running the length of the fruit and slightly more tender flesh when young. Harvest fruits at 6–9 inches long while skin is still soft and the ridges are firm and green — the young flesh is mild, slightly sweet, and commonly used in Indian dal, stir-fries, and curries. Like smooth luffa, fruits left to fully mature develop a tough fiber skeleton and can serve as a scrubbing sponge.

plant_note: Sow in early spring and again in late summer; bred for baby-size harvest.
directions: 'Robin' was bred for harvesting at baby size, delivering well-rounded, tender, sweet 1 to 1.5 inch roots with high bolt resistance. Direct sow the seed clusters 1/2 inch deep and thin seedlings to 2-3 inches apart, since each cluster can produce several plants. Keep moisture even for crisp, crack-free roots, and harvest young for the best texture. The tender baby greens are also excellent raw or cooked.

plant_note: Direct sow in blocks after frost for good pollination; isolate from other corn.
directions: 'Robust Pop R400MR' produces large, round popped kernels sturdy enough to hold coatings, unlike the more common butterfly popcorn. Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil in blocks of at least four short rows to ensure good wind pollination. Give plants full sun, steady moisture, and rich soil through the long season. Let ears dry fully on the stalk before harvesting, then cure the kernels further before popping. Isolate from sweet corn to keep quality true.

plant_note: Sow in spring or fall; perennial in mild zones 6-10.
directions: 'Rocky' is a fast-growing wild-type arugula forming a compact rosette with a stronger, more complex, nutty-peppery flavor than salad arugula. Direct sow shallowly and thin to 4-6 inches, harvesting leaves young and often for the mildest taste. It tolerates cold well and behaves as a short-lived perennial in USDA zones 6-10, resprouting after cutting. Grow in cool weather and shear back before flowering to prolong tender leaf production.

plant_note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost; unlike most peppers, rocoto prefers cool nights and actually performs poorly in intense summer heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep at a cooler 65–70°F — unlike other peppers, Capsicum pubescens actually germinates better in cooler soil and is damaged by excessive heat. This Andean species is unique among cultivated peppers for its purple flowers and distinctive black seeds; it tolerates light frost better than any other pepper species. Transplant to a position with afternoon shade in hot climates, as rocoto stops setting fruit when temperatures exceed 90°F — coastal or mountain gardens suit it best. Fruits resemble small apples (hence 'manzano') and ripen to yellow, orange, or red with thick, sweet flesh and intense, burning heat.

plant_note: Direct sow after all frost danger once soil is warm.
directions: 'Roma II' is a bush-type Italian flat snap bean with a rich, nutty flavor rarely found in stores, borne on compact plants that need no support. Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil, spacing them 3-4 inches apart in full sun. The 4-5 inch pods stay tender and slow to develop fiber, so harvest regularly to keep plants producing. Excellent fresh, frozen, or canned; good in containers.

plant_note: Start indoors about six weeks before the last frost, then transplant once nights are warm.
directions: Sow indoors six weeks ahead of transplanting and set plants deep to build strong roots. Roma is a compact, determinate paste tomato producing firm, meaty three-inch fruits with few seeds, ideal for sauces and canning. A single stake or small cage supports the tidy vines, which set most of their fruit in a concentrated window. Water evenly and mulch to prevent blossom-end rot, and expect a heavy flush of fruit that ripens over a few weeks.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost; for fall, sow 8–10 weeks before first frost.
directions: Sow seed at 1/8 in depth in cool soil and thin to 8–12 in; romaine forms an upright, elongated head that needs slightly more space than loose-leaf types. Keep soil evenly moist to prevent tip burn, which appears as brown leaf edges due to calcium deficiency from moisture stress. Harvest by cutting the whole head at soil level when it is 8–10 in tall and the inner leaves feel firm. Outer leaves can be removed individually as the head develops to extend the harvest period.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant so heads mature in cool fall weather; aim to transplant about 16 weeks before first frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep indoors 6–8 weeks before transplant date; romanesco demands consistent temperatures between 60–65°F during head formation or it fails to produce proper fractal spirals. Transplant into deeply amended soil and water evenly throughout the season, as any moisture stress affects head quality. Harvest the single central head when the spiral turrets are sharp and well-defined but before any yellowing begins. Unlike broccoli, romanesco rarely produces usable side shoots after the main head is cut.

plant_note: Best grown for fall harvest so the heads mature in cool weather; also a spring crop in mild areas.
directions: Start seeds indoors and transplant into rich, consistently moist soil, giving each plant ample room to develop. This Italian heirloom forms mesmerizing lime-green heads of spiraling Fibonacci cones with a mild, nutty flavor. It grows slowly and prefers cool conditions, so time plantings so heads mature before summer heat or in the cool of fall. Harvest the whole head while the fractal spirals are tight and firm, cutting with several inches of stem.

plant_note: Start indoors; often best as a fall crop maturing in cool weather.
directions: 'Romanesco' produces mesmerizing chartreuse, fractal-spiraled 5-6 inch heads with a delicate, nutty flavor between broccoli and cauliflower. Start seeds indoors about 6 weeks before transplanting and set plants 18-24 inches apart in rich, full-sun soil. Steady moisture and cool weather are essential for well-formed heads, so it often performs best as a fall crop. Harvest while the spirals are tight and firm.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or late summer; long days and heat can trigger bolting.
directions: 'Romanesco', a Florence (bulbing) fennel, forms a crisp, anise-flavored bulb prized raw, sauteed, or in soups and pasta, with feathery edible foliage. Direct sow shallowly and thin to 6-12 inches, keeping the plants evenly watered to encourage plump bulbs and discourage bolting. Mounding soil around the base as bulbs swell keeps them tender and pale. Grow in the cool ends of the season and harvest bulbs once they reach the size of a fist.

plant_note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes for a midsummer harvest.
directions: Romanian Red is a porcelain hardneck garlic that yields large, dense bulbs of usually 4-5 easy-to-peel cloves with a hot, robust flavor and excellent storage. Separate bulbs into cloves just before planting and set each clove pointed end up, 2 inches deep and 4-6 inches apart, in fall. Mulch heavily after the ground cools to protect against heaving over winter. Snap off the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into bulb sizing. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy spot for two to three weeks.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring for roots to harvest in fall; this drought-tolerant perennial establishes easily.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly in loose, deep soil so the taproots can grow long and straight. This hardy, drought-resistant perennial produces nutrient-dense greens and thick roots traditionally roasted as a coffee substitute. Thin seedlings to give each plant room, and keep beds weeded while young plants establish. Dig roots in fall of the first year for coffee-style use, and enjoy the bitter young leaves in salads throughout the season.

plant_note: Start indoors early with warmth; transplant only when nights stay warm.
directions: 'Rosa Bianca' is an Italian heirloom eggplant bearing plump, scalloped 4-6 inch fruits in pinkish-purple with white streaks, with mild, creamy, seedy-free flesh and no bitterness. Start seeds indoors 8 weeks before the last frost with bottom heat, transplanting only after nights warm up. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and warmth, staking the plants as fruit sets. Harvest while the skin is glossy for the best texture.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; roselle needs a long growing season and should be transplanted out only after all danger of frost is past.
directions: Start seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix; germination is fastest at 80–85°F. Transplant after last frost into fertile, well-drained soil and water deeply but infrequently once established. The calyces (fleshy sepals surrounding the seedpods) are the harvested vegetable, ready when they are plump, bright red, and still tender — about a week after the flower drops. Harvest calyces promptly every few days as they become fibrous if left on the plant too long; they are used fresh for teas, jams, and sauces.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer; excellent fall green.
directions: Tatsoi forms a flat, decorative rosette of spoon-shaped, dark green leaves with a mild, slightly mustardy flavor and high calcium and vitamin content. Direct sow shallowly and thin to 6-10 inches apart, or harvest crowded plants young as baby greens. It thrives in the cool weather of spring and fall and tolerates light frost, which sweetens the leaves. Cut the whole rosette or pick outer leaves for salads, stir-fries, and soups.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer; notably cold tolerant for overwintering in mild areas.
directions: This 1800s French heirloom romaine forms a compact, 12-inch head with a tender green heart wrapped in bronze-red leaves and a rich, buttery flavor. Sow seeds shallowly, barely covering them, and thin to 8-12 inches apart in full sun to partial shade. True to its name meaning 'Red Winter', it is quite cold tolerant and good for fall and mild-climate overwinter crops. Keep soil evenly moist and harvest heads before hot weather brings bitterness.

plant_note: As a winter radish, sow in mid to late summer for fall harvest and storage.
directions: 'Round Black Spanish' is a large winter radish with rough black skin and pungent, crisp white flesh grown in the U.S. since the 1800s. Sow in mid to late summer for a fall harvest, since winter radishes need a longer season and cooler weather than spring types. Thin to 4-6 inches apart and keep soil evenly moist for smooth, non-woody roots. The spiciness mellows when cooked, and roots store exceptionally well through winter.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; germinates fast in warmth.
directions: Sow seeds one inch deep in warm, fertile soil, planting a couple of seeds per spot and thinning to the strongest. This heirloom summer squash produces charming globe-shaped fruits about three to four inches across that are ideal for stuffing. Keep the plants well watered and harvest fruits young, when they are tender and the skin is still glossy. Picking frequently keeps the productive plants setting new fruit throughout the warm season.

plant_note: Direct sow once soil is warm.
directions: This round zucchini bears tender, ball-shaped fruits especially suited for stuffing or serving as individual soup bowls. Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil and give the bushy plants full sun and room to spread. Harvest fruits young at 3-4 inches wide for the best flavor, picking often to keep the plant productive. The edible blossoms are delicious fried, and consistent watering supports steady fruit set.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil; sow again every few weeks for a continuous harvest.
directions: Direct sow seeds one inch deep once frost danger has passed and soil is warm, with no trellis needed for these compact plants. The 15-20 inch bushes are well suited to containers and small gardens, producing deep-purple, stringless pods that stand out against the foliage. The pods turn green when cooked, making harvest easy since the purple is simple to spot. Pick regularly while pods are young and tender to keep the plants producing.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; germinates in cooler soil better than most beans.
directions: 'Royal Burgundy' produces striking purple pods that stand out against green foliage and turn green when cooked. Direct sow 1 inch deep, spacing seeds 3-4 inches apart in full sun; this variety germinates better in cool soil than most and is unlikely to be bothered by bean beetles. Harvest the tender pods often to keep plants producing, and enjoy them raw, steamed, or in containers. Disease resistant and dependable.

plant_note: Sow in early spring and again in late summer; beets grow best in cool weather.
directions: Sow the seed clusters half an inch deep and thin seedlings so each root has a few inches to swell. This 1957 All-America Selections winner produces round, dark-red roots with mild, sweet flavor and tender texture. Keep soil evenly moist for smooth, non-woody roots, and do not skip thinning, since each cluster yields several plants. Harvest roots when they reach two to three inches, and enjoy the nutritious greens as an added bonus.

plant_note: Sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall roots.
directions: 'Ruby Queen' is a heirloom beet with uniform, round, deeply colored roots, short green tops, and maroon leaf veins, plus mild sweet flavor. Direct sow the seed clusters 1/2 inch deep and thin seedlings to 3-4 inches apart. Frost tolerant and productive even in poor soils, it keeps its ringless color through canning and cooking. Harvest roots at 2-3 inches for the most tender texture, and use the young tops as greens.

plant_note: Sow in spring for a long harvest through fall; year-round in mild climates.
directions: 'Ruby Red' is an 1850s heirloom chard with vivid red stems and veins that beautifies the garden and provides greens from early summer into fall.
