directions: Sow the seed densely across a shallow tray of moist growing mix and keep it evenly moist under bright light. This Asian blend of savory and spicy brassica-type greens is harvested very young, when the first true leaves appear, roughly one to three weeks after sowing. Snip the tender shoots at soil level with scissors and use the complex, umami-rich mix as a garnish for Asian dishes; sow in succession for a steady supply indoors year-round.

plant_note: Sow in trays indoors, or direct sow outdoors in cool weather as a leafy green.
directions: Sow seed densely in a tray for microgreens or thinly outdoors in cool soil to grow as a peppery leafy green. Also called creasy greens, upland cress is high in vitamins A and C and delivers a sharp, watercress-like bite without needing running water. Keep it consistently moist, since dryness makes it more pungent and prone to bolting; harvest microgreens at the true-leaf stage or pick mature outer leaves for sandwiches and salads.

plant_note: Start indoors well ahead of transplanting due to slow growth.
directions: Start Utah celery seeds indoors 10 to 12 weeks before transplanting, pressing the tiny seeds onto the surface since they need light to germinate. Transplant seedlings 6 to 10 inches apart into rich, constantly moist soil. This All-America Selections heirloom produces thick, upright, nearly stringless stalks up to 12 inches tall. Feed regularly and never let the soil dry out, as drought stress causes bitter, hollow stalks.

plant_note: Start indoors early; celery needs a long, mild, moist growing season.
directions: Start the tiny seed indoors 10 to 12 weeks before transplanting, pressing it into the surface since it needs light to germinate and is slow to sprout. Celery grows best in climates with long, mild-temperature seasons and demands consistently rich, moist soil to develop tender stalks. Harvest individual stalks when they reach about 6 inches, or wait for the full bunch to mature; keep the plants well watered throughout to avoid stringy, bitter stems.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; give vines room to run.
directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after all danger of frost, giving the vines plenty of room to spread. This heirloom is unique among winter squashes: when cooked, the flesh separates into spaghetti-like strands that stay tender yet al dente. Let fruits fully ripen to a hard, golden rind and cure them before storing; the mild, low-calorie flesh is delicious with a bit of butter or a favorite sauce.

plant_note: Start indoors, then transplant into a permanent bed after frost.
directions: Start Victoria rhubarb seeds indoors 8 to 10 weeks before setting plants into a permanent, fertile bed spaced 3 to 4 feet apart. This cold-hardy heirloom perennial develops deep crimson-tinged stalks with a rich sweet-tart flavor and returns for many years. Do not harvest the first year and take only a few stalks the second, letting the crown establish. Never eat the leaves, which are toxic, and remove flower stalks to keep energy in the roots.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall so seeds receive natural cold stratification over winter; spring germination will occur when soil warms. Alternatively, cold-stratify seed in the refrigerator for 60–90 days before spring sowing.
directions: Sow seeds in fall directly in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in deep to partial shade; this native eastern woodland plant is one of the few vegetables that genuinely thrives in full shade. The deeply lobed young leaves and stem tips that emerge in early spring have a mild, pleasant flavor and were an important spring green for many Indigenous peoples and early settlers. Harvest only young growth before the plant begins to flower, as older leaves become coarser. The plant spreads by rhizome to form colonies and self-sows readily, making it a productive and low-maintenance component of an edible shade garden. Goes dormant in summer heat.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or again in late summer for fall.
directions: Sow Viroflay seeds half an inch deep in cool soil, thinning to 4 to 6 inches apart for full-size plants. This vigorous French heirloom produces large, tender leaves up to 10 inches long with a mild, sweet taste. Harvest baby leaves early or let plants mature for hearty cooking greens, and keep the soil evenly moist. Grow it in the cool weather of spring, fall, or winter, since heat and long days trigger bolting.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant in cool weather; bolt resistant for later sowing.
directions: Sow seed shallowly in cool weather, keeping the soil moist and cool for good germination, and make succession sowings for continuous salads. This emerald-green romaine forms tight heads that reach a striking 12 to 16 inches tall and 4 to 6 inches across, giving large, sturdy leaves ideal for salads and lettuce wraps. Its bolt resistance and vigorous growth let it hold longer into warm weather than many romaines.

plant_note: Start indoors or overwinter in mild areas; a long-day type that matures early.
directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant out once the soil is workable, or sow in fall to overwinter in mild-winter regions. This famous long-day onion traveled from Italy to Walla Walla, Washington, and produces enormous, juicy, sweet bulbs that are one of the best for eating fresh. It matures early enough to grow as an intermediate-day type; these sweet onions do not store long, so enjoy them soon after harvest.

plant_note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant in early spring.
directions: Start Walla Walla seeds indoors 8 to 10 weeks before your last frost and transplant seedlings 4 to 6 inches apart. This long-day heirloom produces exceptionally sweet, crisp, juicy yellow bulbs and performs best in northern gardens. Grow in full sun with consistent moisture and keep the bed weeded while the large bulbs form. These onions are sweet but do not store long, so use them fresh soon after the tops fall and dry.

plant_note: Start for a fall crop, or in early spring for a spring harvest.
directions: Start Waltham 29 seeds indoors 5 to 6 weeks before transplanting, setting plants 18 to 24 inches apart in rich soil. This cold-tolerant heirloom is bred for fall harvests, producing large blue-green central heads followed by side shoots. Keep the plants well watered and feed with nitrogen to encourage big, tight heads. Cut the central head while the buds are still firm and closed, then keep harvesting the smaller side shoots for weeks.

plant_note: Start indoors for spring, or sow in summer for a fall crop; adapted to fall.
directions: Start seed indoors for a spring crop or sow in mid to late summer for a fall harvest, spacing transplants 18 to 24 inches apart. This heirloom is specially adapted to fall growing and produces 4 to 6 inch blue-green central heads followed by a prolific flush of side shoots. Cut the central head while the buds are still tight, then keep harvesting the smaller side shoots for weeks; the plants tolerate frost well.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; give vines plenty of space to run.
directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after all danger of frost, allowing the long vines room to spread. This classic butternut yields four to five tan, richly sweet fruits per plant, and its solid stems help resist squash vine borers. Let the squash mature fully to a hard rind and cure it in a warm, dry spot; properly cured butternut stores for months and is excellent roasted, baked, or pureed into soup.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as the soil can be worked; more heat tolerant than most peas.
directions: Direct sow seed as soon as the ground can be worked in spring, since peas germinate and grow best in cool soil. Introduced in 1943 as a cross between Laxton's Progress and Perfection, this heirloom is both cold tolerant for early sowing and more heat tolerant than most peas, extending the season on both ends. The sturdy 24 to 30 inch plants carry seven to nine medium peas per pod; provide light support and pick regularly to keep them producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; cold hardy and can overwinter in many areas.
directions: Direct sow seed in spring for summer harvests, thinning to about an inch apart, or leave clumps to overwinter. This 2016 All-America Selections winner is a cold-hardy bunching onion with crisp texture and a strong, pungent flavor, and it resists pink root rot. Harvest by pulling whole clusters of upright green stalks, or cut individual stems and let the plants regrow for repeated cut-and-come-again picking.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall in cool, constantly moist, highly oxygenated, slightly acidic to neutral, rich soil in partial to full shade; wasabi requires temperatures between 46–70°F and is extremely temperature-sensitive — it will not survive zone 9 summers without deep shade, cooling irrigation, and careful microclimate management.
directions: Plant in consistently moist, cool, well-oxygenated, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial to full shade; wasabi is one of the most challenging and rewarding edible plants a gardener can grow, requiring very cool temperatures (46–70°F) and near-constant moisture. In Japan it grows along cold mountain streams in deep shade where water temperature remains near 55°F year-round. The thick rhizome (the 'root') takes 12–18 months to reach harvest size, grated fresh at the table in a circular motion on a sharkskin grater to produce the intensely pungent, clean-heat paste. The authentic flavor is entirely different from the horseradish-and-dye paste sold as 'wasabi' in most restaurants. In zone 7–8, wasabi can be grown in a deeply shaded, cool, constantly moist garden bed or container with refrigerated-water irrigation; in zone 9 it requires careful site selection, mulching to keep roots cool, and may struggle in summer heat. The large, heart-shaped leaves are also edible and pungent.

plant_note: Plant sprouted corms 2–4 weeks after last frost once water temperatures exceed 60°F in a container or shallow pond.
directions: Sprout small corms in a tray of moist soil indoors a few weeks before transplanting; plant sprouted corms 1–2 inches deep in the mud bottom of containers, tubs, or shallow water features kept filled to 2–4 inches above the soil surface. Water chestnuts are tropical grass-like sedges grown for their crunchy, sweet corms that form in the mud at the base of the rush-like stems. Drain the growing container in early fall before frost and allow the foliage to die back completely, then harvest corms by sifting through the mud; the crisp, mild corms can be eaten raw or cooked and retain their crunch even after stir-frying. In frost-prone climates store harvested corms in moist sand in a cool (40°F) location and re-plant the following spring.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant stem cuttings 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil and water temperatures are consistently warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep or root stem cuttings in moist to waterlogged soil; this semi-aquatic vine thrives in boggy beds, the margins of ponds, or in containers of shallow water. Harvest young shoot tips and tender leaves 4–6 inches long by snipping with scissors; plants regrow quickly and can be cut every 2–3 weeks throughout the warm season. The hollow stems and arrowhead-shaped leaves are a staple of Thai, Filipino, Chinese, and Indonesian cooking, most famously stir-fried with garlic and shrimp paste. Note that cultivation is restricted in some U.S. states due to invasive potential in warm climates; check local regulations before planting outdoors.

plant_note: Sow in cool, wet conditions 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also be started from cuttings rooted in water.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of consistently moist, fertile soil near a stream, pond edge, or other reliably wet site; watercress requires more water than almost any other salad green. In garden beds without standing water, water deeply every day and mulch heavily to retain moisture. Harvest stem tips of 4–6 in regularly to encourage bushy growth; the plant becomes bitter and coarse after flowering. Succession plantings from rooted stem cuttings placed in shallow water provide a continuous supply through the cool season.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches at least 70°F; or start indoors 2–3 weeks early.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, sandy loam hills with generous compost worked in; watermelons thrive with long, hot summers. Avoid overhead irrigation once fruits are sizing up, as uneven moisture causes cracking. Ripeness indicators include a dull surface sheen replacing the glossy skin, a cream or yellow ground spot on the underside, and a dry tendril nearest the fruit. Thump the fruit and listen for a deep, hollow resonance to confirm ripeness.

plant_note: Best sown in late summer for a fall harvest to avoid bolting.
directions: Sow watermelon radish seeds half an inch deep and thin to 3 to 4 inches apart to give the large daikon-type roots room. This heirloom, also called Shinrimei, forms round roots with pale green shoulders and a vivid rose-pink interior that has a sweet, mild flavor. It grows best as a fall crop, since cool weather keeps the flesh crisp and prevents bolting. Harvest before hard freezes and store the roots cool for weeks of eating.

plant_note: Sow 8–10 weeks before first fall frost for the sweetest, most vibrant roots; spring plantings are possible but fall is strongly preferred.
directions: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in loose, deeply prepared, stone-free soil in late summer or early fall; thin seedlings to 4–6 inches for proper root development. Watermelon radish is a Chinese heirloom daikon type with a pale green exterior that conceals a vivid magenta-to-pink interior resembling watermelon flesh. Fall planting is strongly preferred over spring — cool nights concentrate sugars and deepen the interior color while reducing pungency. Harvest when roots reach 2–3 inches in diameter; slice thinly for salads, pickle in rice vinegar, or roast where the color intensifies to brilliant crimson.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in spring; fall sowing in mild climates produces overwintered clumps ready for early spring harvest.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in rows and thin to 4–6 inches, or start in trays and transplant in clusters of 3–5 seedlings. Welsh onion does not form a bulb but produces clumps of hollow, cylindrical green stalks that are harvested like scallions; the entire green top and white base are edible. Harvest outer stalks from established clumps as needed, leaving the center to continue growing; clumps also divide easily and can be split every 2–3 years. Extremely cold-hardy and virtually maintenance-free once established, it serves as a permanent perennial scallion in most temperate gardens.

plant_note: Soak seed, then sow densely in trays indoors year-round for juicing.
directions: Soak the hard red winter wheat seed overnight, then spread it densely over a tray of moist growing mix and keep it evenly moist and bright. The seed sprouts rapidly into lush green blades that are ready to cut in about eight to twelve days, when they reach 6 to 9 inches tall. Snip the grass at the base for juicing, which yields a drink rich in vitamins, minerals, and chlorophyll; sow fresh trays in succession for a continuous supply.

plant_note: Start indoors in winter; a short-day type best for southern gardens.
directions: Start White Grano seeds indoors 8 to 10 weeks before transplanting, setting seedlings 4 to 6 inches apart. This short-day heirloom forms smooth white bulbs about 3 inches across with mild, low-pungency flavor and does best in southern regions. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and keep the bed free of weeds while bulbs develop. Harvest when the tops fall over, then cure in a dry, airy place before short-term storage.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; slow to germinate, so keep soil moist.
directions: Sow White Half Long seeds half an inch deep in deep, loose soil and keep the surface consistently moist, since parsnips germinate slowly and unevenly. Use fresh seed each year and thin seedlings to 3 to 6 inches apart. This heirloom develops smooth, creamy-white roots with a sweet, nutty flavor that deepens after frost. Leave the roots in the ground through cold weather and harvest as needed, since freezing converts starches to sugars.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: Direct sow seed in cool weather for spring and fall crops, thinning seedlings to two to four inches apart. This premium Japanese salad turnip forms pure white, smooth-textured roots that are mild and sweet, delicious raw in salads and crudite trays as well as pickled or cooked. Grow it quickly with steady moisture and harvest young as a baby turnip for the best crisp, tender quality.

plant_note: Direct sow in succession spring through summer; heat and cold tolerant.
directions: Direct sow seed in succession from spring through summer, thinning to about an inch apart for a steady supply of scallions. Popular since the 1700s, this dependable heirloom green onion thrives in a wide range of soils and tolerates both heat and cold. Blanch the stems by hilling soil around the bases for longer white shanks, and harvest young for mild flavor before any bulb forms.

plant_note: Tolerates cooler soil than common beans but still frost tender.
directions: White-flowered runner bean prized for its large white seeds and vigorous ornamental vines. Sow seeds 1.5 inches deep at the base of a tall trellis or teepee once soil warms to 60 F. Keep soil evenly moist during flowering to ensure good pod set, as heat above 90 F can cause blossom drop. Harvest young pods for snap use or allow to dry on the vine for shell beans. In mild climates the fleshy roots may overwinter and resprout the following spring.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or late summer; flavor sweetens with cold weather.
directions: Direct sow or transplant this kale in spring or late summer, spacing plants 12 to 18 inches apart. Introduced in 1994, White Russian is a white-ribbed cross between Red Russian and Siberian kale that grows especially sweet in cool weather and stands up well to frost. Harvest young leaves for tender baby greens or let plants mature for full-sized leaves; a hard frost only improves the sweet flavor.

plant_note: Deep sandy loam gives the straightest, smoothest roots.
directions: A creamy white carrot with crisp, sweet, mild flesh and no pigment, popular in mixed rainbow bunches. Sow shallowly in deeply worked sandy loam and maintain constant surface moisture until emergence. Thin carefully to prevent crowding, which causes twisted or stunted roots. Keep beds weeded early since carrot seedlings are easily overtaken. Harvest at full size, wash gently, and store in cold, humid conditions to retain crispness.

plant_note: Direct sow once the soil is warm and frost has passed.
directions: Sow White Scallop seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil, thinning to one strong plant every 24 to 36 inches. This heirloom patty pan squash produces flat, scalloped-edge fruits with tender white flesh on productive bush plants. Harvest the fruits young at 2 to 4 inches across for the best flavor and texture, picking often to keep new ones forming. Keep the soil evenly moist and watch for squash bugs during warm weather.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Sow White Vienna seeds half an inch deep and thin to 4 to 6 inches apart for well-formed bulbs. This heirloom cole crop develops smooth, pale green globes with crisp, mild, slightly sweet flesh where the stem swells above the soil. Keep growth fast and steady with even moisture, and harvest the bulbs while they are 2 to 3 inches across before they turn woody. Both the swollen stem and the young leaves are edible raw or cooked.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 2 inches deep in fall beneath deciduous trees; the plant grows actively in late winter and spring before the tree canopy leafs out, then goes dormant in summer.
directions: Plant bulbs in autumn in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to partial shade beneath deciduous trees; wild garlic is a spring ephemeral of European and Asian deciduous forests that completes its entire growing cycle from late winter through late spring before going fully dormant. The broad, lance-shaped leaves with a distinctive garlic aroma can be harvested as soon as they emerge in late winter for salads, pesto, and compound butters; they are milder and more versatile than cultivated garlic leaves. White star-shaped flowers in spring are also edible and edible. The plant naturalizes freely by self-seeding to form large carpets — plant densely from the start for a showy spring display under trees.

plant_note: Direct sow very early in spring, or in fall in mild-winter regions.
directions: Direct sow seed very early in spring as soon as the soil can be worked, or in fall where winters are mild, since fava beans need cool weather to set pods. The sturdy, upright plants bear ornamental white-and-black flowers and reach several feet tall. Eat the young pods like snap beans, pinch the tender growing tips as a spring green, or shell the beans and cook them while still green for a sweet, buttery treat.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 70°F; a truly tropical legume requiring a long, warm, humid season.
directions: Scarify or soak seeds 12–24 hours before sowing 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained, low-fertility soil; inoculate with cowpea rhizobium to promote nitrogen fixation. Provide a sturdy trellis or fence at least 6 feet tall as soon as plants emerge, as vines climb rapidly and are very productive when vertical. Virtually every part of the winged bean is edible — harvest young pods at 3–4 inches with frilly wings still tender, leaves and shoot tips as greens, flowers as a colorful salad garnish, and mature dried seeds as high-protein pulses. In the tropics, tuberous roots also form and can be harvested and eaten like yam.

plant_note: Plant whole small tubers or tuber sections 3–4 inches deep after soil warms to 65°F; provide a sturdy trellis or fence for the rapidly growing twining stems.
directions: Plant tuber sections with at least one dormant eye in fertile, loose, deeply dug soil (tubers can reach 18 inches long) in full sun to partial shade; provide a strong trellis as vines grow vigorously to 15–20 feet. The large, starchy tubers are harvested after the vines die back in fall; a single tuber can weigh 20–50 pounds in good conditions. The most widely cultivated true yam in the world, Dioscorea alata is eaten boiled, baked, fried, or pounded into fufu and is a dietary staple across the tropics. In zone 8 the tubers may survive mild winters if mulched deeply; in zones 9–11 the plant can become invasive — check local regulations and avoid planting near natural areas.

plant_note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes and soil is warm.
directions: Sow 1/2 inch deep in rich, warm soil after last frost; winter melon is a large-fruited cucurbit that needs a long warm season and a very sturdy trellis or ample ground space. The developing fruits are covered in fine white hairs that mature into a waxy white coating, which gives the fruit exceptional storage life of up to a year at room temperature. Harvest mature fruits when the waxy coating is fully developed and the fruit sounds hollow when tapped. Young fruits can also be harvested at 6–8 inches for immediate cooking in soups and stir-fries.

plant_note: Transplant small rhizome divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in full shade; it self-sows to naturalize where conditions are right.
directions: Transplant rhizome divisions into consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade; this delicate woodland plant with clover-like three-part leaves and white or pale pink flowers naturally carpets the floor of coniferous and mixed forests in temperate regions around the world. The leaves have a bright, tart, lemony flavor from oxalic acid and have been used as a trail snack, salad green, and flavoring for centuries — the same sour flavor as rhubarb or sorrel but milder. Consume in moderation, as large quantities of oxalic acid can interfere with calcium absorption. The plant goes dormant in summer heat; it is best suited to northern gardens and cool maritime climates.

plant_note: Plant sprouted crown divisions at or just after last frost once soil is 60°F; start in pots indoors 4–6 weeks earlier in short-season climates.
directions: Divide the crown (the reddish propagating buds at the top of the storage root cluster) in early spring, allowing cut surfaces to dry for a day before planting 3–4 inches deep. Yacon grows into a large, sunflower-family plant producing crisp, sweet, apple-flavored storage tubers at the base. Harvest after the first frost kills the tops, digging the entire root cluster carefully; separate the large tan storage roots from the reddish crown pieces, saving the crowns in a frost-free location for replanting. Let harvested storage roots cure in sunlight for a few weeks to convert starch to fructooligosaccharides and intensify sweetness.

plant_note: Sow 2–6 weeks after last frost once soil is warm; yardlong beans love Gulf Coast heat and humidity and outperform regular beans all summer.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep once soil is reliably warm; yardlong beans are vigorous climbers that need a sturdy trellis or fence at least 6 feet tall. Unlike common beans, they thrive in high heat and set pods prolifically during summer weather that causes snap beans to drop their blossoms. Harvest when pods are pencil-thin and roughly 12–18 in long, well before they reach their potential 36 in — longer pods become fibrous and bitter. Check daily once pods begin forming, as they move from prime to over-mature in just 2–3 days.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all frost danger passes.
directions: Start Yellow Bell seeds indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost in warm soil, then transplant 12 to 18 inches apart once nights stay warm. This heirloom ripens from green to a bright golden yellow with sweet, crisp, thick-walled flesh. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and stake plants carrying a heavy fruit load. Harvest peppers green for firmness or leave them to color up fully for maximum sweetness.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches at least 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in hills of 2–3, thinning to one plant once established, in soil amended with compost for steady nutrient supply. Water consistently at the base to keep foliage dry and reduce disease. The distinctive curved neck makes ripe fruit easy to spot. Harvest fruits at 4–6 inches long with the skin still tender enough to dent with a fingernail for the best eating quality.

plant_note: Start indoors in winter; a short-day type for southern gardens.
directions: Start seed indoors in winter and transplant to the garden while still cool, since this is a short-day onion that bulbs best in southern regions. For really sweet, mild bulbs, grow it in soil that is low in sulfur; this pink-root-resistant type is among the few approved to be sold as a Vidalia onion. Keep plants weed-free and evenly watered, and cure the harvested bulbs before use, as sweet onions do not store long.

plant_note: Start indoors early; germination is slow and needs warmth.
directions: Start Yellow Habanero seeds indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, keeping the soil around 85F since this species germinates slowly. Transplant the plants 18 to 24 inches apart into a warm, sunny bed. The lantern-shaped fruits ripen golden-yellow with intense heat around 100,000 to 350,000 Scoville units and a sweet, tropical, fruity flavor. Handle the ripe pods with gloves, and harvest steadily to keep the productive plants setting fruit through the warm season.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost and transplant after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: This indeterminate heirloom produces heavy clusters of small, bright yellow pear-shaped fruits with a mild, tangy flavor. Provide sturdy caging or staking, since vines sprawl vigorously all season. Pinch suckers if you want a more manageable plant and pick fruit regularly to keep production going. Water consistently at the base to avoid cracking and blossom-end rot.

plant_note: Direct sow 1 to 2 weeks after the last frost once soil is thoroughly warm.
directions: This compact, early-maturing heirloom yields round 5 to 7 pound melons with sweet yellow flesh, ideal for small gardens and containers. Sow in warm soil and give vines room to run along the ground or a low trellis with fruit support. Water deeply but reduce moisture as fruits ripen to concentrate sweetness. Harvest when the tendril nearest the fruit browns and the ground spot turns creamy.

plant_note: Start indoors 10 to 8 weeks before the last frost and transplant while weather is still cool.
directions: This heirloom long-day variety forms large, golden globe bulbs with a mild, sweet flavor, best suited to northern gardens with long summer days. Start seed early indoors and transplant seedlings while cool weather encourages leafy growth before bulbing. Keep beds weed-free and evenly moist, then taper off water as tops fall over. Cure the harvested bulbs in a dry, airy spot before storage.

common_name: Yellow Sweet Spanish Utah Bulb Onion
plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out; a long-day type for northern gardens.
directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant out as soon as the soil can be worked, spacing plants four to six inches apart. This long-day heirloom, grown in the U.S. since at least 1916, forms large 3 to 6 inch globe-shaped bulbs with mild, sweet flavor and grows best in states north of the 37th parallel. Keep the soil evenly moist during bulbing, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place before storing.

plant_note: Grown mainly as a dry bean; needs full season to mature seed.
directions: Also called orca or calypso bean, this dry bush bean bears striking black-and-white seeds resembling the yin-yang symbol. Direct sow 1 inch deep after frost danger passes, spacing 4 inches apart in rows. Allow pods to dry fully on the plant until they rattle before harvesting. Pull whole plants and cure them in a dry, airy spot if wet weather threatens the maturing crop. Reduce watering as pods fill to encourage even drying and prevent mold.

plant_note: Harvest flowering stems just as buds open for best flavor.
directions: Grown for its tender flowering stems, yu choy is a fast staple of Cantonese cooking with a mild, sweet flavor. Direct sow and thin to about 4 inches, keeping the soil consistently moist for quick, succulent growth. Harvest the main stem with a few leaves and a cluster of yellow buds when the first flowers just begin to open. Cutting the central stalk encourages tender side shoots for a second picking. Grow in cool weather to avoid the bitterness and toughness that come with heat.

plant_note: Start indoors from seed in late winter and transplant out in early spring.
directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant seedlings out in early spring, spacing them several inches apart. This eschalion, or banana-type shallot, produces large, elongated, easy-to-peel bulbs with coppery-pink skin and a sweet, mild flavor prized by chefs. Grow it much like an onion with steady moisture and weed control, then cure the harvested bulbs in a dry, airy spot; they store well for months.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil is warm; can also start indoors 2–3 weeks before transplanting.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, compost-enriched soil arranged in hills of 2–3 seeds, then thin to the strongest plant. Water at the soil level rather than overhead to reduce the risk of powdery mildew, which is the most common problem. Check plants daily once fruiting begins, as zucchini grows very rapidly. Harvest fruits at 6–8 inches for the best texture, leaving a short stem attached.
